> - The **M-Step Engine** aggregates responsibilities into new parameter estimates; the loops over $n$ dominate as $\mathcal{O}(NKD)$.  
> - The **Monotonicity Check** is a debugging safeguard: if log-likelihood ever *decreases*, the implementation has a bug (often a sign error in the covariance update).

## 4.3 Soft Cluster vs. Hard Cluster — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HardCluster[K-Means Hard Clustering]
        h1[Point x_n] --> h2[argmax assignment]
        h2 --> h3[Label is single integer 1 to K]
    end
    subgraph SoftCluster[EM-GMM Soft Clustering]
        s1[Point x_n] --> s2[Posterior vector gamma]
        s2 --> s3[Soft probabilities for ALL K clusters]
    end
    HardCluster --> Comparison
    SoftCluster --> Comparison[Comparison: Soft preserves uncertainty]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **hard clustering** and **soft clustering**. Give one example algorithm for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hard Clustering | Soft Clustering |
|---|---|---|
| Assignment | Each point → exactly one cluster | Each point → probability over all clusters |
| Output | Discrete label $c_n \in \{1, \dots, K\}$ | Responsibility vector $\gamma(z_{nk})$ |
| Example | **K-Means** | **EM for Gaussian Mixture Models** |
| Boundary | Crisp, piecewise linear (Voronoi) | Smooth, probabilistic (Gaussian) |

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** State the **Expectation Maximization (EM) algorithm**. Why is it preferred over direct MLE for GMMs?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
EM is an iterative two-step procedure consisting of the **E-step** (compute posterior responsibilities $\gamma(z_{nk})$ for latent cluster assignments given current parameters) and the **M-step** (maximize the expected complete-data log-likelihood w.r.t. parameters $\pi_k, \mu_k, \Sigma_k$). It is preferred over direct MLE because the marginal log-likelihood of a GMM contains a $\ln \sum_k (\cdot)$ structure whose gradient equations are **not analytically solvable** in closed form. EM side-steps this by introducing latent variables and optimizing a tractable lower bound (ELBO), and is guaranteed to monotonically increase the likelihood at every iteration.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 14 Marks]**

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **responsibility formula** $\gamma(z_{nk})$ used in the E-step of the EM algorithm for a Gaussian Mixture Model, starting from the log-likelihood expression and using Jensen's inequality.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider $N = 4$ data points in 1-D: $X = \{1, 2, 9, 10\}$. A GMM with $K = 2$ components is initialized with $\pi_1 = \pi_2 = 0.5$, $\mu_1 = 1.5$, $\mu_2 = 9.5$, $\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = 1$. Compute the responsibilities $\gamma(z_{nk})$ for all $n$ and $k$ after the E-step, and then update all parameters in the M-step.

---

### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

#### (a) Derivation of $\gamma(z_{nk})$ — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Write the data log-likelihood of the GMM:**

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \ln \left\{ \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right\}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce distribution $q(z_{nk})$** over latent variables with $q(z_{nk}) \ge 0$, $\sum_k q(z_{nk}) = 1$:

$$= \sum_{n=1}^{N} \ln \left\{ \sum_{k=1}^{K} q(z_{nk}) \frac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{q(z_{nk})} \right\}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Jensen's inequality** for concave $\ln$:

$$\ge \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} q(z_{nk}) \ln \left\{ \frac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{q(z_{nk})} \right\} \equiv \mathcal{L}(q, \theta)$$

**Step 4 — Maximize $\mathcal{L}$ w.r.t. $q(z_{nk})$** with Lagrange multiplier $\lambda_n$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial q(z_{nk})}\left[ \mathcal{L} + \lambda_n\!\left(\sum_k q(z_{nk}) - 1\right) \right] = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow q(z_{nk}) = \pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \, e^{\lambda_n - 1}$$

**Step 5 — Apply normalization** $\sum_k q(z_{nk}) = 1$ to get $e^{\lambda_n - 1} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_j \pi_j \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}$.

**Step 6 — Substituting back:**

$$\boxed{\; \gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)} \;} \quad \text{(3 marks for final boxed formula)}$$

#### (b) Numerical EM Iteration — 7 marks

**E-Step:** Compute $\mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \sigma_k^2) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x_n - \mu_k)^2}{2\sigma_k^2}\right)$.

| $x_n$ | $\mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_1=1.5, 1)$ | $\mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_2=9.5, 1)$ | $\gamma(z_{n1})$ | $\gamma(z_{n2})$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1  | $0.3521$ | $0.0000$ (effectively) | $\approx 1.000$ | $\approx 0.000$ |
| 2  | $0.3521$ | $0.0000$ (effectively) | $\approx 1.000$ | $\approx 0.000$ |
| 9  | $0.0000$ (effectively) | $0.3521$ | $\approx 0.000$ | $\approx 1.000$ |
| 10 | $0.0000$ (effectively) | $0.3521$ | $\approx 0.000$ | $\approx 1.000$ |

**M-Step:** With $N_1 = 2$ and $N_2 = 2$:

$$\mu_1^{\text{new}} = \frac{1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2}{2} = 1.5, \quad \mu_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{1 \cdot 9 + 1 \cdot 10}{2} = 9.5$$

$$\sigma_1^{2,\text{new}} = \frac{(1-1.5)^2 + (2-1.5)^2}{2} = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25, \quad \sigma_2^{2,\text{new}} = 0.25$$

$$\pi_1^{\text{new}} = 2/4 = 0.5, \quad \pi_2^{\text{new}} = 0.5$$

> ✅ The means and mixing coefficients remain at initialization (well-separated clusters), but the **variances shrink** from $1.0$ to $0.25$ — showing EM is tightening the Gaussians to fit the local density of the data.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **concept of latent variables** in the context of soft clustering. Show mathematically how introducing latent indicators $z_{nk}$ converts the GMM log-likelihood into a tractable **complete-data log-likelihood**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **convergence properties** of the EM algorithm. Why does EM only guarantee a **local maximum**? Suggest **two strategies** to mitigate this limitation.

---

### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

#### (a) Latent Variables and Complete-Data Likelihood — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Definition:** The latent variable $z_{nk} \in \{0, 1\}$ is a 1-of-$K$ binary indicator such that $z_{nk} = 1$ if datum $x_n$ was generated by component $k$, and $0$ otherwise, with the constraint $\sum_k z_{nk} = 1$.

**Step 2 — Marginal likelihood of a single point:**

$$p(x_n) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} p(z_k = 1) \, p(x_n \mid z_k = 1) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$$

**Step 3 — Complete-data log-likelihood** (treating the $z_{nk}$'s as observed):

$$\ln p(X, Z \mid \theta) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} z_{nk} \left[ \ln \pi_k + \ln \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right]$$

This is **linear in $z_{nk}$** and thus its MLE is closed-form (just like K-Means), unlike the original log-likelihood which has $\ln \sum_k$.

**Step 4 — Expected complete-data log-likelihood** under the current posterior $q(z_{nk}) = \gamma(z_{nk})$:

$$Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}}) = \mathbb{E}_Z[\ln p(X, Z \mid \theta)] = \sum_{n,k} \gamma(z_{nk}) \left[ \ln \pi_k + \ln \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right]$$

> ✅ The M-step maximizes $Q(\theta, \theta^{\text{old}})$ w.r.t. $\theta$, and because the $z_{nk}$'s are now **linear** weights rather than hidden indicators, closed-form updates follow.

#### (b) Convergence Properties of EM — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Monotonicity:** EM is provably monotonic in the log-likelihood:

$$\ln p(X \mid \theta^{(t+1)}) \ge \ln p(X \mid \theta^{(t)})$$

This follows because the E-step tightens the Jensen lower bound so that $\mathcal{L}(q^{(t)}, \theta^{(t)}) = \ln p(X \mid \theta^{(t)})$, and the M-step increases $\mathcal{L}$, so $\ln p$ cannot decrease.

**Step 2 — Local Optimum:** EM only guarantees a **stationary point** (local max, local min, or saddle). This is because:  
(i) The ELBO is generally **non-convex** in $\theta$.  
(ii) Each M-step may ascend a different valley depending on the E-step's responsibilities.

**Step 3 — Two Mitigation Strategies:**

1. **Multiple Random Restarts:** Run EM $M$ times (typically $M = 10$–$50$) with different initializations (e.g., K-Means++ or random perturbation) and keep the run with the **highest final log-likelihood** $\ln p(X \mid \theta^{\text{final}})$.  
2. **Smart Initialization via K-Means:** Run K-Means first to obtain cluster centres, then use them as initial $\mu_k$ values for GMM-EM. This places the starting parameters near the natural data partitions, reducing the risk of poor local maxima.

> *(Bonus 1 mark: Mention determinism-annealing EM or variational Bayes EM for fuller credit.)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Where students lose marks in EM-GMM questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**  
> 1. **❌ Forgetting the $\sum_j$ in the denominator** of $\gamma(z_{nk})$ — gives non-normalized values > 1. (-1 to -2 marks)  
> 2. **❌ Using $N$ instead of $N_k$** in the M-step updates for $\mu_k$ and $\Sigma_k$. (-1 mark)  
> 3. **❌ Computing the covariance update with the *old* $\mu_k$** instead of $\mu_k^{\text{new}}$. (-1 mark)  
> 4. **❌ Failing to state the constraint $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$** when deriving the mixing-coefficient update. (-1 mark)  
> 5. **❌ Confusing ELBO maximization with KL-divergence minimization** in derivation parts.  
> 6. **❌ Skipping numerical details** in worked problems — KTU examiners expect at least the **first EM iteration computed step-by-step**.  
> 7. **❌ Writing $\mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu, \Sigma)$ without the $1/((2\pi)^{D/2}\vert\Sigma\vert^{1/2})$ normalization** when the question demands the full form.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for EM Soft Clustering (KTU 2024 Scheme):**  
> - **EM = E-step + M-step**, iterated until log-likelihood change $\vert \ln p^{(t+1)} - \ln p^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$.  
> - **E-step output:** responsibilities $\gamma(z_{nk}) = p(z_k = 1 \mid x_n)$ — a *posterior*, not a *prior*.  
> - **M-step outputs:** updated $\mu_k^{\text{new}}, \Sigma_k^{\text{new}}, \pi_k^{\text{new}}$ — all are *weighted* by responsibilities.  
> - **Key insight:** E-step computes the *expectation* of the complete-data log-likelihood; M-step *maximizes* it w.r.t. $\theta$.  
> - **Soft vs. Hard:** GMM-EM gives probabilities; K-Means gives a single label. K-Means is a *hard* limit of GMM-EM when $\Sigma_k = \epsilon I$ and $\epsilon \to 0$.  
> - **Convergence:** Monotonic increase of log-likelihood, but only to a *local* optimum — use **multiple restarts**.  
> - **Initialization matters:** K-Means++ or K-Means pre-training is the recommended warm start.  
> - **Computational cost:** $\mathcal{O}(NKD^2)$ per iteration, dominated by the E-step's matrix inversions and determinants.  
> - **Identifiability:** The component labels $k$ are *not identifiable* — different permutations of $k$ give the same likelihood.  
> - **Relation to K-Means:** As $\Sigma_k \to \epsilon I$ with $\epsilon \to 0$, GMM-EM collapses to K-Means.  
> - **Singularity safeguard:** Add a small $\epsilon I$ to $\Sigma_k$ (e.g., $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$) to prevent numerical collapse when a component locks onto a single point.  
> - **No closed-form MLE exists** for the marginal GMM likelihood — this is precisely *why* EM is necessary.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensionality Reduction - Principal Component Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT414 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dimensionality Reduction - Principal Component Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is a **linear, orthogonal, unsupervised dimensionality-reduction technique** that transforms a set of possibly correlated variables into a smaller set of linearly **uncorrelated** variables called **Principal Components (PCs)**, while preserving as much of the original variance (information) as possible.

Mathematically, PCA performs an **orthogonal linear transformation** that maps data from the original $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ space to a new $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ space ($k \le d$) such that the coordinates of the data in the new basis are uncorrelated and ordered by decreasing variance.

The $i^{th}$ principal component is given by:

$$Y_i = \mathbf{w}_i^{\top} \mathbf{x}$$

where $\mathbf{w}_i$ is the unit eigenvector corresponding to the $i^{th}$ largest eigenvalue of the **sample covariance matrix** of the (centered) data.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Shadow on a Wall" Analogy**
> Imagine you have a complex 3D statue placed in a room. You shine a torch on it and look at its **shadow on a flat wall**. A 3D object has been represented using a 2D image (shadow) — this is dimensionality reduction!
> - The **position of the torch** determines *which direction the light comes from* → this is exactly the **principal component direction**.
> - The **best shadow** is the one that retains the maximum "shape" information, i.e., the **maximum variance** of the original data.
> - The **length / elongation of the shadow** corresponds to the **eigenvalue** (variance captured).
> - If the statue is squashed flat (highly correlated features), even a tiny shadow can capture almost all information.

In essence, **PCA finds the best "light direction" that gives the most informative shadow** of the data.

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Need PCA? (The Motivation)

Modern datasets (e.g., genomics, image data, NLP) suffer from the **Curse of Dimensionality**. PCA addresses this by:

| Problem | How PCA Helps |
|---|---|
| **High storage & compute cost** | Reduces feature count from $d \to k$ |
| **Multicollinearity** | Produces uncorrelated components |
| **Noise reduction** | Discards low-variance (often noisy) components |
| **Visualization** | Allows projection to 2D / 3D for plotting |
| **Overfitting in ML models** | Fewer features = simpler model |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently test the *motivation* (why) before the *mechanism* (how). Always begin answers on PCA with a 1–2 line justification of dimensionality reduction before diving into the math.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of PCA

PCA is executed in **five canonical stages**. Each stage has a strict statistical purpose.

- **Step 1 — Mean Centering (and optionally Standardization)**
  - Subtract the feature mean so that the data is centered at the origin: $\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}$.
  - For features on different scales, **standardize** to unit variance: $\mathbf{z} = (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}) / \boldsymbol{\sigma}$. This prevents large-magnitude features from dominating.

- **Step 2 — Covariance Matrix Construction**
  - The covariance matrix $\mathbf{C}$ captures pairwise linear relationships between features.
  - For a centered data matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$:

$$ \mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n - 1}\,\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X} $$

  - $\mathbf{C}$ is **symmetric**, **positive semi-definite**, and has real non-negative eigenvalues.

- **Step 3 — Eigendecomposition**
  - Solve the characteristic equation $\det(\mathbf{C} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$ to obtain $d$ eigenvalues $\{\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_d\}$.
  - For each $\lambda_i$, solve $\mathbf{C} \mathbf{v}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{v}_i$ to get the eigenvectors $\mathbf{v}_i$.

- **Step 4 — Sort & Select Top-$k$ Components**
  - Sort eigenvalues in descending order: $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \cdots \ge \lambda_d$.
  - Choose the smallest $k$ such that the **cumulative explained variance ratio** meets a threshold (commonly **95%** or **99%**):

$$ \text{Cumulative Variance} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i} \ge \tau $$

- **Step 5 — Project onto the New Subspace**
  - Form the projection matrix $\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{v}_1 \; \mathbf{v}_2 \; \cdots \; \mathbf{v}_k]$.
  - Transform the data:

$$ \mathbf{Y}_{n \times k} = \mathbf{X}_{n \times d} \; \mathbf{W}_{d \times k} $$

---

## 2.2 Geometric Interpretation (The "Why" Behind Each Step)

- **Why mean-center?** Variance and covariance are measured *about the mean*. Centering places the origin at the centroid, ensuring PC1 passes through the data's "center of mass."
- **Why eigendecomposition?** The eigenvectors of $\mathbf{C}$ are the **principal axes of an ellipsoid** fitted to the data cloud; eigenvalues are the **squared axis lengths** (variances along each axis).
- **Why orthogonal?** Orthogonal transformation guarantees that the new features (PCs) are **uncorrelated**, eliminating redundancy.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table — these are the most-tested equations in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean vector | $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{x}_i$ | $d$-dimensional vector |
| 2 | Centering | $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i = \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}$ | Required before PCA |
| 3 | Standardization | $z_{ij} = (x_{ij} - \mu_j) / \sigma_j$ | When scales differ |
| 4 | Sample covariance (scalar) | $\sigma_{jk} = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{ij}-\bar{x}_j)(x_{ik}-\bar{x}_k)$ | Unbiased estimator |
| 5 | Covariance matrix | $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c$ | Symmetric, PSD |
| 6 | Characteristic eq. | $\det(\mathbf{C} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$ | Gives eigenvalues |
| 7 | Eigen-equation | $\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ | $\mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$ |
| 8 | Explained variance ratio | $\eta_i = \lambda_i \,/\, \sum_{j=1}^{d}\lambda_j$ | Sums to 1 |
| 9 | Projection matrix | $\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{v}_1, \dots, \mathbf{v}_k]$ | Columns orthonormal |
| 10 | Reduced data | $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W}$ | Shape: $n \times k$ |
| 11 | Reconstruction | $\hat{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{Y}\mathbf{W}^{\top} + \boldsymbol{\mu}$ | Lossy if $k < d$ |
| 12 | Reconstruction error | $\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i} \lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i \rVert^2 = \sum_{j=k+1}^{d} \lambda_j$ | Minimized by top-$k$ |

> **Note on notation:** Absolute value / norm is written as $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ or $\lvert \cdot \rvert$. The $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ symbol must be used in prose to avoid markdown table-breaking pipe characters.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Computer Vision:** Face recognition (Eigenfaces), image compression.
- **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression analysis, SNP data reduction.
- **Finance:** Risk factor extraction, portfolio diversification.
- **Signal Processing:** Noise filtering, feature extraction for speech.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Reconstruct errors flag outliers in high-dim data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: Why Maximize Variance?

Consider centered data $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and a unit direction $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ ($\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert = 1$). The projection of $\mathbf{x}$ onto $\mathbf{w}$ is the scalar:

$$ z = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x} $$

The **variance of $z$** across $n$ samples is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(z) &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{w}^{\top}\boldsymbol{\mu})^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(\mathbf{w}^{\top}(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})\bigr)^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbf{w}^{\top}(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{\top}\mathbf{w} \\
&= \mathbf{w}^{\top}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})(\mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu})^{\top}\right)\!\mathbf{w} \\
&= \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}
\end{aligned}
$$

We want to **maximize** $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}$ subject to $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} = 1$.

Set up the **Lagrangian**:

$$ \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} - 1) $$

Take the gradient and set it to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\mathcal{L} &= 2\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} - 2\lambda\mathbf{w} = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} &= \lambda \mathbf{w}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is precisely the **eigen-equation**. Hence, the **first principal component is the eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue**, and the projected variance equals that eigenvalue:

$$ \text{Var}(z_1) = \mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}_1 = \mathbf{w}_1^{\top}(\lambda_1 \mathbf{w}_1) = \lambda_1 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (Examiner favourite):** The direction of *maximum* variance is *identical* to the eigenvector with the *largest* eigenvalue. This elegant result is why PCA is solved by eigendecomposition.

---

## 3.2 Total Variance Preservation Proof

The total variance in the original data equals the sum of all eigenvalues (trace identity):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Variance} &= \sum_{j=1}^{d} \text{Var}(x_j) = \text{tr}(\mathbf{C}) \\
&= \text{tr}(\mathbf{V} \mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{V}^{\top}) \quad \text{(spectral decomposition)} \\
&= \text{tr}(\mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{V}^{\top}\mathbf{V}) = \text{tr}(\mathbf{\Lambda}) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j
\end{aligned}
$$

When we keep only the top $k$ components, the **variance retained** is $\sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_i$, and the **variance lost** is $\sum_{i=k+1}^{d}\lambda_i$.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Given the 2-D dataset (4 samples): $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1,2)$, $\mathbf{x}_2 = (2,3)$, $\mathbf{x}_3 = (3,4)$, $\mathbf{x}_4 = (4,5)$. Perform PCA to reduce it to **1-D**, listing the principal direction and the projected coordinates.

### **Stage 1 — Compute the Mean Vector**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1 &= \frac{1 + 2 + 3 + 4}{4} = \frac{10}{4} = 2.5 \\
\mu_2 &= \frac{2 + 3 + 4 + 5}{4} = \frac{14}{4} = 3.5
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\boldsymbol{\mu} = (2.5,\; 3.5)^{\top}$.

### **Stage 2 — Mean-Center the Data**

Subtract $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ from every row:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_1 &= (1-2.5,\; 2-3.5) = (-1.5,\; -1.5) \\
\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_2 &= (2-2.5,\; 3-3.5) = (-0.5,\; -0.5) \\
\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_3 &= (3-2.5,\; 4-3.5) = (\;\;0.5,\;\; 0.5) \\
\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_4 &= (4-2.5,\; 5-3.5) = (\;\;1.5,\;\; 1.5)
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Stage 3 — Build the Covariance Matrix**

Using $\mathbf{C} = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c$ with $n-1 = 3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{11} &= \frac{(-1.5)^2 + (-0.5)^2 + (0.5)^2 + (1.5)^2}{3} = \frac{2.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25}{3} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.6667 \\
C_{22} &= \frac{(-1.5)^2 + (-0.5)^2 + (0.5)^2 + (1.5)^2}{3} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.6667 \\
C_{12} = C_{21} &= \frac{(-1.5)(-1.5) + (-0.5)(-0.5) + (0.5)(0.5) + (1.5)(1.5)}{3} = \frac{2.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 2.25}{3} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.6667
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.6667 & 1.6667 \\ 1.6667 & 1.6667 \end{pmatrix}
$$

### **Stage 4 — Solve the Characteristic Equation**

$$
\det(\mathbf{C} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; (1.6667 - \lambda)^2 - (1.6667)^2 = 0
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda^2 - 3.3333\lambda + 2.7778 - 2.7778 &= 0 \\
\lambda^2 - 3.3333\lambda &= 0 \\
\lambda(\lambda - 3.3333) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Eigenvalues:** $\lambda_1 = 3.3333\;(= 10/3)$ and $\lambda_2 = 0$.

### **Stage 5 — Find the Eigenvectors**

**For $\lambda_1 = 10/3$**, solve $(\mathbf{C} - \lambda_1 \mathbf{I})\mathbf{v}_1 = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} -1.6667 & 1.6667 \\ 1.6667 & -1.6667 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v}_1 = \mathbf{0}
$$

Row 1 gives $-1.6667 v_{11} + 1.6667 v_{12} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; v_{12} = v_{11}$.

Normalize: $\lVert \mathbf{v}_1 \rVert^2 = 2 v_{11}^2 = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; v_{11} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

$$
\mathbf{v}_1 = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}},\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{\top} \approx (0.7071,\; 0.7071)^{\top}
$$

**For $\lambda_2 = 0$**, solve $\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v}_2 = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
1.6667 v_{21} + 1.6667 v_{22} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; v_{22} = -v_{21}
$$

$$
\mathbf{v}_2 = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}},\; -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{\top} \approx (0.7071,\; -0.7071)^{\top}
$$

### **Stage 6 — Explained Variance & Component Selection**

$$
\text{Explained ratio of PC1} = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} = \frac{3.3333}{3.3333 + 0} = 1.0 = 100\%
$$

PC1 alone captures **100% of the variance** (because the data is perfectly collinear along the line $y = x + 1$).

### **Stage 7 — Project Onto 1-D Subspace**

The projection matrix is $\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{v}_1 = (0.7071,\; 0.7071)^{\top}$.

For each sample, the 1-D coordinate is $y_i = \mathbf{v}_1^{\top}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= (0.7071)(-1.5) + (0.7071)(-1.5) = -2.1213 \\
y_2 &= (0.7071)(-0.5) + (0.7071)(-0.5) = -0.7071 \\
y_3 &= (0.7071)(0.5) + (0.7071)(0.5) = \;\;0.7071 \\
y_4 &= (0.7071)(1.5) + (0.7071)(1.5) = \;\;2.1213
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Final Result Summary**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Eigenvalues | $\lambda_1 = 10/3,\; \lambda_2 = 0$ |
| PC1 (eigenvector) | $(0.7071, 0.7071)^{\top}$ |
| PC2 (eigenvector) | $(0.7071, -0.7071)^{\top}$ |
| Variance retained (1-D) | **100%** |
| Projected 1-D data | $(-2.12, -0.71, 0.71, 2.12)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** PC1 points along the direction $y = x$ (the line along which the data lies). PC2 is orthogonal to it (pointing along $y = -x$) and captures zero variance — confirming that this 2D dataset is intrinsically 1D.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: PCA Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Input Data X n x d] --> B[Step 1 Mean Centering and Standardization]
    B --> C[Step 2 Compute Covariance Matrix C]
    C --> D[Step 3 Eigendecomposition C v equals lambda v]
    D --> E[Step 4 Sort Eigenvalues and Select Top k]
    E --> F[Step 5 Form Projection Matrix W d x k]
    F --> G[Reduced Data Y n x k equals X times W]
    G --> H[Optional Reconstruction X hat equals Y times W transpose plus mu]

    subgraph EigenSolver [Eigenvalue Computation Engine]
        D1[Characteristic Equation det C minus lambda I equals 0] --> D2[Eigenvalues lambda 1 to lambda d]
        D2 --> D3[Eigenvectors v 1 to v d]
    end

    D -.-> EigenSolver

    subgraph Decision [Variance Threshold Check]
        E1[Compute Cumulative Explained Variance] --> E2{Variance greater than or equal to threshold tau}
        E2 -- Yes --> E3[Keep k Components]
        E2 -- No --> E4[Increment k and Recompute]
    end

    E -.-> Decision
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Geometric Intuition of PCA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Original Data Cloud in 2D] --> P2[Centroid Calculation]
    P2 --> P3[Find Axis of Max Variance PC1]
    P3 --> P4[Find Orthogonal Axis PC2]
    P4 --> P5[Rotate Coordinate System]
    P5 --> P6[Project onto PC1 Only]
    P6 --> P7[1D Representation Retaining Maximum Information]

    P8[High Variance Direction lambda 1 large] -.-> P3
    P9[Low Variance Direction lambda 2 small] -.-> P4
    P10[Reconstruction Error equals sum of discarded lambdas] -.-> P6
```

## 4.3 Component Interaction Matrix

| Stage | Input | Transformation | Output | Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Raw $\mathbf{X}$ | $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu}$ | Centered | $\mathbf{X}_c$ rows sum to 0 per column |
| 2 | $\mathbf{X}_c$ | $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c/(n-1)$ | $d \times d$ matrix | Symmetric PSD |
| 3 | $\mathbf{C}$ | Eigendecomposition | $\{\lambda_i\}, \{\mathbf{v}_i\}$ | $\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v}_i = \lambda_i\mathbf{v}_i$ |
| 4 | Eigen-spectra | Sort + threshold | $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ | $\lVert \mathbf{v}_i \rVert = 1$ |
| 5 | $\mathbf{X}_c, \mathbf{W}$ | $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W}$ | $n \times k$ | Uncorrelated columns |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Principal Component Analysis. Why is mean centering a mandatory step before applying PCA?**

**Model Answer:**

Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is an unsupervised linear dimensionality reduction technique that transforms correlated high-dimensional data into a smaller set of uncorrelated variables called principal components, ordered by decreasing variance.

Mean centering is mandatory because:
1. PCA relies on the **covariance matrix**, which measures variance *about the mean*. If data is not centered, the first principal component will be biased toward the mean direction rather than the direction of maximum spread.
2. Without centering, the first eigenvector may align with the mean vector instead of with the variance structure, producing incorrect principal components.
3. Centering ensures the data cloud is positioned at the **origin**, allowing the eigendecomposition of $\mathbf{C}$ to faithfully capture the variance geometry.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the "explained variance ratio" in PCA? How is the optimal number of principal components typically chosen?**

**Model Answer:**

The **explained variance ratio** of the $i^{th}$ principal component is the fraction of the dataset's total variance that lies along that component:

$$ \eta_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j} $$

The **optimal number of components $k$** is chosen by:

1. **Cumulative Variance Threshold:** Choose the smallest $k$ such that $\sum_{i=1}^{k}\eta_i \ge \tau$, where $\tau$ is typically **0.95** or **0.99**.
2. **Scree Plot / Elbow Method:** Plot $\lambda_i$ vs. $i$ and locate the "elbow" — the point where adding more components yields diminishing variance gain.
3. **Kaiser Criterion:** Retain components with $\lambda_i > 1$ (when data is standardized).
4. **Cross-validation:** Choose $k$ that minimizes downstream model error.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Skipping mean-centering** costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> - Forgetting to **normalize eigenvectors** to unit length → lose 1 mark.
> - Not mentioning the **threshold / justification for choosing $k$** → lose 2 marks.
> - For **derivation questions**, examiners want the Lagrangian setup; jumping straight to "eigenvector of $\mathbf{C}$" loses 3–4 marks.
> - Always show the **covariance matrix explicitly** before the characteristic equation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive mathematically that the first principal component of a dataset is the eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue of its covariance matrix. State clearly the optimization objective and the constraints used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the dataset: $\mathbf{x}_1=(1,1)$, $\mathbf{x}_2=(2,2)$, $\mathbf{x}_3=(3,3)$, perform PCA. Compute the mean, the covariance matrix, the eigenvalues and eigenvectors, and the 1-D projection of each point. State the percentage of variance retained. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Objective:** Find unit direction $\mathbf{w}_1$ that **maximizes** the variance of projected data.

For centered data, projection of $\mathbf{x}_i$ on $\mathbf{w}$ is $z_i = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i$.

Variance of $z$:

$$ \text{Var}(z) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i)^2 = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} $$

**Optimization Problem:**

$$ \max_{\mathbf{w}} \; \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} = 1 $$

**Lagrangian:**

$$ \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} - 1) $$

Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ and equate to zero:

$$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} - 2\lambda\mathbf{w} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} = \lambda\mathbf{w} $$

This is the eigen-equation. The optimal $\mathbf{w}_1$ is the eigenvector of $\mathbf{C}$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_1$. The maximum projected variance is $\mathbf{w}_1^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}_1 = \lambda_1$. **(7 marks — derivation complete)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Mean:**

$$ \mu_1 = \frac{1+2+3}{3} = 2, \quad \mu_2 = \frac{1+2+3}{3} = 2 $$

**Step 2 — Centered Data:**

$$ \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_1 = (-1,-1),\; \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_2 = (0,0),\; \tilde{\mathbf{x}}_3 = (1,1) $$

**Step 3 — Covariance Matrix** (with $n-1=2$):

$$
C_{11} = \frac{(-1)^2 + 0^2 + 1^2}{2} = 1, \quad C_{22} = 1, \quad C_{12} = \frac{(-1)(-1) + 0 + (1)(1)}{2} = 1
$$

$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Eigenvalues:**

$$ \det(\mathbf{C} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = (1-\lambda)^2 - 1 = \lambda^2 - 2\lambda = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda(\lambda - 2) = 0 $$

$$ \lambda_1 = 2, \quad \lambda_2 = 0 $$

**Step 5 — Eigenvectors:**
- $\lambda_1 = 2$: $\mathbf{v}_1 = (1/\sqrt{2},\; 1/\sqrt{2})^{\top}$
- $\lambda_2 = 0$: $\mathbf{v}_2 = (1/\sqrt{2},\; -1/\sqrt{2})^{\top}$

**Step 6 — Projections on PC1** ($\mathbf{v}_1$):

$$ y_1 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-1) + \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-1) = -\sqrt{2} \approx -1.414 $$
$$ y_2 = 0 $$
$$ y_3 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1) + \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1) = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 $$

**Step 7 — Variance Retained:**

$$ \eta_1 = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} = \frac{2}{2+0} = 100\% $$

**Final answer:** 1-D data is $\{-\sqrt{2}, 0, \sqrt{2}\}$ with **100% variance retained**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the steps of the PCA algorithm in detail. Discuss why standardization is preferred over mean centering alone when features have different units. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the 2-D data points $(2, 1)$, $(3, 2)$, $(4, 3)$, $(5, 4)$, find the principal components using PCA. Compute the eigenvalues, eigenvectors, and the reduced 1-D representation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Steps of PCA Algorithm:**

1. **Mean Centering:** Compute $\mu_j = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i x_{ij}$ and form $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu}$.
2. **Standardization (optional):** $z_{ij} = (x_{ij}-\mu_j)/\sigma_j$ to give each feature unit variance.
3. **Covariance Matrix:** $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c$.
4. **Eigendecomposition:** Solve $\mathbf{C}\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ for all $(\lambda_i, \mathbf{v}_i)$ pairs.
5. **Sort and Select:** Sort eigenvalues in decreasing order; select top $k$ based on explained variance threshold.
6. **Projection:** Form $\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{v}_1 \ldots \mathbf{v}_k]$ and compute $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W}$.

**Why Standardization is preferred when units differ:**
- If feature $x_1$ is in *meters* (range ~10) and feature $x_2$ is in *millimeters* (range ~10,000), the covariance matrix becomes dominated by $x_2$, not because $x_2$ is more informative, but because of unit scaling.
- Standardization rescales every feature to have **zero mean and unit variance**, ensuring each contributes equally to the principal components.
- Without standardization, the leading principal components will simply reflect features with the largest numerical magnitude, defeating the purpose of PCA. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Mean:**

$$ \mu_1 = \frac{2+3+4+5}{4} = 3.5, \quad \mu_2 = \frac{1+2+3+4}{4} = 2.5 $$

**Step 2 — Centered Data:**

$$ (-1.5, -1.5),\; (-0.5, -0.5),\; (0.5, 0.5),\; (1.5, 1.5) $$

**Step 3 — Covariance Matrix** (using $n-1=3$):

$$ C_{11} = \frac{2.25+0.25+0.25+2.25}{3} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.667 $$
$$ C_{22} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.667, \quad C_{12} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.667 $$

$$
\mathbf{C} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.667 & 1.667 \\ 1.667 & 1.667 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Eigenvalues:**

$$ (1.667 - \lambda)^2 - (1.667)^2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda^2 - 3.333\lambda = 0 $$
$$ \lambda_1 = 3.333\;(=10/3), \quad \lambda_2 = 0 $$

**Step 5 — Eigenvectors:**
- $\lambda_1 = 10/3$: $\mathbf{v}_1 = (1/\sqrt{2},\; 1/\sqrt{2})^{\top} \approx (0.707, 0.707)^{\top}$
- $\lambda_2 = 0$: $\mathbf{v}_2 = (1/\sqrt{2},\; -1/\sqrt{2})^{\top}$

**Step 6 — 1-D Projections:**

$$ y_i = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\bigl((x_{i1} - 3.5) + (x_{i2} - 2.5)\bigr) $$

| $i$ | $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i$ | $y_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(-1.5, -1.5)$ | $-2.121$ |
| 2 | $(-0.5, -0.5)$ | $-0.707$ |
| 3 | $(0.5, 0.5)$ | $0.707$ |
| 4 | $(1.5, 1.5)$ | $2.121$ |

**Variance retained:** $100\%$ (since $\lambda_2 = 0$). The 1-D representation is the projection of each point onto the line $y = x - 1$. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **PCA = linear, orthogonal, unsupervised** dimensionality reduction.
- [x] **Direction of max variance = eigenvector of $\mathbf{C}$ with largest eigenvalue** (proved via Lagrangian).
- [x] Always **mean-center** data before computing $\mathbf{C}$.
- [x] **Standardize** when features have different units / scales.
- [x] **Covariance matrix** is symmetric, $d \times d$, and positive semi-definite.
- [x] Eigendecomposition produces $d$ orthonormal eigenvectors and $d \ge 0$ eigenvalues.
- [x] **Total variance** = $\text{tr}(\mathbf{C}) = \sum_i \lambda_i$.
- [x] **Explained variance ratio** $\eta_i = \lambda_i / \sum_j \lambda_j$.
- [x] Choose $k$ via **cumulative variance threshold** (95–99%) or **scree plot**.
- [x] **Projection:** $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{W}$ (where $\mathbf{W}$ holds top-$k$ eigenvectors as columns).
- [x] **Reconstruction error** = $\sum_{i=k+1}^{d}\lambda_i$ (sum of discarded eigenvalues).
- [x] PCA assumes **linearity**, **large variance = high information**, and **orthogonality** of components.
- [x] **Limitations:** sensitive to outliers, assumes linear relationships, interpretability of PCs is low, sensitive to feature scaling.
- [x] **Variants & relatives:** Kernel PCA (nonlinear), Incremental PCA (large data), Sparse PCA (interpretability), t-SNE / UMAP (nonlinear visualization).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factor Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT414 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factor Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Factor Analysis (FA)** is a multivariate statistical technique used to describe the variability among observed, correlated variables in terms of a potentially lower number of unobserved variables called **factors**. It is fundamentally a **model-based dimensionality reduction** and **latent variable discovery** method, distinct from purely geometric methods like Principal Component Analysis (PCA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** Factor Analysis is a linear statistical model that assumes observed random variables $\mathbf{X} = (X_1, X_2, \dots, X_p)^T$ are linear combinations of a smaller number of unobservable common factors $\mathbf{F} = (F_1, F_2, \dots, F_k)^T$ plus unique error terms. Mathematically: $\mathbf{X} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{L}\mathbf{F} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$.

Where:
- $\mathbf{L}$ is the **$(p \times k)$ factor loading matrix**
- $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ is the **$(p \times 1)$ specific factor (uniqueness) vector**
- $k < p$ is the number of latent factors
- $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ is the mean vector of observed variables

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — The "Student Report Card" Example:**
>
> Imagine you have a student's marks in **8 different subjects**: Physics, Chemistry, Math, Biology, History, Geography, English, Hindi. These 8 variables seem "correlated" — a student good at Math is usually good at Physics. Why? Because there's an **underlying hidden ability** (let's call it the "**STEM Intelligence**") driving both scores. There's another hidden factor — "**Language Aptitude**" — driving English and Hindi. And another — "**Memory/General Knowledge**" — driving History and Geography.
>
> Factor Analysis is the detective that uncovers these **hidden constructs (factors)** that explain the correlations in observed data, even though we never directly measured "STEM Intelligence." It reduces 8 noisy, correlated measurements into **3 meaningful latent dimensions**.

### 1.3 Why Factor Analysis is Used in Machine Learning

| Application | Description |
|---|---|
| **Dimensionality Reduction** | Reduces $p$ correlated features to $k$ uncorrelated/semi-uncorrelated factors ($k \ll p$) |
| **Latent Construct Identification** | Discovers hidden variables (e.g., "Customer Satisfaction," "Intelligence") |
| **Noise Removal** | Separates common variance (signal) from unique variance (noise) |
| **Survey Design & Psychometrics** | Validates questionnaires by identifying underlying constructs |
| **Finance** | Identifies latent risk factors driving asset returns |
| **Image & Genomics** | Feature extraction in high-dimensional biological/image data |

## 1.4 Factor Analysis vs. PCA — A Critical Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU examiners LOVE asking this comparison. Memorize it.**

| Aspect | Factor Analysis | Principal Component Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Model the **covariance structure** | Find directions of **maximum variance** |
| **Assumes underlying model** | Yes ($\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{LF} + \varepsilon$) | No (pure transformation) |
| **Unique variance** | Explicitly modeled ($\varepsilon$) | Assumed to be noise; discarded |
| **Components/Factors** | Latent constructs | Linear combinations of original variables |
| **Number retained** | Based on **theory** + Kaiser criterion | Based on **variance explained** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Formal Factor Analysis Model

The **Orthogonal Factor Model** (most commonly tested) assumes:

$$\mathbf{X} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{L}\mathbf{F} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{X}$ : $(p \times 1)$ vector of observed variables (centered/standardized)
- $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ : $(p \times 1)$ mean vector (often 0 after centering)
- $\mathbf{L}$ : $(p \times k)$ factor loading matrix; entry $l_{ij}$ is the loading of variable $i$ on factor $j$
- $\mathbf{F}$ : $(k \times 1)$ vector of **common factors**, with $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{F}] = \mathbf{0}$ and $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{F}) = \mathbf{I}_k$
- $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ : $(p \times 1)$ vector of **specific/unique factors**, with $\mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}] = \mathbf{0}$ and $\text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) = \boldsymbol{\Psi}$ (diagonal matrix)

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1: Standardize the data**
- Center each variable: $X_i^{centered} = X_i - \bar{X}_i$
- Optionally standardize: $Z_i = (X_i - \bar{X}_i)/s_i$ (this is required when variables have different units)

**Step 2: Compute the correlation matrix $\mathbf{R}$**
- $\mathbf{R}$ is a $(p \times p)$ symmetric positive semi-definite matrix
- Diagonal entries = 1 (if standardized)
- Off-diagonal entries = pairwise Pearson correlations

**Step 3: Factor Extraction**
- Estimate the factor loading matrix $\mathbf{L}$ such that the model covariance structure is reproduced
- Methods: **Principal Axis Factoring (PAF)**, **Maximum Likelihood (ML)**, **Principal Components**

**Step 4: Determine the number of factors ($k$)**
- **Kaiser Criterion**: Retain factors with eigenvalue $\lambda > 1$
- **Scree Plot**: Retain factors before the "elbow"
- **Cumulative Variance**: Retain factors explaining ≥ 70–80% of total variance
- **Parallel Analysis**: Compare eigenvalues to those from random data

**Step 5: Factor Rotation**
- Rotate the loading matrix $\mathbf{L}$ to achieve **simple structure** (each variable loads highly on one factor, near zero on others)
- **Orthogonal rotations**: Varimax, Quartimax, Equamax
- **Oblique rotations**: Promax, Oblimin, Oblimax (allow correlated factors)

**Step 6: Interpretation**
- High loadings ($\vert l_{ij}\vert > 0.5$) indicate a strong relationship
- Name each factor based on variables it loads on

## 2.2.1 Detailed Assumptions of Factor Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 expects you to list these assumptions explicitly for full marks.**

1. **Linearity**: Relationships between variables and factors are linear
2. **Mean-zero factors**: $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{F}] = \mathbf{0}$
3. **Uncorrelated factors** (orthogonal model): $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{F}) = \mathbf{I}_k$
4. **Uncorrelated errors**: $\text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) = \boldsymbol{\Psi} = \text{diag}(\psi_1, \dots, \psi_p)$
5. **Independence**: Factors and errors are independent: $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{F}, \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) = \mathbf{0}$
6. **Multivariate normality** (for ML estimation only)

## 2.3 The Covariance Structure Equation

From the model $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{LF} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ (with $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{0}$):

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \text{Cov}(\mathbf{X}) = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^T]$$

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L}\,\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{F}\mathbf{F}^T]\,\mathbf{L}^T + \mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^T] + \text{cross terms (zero)}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T + \mathbf{\Psi}}$$

This is the **fundamental equation of factor analysis** — the entire discipline revolves around estimating $\mathbf{L}$ and $\mathbf{\Psi}$ such that $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T + \mathbf{\Psi}$ best approximates $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ (or $\mathbf{R}$ for standardized variables).

### 2.3.1 Decomposition of Total Variance

For variable $X_i$, the variance decomposes as:

$$\text{Var}(X_i) = \sigma_i^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{k} l_{ij}^2 + \psi_i$$

Where:
- $h_i^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{k} l_{ij}^2$ is the **communality** (variance shared with other variables via common factors)
- $\psi_i$ is the **uniqueness / specific variance** (variance unique to $X_i$ plus error)
- $0 \le h_i^2 \le 1$, and $\psi_i = 1 - h_i^2$ (in standardized form)

> [!NOTE]
> **Communalities** answer: "How much of $X_i$'s variance is explained by the common factors?"
> **Uniqueness** answers: "How much of $X_i$'s variance is unique to itself (or noise)?"

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{X} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{LF} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ | The Factor Analysis Model |
| 2 | $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{LL}^T + \mathbf{\Psi}$ | Covariance decomposition |
| 3 | $\sigma_i^2 = h_i^2 + \psi_i$ | Variance decomposition per variable |
| 4 | $h_i^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{k} l_{ij}^2$ | Communality of variable $i$ |
| 5 | $\psi_i = 1 - h_i^2$ | Uniqueness of variable $i$ |
| 6 | $\text{Cov}(X_i, F_j) = l_{ij}$ | Factor loadings as covariances (standardized) |
| 7 | $\text{Corr}(X_i, F_j) = l_{ij}$ | Loadings as correlations (when standardized) |
| 8 | $\rho_{X_i, X_m} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} l_{ij}\,l_{mj}$ | Reproduced correlation |
| 9 | $\mathbf{L} = \mathbf{V}\, \boldsymbol{\Lambda}^{1/2}$ | Loading matrix via eigen-decomposition (PC method) |
| 10 | $\text{Proportion of Variance}_j = \lambda_j / \sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i$ | Variance explained by factor $j$ |
| 11 | $\det(\mathbf{R} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$ | Eigenvalue equation for factors |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Note on Units:** Factor analysis is **dimensionless** when applied to the correlation matrix $\mathbf{R}$. All loadings, communalities, and uniquenesses are unit-free and lie between $-1$ and $+1$ (loadings) or $0$ and $1$ (communalities).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Covariance Equation $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{LL}^T + \mathbf{\Psi}$

We begin with the model (assuming $\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{0}$ for simplicity):

$$\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{F} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 1: Compute $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}]$**

$$\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}] = \mathbf{L}\,\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{F}] + \mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}] = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{0} + \mathbf{0} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 2: Compute the covariance matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \text{Cov}(\mathbf{X})$**

By definition:

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbb{E}\bigl[(\mathbf{X} - \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}])(\mathbf{X} - \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}])^T\bigr] = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^T]$$

**Step 3: Substitute the model into $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^T]$**

$$\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^T] = \mathbb{E}\bigl[(\mathbf{L}\mathbf{F} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon})(\mathbf{L}\mathbf{F} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon})^T\bigr]$$

**Step 4: Expand the product**

$$\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^T] = \mathbb{E}\bigl[\mathbf{L}\mathbf{F}\mathbf{F}^T\mathbf{L}^T + \mathbf{L}\mathbf{F}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^T + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\mathbf{F}^T\mathbf{L}^T + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^T\bigr]$$

**Step 5: Apply linearity of expectation**

$$\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^T] = \mathbf{L}\,\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{F}\mathbf{F}^T]\,\mathbf{L}^T + \mathbf{L}\,\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{F}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^T] + \mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\mathbf{F}^T]\,\mathbf{L}^T + \mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^T]$$

**Step 6: Apply assumptions**
- $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{F}\mathbf{F}^T] = \text{Cov}(\mathbf{F}) + \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{F}]\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{F}]^T = \mathbf{I}_k + \mathbf{0} = \mathbf{I}_k$
- $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{F}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^T] = \text{Cov}(\mathbf{F}, \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) = \mathbf{0}$ (independence)
- $\mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^T] = \text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) + \mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}]\mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}]^T = \boldsymbol{\Psi} + \mathbf{0} = \boldsymbol{\Psi}$

**Step 7: Simplify**

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L}\,\mathbf{I}_k\,\mathbf{L}^T + \mathbf{0} + \mathbf{0} + \boldsymbol{\Psi}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T + \boldsymbol{\Psi}} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Communality from the Diagonal of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$

The diagonal of $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T + \boldsymbol{\Psi}$ gives the variances:

$$(\mathbf{\Sigma})_{ii} = \sigma_i^2 = (\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T)_{ii} + (\boldsymbol{\Psi})_{ii}$$

**Step 1: Compute the $(i,i)$ entry of $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T$**

$$(\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T)_{ii} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} l_{ij} \cdot l_{ij} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} l_{ij}^2$$

**Step 2: Recognize this as the communality**

$$h_i^2 \equiv \sum_{j=1}^{k} l_{ij}^2$$

**Step 3: Recognize $(\boldsymbol{\Psi})_{ii} = \psi_i$ (the uniqueness)**

**Step 4: Final result**

$$\boxed{\sigma_i^2 = h_i^2 + \psi_i}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Reproduced Correlation from Off-Diagonal

The off-diagonal entry $(\mathbf{\Sigma})_{im}$ for $i \neq m$:

$$(\mathbf{\Sigma})_{im} = \text{Cov}(X_i, X_m) = \rho_{X_i, X_m}\sigma_i\sigma_m$$

**Step 1: Off-diagonal of $\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T$**

$$(\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T)_{im} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} l_{ij}\,l_{mj}$$

**Step 2: Off-diagonal of $\boldsymbol{\Psi}$ is zero (diagonal matrix)**

**Step 3: Thus**

$$\boxed{\text{Cov}(X_i, X_m) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} l_{ij}\,l_{mj}}$$

**Step 4: For standardized variables ($\sigma_i = \sigma_m = 1$), this becomes the correlation**

$$\rho_{X_i, X_m} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} l_{ij}\,l_{mj}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Method: Principal Axis Factoring (PAF) — Iterative Procedure

PAF is the **most commonly asked extraction method** in KTU exams. It solves the eigen-equation using a **reduced correlation matrix** where diagonals are replaced by initial communality estimates (not 1).

**Step 1: Initial communality estimation**
- Use the **squared multiple correlation** $R_i^2$ (regress $X_i$ on all other variables) as initial $h_i^{2(0)}$
- Or simply use $h_i^{2(0)} = \max(\vert r_{ij}\vert)$ across $j \neq i$ (simple method)

**Step 2: Construct reduced matrix $\mathbf{R}^* = \mathbf{R} - \mathbf{\Psi}_{\text{init}}$**

$$(\mathbf{R}^*)_{ii} = h_i^{2(0)}, \quad (\mathbf{R}^*)_{im} = r_{im} \text{ for } i \neq m$$

**Step 3: Eigen-decomposition**

$$\mathbf{R}^* = \mathbf{V} \boldsymbol{\Lambda} \mathbf{V}^T$$

where $\boldsymbol{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_p)$ with $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_p \geq 0$, and $\mathbf{V}$ contains eigenvectors.

**Step 4: Retain top-$k$ factors (where $k$ satisfies Kaiser Criterion $\lambda > 1$)**

$$\mathbf{\Lambda}_k = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_k), \quad \mathbf{V}_k = [v_1 \mid v_2 \mid \dots \mid v_k]$$

**Step 5: Compute initial loading matrix**

$$\mathbf{L}^{(1)} = \mathbf{V}_k\, \mathbf{\Lambda}_k^{1/2}$$

Each column of $\mathbf{L}^{(1)}$ is $l_j = \sqrt{\lambda_j}\,v_j$.

**Step 6: Update communalities and iterate**

$$h_i^{2(1)} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} (l_{ij}^{(1)})^2$$

Replace diagonals of $\mathbf{R}$ with $h_i^{2(1)}$ and repeat Steps 2–6 until convergence: $\vert h_i^{2(t+1)} - h_i^{2(t)} \vert < \epsilon$.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** Given a $3 \times 3$ correlation matrix:
> $$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.00 & 0.63 & 0.45 \\ 0.63 & 1.00 & 0.42 \\ 0.45 & 0.42 & 1.00 \end{bmatrix}$$
> Extract **one factor** using the Principal Component method. Compute loadings, communalities, and uniqueness.

### Solution

**Step 1: Set up the eigenvalue problem**

$$\det(\mathbf{R} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$$

$$\begin{vmatrix} 1-\lambda & 0.63 & 0.45 \\ 0.63 & 1-\lambda & 0.42 \\ 0.45 & 0.42 & 1-\lambda \end{vmatrix} = 0$$

**Step 2: Expand the determinant**

$$(1-\lambda)\bigl[(1-\lambda)^2 - 0.42^2\bigr] - 0.63\bigl[0.63(1-\lambda) - 0.42 \cdot 0.45\bigr] + 0.45\bigl[0.63 \cdot 0.42 - 0.45(1-\lambda)\bigr] = 0$$

Let $a = 1-\lambda$ for brevity. Then:

$$a(a^2 - 0.1764) - 0.63(0.63a - 0.189) + 0.45(0.2646 - 0.45a) = 0$$

$$a^3 - 0.1764a - 0.3969a + 0.11907 + 0.11907 - 0.2025a = 0$$

$$a^3 - 0.7758a + 0.23814 = 0$$

**Step 3: Solve for $\lambda$ numerically**

Substituting back $a = 1-\lambda$ and solving the cubic yields eigenvalues:

$$\lambda_1 \approx 1.961, \quad \lambda_2 \approx 0.679, \quad \lambda_3 \approx 0.360$$

**Step 4: Verify trace**

$$\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 = 1.961 + 0.679 + 0.360 = 3.000 = \text{tr}(\mathbf{R}) \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Retain $k=1$ factor (largest eigenvalue)**

For $k = 1$, the loading vector is $l_1 = \sqrt{\lambda_1}\,v_1$. The eigenvector $v_1$ corresponding to $\lambda_1 = 1.961$ satisfies:

$$(\mathbf{R} - 1.961\mathbf{I})v_1 = 0$$

This yields the proportional eigenvector $v_1 \propto (0.620, 0.589, 0.518)^T$ (numerical solution). Normalizing to unit length:

$$\|v_1\| = \sqrt{0.620^2 + 0.589^2 + 0.518^2} = \sqrt{0.3844 + 0.3469 + 0.2683} = \sqrt{0.9996} \approx 1.000$$

So $v_1 \approx (0.620, 0.589, 0.518)^T$.

**Step 6: Compute loadings**

$$l_1 = \sqrt{1.961} \cdot v_1 = 1.400 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.620 \\ 0.589 \\ 0.518 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.868 \\ 0.825 \\ 0.725 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7: Compute communalities**

$$h_1^2 = 0.868^2 = 0.753, \quad h_2^2 = 0.825^2 = 0.681, \quad h_3^2 = 0.725^2 = 0.526$$

**Step 8: Compute uniquenesses**

$$\psi_1 = 1 - 0.753 = 0.247, \quad \psi_2 = 1 - 0.681 = 0.319, \quad \psi_3 = 1 - 0.526 = 0.474$$

**Step 9: Verify covariance reconstruction**

$$\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0.753 & 0.716 & 0.629 \\ 0.716 & 0.681 & 0.598 \\ 0.629 & 0.598 & 0.526 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T + \boldsymbol{\Psi} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.000 & 0.716 & 0.629 \\ 0.716 & 1.000 & 0.598 \\ 0.629 & 0.598 & 1.000 \end{bmatrix}$$

The reproduced off-diagonals (0.716, 0.629, 0.598) approximate the original correlations (0.63, 0.45, 0.42) — not perfect because we used only 1 factor, but the structure is captured.

## 3.6 Factor Rotation Mathematics (Varimax Criterion)

After extraction, the loading matrix is rotated for interpretability. The **Varimax** criterion maximizes:

$$\mathbf{V}_{\text{varimax}} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \left[\sum_{i=1}^{p} \tilde{l}_{ij}^4 - \frac{1}{p}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{p} \tilde{l}_{ij}^2\right)^2\right]$$

where $\tilde{l}_{ij} = l_{ij} / h_i$ are **normalized loadings**. The goal: make squared loadings either very large (close to 1) or very small (close to 0) within each column.

Rotation is performed by an **orthogonal matrix $\mathbf{T}$** (where $\mathbf{T}^T\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{I}$) such that:

$$\mathbf{L}^* = \mathbf{L}\,\mathbf{T}$$

For a 2-factor case with rotation angle $\theta$:

$$\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

The angle $\theta$ is found by maximizing $\mathbf{V}_{\text{varimax}}$ (usually solved iteratively via quartimax or analytical formula for $k = 2$).

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Factor Analysis Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Observed Variables X1, X2, ..., Xp] --> nodeB[Correlation Matrix R]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Factor Extraction Method}
    nodeC -->|Principal Axis| nodeD[Estimate Loading Matrix L]
    nodeC -->|Maximum Likelihood| nodeD
    nodeC -->|Principal Components| nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Determine Number of Factors k]
    E1[Kaiser Criterion lambda greater than 1] --> nodeE
    E2[Scree Plot Elbow] --> nodeE
    E3[Cumulative Variance greater than 70 percent] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF{Factor Rotation}
    F1[Orthogonal Varimax] --> nodeF
    F2[Oblique Promax] --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Rotated Loading Matrix L Star]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Interpretation and Naming]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Factor 1 Name] 
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Factor 2 Name]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Factor k Name]
    nodeG --> nodeL[Compute Communalities h squared]
    nodeG --> nodeM[Compute Uniquenesses psi]
    L --> nodeN[Final Model X = mu + L Star F + epsilon]
    M --> nodeN
```

```mermaid
    nodeF1[Factor F1 STEM Ability] -. Load l_11 .-> nodeX1[X1 Physics]
    nodeF1 -. Load l_21 .-> nodeX2[X2 Chemistry]

    nodeX2 -. epsilon_2 .-> nodeN2[Unique Noise 2]
    nodeX3 -. epsilon_3 .-> nodeN3[Unique Noise 3]
    nodeX4 -. epsilon_4 .-> nodeN4[Unique Noise 4]

    nodeX8 -. epsilon_8 .-> nodeN8[Unique Noise 8]
```

## 4.3 Workflow Pipeline for Factor Analysis Implementation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Data Collection p variables and n samples] --> B[Step 2 Standardize or Normalize]
    B --> C[Step 3 Check Suitability]
    C --> C1[Bartlett Test of Sphericity]
    C --> C2[KMO Measure greater than 0.6]
    C --> D[Step 4 Compute Correlation Matrix R]
    D --> E[Step 5 Extract Factors]
    E --> E1[Principal Axis Factoring]
    E --> E2[Maximum Likelihood]

    G --> G2[Promax Oblique]

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    A --> C[Uniqueness psi_i]
    B --> D[Shared via Common Factors]
    C --> E[Specific to Variable plus Error]
    D --> F[Sum over j equals 1 to k of l_ij squared]
    E --> G[Equals 1 minus h_i squared]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Factor Analysis. Mention any two assumptions of the factor analysis model.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Factor Analysis is a multivariate statistical technique that explains the covariance structure of observed random variables in terms of a smaller number of unobserved random variables called **factors**. The model is:

$$\mathbf{X} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{L}\mathbf{F} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

where $\mathbf{L}$ is the loading matrix, $\mathbf{F}$ contains common factors, and $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ contains unique factors.

**Two Assumptions:**
1. $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{F}] = \mathbf{0}$ and $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{F}) = \mathbf{I}_k$ (mean-zero, uncorrelated common factors).
2. $\mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}] = \mathbf{0}$ and $\text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) = \boldsymbol{\Psi}$ (diagonal matrix — errors are uncorrelated with each other).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define communality and uniqueness. How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Communality ($h_i^2$):** The portion of variance of variable $X_i$ that is shared with other variables through the common factors. It is computed as:

$$h_i^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{k} l_{ij}^2$$

- **Uniqueness ($\psi_i$):** The portion of variance of $X_i$ that is **not** explained by common factors — it is unique to $X_i$ (and includes measurement error). 

**Relationship:** For standardized variables, the two partition the total variance:

$$\boxed{h_i^2 + \psi_i = 1}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the orthogonal factor model with the necessary assumptions. Derive the expression $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T + \boldsymbol{\Psi}$. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the steps involved in performing factor analysis. Explain the Principal Axis Factoring method. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution A(a) (7 Marks)

**Orthogonal Factor Model:**

The orthogonal factor model expresses each observed variable $X_i$ as a linear combination of $k$ common factors $F_1, F_2, \dots, F_k$ plus a unique factor $\varepsilon_i$:

$$X_i = \mu_i + l_{i1}F_1 + l_{i2}F_2 + \dots + l_{ik}F_k + \varepsilon_i$$

In matrix form:

$$\mathbf{X}_{(p \times 1)} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_{(p \times 1)} + \mathbf{L}_{(p \times k)}\,\mathbf{F}_{(k \times 1)} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}_{(p \times 1)}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{F}] = \mathbf{0}$
2. $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{F}) = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{F}\mathbf{F}^T] = \mathbf{I}_k$ (factors are uncorrelated, unit variance)
3. $\mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}] = \mathbf{0}$
4. $\text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) = \boldsymbol{\Psi}$ where $\boldsymbol{\Psi}$ is diagonal (specific factors are uncorrelated)
5. $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{F}, \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) = \mathbf{0}$ (independence)

**Derivation of $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T + \boldsymbol{\Psi}$:**

Given $\mathbf{X} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{L}\mathbf{F} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$, and centering ($\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{0}$):

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \text{Cov}(\mathbf{X}) = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}^T]$$

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbb{E}\bigl[(\mathbf{L}\mathbf{F} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon})(\mathbf{L}\mathbf{F} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon})^T\bigr]$$

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L}\,\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{F}\mathbf{F}^T]\,\mathbf{L}^T + \mathbf{L}\,\underbrace{\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{F}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^T]}_{\mathbf{0}} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\mathbf{F}^T]}_{\mathbf{0}}\,\mathbf{L}^T + \mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^T]$$

Applying assumption 2 and 4:

$$\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L}\,\mathbf{I}_k\,\mathbf{L}^T + \boldsymbol{\Psi}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T + \boldsymbol{\Psi}}$$

---

### Model Solution A(b) (7 Marks)

**Steps in Factor Analysis:**

1. **Data preparation**: Standardize variables to have mean 0 and variance 1 (essential when variables have different units).
2. **Compute correlation matrix $\mathbf{R}$**: A $(p \times p)$ symmetric matrix.
3. **Check factorability**: Bartlett's Test of Sphericity ($p < 0.05$) and Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO) measure (should be $> 0.6$).
4. **Extract factors**: Using Principal Axis Factoring, Maximum Likelihood, or Principal Components.
5. **Determine number of factors**: Kaiser criterion (eigenvalue $> 1$), scree plot, or cumulative variance threshold.
6. **Rotate factors**: Apply Varimax (orthogonal) or Promax (oblique) for simple structure.
7. **Interpret factors**: Name each factor based on high-loading variables.
8. **Compute factor scores**: Use regression or Bartlett's method.

**Principal Axis Factoring (PAF) — Detailed Procedure:**

PAF iteratively solves for the loading matrix $\mathbf{L}$ by eigen-decomposition of a **reduced correlation matrix** whose diagonals are communality estimates (not 1).

**Algorithm:**
- **Step 1:** Initialize communalities. The simplest choice is $h_i^{2(0)} = \max_{j \neq i} \vert r_{ij} \vert$, or use squared multiple correlation $R_i^2$.
- **Step 2:** Form the reduced matrix $\mathbf{R}^* = \mathbf{R}$ with diagonals replaced by $h_i^{2(0)}$.
- **Step 3:** Solve the eigenvalue problem $\mathbf{R}^* \mathbf{v}_j = \lambda_j \mathbf{v}_j$.
- **Step 4:** Retain $k$ factors where $\lambda_j > 1$ (Kaiser criterion).
- **Step 5:** Compute loading matrix $\mathbf{L}^{(1)} = [\sqrt{\lambda_1}\mathbf{v}_1 \mid \sqrt{\lambda_2}\mathbf{v}_2 \mid \dots \mid \sqrt{\lambda_k}\mathbf{v}_k]$.
- **Step 6:** Update communalities $h_i^{2(1)} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} (l_{ij}^{(1)})^2$.
- **Step 7:** Iterate Steps 2–6 until $\vert h_i^{2(t+1)} - h_i^{2(t)} \vert < \epsilon$.

Unlike PCA, PAF **explicitly accounts for unique variance** by substituting communality estimates for the diagonal 1's, producing factors that better reflect the **shared covariance structure**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Distinguish between Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA) and Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA). (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the Varimax rotation method. Discuss why factor rotation is necessary with suitable examples. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution B(a) (7 Marks)

| Aspect | Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA) | Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Discover the **number and nature** of latent factors | **Test a hypothesized** factor structure |
| **Prior knowledge** | Little or no prior theory about factors | Strong theoretical model specifying which variables load on which factors |
| **Number of factors** | Determined from data (e.g., Kaiser, scree) | Specified **a priori** by researcher |
| **Loadings** | All variables allowed to load on all factors | Constraints imposed (some loadings fixed to 0) |
| **Methodology** | Principal Axis, ML with rotation | Structural Equation Modeling (SEM), ML estimation |
| **Output** | Suggested factor structure | Goodness-of-fit indices (CFI, RMSEA, Chi-square) |
| **Use case** | Early-stage survey validation | Confirming theoretical model fit |

**Mathematical Distinction:**
- In **EFA**, $\mathbf{L}$ is fully estimated: $\hat{\mathbf{L}}$ has no zero constraints.
- In **CFA**, $\mathbf{L}$ is constrained: e.g., $l_{ij} = 0$ for specific $(i, j)$ pairs, reflecting the theory that $X_i$ does not depend on factor $F_j$.

**Example:**
- EFA: A psychologist collects responses to 30 personality items and asks, "How many traits underlie these?"
- CFA: The psychologist tests whether these 30 items fit the established **Big Five** model.

---

### Model Solution B(b) (7 Marks)

**Why Factor Rotation is Necessary:**

Initial factor solutions from PAF or ML extraction are often **not interpretable** — many variables have moderate loadings on multiple factors. Rotation **redistributes** the variance explained to achieve **simple structure**: each variable should load highly on **one** factor and near zero on others.

**Without rotation:** Imagine a factor where $X_1$ loads 0.6, $X_2$ loads 0.5, $X_3$ loads 0.4 on Factor 1 — ambiguous, hard to name.
**With rotation:** After rotation, $X_1$ and $X_2$ load 0.85 on Factor 1, $X_3$ loads 0.05; clear split — factor can be named "Verbal Ability."

**Varimax Rotation:**

Varimax is the most popular **orthogonal** rotation. It maximizes the variance of squared loadings within each factor (column of $\mathbf{L}$):

$$V_{\text{varimax}} = \sum_{j=1}^{k}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{p}\tilde{l}_{ij}^4 - \frac{1}{p}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{p}\tilde{l}_{ij}^2\right)^2\right]$$

where $\tilde{l}_{ij} = l_{ij}/h_i$ are the **normalized loadings** (loadings divided by variable's communality).

**For $k = 2$ factors with rotation angle $\theta$:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} l_{i1}^* \\ l_{i2}^* \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} l_{i1} \\ l_{i2} \end{bmatrix}$$

The optimal $\theta$ is found by:

$$\tan(4\theta) = \frac{D - 2AB}{C - (A^2 - B^2)}$$

where:
$$A = \sum_i \tilde{l}_{i1}^2 - \tilde{l}_{i2}^2, \quad B = 2\sum_i \tilde{l}_{i1}\tilde{l}_{i2}, \quad C = \sum_i (\tilde{l}_{i1}^2 - \tilde{l}_{i2}^2)^2, \quad D = 4\sum_i \tilde{l}_{i1}\tilde{l}_{i2}(\tilde{l}_{i1}^2 - \tilde{l}_{i2}^2)$$

**Key Property:** Varimax is **orthogonal**, so rotated factors remain **uncorrelated**. If correlated factors are expected, use **oblique** rotations like Promax instead.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the mean** when applying the factor model — always mention centering.
> 2. **Confusing PCA with FA** — PCA finds components that maximize variance; FA finds factors that **explain correlation**. State the model equation explicitly.
> 3. **Assuming loadings are unique** — the factor loading matrix $\mathbf{L}$ has an **infinite family** of solutions due to rotational indeterminacy. Hence, rotation is needed for identification.
> 4. **Skipping the assumptions** in derivations — KTU board expects you to list ALL 5 assumptions before the $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ derivation.
> 5. **Confusing communality with variance** — communality is the variance of $X_i$ explained by common factors; it is **not** the total variance.
> 6. **Not normalizing loadings** in Varimax — the criterion uses $\tilde{l}_{ij} = l_{ij}/h_i$, not raw $l_{ij}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Factor Analysis**

- ✅ Factor Analysis models the **covariance structure** via $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \mathbf{L}\mathbf{L}^T + \boldsymbol{\Psi}$
- ✅ The model is $\mathbf{X} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{L}\mathbf{F} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ — must write explicitly in derivations
- ✅ $k < p$: factors are fewer than observed variables (dimensionality reduction)
- ✅ Five assumptions: $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{F}]=\mathbf{0}$, $\text{Cov}(\mathbf{F})=\mathbf{I}_k$, $\mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}]=\mathbf{0}$, $\text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})=\boldsymbol{\Psi}$ (diagonal), $\mathbf{F} \perp \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$
- ✅ **Communality** $h_i^2 = \sum_j l_{ij}^2$ — shared variance
- ✅ **Uniqueness** $\psi_i = 1 - h_i^2$ — specific variance
- ✅ **Kaiser Criterion**: Retain factors with eigenvalue $> 1$
- ✅ **Scree Plot**: Look for the "elbow"
- ✅ **Principal Axis Factoring (PAF)**: Iterative, uses reduced correlation matrix
- ✅ **Maximum Likelihood (ML)**: Assumes multivariate normality; allows significance tests
- ✅ **Varimax**: Orthogonal rotation; maximizes variance of squared normalized loadings
- ✅ **Promax / Oblimin**: Oblique rotations; allow correlated factors
- ✅ **EFA** vs **CFA**: Discovery vs. confirmation
- ✅ **Rotational Indeterminacy**: $\mathbf{L}$ is not unique; $\mathbf{LT}$ also valid for orthogonal $\mathbf{T}$
- ✅ **Bartlett's Test**: Tests if $\mathbf{R}$ is significantly different from identity (must reject)
- ✅ **KMO Measure**: Sampling adequacy; values $> 0.6$ are acceptable, $> 0.8$ are good
- ✅ **Factor Scores**: Computed via regression (Thomson) or Bartlett methods
- ✅ **Communalities are between 0 and 1**; loadings are between $-1$ and $+1$
- ✅ **Cumulative variance**: Common threshold is 70–80% of total variance explained
- ✅ **Difference from PCA**: FA explicitly models unique variance $\boldsymbol{\Psi}$; PCA discards it

---

**End of Notes — Factor Analysis | KTU 2024 Scheme | PECBT414 — Module 2**
