Where $C > 0$ is the regularization parameter balancing margin size and misclassification penalty.

---

#### Part (b): Kernel Trick & Kernel Comparison

**The Kernel Trick:**

Many real-world datasets are not linearly separable in their original feature space. Theoretically, one could apply a non-linear transformation $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ to map data into a higher-dimensional space where a linear separator exists. However, computing $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ explicitly may be **infeasible** (e.g., infinite-dimensional feature spaces).

**Key Insight:** The SVM dual formulation depends on data only through **inner products** $\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$. If we replace these with a **kernel function** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^T \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$, we never need to compute $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ explicitly.

**Mathematical Justification:** A function $K$ is a valid kernel if and only if it is **symmetric** ($K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = K(\mathbf{x}_j, \mathbf{x}_i)$) and **positive semi-definite** (the Gram matrix is PSD). This is **Mercer's Theorem**.

**Comparison of Kernels:**

| Kernel | Mathematical Form | Practical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$ | Text classification with high-dimensional sparse features (e.g., spam detection using bag-of-words) |
| **Polynomial** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$ | Image processing where interaction terms between pixel intensities are meaningful; degree $d$ controls non-linearity |
| **RBF (Gaussian)** | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2)$ | Most general-purpose kernel; works well on non-linearly separable data like medical diagnosis and handwritten digit recognition |

**Final Decision Function with Kernels:**
$$\hat{y} = \text{sign}\left( \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b \right)$$

The sum runs only over **support vectors** (those with $\alpha_i > 0$), making prediction efficient.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

### 🔹 Neural Networks
- ✅ A **perceptron** computes $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$ and applies a non-linear activation $\phi$.
- ✅ The **universal approximation theorem** states that an MLP with at least one hidden layer and a non-linear activation can approximate **any continuous function** to arbitrary accuracy.
- ✅ **Activation functions** introduce non-linearity: **Sigmoid** (output layer, binary), **Tanh** (hidden, zero-centered), **ReLU** (hidden, default in deep nets, avoids vanishing gradient).
- ✅ **Backpropagation** is the chain rule applied across layers — forward pass computes activations and loss; backward pass computes gradients using the error signal $\delta^{(l)}$.
- ✅ The **gradient descent update rule** is $W \leftarrow W - \eta \nabla_W J$; with $\eta$ as the **learning rate**.
- ✅ **Vanishing gradient** is a major problem in deep networks — ReLU mitigates it since its derivative is 0 or 1.
- ✅ Cross-entropy loss paired with sigmoid yields the elegant gradient $\hat{y} - y$ (memorize this identity).

### 🔹 Support Vector Machines
- ✅ SVM finds the **maximum-margin hyperplane** $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ separating binary classes.
- ✅ The **margin width** is $\frac{2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ — derive this by computing distances to supporting hyperplanes $\pm 1$.
- ✅ **Hard margin** assumes perfect linear separability; **soft margin** introduces slack variables $\xi_i \geq 0$ and penalty $C$.
- ✅ **Support vectors** are points lying on the margin — they have $\alpha_i > 0$ in the dual formulation and are the **only** points that determine the decision boundary.
- ✅ The **kernel trick** allows non-linear classification by replacing inner products with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^T \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ — no explicit mapping needed.
- ✅ **Common kernels:** Linear (text), Polynomial (image features), RBF (general non-linear default).
- ✅ SVM is a **structural risk minimizer** with strong generalization guarantees (VC dimension theory); NN is an **empirical risk minimizer**.

### 🔹 Examiner's Quick-Fire Tips
- 🎯 Always state the **optimization problem** in standard form before solving SVM questions.
- 🎯 Draw a **neat labeled diagram** for perceptron/MLP questions — it is worth 1–2 marks even before your derivation.
- 🎯 Mention **Mercer's condition** (kernel symmetry + positive semi-definiteness) when discussing kernel validity.
- 🎯 Highlight the role of **C** (soft-margin SVM) and $\eta$ (NN) as hyperparameters that require tuning.
- 🎯 Use the **factor $\frac{1}{2}$** in the SVM objective — examiners notice this.
- 🎯 In numerical problems, **verify margin constraints** by plugging solutions back: $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ should hold strictly for non-support points.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NN - Multilayer Feed Forward Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT414 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** NN - Multilayer Feed Forward Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Multilayer Feed Forward Network (MLFFN)**, also known as a **Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)**, is a class of artificial neural network architecture in which information flows strictly in one direction — from the input layer through one or more *hidden layers* to the output layer — without forming any cycles, loops, or feedback connections.

Formally, an MLFFN is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of artificial neurons organized into at least three distinct layers:

- **Input Layer** — receives the raw feature vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$.
- **One or More Hidden Layers** — perform non-linear transformations via weighted summation and activation.
- **Output Layer** — produces the final prediction $\mathbf{y}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Multilayer Feed Forward Network is a neural network with one or more hidden layers, where each neuron in a layer is connected to **every** neuron in the subsequent layer (hence "fully connected"), and signals propagate only in the forward direction using a non-linear activation function.

The network is *fully connected* because every node in layer $l$ is connected to every node in layer $l+1$ by a trainable weight parameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Used in KTU Examinations**
> - **Synaptic Weight** ($w_{ij}$): the strength of the connection between neuron $i$ and neuron $j$.
> - **Bias** ($b_j$): an additional learnable parameter added before activation to shift the decision boundary.
> - **Activation Function** ($\sigma$): a non-linear function applied to the weighted sum of inputs.
> - **Epoch**: one complete pass of the entire training dataset through the network.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large corporate decision-making pipeline** in a multinational bank:

1. **Input Layer (Reception Desk):** Raw customer data arrives — name, income, credit history, age. This is just the *raw information*; no decision is made here.

2. **Hidden Layer 1 (Junior Analysts):** Each junior analyst looks at *all* the data (fully connected) and produces a preliminary score. Some analysts focus on income patterns, others on spending behaviour. They apply their own "rules of thumb" (activation function) to convert raw numbers into meaningful signals.

3. **Hidden Layer 2 (Senior Managers):** The senior managers receive the *outputs* of all junior analysts (not the original data), re-evaluate them through more sophisticated combinations, and pass refined signals upward.

4. **Output Layer (Final Committee):** Based purely on what the senior managers say, a final decision is made: **Approve** or **Reject** the loan.

Crucially, the data **only flows upward**. No manager sends feedback *back* to the reception desk during the decision process. The "learning" happens later, by comparing the final decision with the actual outcome (e.g., did the customer actually default?) and then adjusting everyone's "weights" of importance accordingly — this is **backpropagation**, which is discussed in Section 2.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** An MLFFN is essentially a *stack of voting committees* where each committee refines the previous committee's opinion until a final, highly nuanced answer emerges. Adding more layers allows the network to learn **hierarchical features** — simple patterns in early layers and complex abstractions in deeper layers.

---

## 1.3 Why a Single Layer Is Not Enough

A single-layer perceptron (SLP) using the **Heaviside step activation** can only solve **linearly separable** problems. It famously fails on the **XOR problem**, as proved by Minsky and Papert in **1969**.

| Architecture | Capability |
|---|---|
| Single-Layer Perceptron | Linear decision boundary only |
| MLFFN with 1 hidden layer | Can solve XOR and any bounded region (Universal Approximation) |
| MLFFN with 2+ hidden layers | Deep representations; used in Deep Learning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Highlight (KTU Frequently Asked):** The XOR problem triggered the **"AI Winter"** of the 1970s–80s. The revival came with the **Backpropagation algorithm** rediscovered by Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams in **1986**, which made training MLFFNs practically feasible.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical & Mathematical Constants of the Architecture

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $n$ | Number of input features | $2$ to $10^{4+}$ |
| $H_l$ | Number of neurons in hidden layer $l$ | $16$ to $1024$ |
| $m$ | Number of output neurons | $1$ (regression/binary) to $K$ (multi-class) |
| $\eta$ | Learning rate | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| $\mathbf{W}^{(l)}$ | Weight matrix between layer $l{-}1$ and layer $l$ | $\mathbb{R}^{H_{l-1} \times H_l}$ |
| $\mathbf{b}^{(l)}$ | Bias vector for layer $l$ | $\mathbb{R}^{H_l}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> All **weights** and **biases** are the *learnable parameters* of the network. A network with $L$ layers and architecture $[n, H_1, H_2, \dots, m]$ has a total parameter count of $\sum_{l=1}^{L} (H_{l-1} \cdot H_l + H_l)$ trainable values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Multilayer Feed Forward Network

Consider a standard MLFFN with:
- Input layer: $n$ neurons (carrying features $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$)
- One hidden layer: $h$ neurons
- Output layer: $m$ neurons

The information flow is:

$$\mathbf{x} \;\longrightarrow\; \mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}^{(1)}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}) \;\longrightarrow\; \mathbf{z}^{(2)} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}^{(2)}\mathbf{z}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}) \;=\; \mathbf{y}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the input feature vector
- $\mathbf{W}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{h \times n}$ is the weight matrix from input → hidden layer
- $\mathbf{b}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ is the bias vector of the hidden layer
- $\mathbf{z}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ is the hidden layer output
- $\mathbf{W}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times h}$ is the weight matrix from hidden → output
- $\mathbf{b}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the bias vector of the output layer
- $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the network's final output

---

## 2.2 Operational Walkthrough — Why and How Each Step Works

### Step 1: Weighted Summation (Pre-Activation)
Each neuron in a layer computes a **linear combination** of all outputs from the previous layer.

$$u_j^{(l)} = \sum_{i=1}^{H_{l-1}} w_{ij}^{(l)} \, z_i^{(l-1)} + b_j^{(l)}$$

**Why?** A linear combination allows the network to assign relative importance to each input signal. Without this weighting, every input would contribute equally and the network could not learn useful patterns.

### Step 2: Non-Linear Activation
The pre-activation $u_j^{(l)}$ is then passed through a non-linear activation function $\sigma$:

$$z_j^{(l)} = \sigma\!\left(u_j^{(l)}\right)$$

**Why?** Without non-linearity, stacking multiple layers would collapse mathematically into a single linear transformation. Non-linearity is what gives MLFFNs their **universal approximation** power.

### Step 3: Loss Computation
The network's output $\mathbf{y}$ is compared with the true target $\mathbf{t}$ using a **loss function** $E$.

**Why?** The loss is a scalar measure of "how wrong" the network is. It serves as the compass that guides all weight updates.

### Step 4: Backpropagation (Weight Update)
Gradients of the loss with respect to every weight are computed using the **chain rule**, and weights are updated via **gradient descent**:

$$w_{ij}^{(\text{new})} = w_{ij}^{(\text{old})} - \eta \, \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}}$$

**Why?** Updating weights in the direction opposite to the gradient minimizes the loss, making the network's predictions progressively closer to the true targets.

---

## 2.3 Common Activation Functions

| Function | Formula | Range | Derivative | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sigmoid (Logistic)** | $\sigma(u) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-u}}$ | $(0, 1)$ | $\sigma(u)(1 - \sigma(u))$ | Binary classification output |
| **Tanh (Hyperbolic Tangent)** | $\tanh(u) = \dfrac{e^{u} - e^{-u}}{e^{u} + e^{-u}}$ | $(-1, 1)$ | $1 - \tanh^2(u)$ | Hidden layers (zero-centred) |
| **ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit)** | $\text{ReLU}(u) = \max(0, u)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | $\begin{cases} 1 & u > 0 \\ 0 & u \le 0 \end{cases}$ | Default for deep networks |
| **Softmax** | $\sigma(u_k) = \dfrac{e^{u_k}}{\sum_{j} e^{u_j}}$ | $(0, 1)$, sums to $1$ | $\sigma(u_k)(\delta_{kj} - \sigma(u_j))$ | Multi-class output layer |
| **Linear (Identity)** | $\sigma(u) = u$ | $(-\infty, \infty)$ | $1$ | Regression output layer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** You are expected to know that the **Sigmoid** function suffers from the **vanishing gradient problem** for large $\vert u \vert$ because $\sigma'(u) \to 0$ as $u \to \pm\infty$. This is the primary reason **ReLU** is preferred in modern deep networks.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every critical equation for MLFFN analysis. **No vertical pipes are used inside table cells** (use `\vert` instead of `\mid` for absolute value notation to preserve table syntax).

| # | Concept | Equation | Dimension / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pre-activation of neuron $j$ in layer $l$ | $u_j^{(l)} = \sum_{i=1}^{H_{l-1}} w_{ij}^{(l)} \, z_i^{(l-1)} + b_j^{(l)}$ | Scalar |
| 2 | Output of neuron $j$ in layer $l$ | $z_j^{(l)} = \sigma(u_j^{(l)})$ | Scalar |
| 3 | Vectorized forward pass | $\mathbf{z}^{(l)} = \sigma(\mathbf{W}^{(l)} \mathbf{z}^{(l-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(l)})$ | $\mathbb{R}^{H_l}$ |
| 4 | Mean Squared Error (regression) | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{m} (t_k - y_k)^2$ | Scalar |
| 5 | Cross-Entropy Loss (classification) | $E = -\sum_{k=1}^{m} t_k \log(y_k)$ | Scalar (nats) |
| 6 | Output-layer delta (error signal) | $\delta_k^{(L)} = (y_k - t_k) \, \sigma'(u_k^{(L)})$ | Scalar |
| 7 | Hidden-layer delta (backprop) | $\delta_j^{(l)} = \left(\sum_{k} w_{jk}^{(l+1)} \, \delta_k^{(l+1)}\right) \sigma'(u_j^{(l)})$ | Scalar |
| 8 | Weight gradient | $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}^{(l)}} = \delta_j^{(l)} \, z_i^{(l-1)}$ | Scalar |
| 9 | Bias gradient | $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial b_j^{(l)}} = \delta_j^{(l)}$ | Scalar |
| 10 | Weight update (Gradient Descent) | $w_{ij}^{(l)} \leftarrow w_{ij}^{(l)} - \eta \, \delta_j^{(l)} \, z_i^{(l-1)}$ | Scalar |
| 11 | Bias update | $b_j^{(l)} \leftarrow b_j^{(l)} - \eta \, \delta_j^{(l)}$ | Scalar |
| 12 | Total trainable parameters | $P = \sum_{l=1}^{L} (H_{l-1} \cdot H_l + H_l)$ | Integer |
| 13 | Universal Approximation (informal) | A 1-hidden-layer MLFFN can approximate any continuous function on a compact set to arbitrary precision, given enough hidden neurons | — |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multilayer Feed Forward Networks are the foundational building block of nearly all modern deep-learning systems. They are deployed in:

- **Banking & Finance** — credit scoring, fraud detection, stock-price prediction.
- **Healthcare** — disease diagnosis from tabular patient data, ECG signal classification.
- **Manufacturing** — predictive maintenance, sensor-fusion fault detection.
- **Natural Language Processing** — as feature extractors inside transformer-based models.
- **Computer Vision** — as the *classifier head* on top of CNN backbones.
- **Telecommunications** — traffic prediction, anomaly detection in network logs.

The MLFFN's power lies in its ability to learn **non-linear decision boundaries**, **hierarchical representations**, and **generalize** from training data to unseen samples.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Setup: Notation for a 2-Layer MLFFN

We consider a fully connected MLFFN with:
- Input vector: $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^T$
- Single hidden layer with $h$ neurons
- Output layer with $m$ neurons
- Target vector: $\mathbf{t} = (t_1, t_2, \dots, t_m)^T$

Weight matrices:
- $\mathbf{V} = (v_{ij})$: weights from input layer to hidden layer, where $v_{ij}$ connects input $x_i$ to hidden neuron $j$. Dimension: $n \times h$.
- $\mathbf{W} = (w_{jk})$: weights from hidden layer to output layer, where $w_{jk}$ connects hidden neuron $j$ to output neuron $k$. Dimension: $h \times m$.

Bias vectors:
- $\mathbf{b}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{h}$ (hidden layer bias)
- $\mathbf{b}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ (output layer bias)

Activation function: $\sigma(\cdot)$ (we use the **sigmoid** for derivation, but the result generalizes to any differentiable activation).

---

## 3.2 Forward Propagation Derivation

### 3.2.1 Hidden Layer Pre-Activation

For each hidden neuron $j \in \{1, 2, \dots, h\}$:

$$u_j^{(h)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_{ij} \, x_i + b_j^{(1)}$$

**Explanation:** Each hidden neuron receives *all* $n$ inputs, multiplies each by its corresponding weight, sums them up, and adds a bias term. This is the standard linear combination used in perceptrons.

In matrix form:

$$\mathbf{u}^{(h)} = \mathbf{V}^{T} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$$

### 3.2.2 Hidden Layer Output (Activation)

$$z_j = \sigma(u_j^{(h)}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-u_j^{(h)}}}$$

**Explanation:** The pre-activation is passed through the non-linear sigmoid to produce the hidden neuron's output. This non-linearity is what allows the network to learn non-linear patterns.

In vector form:

$$\mathbf{z} = \sigma(\mathbf{u}^{(h)}) = \sigma(\mathbf{V}^{T} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)})$$

### 3.2.3 Output Layer Pre-Activation

For each output neuron $k \in \{1, 2, \dots, m\}$:

$$u_k^{(o)} = \sum_{j=1}^{h} w_{jk} \, z_j + b_k^{(2)}$$

**Explanation:** Each output neuron receives *all* $h$ hidden-layer outputs, weighted and summed, plus its own bias.

In matrix form:

$$\mathbf{u}^{(o)} = \mathbf{W}^{T} \mathbf{z} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$$

### 3.2.4 Network Output

$$y_k = \sigma(u_k^{(o)})$$

$$\mathbf{y} = \sigma(\mathbf{u}^{(o)}) = \sigma(\mathbf{W}^{T} \mathbf{z} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)})$$

**End-to-end vectorized forward pass:**

$$\mathbf{y} = \sigma\!\left( \mathbf{W}^{T} \, \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{V}^{T} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}\right) + \mathbf{b}^{(2)} \right)$$

---

## 3.3 Loss Function (Mean Squared Error)

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{m} (t_k - y_k)^2$$

**Explanation:** The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is a mathematical convenience — it cancels with the exponent $2$ when we differentiate. The error is summed over all $m$ output neurons, so it represents the total deviation of the network's output from the desired target.

---

## 3.4 Backpropagation Derivation — Generalized Delta Rule

### 3.4.1 Output-Layer Error Signal $\delta_k^{(o)}$

We want to compute $\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}}$ (how the loss changes when we tweak the weight $w_{jk}$).

Using the **chain rule**:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k}{\partial u_k^{(o)}} \cdot \frac{\partial u_k^{(o)}}{\partial w_{jk}}$$

Computing each piece:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y_k}\left[\frac{1}{2} \sum_{c=1}^{m}(t_c - y_c)^2\right] = -(t_k - y_k) = (y_k - t_k)$$

$$\frac{\partial y_k}{\partial u_k^{(o)}} = \sigma'(u_k^{(o)})$$

$$\frac{\partial u_k^{(o)}}{\partial w_{jk}} = z_j$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}} = (y_k - t_k) \, \sigma'(u_k^{(o)}) \, z_j$$

**Define the output-layer delta:**

$$\delta_k^{(o)} = (y_k - t_k) \, \sigma'(u_k^{(o)})$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}} = \delta_k^{(o)} \, z_j$$

### 3.4.2 Hidden-Layer Error Signal $\delta_j^{(h)}$

Now we compute $\frac{\partial E}{\partial v_{ij}}$ (how the loss changes when we tweak a hidden-layer weight).

Applying the **chain rule** through the hidden layer:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial v_{ij}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial z_j} \cdot \frac{\partial z_j}{\partial u_j^{(h)}} \cdot \frac{\partial u_j^{(h)}}{\partial v_{ij}}$$

First, $\frac{\partial E}{\partial z_j}$ must be expanded over all output neurons (since $z_j$ influences every $y_k$):

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial z_j} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k}{\partial u_k^{(o)}} \cdot \frac{\partial u_k^{(o)}}{\partial z_j} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} (y_k - t_k) \, \sigma'(u_k^{(o)}) \, w_{jk}$$

Substituting $\delta_k^{(o)} = (y_k - t_k) \sigma'(u_k^{(o)})$:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial z_j} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \delta_k^{(o)} \, w_{jk}$$

Second:

$$\frac{\partial z_j}{\partial u_j^{(h)}} = \sigma'(u_j^{(h)})$$

Third:

$$\frac{\partial u_j^{(h)}}{\partial v_{ij}} = x_i$$

Combining:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial v_{ij}} = \left( \sum_{k=1}^{m} \delta_k^{(o)} \, w_{jk} \right) \sigma'(u_j^{(h)}) \, x_i$$

**Define the hidden-layer delta:**

$$\delta_j^{(h)} = \left( \sum_{k=1}^{m} \delta_k^{(o)} \, w_{jk} \right) \sigma'(u_j^{(h)})$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial v_{ij}} = \delta_j^{(h)} \, x_i$$

### 3.4.3 Generalized Pattern (Any Layer)

The two results reveal a beautiful recursive structure. For **any layer** $l$:

$$\delta_j^{(l)} = \left( \sum_{k} w_{jk}^{(l+1)} \, \delta_k^{(l+1)} \right) \sigma'(u_j^{(l)})$$

**The error signal at layer $l$ is the weighted sum of the next layer's deltas, multiplied by the local activation derivative.** This is the essence of the backpropagation algorithm.

---

## 3.5 Final Weight Update Equations

Applying **gradient descent** with learning rate $\eta$:

**Hidden-to-output weights:**

$$w_{jk}^{(\text{new})} = w_{jk}^{(\text{old})} - \eta \, \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}} = w_{jk}^{(\text{old})} - \eta \, \delta_k^{(o)} \, z_j$$

**Input-to-hidden weights:**

$$v_{ij}^{(\text{new})} = v_{ij}^{(\text{old})} - \eta \, \frac{\partial E}{\partial v_{ij}} = v_{ij}^{(\text{old})} - \eta \, \delta_j^{(h)} \, x_i$$

**Bias updates:**

$$b_k^{(2,\text{new})} = b_k^{(2,\text{old})} - \eta \, \delta_k^{(o)}$$

$$b_j^{(1,\text{new})} = b_j^{(1,\text{old})} - \eta \, \delta_j^{(h)}$$

---

## 3.6 Summary of the Backpropagation Algorithm (Pseudocode Logic)

The complete training procedure for a 2-layer MLFFN is:

1. **Initialize** all weights $v_{ij}$, $w_{jk}$ and biases $b_j^{(1)}, b_k^{(2)}$ with small random values (e.g., uniform $\sim \mathcal{U}(-0.5, 0.5)$ or Xavier initialization).
2. **Repeat for each epoch** until convergence:
   - For each training sample $(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{t})$:
     - **Forward pass:** compute $z_j$ and $y_k$ using Section 3.2 equations.
     - **Compute loss:** $E$ using Section 3.3.
     - **Backward pass:** compute $\delta_k^{(o)}$ and $\delta_j^{(h)}$ using Section 3.4.
     - **Update weights and biases** using Section 3.5.
3. **Stop** when loss $E$ falls below a threshold or after a fixed number of epochs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Generalized" Delta Rule?** The classical **delta rule** updates weights of a *single-layer* perceptron: $w \leftarrow w + \eta(t - y)x$. The **generalized delta rule** extends this idea to *any number of layers* by computing layer-wise deltas via the chain rule — hence "generalized."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Full MLFFN Architecture

The following block diagram illustrates a 2-hidden-layer MLFFN for a binary classification task (architecture: 3 inputs → 4 hidden-1 → 4 hidden-2 → 2 outputs). Every connection carries a trainable weight.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph IN[INPUT LAYER N EQUALS 3]
        x1[INPUT X1]
        x2[INPUT X2]
        x3[INPUT X3]
    end

    subgraph H1[HIDDEN LAYER 1 N EQUALS 4]
        h1a[NEURON H11]
        h1b[NEURON H12]
        h1c[NEURON H13]
        h1d[NEURON H14]
    end

    subgraph H2[HIDDEN LAYER 2 N EQUALS 4]
        h2a[NEURON H21]
        h2b[NEURON H22]
        h2c[NEURON H23]
        h2d[NEURON H24]
    end

    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT LAYER N EQUALS 2]
        y1[OUTPUT Y1 CLASS 0]
        y2[OUTPUT Y2 CLASS 1]
    end

    x1 --> h1a
    x1 --> h1b
    x1 --> h1c
    x1 --> h1d
    x2 --> h1a
    x2 --> h1b
    x2 --> h1c
    x2 --> h1d
    x3 --> h1a
    x3 --> h1b
    x3 --> h1c
    x3 --> h1d

    h1a --> h2a
    h1a --> h2b
    h1a --> h2c
    h1a --> h2d
    h1b --> h2a
    h1b --> h2b
    h1b --> h2c
    h1b --> h2d
    h1c --> h2a
    h1c --> h2b
    h1c --> h2c
    h1c --> h2d
    h1d --> h2a
    h1d --> h2b
    h1d --> h2c
    h1d --> h2d

    h2a --> y1
    h2a --> y2
    h2b --> y1
    h2b --> y2
    h2c --> y1
    h2c --> y2
    h2d --> y1
    h2d --> y2
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This Mermaid diagram maps the **information flow** and the **mathematical operations** performed at each stage of the network.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[RAW INPUT FEATURE VECTOR X] --> B[STEP 1 WEIGHTED SUMMATION AT HIDDEN LAYER]
    B --> C[STEP 2 ADD BIAS VECTOR B1]
    C --> D[STEP 3 APPLY ACTIVATION SIGMA]
    D --> E[HIDDEN LAYER OUTPUT Z]
    E --> F[STEP 4 WEIGHTED SUMMATION AT OUTPUT LAYER]
    F --> G[STEP 5 ADD BIAS VECTOR B2]
    G --> H[STEP 6 APPLY ACTIVATION SIGMA]
    H --> I[NETWORK OUTPUT Y]
    I --> J[STEP 7 COMPARE WITH TARGET T]
    J --> K[COMPUTE LOSS FUNCTION E]
    K --> L[STEP 8 BACKPROPAGATION COMPUTE OUTPUT DELTA]
    L --> M[STEP 9 BACKPROPAGATE DELTA TO HIDDEN LAYER]
    M --> N[STEP 10 COMPUTE WEIGHT GRADIENTS]
    N --> O[STEP 11 UPDATE WEIGHTS USING GRADIENT DESCENT]
    O --> P[STEP 12 REPEAT FOR NEXT EPOCH]
```

---

## 4.3 Signal Processing View of a Single Neuron

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[INPUT X1] --> M1[WEIGHT V1]
    S2[INPUT X2] --> M2[WEIGHT V2]
    S3[INPUT X3] --> M3[WEIGHT V3]
    S4[BIAS B] --> SUM
    M1 --> SUM[SUMMATION NODE]
    M2 --> SUM
    M3 --> SUM
    SUM --> ACT[ACTIVATION FUNCTION SIGMA]
    ACT --> OUT[OUTPUT Y]
```

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observations from the Diagrams**
> - Every **input** is connected to **every neuron** in the next layer — this is what makes the network "fully connected."
> - There are **no backward arrows** during the forward pass — the network is **strictly feedforward**.
> - The **bias** is treated as an input with a constant value of **1** and its own trainable weight, which shifts the activation function left or right.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Part A carries 3 marks each. Part B carries 14 marks each, split internally into sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Internal choice is mandatory — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define a Multilayer Feed Forward Network. List any two advantages it has over a single-layer perceptron.**

**Model Answer:**

A Multilayer Feed Forward Network (MLFFN) is a neural network architecture consisting of an input layer, one or more hidden layers, and an output layer, in which signals propagate strictly from input to output (no feedback loops) and each neuron in one layer is connected to every neuron in the next layer via trainable weights.

**Two advantages over a single-layer perceptron:**

1. **Ability to solve non-linearly separable problems** — A single-layer perceptron can only model a linear decision boundary (e.g., AND, OR gates) and fails on the XOR problem. An MLFFN with at least one hidden layer using a non-linear activation can learn arbitrary non-linear decision boundaries.

2. **Hierarchical feature learning** — Hidden layers learn increasingly abstract representations of the input. Early layers capture simple patterns (edges, low-level correlations) and deeper layers combine them into complex, high-level features, enabling the network to model intricate relationships in data.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 each for two advantages with brief explanation.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Explain the role of the activation function in a Multilayer Feed Forward Network. Why cannot a linear activation function be used in hidden layers?**

**Model Answer:**

The activation function in an MLFFN introduces **non-linearity** into the network. It is applied to the pre-activation (weighted sum + bias) of every neuron to produce its output. Without a non-linear activation, an MLFFN with any number of layers would mathematically collapse into a **single linear transformation**, because the composition of linear functions is itself linear.

**Why linear activations fail in hidden layers:**

If $\sigma(u) = u$ (linear/identity), then the forward pass becomes:
$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{W}_{\text{eff}} \mathbf{x}$$
which is equivalent to a single-layer network with effective weight matrix $\mathbf{W}_{\text{eff}}$. All the hidden layers become redundant, and the network loses its power to model non-linear relationships.

Common non-linear activations include **sigmoid**, **tanh**, and **ReLU**.

*(3 marks: 1 for role + 2 for linear collapse explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Always** show the **chain rule expansion** when deriving backpropagation — partial marks are awarded for each correct intermediate derivative.
> - **Never** forget the **negative sign** in the gradient descent update (weights move *opposite* to the gradient).
> - **State your notation** clearly at the start of derivations — undefined symbols are a top reason for losing 1–2 marks.
> - For diagram questions, label all **inputs**, **hidden layers**, **weights**, and **activations** — a diagram without labels gets zero.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Derive the backpropagation algorithm for a two-layer MLFFN with sigmoid activation, showing the generalized delta rule clearly.**

**Model Solution:**

**Notation:**
- Input: $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \dots, x_n)^T$, Hidden layer output: $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, \dots, z_h)^T$, Network output: $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, \dots, y_m)^T$, Target: $\mathbf{t} = (t_1, \dots, t_m)^T$.
- Weights: $v_{ij}$ (input→hidden), $w_{jk}$ (hidden→output).
- Biases: $b_j^{(1)}$ (hidden), $b_k^{(2)}$ (output).
- Activation: sigmoid $\sigma(u) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-u}}$, with $\sigma'(u) = \sigma(u)(1 - \sigma(u))$.

**Step 1: Forward pass**

$$u_j^{(h)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_{ij} x_i + b_j^{(1)}, \quad z_j = \sigma(u_j^{(h)})$$

$$u_k^{(o)} = \sum_{j=1}^{h} w_{jk} z_j + b_k^{(2)}, \quad y_k = \sigma(u_k^{(o)})$$

**Step 2: Loss**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{m} (t_k - y_k)^2$$

**Step 3: Output-layer delta derivation**

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k}{\partial u_k^{(o)}} \cdot \frac{\partial u_k^{(o)}}{\partial w_{jk}} = -(t_k - y_k) \cdot \sigma'(u_k^{(o)}) \cdot z_j$$

Define $\delta_k^{(o)} = (y_k - t_k) \, \sigma'(u_k^{(o)})$.

Then $\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}} = -\delta_k^{(o)} z_j$.

**Step 4: Hidden-layer delta derivation**

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial v_{ij}} = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k}{\partial u_k^{(o)}} \cdot \frac{\partial u_k^{(o)}}{\partial z_j} \cdot \frac{\partial z_j}{\partial u_j^{(h)}} \cdot \frac{\partial u_j^{(h)}}{\partial v_{ij}}$$

$$= \sum_{k=1}^{m} (y_k - t_k) \, \sigma'(u_k^{(o)}) \, w_{jk} \, \sigma'(u_j^{(h)}) \, x_i = \left(\sum_{k=1}^{m} \delta_k^{(o)} w_{jk}\right) \sigma'(u_j^{(h)}) x_i$$

Define $\delta_j^{(h)} = \left(\sum_{k=1}^{m} \delta_k^{(o)} w_{jk}\right) \sigma'(u_j^{(h)})$.

Then $\frac{\partial E}{\partial v_{ij}} = -\delta_j^{(h)} x_i$.

**Step 5: Weight update (gradient descent)**

$$w_{jk}^{(\text{new})} = w_{jk}^{(\text{old})} - \eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{jk}} = w_{jk}^{(\text{old})} + \eta \, \delta_k^{(o)} \, z_j$$

$$v_{ij}^{(\text{new})} = v_{ij}^{(\text{old})} + \eta \, \delta_j^{(h)} \, x_i$$

This constitutes the **Generalized Delta Rule** for MLFFN training. ∎

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks] Explain the vanishing gradient problem in MLFFNs. Discuss how the ReLU activation function helps mitigate it.**

**Model Solution:**

**The Vanishing Gradient Problem:**

During backpropagation, gradients in early layers are obtained by multiplying the error signal with the activation derivative $\sigma'(u)$ at each layer. For the **sigmoid** activation, $\sigma'(u) = \sigma(u)(1 - \sigma(u))$, which attains a maximum value of **0.25** and approaches **0** rapidly as $\vert u \vert$ increases.

For a deep network with $L$ layers, the gradient at layer $l$ involves the product of $L - l$ such small terms. If each term is at most $0.25$, then:

$$\left\vert \frac{\partial E}{\partial w^{(1)}} \right\vert \;\le\; \eta \, (0.25)^{L-1} \cdot \prod_{k=1}^{L-1} \max_k \vert w^{(k+1)} \vert$$

As $L$ grows, the gradient in early layers becomes **exponentially small** — often smaller than machine precision. The early layers then receive negligible updates, and the network effectively stops learning in those layers.

**Symptoms in practice:** Training loss plateaus quickly; the network behaves like a shallow model.

**How ReLU Mitigates This:**

The ReLU activation is defined as $\text{ReLU}(u) = \max(0, u)$, with derivative:

$$\text{ReLU}'(u) = \begin{cases} 1, & u > 0 \\ 0, & u \le 0 \end{cases}$$

**Two key advantages:**

1. **Constant gradient for active neurons:** When $u > 0$, the gradient of ReLU is exactly **1**, not a vanishingly small number. This means the backpropagated signal does not decay as it passes through the network, so early layers continue to receive meaningful updates.

2. **Sparsity:** Since ReLU outputs exactly **0** for negative inputs, it produces sparse activations. This sparsity acts as implicit regularization, reducing overfitting.

**Caveat — Dying ReLU Problem:** If a neuron's pre-activation is always negative, its gradient is always **0** and it "dies," never updating. Variants such as **Leaky ReLU** ($\text{LReLU}(u) = \max(\alpha u, u)$ with small $\alpha > 0$) and **ELU** are used to address this.

Hence, **ReLU is the default choice** for hidden layers in modern deep MLFFNs because it enables efficient gradient flow and faster convergence.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Draw the architecture of a 2-hidden-layer MLFFN with 3 inputs, 4 neurons in each hidden layer, and 2 outputs. Explain the forward propagation with mathematical equations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture Diagram:**

```
   x1 ──┐
        ├──→ [h11] ──┐
   x2 ──┤            ├──→ [h21] ──┐
        ├──→ [h12] ──┤            ├──→ [y1]
        │            ├──→ [h22] ──┤
   x3 ──┤            │            ├──→ [y2]
        ├──→ [h13] ──┤            │
        │            ├──→ [h23] ──┘
        └──→ [h14] ──┤
                     └──→ [h24]
```

(Every node in one layer is connected to every node in the next layer; each connection carries a distinct weight.)

**Notation:**
- Input vector: $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$
- Hidden layer 1: $H_1 = 4$ neurons with output $\mathbf{z}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{4}$
- Hidden layer 2: $H_2 = 4$ neurons with output $\mathbf{z}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{4}$
- Output: $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$
- Weight matrices: $\mathbf{W}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 4}$, $\mathbf{W}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 4}$, $\mathbf{W}^{(3)} \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 2}$

**Forward Propagation Equations:**

**Hidden Layer 1 (pre-activation and output):**

$$\mathbf{u}^{(1)} = \mathbf{W}^{(1)T} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}$$

$$\mathbf{z}^{(1)} = \sigma(\mathbf{u}^{(1)})$$

**Hidden Layer 2 (pre-activation and output):**

$$\mathbf{u}^{(2)} = \mathbf{W}^{(2)T} \mathbf{z}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}$$

$$\mathbf{z}^{(2)} = \sigma(\mathbf{u}^{(2)})$$

**Output Layer:**

$$\mathbf{u}^{(3)} = \mathbf{W}^{(3)T} \mathbf{z}^{(2)} + \mathbf{b}^{(3)}$$

$$\mathbf{y} = \sigma(\mathbf{u}^{(3)})$$

**Compact end-to-end form:**

$$\mathbf{y} = \sigma\!\left( \mathbf{W}^{(3)T} \, \sigma\!\left( \mathbf{W}^{(2)T} \, \sigma\!\left( \mathbf{W}^{(1)T} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}\right) + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}\right) + \mathbf{b}^{(3)}\right)$$

**Scalar form for one neuron:** For the $j$-th neuron in hidden layer 1:

$$u_j^{(1)} = v_{1j} x_1 + v_{2j} x_2 + v_{3j} x_3 + b_j^{(1)}$$

$$z_j^{(1)} = \sigma(u_j^{(1)})$$

And similarly for subsequent layers. The total number of trainable parameters is:

$$P = (3 \cdot 4 + 4) + (4 \cdot 4 + 4) + (4 \cdot 2 + 2) = 16 + 20 + 10 = 46$$

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks] Discuss the Generalized Delta Rule. Show mathematically how the error signal propagates from the output layer to the input layer in a 3-layer network.**

**Model Solution:**

The **Generalized Delta Rule (GDR)** is the extension of the original delta rule (for single-layer perceptrons) to networks with **multiple hidden layers**. The fundamental idea is to compute a local "error signal" $\delta_j^{(l)}$ at every neuron in every layer, by propagating the output-layer error **backwards** through the network using the chain rule.

**Setup (3-layer network):** Input → Hidden 1 → Hidden 2 → Output.

**Step 1: Output-layer error signal**

For the output layer (layer $L$):

$$\delta_k^{(L)} = (y_k - t_k) \, \sigma'(u_k^{(L)})$$

**Step 2: Backpropagation to Hidden Layer 2**

The error at hidden layer 2 neuron $j$ depends on its contribution to *all* output neurons:

$$\delta_j^{(L-1)} = \left(\sum_{k=1}^{m} w_{jk}^{(L)} \, \delta_k^{(L)}\right) \sigma'(u_j^{(L-1)})$$

**Step 3: Backpropagation to Hidden Layer 1**

Similarly:

$$\delta_i^{(L-2)} = \left(\sum_{j=1}^{H_2} w_{ij}^{(L-1)} \, \delta_j^{(L-1)}\right) \sigma'(u_i^{(L-2)})$$

**General Recursive Form:** For any layer $l$:

$$\delta^{(l)} = \left( (\mathbf{W}^{(l+1)})^T \delta^{(l+1)} \right) \odot \sigma'(\mathbf{u}^{(l)})$$

where $\odot$ denotes the **element-wise (Hadamard) product**.

**Step 4: Weight update (generalized delta rule)**

For weight connecting neuron $i$ in layer $l-1$ to neuron $j$ in layer $l$:

$$w_{ij}^{(l,\text{new})} = w_{ij}^{(l,\text{old})} + \eta \, \delta_j^{(l)} \, z_i^{(l-1)}$$

**Significance of the GDR:**

1. **Locality of computation:** Each neuron only needs information that is "local" to it — its own pre-activation and the deltas of the next layer — to compute its delta and weight updates. This is what makes the algorithm biologically plausible and computationally efficient.

2. **Scalability to deep networks:** The recursive structure allows the same algorithm to train networks with any number of hidden layers, which is the foundation of modern deep learning.

3. **Generalization of the perceptron rule:** The original delta rule $w \leftarrow w + \eta(t - y)x$ is recovered as a special case when there is one layer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Definition:** MLFFN = input + ≥1 hidden + output layers, fully connected, strictly feedforward.
- [x] **Why hidden layers?** They introduce non-linearity, enabling the network to solve problems like XOR that single-layer perceptrons cannot.
- [x] **Forward pass:** $u_j = \sum_i w_{ij} z_i + b_j$, then $z_j = \sigma(u_j)$.
- [x] **Loss function (MSE):** $E = \frac{1}{2} \sum_k (t_k - y_k)^2$.
- [x] **Output delta:** $\delta_k^{(L)} = (y_k - t_k) \, \sigma'(u_k^{(L)})$.
- [x] **Hidden delta (backprop formula):** $\delta_j^{(l)} = \left(\sum_k w_{jk}^{(l+1)} \delta_k^{(l+1)}\right) \sigma'(u_j^{(l)})$.
- [x] **Weight update (gradient descent):** $w_{ij} \leftarrow w_{ij} + \eta \, \delta_j^{(l)} \, z_i^{(l-1)}$.
- [x] **Bias update:** $b_j \leftarrow b_j + \eta \, \delta_j^{(l)}$.
- [x] **Activation functions:** Sigmoid (binary output), Tanh (hidden), ReLU (deep hidden layers), Softmax (multi-class output).
- [x] **Vanishing gradient:** Sigmoid derivative ≤ 0.25 causes gradient decay; ReLU mitigates by giving gradient = 1 for $u > 0$.
- [x] **Universal Approximation Theorem:** A 1-hidden-layer MLFFN with sufficient neurons can approximate any continuous function on a compact set to arbitrary accuracy.
- [x] **Total parameters:** $P = \sum_{l=1}^{L}(H_{l-1} \cdot H_l + H_l)$.
- [x] **GDR (Generalized Delta Rule):** Extension of the perceptron delta rule to multi-layer networks using the chain rule.
- [x] **History:** MLFFNs became practical after Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams' 1986 backpropagation paper.
- [x] **No cycles:** "Feed Forward" means no feedback connections — information flows only one way.
- [x] **Fully Connected:** Every node in layer $l$ connects to every node in layer $l+1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** In derivation questions, **write the chain rule expansion explicitly** and **box your final deltas and weight-update equations** — examiners award marks for each clearly stated intermediate step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activation Functions (Sigmoid, ReLU, Tanh)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT414 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Activation Functions (Sigmoid, ReLU, Tanh)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Activation Function (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> An *activation function* in an artificial neuron is a mathematical transformation $f(\cdot)$ applied to the net weighted input $z = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b$ of a neuron, producing the output $a = f(z)$ that is propagated to the next layer. Its primary role is to introduce **non-linearity** into the network, enabling it to learn complex, non-linear decision boundaries from data.

Mathematically, for a single neuron in layer $l$:

$$z^{(l)} = \mathbf{w}^{(l)} \cdot \mathbf{a}^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}$$

$$a^{(l)} = f\!\left(z^{(l)}\right)$$

where $f(\cdot)$ is the activation function, $\mathbf{w}^{(l)}$ is the weight vector, $b^{(l)}$ is the bias, and $\mathbf{a}^{(l-1)}$ is the activation vector from the previous layer.

## 1.2 Why Do We Need Activation Functions?

If we stack multiple linear transformations without a non-linear activation, the entire network collapses into a single linear model — no matter how many layers we add. Formally, the composition of linear functions is itself linear:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = W_2(W_1\mathbf{x} + b_1) + b_2 = (W_2 W_1)\mathbf{x} + (W_2 b_1 + b_2)$$

This is why a non-linear $f(\cdot)$ is **indispensable** for deep learning.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Examiners often ask *"Why can't we use a linear activation in hidden layers?"* — the answer lies in the above collapse argument.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a neuron as a **thermostat-controlled light bulb**:

- The **weighted sum** $z$ is the *raw electrical signal* arriving at the bulb.
- The **activation function** is the *thermostat knob* that decides how strongly the bulb glows.
  - A **Sigmoid** thermostat maps any input smoothly to a glow between 0% and 100% — useful for *probability-like* outputs.
  - A **Tanh** thermostat allows the bulb to glow *positively* (warm) or *negatively* (cool), centred at zero.
  - A **ReLU** thermostat is a simple *on/off switch with dimmer*: if the input is positive, pass it through; otherwise, switch off completely.

This analogy makes it clear why different activation functions suit different problem contexts (classification, regression, hidden layers, output layers).

## 1.4 The Three Activation Functions Under Study

| Function | Full Name | Output Range | Year Popularised |
|----------|-----------|--------------|------------------|
| Sigmoid  | Logistic Sigmoid | $(0, 1)$ | 1980s–1990s |
| Tanh     | Hyperbolic Tangent | $(-1, 1)$ | 1980s–1990s |
| ReLU     | Rectified Linear Unit | $[0, \infty)$ | 2010–present |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECBT414 Module 3):** Students must be able to *derive the gradients*, *explain vanishing-gradient behaviour*, and *compare the three functions* under standard KTU evaluation rubrics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sigmoid Activation Function

### Mathematical Form

$$\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$$

### Operational Properties

- **Range:** $\sigma(x) \in (0, 1)$ — interpretable as a probability.
- **Smooth and differentiable everywhere** — required for gradient-based optimisation.
- **Saturates** at both extremes: $\sigma(x) \to 0$ as $x \to -\infty$, and $\sigma(x) \to 1$ as $x \to +\infty$.
- **Zero-centred?** **No** — outputs are always positive, leading to inefficient gradient updates (a KTU-favourite point).

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

1. Compute the linear combination $z = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b$.
2. Exponentiate the negative input: $e^{-z}$.
3. Add 1 to the result: $1 + e^{-z}$.
4. Take the reciprocal: $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$.
5. Pass the result to the next layer as activation $a$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sigmoid at origin:** $\sigma(0) = 0.5$, exactly. This is a high-yield exam fact.

## 2.2 The Tanh Activation Function

### Mathematical Form

$$\tanh(x) = \frac{e^{x} - e^{-x}}{e^{x} + e^{-x}}$$

A key identity links tanh to sigmoid:

$$\tanh(x) = 2\sigma(2x) - 1$$

### Operational Properties

- **Range:** $\tanh(x) \in (-1, 1)$ — *zero-centred*, which is its main advantage over sigmoid.
- **Saturates** like sigmoid for large $\vert x \vert$.
- **Smooth and differentiable everywhere.**
- **Stronger gradients** near zero than sigmoid (slope = 1 at origin).

## 2.3 The ReLU Activation Function

### Mathematical Form

$$\text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0, x) = \begin{cases} x, & x \geq 0 \\ 0, & x < 0 \end{cases}$$

### Operational Properties

- **Range:** $[0, \infty)$ — *unbounded* above.
- **Computationally cheap** — only a thresholding operation, no exponentials.
- **Does not saturate** for positive inputs — solves the vanishing-gradient problem in the forward half-space.
- **Not differentiable at $x = 0$** — by convention, derivative is set to 0 there.
- **Dying ReLU problem** — neurons can get permanently stuck outputting 0 if they fall into the negative region during training.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these formulas appear in 90% of KTU activation-function questions.**

| Function | Forward Equation $f(x)$ | Derivative $f'(x)$ | Output Range | Zero-Centred? | Saturation? |
|----------|--------------------------|----------------------|---------------|----------------|--------------|
| Sigmoid  | $\dfrac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$   | $\sigma(x)\left[1-\sigma(x)\right]$ | $(0,\,1)$ | No | Yes (both sides) |
| Tanh     | $\dfrac{e^{x}-e^{-x}}{e^{x}+e^{-x}}$ | $1-\tanh^{2}(x)$ | $(-1,\,1)$ | Yes | Yes (both sides) |
| ReLU     | $\max(0,\,x)$           | $\begin{cases} 1, & x>0 \\ 0, & x\leq 0 \end{cases}$ | $[0,\,\infty)$ | No | One-sided (negative) |

### Critical Derivative Forms (exam-essential)

**Sigmoid derivative:**

$$\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)\left[1 - \sigma(x)\right]$$

**Tanh derivative:**

$$\frac{d}{dx}\tanh(x) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(x)$$

**ReLU derivative:**

$$\text{ReLU}'(x) = \begin{cases} 1, & x > 0 \\ 0, & x \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where Each Function is Used

| Application Domain | Preferred Activation | KTU Exam Reasoning |
|--------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Hidden layers of deep CNNs (post-2010) | **ReLU** | Computational efficiency, no vanishing gradient |
| Binary classification output layer | **Sigmoid** | Maps logit to probability in $(0,1)$ |
| Multiclass classification output | **Softmax** (generalised sigmoid) | Produces probability simplex |
| Hidden layers of RNN/LSTM (older) | **Tanh** | Zero-centred, helps gradient flow |
| Regression output layer | **Linear (identity)** | Unbounded real-valued output |
| GAN generators (output layer) | **Tanh** | Matches normalised image pixel range $[-1,1]$ |

## 2.6 The Vanishing Gradient Problem — A KTU Hot Topic

When $\vert x \vert$ is large, the sigmoid derivative approaches 0:

$$\lim_{x \to +\infty} \sigma'(x) = \lim_{x \to +\infty} \sigma(x)\left[1 - \sigma(x)\right] = 1 \cdot (1 - 1) = 0$$

$$\lim_{x \to -\infty} \sigma'(x) = 0 \cdot (1 - 0) = 0$$

In a deep network with $L$ layers, the gradient of the loss $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to early weights is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}^{(1)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \cdot \prod_{l=2}^{L} f'\!\left(z^{(l)}\right) \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(l)}}{\partial a^{(l-1)}}$$

If each $f'(z^{(l)}) \leq 0.25$ (max of sigmoid derivative), the product shrinks as $0.25^{L-1}$, vanishing for large $L$. **ReLU fixes this** because $f'(x) = 1$ for $x > 0$, keeping the product stable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Sigmoid Derivative

We start with the sigmoid function and use the quotient rule carefully.

$$\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$$

**Step 1 — Rewrite as a power for easier differentiation.**

$$\sigma(x) = \left(1 + e^{-x}\right)^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule.**

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dx} = -1 \cdot \left(1 + e^{-x}\right)^{-2} \cdot \frac{d}{dx}\left(1 + e^{-x}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the inner expression.**

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(1 + e^{-x}\right) = 0 + e^{-x} \cdot (-1) = -e^{-x}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back.**

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dx} = -\left(1 + e^{-x}\right)^{-2} \cdot \left(-e^{-x}\right) = \frac{e^{-x}}{\left(1 + e^{-x}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Factor $\sigma(x)$ out of the expression.**

Notice that $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$, so $1 - \sigma(x) = \dfrac{1 + e^{-x} - 1}{1 + e^{-x}} = \dfrac{e^{-x}}{1 + e^{-x}}$.

**Step 6 — Multiply $\sigma(x) \cdot [1 - \sigma(x)]$.**

$$\sigma(x) \cdot \left[1 - \sigma(x)\right] = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}} \cdot \frac{e^{-x}}{1 + e^{-x}} = \frac{e^{-x}}{\left(1 + e^{-x}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 7 — Compare with Step 4 result.**

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dx} = \frac{e^{-x}}{\left(1 + e^{-x}\right)^{2}} = \sigma(x)\left[1 - \sigma(x)\right]$$

$$\boxed{\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)\left[1 - \sigma(x)\right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this form matters:** During backpropagation, the gradient is computed using the *output* $\sigma(x)$, not the input $x$. This allows the network to reuse the forward-pass activation — a memory-efficient trick examiners love to ask about.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Tanh Derivative

We use the quotient rule on the definition.

$$\tanh(x) = \frac{e^{x} - e^{-x}}{e^{x} + e^{-x}}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the quotient rule $\left(\dfrac{u}{v}\right)' = \dfrac{u'v - uv'}{v^{2}}$.**

With $u = e^{x} - e^{-x}$ and $v = e^{x} + e^{-x}$:

$$u' = e^{x} + e^{-x} = v, \qquad v' = e^{x} - e^{-x} = u$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the quotient rule.**

$$\tanh'(x) = \frac{v \cdot v - u \cdot u}{v^{2}} = \frac{v^{2} - u^{2}}{v^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Factor as a difference of squares.**

$$v^{2} - u^{2} = (v - u)(v + u)$$

But more directly:

$$\tanh'(x) = 1 - \left(\frac{u}{v}\right)^{2} = 1 - \tanh^{2}(x)$$

**Step 4 — Final form.**

$$\boxed{\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(x)}$$

## 3.3 ReLU Derivative — Piecewise Analysis

$$\text{ReLU}(x) = \begin{cases} x, & x \geq 0 \\ 0, & x < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate each branch.**

For $x > 0$: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x) = 1$.

For $x < 0$: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(0) = 0$.

**Step 2 — Handle the point $x = 0$.**

The function is **not differentiable** at $x = 0$ (corner point). By convention in deep-learning frameworks:

$$\text{ReLU}'(0) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Final piecewise form.**

$$\boxed{\text{ReLU}'(x) = \begin{cases} 1, & x > 0 \\ 0, & x \leq 0 \end{cases}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Maximum Gradient Magnitudes

This is a frequently-tested comparison.

| Activation | Max Gradient | Gradient at $x=0$ | Behaviour as $\vert x \vert \to \infty$ |
|------------|---------------|--------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Sigmoid    | $0.25$ at $x=0$ | $0.25$ | Saturates to $0$ |
| Tanh       | $1.00$ at $x=0$ | $1.00$ | Saturates to $0$ |
| ReLU       | $1.00$ for $x>0$ | Undefined (set to 0) | Constant ($1$ for $x>0$, $0$ for $x<0$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** The maximum gradient of sigmoid is **only 0.25**, while tanh and ReLU reach **1.0**. This is precisely why ReLU is preferred in deep hidden layers.

## 3.5 Vanishing Gradient — Quantitative Bound

For a chain of $L$ sigmoid-activated layers, the gradient of the loss w.r.t. weight in layer $k$ is bounded:

$$\left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w^{(k)}} \right\vert \leq \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{L-k} \cdot M$$

where $M$ is a constant depending on loss and weights. For $L = 10$:

$$\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{10} = \frac{1}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 9.5 \times 10^{-7}$$

This is essentially **numerical zero** in 32-bit floating point — the network cannot learn the early layers. This calculation is a KTU "show-off" derivation worth full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single Neuron Computational Block Diagram

The figure below shows the role of the activation function inside a single artificial neuron, capturing the complete data flow from input to output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Vector x"] --> B["Weighted Sum z = wT x + b"]
    B --> C["Activation Function f"]
    C --> D["Output Activation a"]
    D --> E["Next Layer"]
    B -.->|"Gradient flows back via df/dz"| F["Backpropagation Path"]
    F --> G["Update weights w and bias b"]
    G --> A
```

## 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram of the Three Activations

The following block diagram compares the internal computation of each activation function. Note how sigmoid and tanh both involve exponentials, whereas ReLU uses only a thresholding operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SG["Sigmoid Block"]
        SG1["Input z"] --> SG2["Compute exp of minus z"]
        SG2 --> SG3["Add 1 to result"]
        SG3 --> SG4["Take reciprocal"]
        SG4 --> SG5["Output in range 0 to 1"]
    end
    subgraph TH["Tanh Block"]
        TH1["Input z"] --> TH2["Compute exp z and exp minus z"]
        TH2 --> TH3["Form numerator and denominator"]
        TH3 --> TH4["Divide numerator by denominator"]
        TH4 --> TH5["Output in range minus 1 to 1"]
    end
    subgraph RL["ReLU Block"]
        RL1["Input z"] --> RL2["Decision z greater than 0"]
        RL2 -->|"Yes"| RL3["Output equals z"]
        RL2 -->|"No"| RL4["Output equals 0"]
    end
    SG5 --> CMP["Decision Stage"]
    TH5 --> CMP
    RL3 --> CMP
    RL4 --> CMP
```

## 4.3 Backpropagation Gradient Flow Diagram

This block-level schematic illustrates how gradients propagate backwards through an activation function. The derivative block $f'(z)$ is the critical component — its magnitude determines whether the network learns or suffers vanishing gradients.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["Loss L"] --> LD["Derivative dL divided by da"]
    LD --> AD["Multiply by activation derivative fp of z"]
    AD --> ZD["Derivative dL divided by dz"]
    ZD --> WD["Derivative dL divided by w"]
    WD --> UP["Weight Update w new = w old minus eta times gradient"]
    UP -.->|"Forward pass for next iteration"| NN["Neural Network"]
    NN --> L
```

## 4.4 Function Shape and Gradient Magnitude — Conceptual Map

This conceptual map correlates the **shape** of each activation with the **shape of its gradient**, helping students visualise why ReLU avoids saturation in the positive half-space.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SAT["Saturation Regions"]
        SIG_SAT["Sigmoid saturates near 0 and 1"]
        TAN_SAT["Tanh saturates near minus 1 and 1"]
    end
    subgraph NON["Non-Saturating Region"]
        RELU_NON["ReLU does not saturate for x greater than 0"]
    end
    subgraph VAN["Vanishing Gradient Source"]
        V1["Sigmoid max gradient 0.25 at x equals 0"]
        V2["Tanh max gradient 1.0 at x equals 0"]
        V3["ReLU gradient is constant 1 for x greater than 0"]
    end
    SIG_SAT --> V1
    TAN_SAT --> V2
    RELU_NON --> V3
    V3 --> WIN["ReLU wins for deep networks"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define an activation function. Why is non-linearity essential in a neural network?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An activation function is a mathematical transformation applied to the net input of a neuron to produce its output. It introduces non-linearity into the network. **(1 mark)**

Without non-linearity, stacking layers is equivalent to a single linear transformation: $f(\mathbf{x}) = (W_2 W_1)\mathbf{x} + (W_2 b_1 + b_2)$, so deep networks cannot model complex patterns. **(1 mark)**

Non-linear activations like sigmoid, tanh, and ReLU enable the network to approximate any continuous function (Universal Approximation Theorem). **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Compare sigmoid and ReLU activation functions based on gradient behaviour and computational cost.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Sigmoid | ReLU |
|--------|---------|------|
| Gradient range | $(0, 0.25]$ | $\{0, 1\}$ |
| Vanishing gradient | Yes (both sides) | Only on negative side |
| Computation | Exponentiation | Simple max operation |
| Output range | $(0, 1)$ | $[0, \infty)$ |

Sigmoid saturates for large inputs, causing vanishing gradients; ReLU maintains a unit gradient for positive inputs. **(1 mark)** ReLU is computationally cheaper as it avoids exponentiation. **(1 mark)** ReLU is therefore preferred in hidden layers of deep networks. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) and (b) typically of 7 marks each. The internal choice must be offered within the same module.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the gradient of the sigmoid activation function. Using this result, explain the vanishing gradient problem in deep networks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the ReLU activation function. Discuss the *dying ReLU* problem and explain how Leaky ReLU and Parametric ReLU address it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Sigmoid Gradient and Vanishing Gradient

**Step 1 — Write the sigmoid function:**

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} = (1 + e^{-z})^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate using the chain rule:**

$$\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = -1 \cdot (1 + e^{-z})^{-2} \cdot (-e^{-z}) = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into the standard form:**

$$\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z) \cdot [1 - \sigma(z)]$$

**Step 4 — Maximum gradient calculation:** Setting $\sigma'(z)$ to maximum by differentiating again or noting that $\sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ is maximised when $\sigma(z) = 0.5$:

$$\sigma'(0) = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25$$

So the gradient is at most **0.25** at $z = 0$, and drops to nearly 0 for $\vert z \vert > 5$.

**Step 5 — Vanishing gradient in deep networks:** For a network of $L$ layers, the gradient flowing back to layer $k$ involves a product of derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w^{(k)}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \cdot \prod_{l=k+1}^{L} \sigma'(z^{(l)}) \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(l)}}{\partial a^{(l-1)}}$$

Each factor $\sigma'(z^{(l)}) \leq 0.25$, so the product is bounded by $0.25^{L-k}$. For $L - k = 10$, this equals $9.5 \times 10^{-7}$ — essentially zero.

**Step 6 — Consequence:** Early layers receive negligible gradient updates, fail to learn useful features, and the network stagnates. **(1 mark)** This is the vanishing gradient problem.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (KTU Valuation):** Many students forget to **explicitly state the maximum gradient value of 0.25** at $z = 0$. This single missing fact can cost you 1 full mark.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — ReLU and its Variants

**Step 1 — Define ReLU:**

$$\text{ReLU}(z) = \max(0, z) = \begin{cases} z, & z \geq 0 \\ 0, & z < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Derivative:**

$$\text{ReLU}'(z) = \begin{cases} 1, & z > 0 \\ 0, & z \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Properties:** Range $[0, \infty)$, computationally cheap (no exponentials), gradient is either 0 or 1, so no vanishing gradient for positive inputs.

**Step 4 — Dying ReLU problem:** When a neuron's weights update such that its net input is always negative, it outputs 0 for **all** training examples. Its gradient is 0, so its weights never update again — the neuron is "dead". With a high learning rate, up to 40% of neurons can die in some networks.

**Step 5 — Leaky ReLU:**

$$\text{LReLU}(z) = \begin{cases} z, & z \geq 0 \\ \alpha z, & z < 0 \end{cases} \quad \text{with } \alpha \approx 0.01$$

This allows a small, non-zero gradient when $z < 0$, preventing the neuron from dying.

**Step 6 — Parametric ReLU (PReLU):**

$$\text{PReLU}(z) = \begin{cases} z, & z \geq 0 \\ \alpha z, & z < 0 \end{cases} \quad \text{where } \alpha \text{ is learned via backprop}$$

PReLU makes $\alpha$ a trainable parameter, often yielding better performance on ImageNet-scale tasks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often describe Leaky ReLU but forget to mention that PReLU's $\alpha$ is *learned*, not fixed. This is a frequently-asked distinction worth 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the derivative of the tanh activation function. Compare it with the sigmoid function in terms of zero-centred output and gradient magnitude. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the practical issues with sigmoid activation in deep networks. How does ReLU solve the vanishing gradient problem, and what is its own limitation? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Tanh Derivative and Comparison

**Step 1 — Define tanh:**

$$\tanh(z) = \frac{e^{z} - e^{-z}}{e^{z} + e^{-z}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply quotient rule:** Let $u = e^{z} - e^{-z}$, $v = e^{z} + e^{-z}$, so $u' = v$, $v' = u$.

$$\tanh'(z) = \frac{u'v - uv'}{v^{2}} = \frac{v \cdot v - u \cdot u}{v^{2}} = \frac{v^{2} - u^{2}}{v^{2}} = 1 - \left(\frac{u}{v}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Final derivative:**

$$\boxed{\tanh'(z) = 1 - \tanh^{2}(z)}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum gradient:** $\tanh'(0) = 1 - 0 = 1$, which is **four times larger** than sigmoid's maximum of $0.25$.

**Step 5 — Zero-centred comparison:**

| Property | Sigmoid | Tanh |
|----------|---------|------|
| Output range | $(0, 1)$ | $(-1, 1)$ |
| Zero-centred | No | **Yes** |
| Max gradient | 0.25 | 1.00 |
| Saturates | Yes | Yes |

**Step 6 — Why zero-centred matters:** When outputs are zero-centred, gradient updates to weights are more balanced — positive and negative error signals can flow symmetrically. With sigmoid's always-positive output, all gradients at a layer have the same sign, leading to zig-zagging gradient descent.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "tanh is better than sigmoid" without justifying *why zero-centred outputs help optimisation*. Memorise the zig-zag argument above for full marks.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Sigmoid Issues and ReLU Trade-offs

**Step 1 — Issues with sigmoid in deep networks:**

1. **Vanishing gradients** — $\sigma'(z) \leq 0.25$, so deep networks have exponentially shrinking gradients.
2. **Not zero-centred** — causes zig-zag updates during gradient descent.
3. **Computationally expensive** — requires $e^{-z}$ calculation per neuron per forward pass.
4. **Saturates both sides** — neurons can get "stuck" in saturated regions where learning is slow.

**Step 2 — How ReLU solves vanishing gradient:**

For $z > 0$, $\text{ReLU}'(z) = 1$, so the gradient product in backpropagation is not multiplied by any diminishing factor on the positive side. The gradient magnitude is preserved across many layers, enabling training of very deep networks (VGG, ResNet, etc.).

**Step 3 — ReLU's own limitation — Dying ReLU:** When $z < 0$, $\text{ReLU}'(z) = 0$. If a neuron's bias shifts strongly negative during training, the gradient becomes zero permanently and the neuron never recovers. **Solutions:** Leaky ReLU, PReLU, ELU, and proper learning-rate selection (e.g., He initialisation).

**Step 4 — Quantitative bound:** The gradient of loss at layer $k$ for ReLU networks is bounded below by:

$$\left\vert \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w^{(k)}} \right\vert \geq \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{L-k} \cdot M$$

assuming half the neurons are in the active (positive) region. This is vastly better than sigmoid's $0.25^{L-k}$.

**Step 5 — Practical guideline (KTU viva-favourite):** Use ReLU (or Leaky ReLU) in hidden layers, sigmoid only in the **output layer for binary classification**, and softmax for **multiclass output**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss:** Students frequently claim ReLU has *no* drawbacks. Always mention the **dying ReLU problem** and at least one variant (Leaky ReLU) to demonstrate complete understanding.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorise Before Every KTU Exam**

- **Sigmoid:** $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$; range $(0,1)$; derivative $\sigma(x)[1-\sigma(x)]$; max gradient $0.25$ at $x=0$.
- **Tanh:** range $(-1,1)$; derivative $1-\tanh^{2}(x)$; max gradient $1.0$ at $x=0$; **zero-centred**.
- **ReLU:** $\max(0, x)$; range $[0, \infty)$; derivative $1$ for $x>0$, $0$ for $x\leq 0$; **no vanishing gradient for positive inputs**.
- **Vanishing gradient:** Product of $L-k$ sigmoid derivatives is bounded by $0.25^{L-k}$ — numerically zero for $L-k \geq 10$.
- **Dying ReLU:** Neurons stuck in negative region output 0 forever; solved by **Leaky ReLU** (fixed $\alpha \approx 0.01$) or **PReLU** (learned $\alpha$).
- **Identity link:** $\tanh(x) = 2\sigma(2x) - 1$ — useful for converting between functions in derivations.
- **Sigmoid ≠ zero-centred** — causes zig-zag gradient descent; tanh solves this.
- **Sigmoid for output only in binary classification**; ReLU in hidden layers; softmax for multiclass output.
- **ReLU is non-differentiable at $x=0$** — convention sets derivative to 0.
- **Universal Approximation Theorem** justifies why a non-linear activation enables a finite network to approximate *any* continuous function on a compact domain.
- **Exponentially small gradients** mean that with sigmoid, only the last 2–3 layers of a deep network actually learn effectively — a 2-mark KTU-favourite conclusion.
- **He initialisation** is the recommended weight initialisation scheme for ReLU networks because it compensates for ReLU's variance-shrinking effect (variance halved per layer).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backpropagation Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT414 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Backpropagation Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backpropagation (Backward Propagation of Errors)** is a supervised learning algorithm used to train **Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs)** and other differentiable artificial neural networks. It is fundamentally an efficient application of the **Chain Rule of Calculus** that computes the **gradient of the loss function** with respect to every weight and bias in the network, then updates these parameters in the opposite direction of the gradient to **minimize the loss** using **Gradient Descent**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, backpropagation is formally decomposed into two synchronized phases operating in opposite directions through the network topology:

| Phase | Direction | Purpose | Computes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Forward Pass** | Input → Output | Signal propagation | Activations and network output |
| **Backward Pass** | Output → Input | Error propagation | Local gradients (deltas) for every neuron |

The algorithm is essentially a recursive application of the **generalized delta rule** to networks with one or more hidden layers, as formalized by **Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams (1986)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Blame Game" Analogy (Mountain Rescue Training)**
>
> Imagine a team of mountain guides spread across three positions: **Lookouts** (input layer), **Coordinators** (hidden layer), and the **Rescue Chief** (output layer). The Chief makes a final decision on which rescue route to take. After the rescue attempt, the team reviews what went wrong.
>
> - The **Chief** knows directly how wrong the final decision was — clear error signal.
> - But what about the **Coordinators**? Their individual contribution is hidden. The Chief must pass **"blame"** backward to each Coordinator in proportion to how much they influenced the (wrong) decision.
> - Each Coordinator, in turn, passes proportional blame back to the Lookouts whose signals they trusted.
>
> This recursive blame-passing is **backpropagation**. The "amount of blame" each unit receives is its **gradient (delta)**. The unit then adjusts its **weights** (how much it trusts each incoming signal) so that next time, the final decision will be better.

The **learning rate** $\eta$ controls how aggressively the weights are adjusted — too high, and the system overcorrects and oscillates; too low, and training takes forever.

## 1.3 Why Backpropagation Matters — Engineering Utility

- **Foundation of Deep Learning**: Every modern CNN, RNN, Transformer, and LLM is trained using a variant of backpropagation (often called **auto-differentiation** in modern frameworks).
- **Scalability**: It computes gradients in $O(W)$ time (where $W$ is the number of weights), the same order as a single forward pass — making training of millions of parameters feasible.
- **Versatility**: Works with **any differentiable activation function** and **any differentiable loss function**.

---

## 1.4 Prerequisites for Backpropagation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For backpropagation to be mathematically valid, the network must satisfy three conditions:
>
> 1. **Differentiability**: All activation functions must be differentiable everywhere (or almost everywhere).
> 2. **Differentiable Loss Function**: The loss $E$ must be a smooth, differentiable function of the network output.
> 3. **Layered Feed-Forward Topology**: Information flows strictly forward during the forward pass, even though error signals flow strictly backward.

---

## 1.5 Key Terminology Glossary (KTU 2024 Mandate)

| Term | Notation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Input vector** | $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ | The features fed into the network |
| **Net input** | $\text{net}_j$ | Weighted sum of inputs to neuron $j$ |
| **Activation** | $a_j$ | Output of neuron $j$ after applying the activation function |
| **Weight** | $w_{ij}$ | Connection strength from neuron $i$ to neuron $j$ |
| **Bias** | $b_j$ | Constant offset added to net input of neuron $j$ |
| **Local gradient / Delta** | $\delta_j$ | "Blame" assigned to neuron $j$ |
| **Learning rate** | $\eta$ | Step size for weight updates |
| **Loss / Error** | $E$ | Scalar measure of prediction error |
| **Target** | $y_k$ | Ground truth label for output neuron $k$ |
| **Predicted output** | $o_k$ | Network's prediction for output neuron $k$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Operational Pipeline

The backpropagation algorithm operates in three tightly coupled stages per training sample (or mini-batch). The KTU 2024 Scheme rubric explicitly tests understanding of each stage.

### Stage 1 — The Forward Pass (Activation Propagation)

1. Present the input vector $\mathbf{x}$ to the input layer.
2. For **every** hidden neuron $j$, compute the **net input**:
$$\text{net}_j = \sum_{i} w_{ij} x_i + b_j$$
3. Apply the **activation function** $\sigma$ to obtain the activation:
$$a_j = \sigma(\text{net}_j)$$
4. Propagate $a_j$ to the next layer; repeat until the **output layer** produces predictions $o_k$.
5. Compute the **loss** $E$ by comparing $o_k$ against target $y_k$.

### Stage 2 — The Backward Pass (Error Propagation)

1. Compute the **local gradient** $\delta_k$ at each **output neuron** $k$ using the loss derivative and activation derivative.
2. For **each** hidden neuron $j$, compute its local gradient $\delta_j$ by **propagating the deltas of all downstream neurons backward** through the weights, multiplied by the derivative of its own activation function.
3. This is the **recursive application of the chain rule** that gives backpropagation its name.

### Stage 3 — The Weight Update (Gradient Descent Step)

For **every weight** $w_{ij}$ in the network:
$$w_{ij}^{\text{new}} = w_{ij}^{\text{old}} - \eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}}$$

Because $\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}} = \delta_j \cdot a_i$ (the activation feeding into the synapse), this simplifies to the **delta rule**:
$$w_{ij}^{\text{new}} = w_{ij}^{\text{old}} - \eta \cdot \delta_j \cdot a_i$$

Similarly, for every bias $b_j$:
$$b_j^{\text{new}} = b_j^{\text{old}} - \eta \cdot \delta_j$$

> [!NOTE]
> The product $\eta \cdot \delta_j \cdot a_i$ is the **"blame-weighted correction"** — a weight is updated more aggressively if (a) the downstream neuron is more "to blame" (large $\delta_j$), and (b) the upstream signal was stronger (large $a_i$). This is the cornerstone of the **Hebbian-inspired learning principle** embedded in backpropagation.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is HIGH-YIELD for KTU University Examinations. Memorize every entry.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Applies To | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sigmoid activation | $\sigma(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-x}}$ | Hidden/Output layers | Smooth, differentiable |
| 2 | Sigmoid derivative | $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)\bigl(1 - \sigma(x)\bigr)$ | Backprop | One of the most-tested identities |
| 3 | Tanh derivative | $\tanh'(x) = 1 - \tanh^2(x)$ | Backprop | Zero-centered alternative |
| 4 | ReLU derivative | $\text{ReLU}'(x) = \begin{cases} 1 & x > 0 \\ 0 & x \le 0 \end{cases}$ | Backprop | Used in deep nets |
| 5 | Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} \sum_{k} \bigl(y_k - o_k\bigr)^2$ | Regression outputs | Factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ cancels the exponent |
| 6 | Cross-entropy loss | $E = -\sum_{k} y_k \ln(o_k)$ | Classification outputs | Pairs with softmax |
| 7 | Net input | $\text{net}_j = \sum_{i} w_{ij} a_i + b_j$ | All neurons | Linear aggregation |
| 8 | Activation | $a_j = \sigma(\text{net}_j)$ | All neurons | Non-linear transformation |
| 9 | Output delta (MSE + sigmoid) | $\delta_k = (o_k - y_k) \cdot o_k (1 - o_k)$ | Output layer | The classical result |
| 10 | Output delta (cross-entropy + softmax) | $\delta_k = o_k - y_k$ | Output layer | Elegantly simplifies |
| 11 | Hidden delta (general) | $\delta_j = \sigma'(\text{net}_j) \cdot \sum_{k} w_{jk} \delta_k$ | Hidden layer | Chain rule recursion |
| 12 | Weight gradient | $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}} = \delta_j \cdot a_i$ | All weights | The core identity |
| 13 | Bias gradient | $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial b_j} = \delta_j$ | All biases | Bias is a weight with $a_i = 1$ |
| 14 | Weight update | $w_{ij}^{\text{new}} = w_{ij}^{\text{old}} - \eta \cdot \delta_j \cdot a_i$ | All weights | Gradient descent step |
| 15 | Bias update | $b_j^{\text{new}} = b_j^{\text{old}} - \eta \cdot \delta_j$ | All biases | Same rule |
| 16 | Epoch | 1 full pass through training set | Training loop | Multiple epochs required |

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Mathematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the output delta formula contain $o_k(1-o_k)$?**
> Because we are applying the **chain rule** to $\frac{\partial E}{\partial \text{net}_k} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial o_k} \cdot \frac{\partial o_k}{\partial \text{net}_k}$. The first factor from MSE is $(o_k - y_k)$, and the second factor is the sigmoid derivative $\sigma'(\text{net}_k) = o_k(1 - o_k)$. The KTU board examiner LOVES this derivation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the hidden delta a sum over $k$?**
> Because a single hidden neuron $j$ fans out to **multiple** downstream neurons $k$. Its total "blame" is the sum of each downstream neuron's blame, weighted by the strength of the connection $w_{jk}$. This is the heart of **distributed credit assignment**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Backpropagation is the engine behind:
- **Computer Vision** (image classification, object detection, medical imaging diagnosis).
- **Natural Language Processing** (machine translation, sentiment analysis, chatbots).
- **Speech Recognition** (Siri, Alexa, Google Assistant).
- **Autonomous Vehicles** (steering, obstacle detection, lane keeping).
- **Recommender Systems** (Netflix, YouTube, Spotify).
- **Scientific Discovery** (AlphaFold for protein folding, weather prediction, materials science).

Every KTU 2024 Scheme ML graduate is expected to understand this algorithm both mathematically and intuitively — it is the **single most important concept** in the entire course.
