| $0 < \alpha_i < C$ | **Margin support vector** | Lies **exactly on** the margin hyperplane ($y_i(\mathbf{w}\cdot\mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$) |
| $\alpha_i = C$ | **Non-margin / bounded support vector** | Lies inside the margin ($0 < \xi_i < 1$) or is misclassified ($\xi_i \geq 1$) |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Identifying Slack Variable Status

**Problem:** Given a 2D Soft Margin SVM with decision hyperplane $\mathbf{w} = (2, 1)$, $b = -3$, parameter $C = 10$, and a single training point $\mathbf{x} = (1, 1)$ with true label $y = -1$, compute the slack variable $\xi$ and classify the point's status.

**Step 1: Compute the functional margin.**

$$y_i (\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) = (-1) \left[ (2)(1) + (1)(1) + (-3) \right]$$

**Step 2: Simplify the inner expression.**

$$(2)(1) + (1)(1) - 3 = 2 + 1 - 3 = 0$$

**Step 3: Compute the functional margin.**

$$y_i (\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) = (-1)(0) = 0$$

**Step 4: Compute the slack variable.**

By the KKT complementarity / constraint:
$$\xi_i = \max(0, \, 1 - y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b)) = \max(0, \, 1 - 0) = 1$$

**Step 5: Classify the point.**

Since $\xi_i = 1$, the point lies **exactly on the decision hyperplane** (boundary case between "margin-respecting" and "misclassified"). The sign of $\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ also confirms this.

**Step 6: Compute the penalty contribution to the objective.**

$$C \cdot \xi_i = 10 \times 1 = 10$$

This point is a **non-margin support vector** (it will have $\alpha_i = C$ in the dual).

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Hard vs. Soft Margin SVM

| **Aspect** | **Hard Margin SVM** | **Soft Margin SVM (C-SVM)** |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| Data Assumption | Perfectly linearly separable | Allows overlapping / noisy data |
| Slack Variables | Not present | $\xi_i \geq 0$ for all $i$ |
| Objective Function | $\frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2$ | $\frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + C \sum \xi_i$ |
| Margin Constraint | $y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ (strict) | $y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ (relaxed) |
| Dual Constraint on $\alpha_i$ | $\alpha_i \geq 0$ | $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ (box constraint) |
| Outlier Sensitivity | Very high (single point can break it) | Low (controlled by C) |
| Overfitting Risk | High on noisy data | Tunable via C |
| Real-world Use | Rarely used directly | Industry standard |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Soft Margin SVM Training & Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Training Dataset] --> B[Feature Scaling and Normalization]
    B --> C[Choose Kernel Function]
    C --> D[Select Penalty Parameter C]
    D --> E[Construct Primal Problem]
    E --> F[Form Lagrangian with alpha_i and mu_i]
    F --> G[Apply Stationarity KKT Conditions]
    G --> H[Derive Dual QP Problem]
    H --> I[Solve Dual via SMO or QP Solver]
    I --> J{Identify Support Vectors}
    J --> K[Alpha equals 0: Non-SV]
    J --> L[0 less than Alpha less than C: Margin SV]
    J --> M[Alpha equals C: Bounded SV Misclassified or Inside Margin]
    L --> N[Reconstruct w and b]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Final Soft Margin Classifier]
    O --> P[New Test Point x]
    P --> Q[Compute sign of w dot x plus b]
    Q --> R[Predicted Class Label]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram: Geometric Layout of the Soft Margin

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Class Negative Region] -->|Negative Margin| B[Negative Margin Hyperplane]
    B --> C[Margin Band of Width 2 over norm w]
    C --> D[Positive Margin Hyperplane]
    D --> E[Class Positive Region]
    F[Support Vector at xi equals 0] --> B
    G[Support Vector at 0 less than xi less than 1] --> C
    H[Misclassified Point at xi greater than 1] --> A
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow: How the Penalty Parameter C Influences the Margin

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose C value] --> Q1{Is C very large}
    Q1 -->|Yes| R1[Strict Penalty, Narrow Margin, Low Bias, High Variance, Overfitting Risk]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is C very small}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R2[Lenient Penalty, Wide Margin, High Bias, Low Variance, Underfitting Risk]
    Q2 -->|No| R3[Balanced Tradeoff, Optimal Generalization]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to draw a Soft Margin SVM diagram, clearly mark the following: (1) the decision hyperplane, (2) both margin hyperplanes, (3) the margin width $\frac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$, (4) at least one support vector on each margin, and (5) **if possible**, one misclassified point with its slack $\xi_i > 1$ annotation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Soft Margin SVM Classifier. What is the role of the penalty parameter C in it?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Soft Margin SVM Classifier** is a generalization of the classical SVM that handles non-linearly separable data by introducing non-negative **slack variables** $\xi_i$ into the margin constraints. It solves the optimization problem:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \quad \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i (\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$

The **penalty parameter C** controls the trade-off between maximizing the margin (keeping $\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert$ small) and minimizing the classification error (keeping $\sum \xi_i$ small). A **large C** imposes heavy penalties on slack values, producing a narrower margin that misclassifies fewer training points (risk of overfitting). A **small C** tolerates more violations, producing a wider margin with better generalization on unseen data (risk of underfitting). **(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for C's role + 1 for trade-off explanation)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Explain the geometric meaning of slack variables $\xi_i$ in a Soft Margin SVM. What does $\xi_i > 1$ signify?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The slack variable $\xi_i$ quantifies **by how much** the $i^{th}$ training point violates the standard hard-margin constraint $y_i(\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$.

Geometrically, three cases arise:

- $\xi_i = 0$ → the point lies on or beyond the correct margin (fully satisfied).
- $0 < \xi_i < 1$ → the point lies **between** the two margin hyperplanes on the correct side of the decision boundary (margin violation, but correctly classified).
- $\xi_i > 1$ → the point lies on the **wrong side** of the decision hyperplane and is therefore **misclassified**.

Hence, $\xi_i > 1$ is a definitive indicator of misclassification by the soft-margin classifier. **(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for case classification + 1 for the $\xi_i > 1$ significance)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Formulate the **primal optimization problem** for the Soft Margin SVM Classifier. Clearly define all variables and constraints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **dual optimization problem** using Lagrangian multipliers and the KKT conditions. Explain the significance of the box constraint $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ in the dual. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Primal Formulation (7 Marks):**

The Soft Margin SVM Classifier extends the Hard Margin formulation to allow controlled misclassification. The primal problem is:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \quad \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

subject to:
$$y_i (\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, N$$

$$\xi_i \geq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, N$$

**Variable Definitions:**

- $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ — weight vector normal to the separating hyperplane.
- $b \in \mathbb{R}$ — bias (offset) of the hyperplane.
- $\xi_i \geq 0$ — slack variable measuring margin violation for the $i^{th}$ point.
- $C > 0$ — regularization parameter balancing margin width and training error.
- $N$ — number of training samples.
- $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ — class label of the $i^{th}$ sample.
- $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ — feature vector of the $i^{th}$ sample.

**Interpretation:** The first term $\frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2$ maximizes the geometric margin $\frac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$. The second term $C \sum \xi_i$ penalizes the cumulative violation. The constraints ensure that each point is either correctly classified (with margin) or its violation is captured by $\xi_i$.

**Part (b) — Dual Derivation and Significance of Box Constraint (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Form the Lagrangian** with multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\mu_i \geq 0$:

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i (\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu_i \xi_i$$

**Step 2: Stationarity conditions:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0 \implies \mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0 \implies \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = 0 \implies C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \implies \alpha_i = C - \mu_i \leq C$$

Since $\alpha_i \geq 0$ and $\mu_i \geq 0$, we obtain the **box constraint** $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.

**Step 3: Substitute back to get the dual:**

$$\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (\mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}_j)$$

subject to:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$$

**Step 4: Significance of the Box Constraint $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$:**

1. **Upper bound C** — Caps the influence of any single training point. In Hard Margin SVM, an outlier could have $\alpha_i \to \infty$ and dominate the classifier. The cap makes the model **robust to outliers and label noise**.
2. **Lower bound 0** — Retains the sparsity property: only support vectors have $\alpha_i > 0$.
3. **Three regimes of $\alpha_i$** (interpreting support vectors):
   - $\alpha_i = 0$ → not a support vector.
   - $0 < \alpha_i < C$ → margin support vector (lies exactly on the margin, $\xi_i = 0$).
   - $\alpha_i = C$ → bounded support vector (lies inside margin or misclassified, $\xi_i > 0$).
4. **Computational implication** — The box constraint transforms the optimization into a **bounded QP** that is always feasible and convex, solvable efficiently via the **Sequential Minimal Optimization (SMO)** algorithm. **(7 marks: 2 for Lagrangian + 2 for KKT/box derivation + 2 for final dual + 1 for significance explanation)**

---

**Q3. Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023 — Alternative Module Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the equivalence between the Soft Margin SVM primal and the **regularized hinge loss minimization** formulation. Write the unconstrained objective function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a Soft Margin SVM with the following training points in 1D:
- $x_1 = 1, \quad y_1 = +1$
- $x_2 = 2, \quad y_2 = +1$
- $x_3 = 3, \quad y_3 = -1$
- $x_4 = 4, \quad y_4 = -1$

Suppose the decision boundary is at $x = 2.5$ (i.e., $w = -1$, $b = 2.5$). Calculate the slack variables $\xi_i$ for each point and identify the **type of point** (margin-respecting, margin-violating, or misclassified) for each. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Equivalence to Hinge Loss (7 Marks):**

The Soft Margin SVM primal problem with explicit slack variables is:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \quad \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i (\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$

By inspection of the KKT condition on $\xi_i$ (specifically, that $\xi_i = 0$ when the constraint is non-active and $\xi_i = 1 - y_i f(\mathbf{x}_i)$ when the constraint is active), we can express $\xi_i$ in closed form as:

$$\xi_i = \max(0, \, 1 - y_i (\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b))$$

Substituting this back into the objective function eliminates $\xi_i$, yielding the **unconstrained formulation**:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \quad \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \max(0, \, 1 - y_i (\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b))$$

The function $\ell(y_i, f(\mathbf{x}_i)) = \max(0, \, 1 - y_i f(\mathbf{x}_i))$ is called the **hinge loss**. This is a convex, piecewise-linear upper bound on the 0-1 classification loss.

**Interpretation:**

- The first term $\frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2$ acts as an **L2 regularizer** (Tikhonov regularizer) controlling model complexity.
- The second term is the **sum of hinge losses** measuring empirical training error.
- The hyperparameter **C** trades off between the two: large C emphasizes training fit, small C emphasizes model simplicity.

This makes the Soft Margin SVM a member of the **Regularized Empirical Risk Minimization (RERM)** family, alongside Ridge Regression and L2-regularized Logistic Regression. **(7 marks: 2 for substitution step + 2 for hinge loss definition + 2 for unconstrained objective + 1 for RERM interpretation)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Slack Variable Calculation (7 Marks):**

Given $w = -1$, $b = 2.5$, the decision function is $f(x) = -x + 2.5$. The functional margin for each point is $y_i (w \cdot x_i + b) = y_i (-x_i + 2.5)$.

**For $x_1 = 1, y_1 = +1$:**

$$f(x_1) = -(1) + 2.5 = 1.5$$
$$y_1 \cdot f(x_1) = (+1)(1.5) = 1.5$$
$$\xi_1 = \max(0, \, 1 - 1.5) = \max(0, -0.5) = 0$$

→ **Type: Margin-respecting** (lies beyond the margin; correctly classified with margin to spare).

**For $x_2 = 2, y_2 = +1$:**

$$f(x_2) = -(2) + 2.5 = 0.5$$
$$y_2 \cdot f(x_2) = (+1)(0.5) = 0.5$$
$$\xi_2 = \max(0, \, 1 - 0.5) = \max(0, 0.5) = 0.5$$

→ **Type: Margin-violating** (lies between the two margins but on the correct side; $\xi_2 < 1$).

**For $x_3 = 3, y_3 = -1$:**

$$f(x_3) = -(3) + 2.5 = -0.5$$
$$y_3 \cdot f(x_3) = (-1)(-0.5) = 0.5$$
$$\xi_3 = \max(0, \, 1 - 0.5) = \max(0, 0.5) = 0.5$$

→ **Type: Margin-violating** (lies between the two margins but on the correct side; $\xi_3 < 1$).

**For $x_4 = 4, y_4 = -1$:**

$$f(x_4) = -(4) + 2.5 = -1.5$$
$$y_4 \cdot f(x_4) = (-1)(-1.5) = 1.5$$
$$\xi_4 = \max(0, \, 1 - 1.5) = \max(0, -0.5) = 0$$

→ **Type: Margin-respecting** (lies beyond the margin; correctly classified with margin to spare).

**Summary Table:**

| Point | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $y_i f(x_i)$ | $\xi_i$ | Type |
|-------|-------|-------|---------------|---------|------|
| 1 | 1 | +1 | 1.5 | 0 | Margin-respecting |
| 2 | 2 | +1 | 0.5 | 0.5 | Margin-violating |
| 3 | 3 | -1 | 0.5 | 0.5 | Margin-violating |
| 4 | 4 | -1 | 1.5 | 0 | Margin-respecting |

**Total penalty contribution:** $C \sum \xi_i = C \times 1.0 = 1.0 \, C$. **(7 marks: 1 mark per correct slack calculation + 1 mark for type identification per point + 1 mark for total penalty computation)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Soft Margin SVM Questions (KTU Examiner's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\xi_i \geq 0$ constraint** in the primal formulation → **−2 marks** instantly. The slack variables are non-negative by definition.
> 2. **Confusing margin-respecting vs. margin-violating vs. misclassified** in numerical problems. Remember: it is **$\xi_i > 1$** (not $\xi_i > 0$) that signifies misclassification.
> 3. **Writing the dual without the box constraint $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$** → this is the **defining difference** between Soft Margin and Hard Margin dual. Missing it → **−2 marks**.
> 4. **Omitting the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor in $\frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2$** — yes, it is there for mathematical convenience (cleaner derivatives). Examiners dock marks for sloppy notation.
> 5. **Mixing up "support vector" definitions** — there are **margin support vectors** ($\alpha_i \in (0, C)$) and **bounded support vectors** ($\alpha_i = C$). Do not call every non-zero $\alpha_i$ point simply a "support vector".
> 6. **Writing hinge loss as $\max(0, y_i f(\mathbf{x}_i))$** — the correct form is $\max(0, 1 - y_i f(\mathbf{x}_i))$. The "1" is the margin threshold, not 0.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist: Soft Margin SVM Classifier**
>
> ✅ **Core Idea:** Allow controlled misclassification using slack variables $\xi_i$ and a penalty parameter $C$ — solves the non-separability problem of Hard Margin SVM.
>
> ✅ **Primal Objective:** $\min \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$
>
> ✅ **Primal Constraints:** $y_i (\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ and $\xi_i \geq 0$
>
> ✅ **Slack Variable Interpretation:**
> - $\xi_i = 0$ → on or beyond margin (margin-respecting)
> - $0 < \xi_i < 1$ → inside margin, correctly classified (margin-violating)
> - $\xi_i \geq 1$ → on wrong side of hyperplane (misclassified)
>
> ✅ **Role of C:** High C = strict (overfitting risk); Low C = lenient (underfitting risk). C is the bias-variance trade-off knob.
>
> ✅ **Equivalent Form (Unconstrained):** $\min \frac{1}{2} \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert^2 + C \sum \max(0, 1 - y_i f(\mathbf{x}_i))$ — this is **regularized hinge loss minimization**.
>
> ✅ **Dual Problem:** $\max \sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$, subject to $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$ and $\boxed{0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C}$ (the box constraint).
>
> ✅ **Box Constraint $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$:** Caps the influence of outliers — the **defining difference** from Hard Margin dual.
>
> ✅ **Three Support Vector Types:**
> - $\alpha_i = 0$ → non-support vector
> - $0 < \alpha_i < C$ → margin support vector ($\xi_i = 0$)
> - $\alpha_i = C$ → bounded support vector ($\xi_i > 0$)
>
> ✅ **Weight Reconstruction:** $\mathbf{w} = \sum \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ — only support vectors contribute.
>
> ✅ **Hinge Loss:** $\ell(y, f) = \max(0, 1 - y f)$ — convex surrogate for 0-1 loss, piecewise linear.
>
> ✅ **Solver:** Convex QP solved by **Sequential Minimal Optimization (SMO)** algorithm in practice.
>
> ✅ **Engineering Application:** Spam filtering, MNIST digit recognition, face detection, bioinformatics (gene expression classification), text categorization.
>
> ✅ **Exam Mantra:** Always write **both** the primal AND dual forms in long-answer questions — partial credit is awarded generously when both are attempted.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Linear SVM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT414 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Non-Linear SVM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Non-Linear Support Vector Machine (Non-Linear SVM)** is a maximum-margin classification algorithm that extends the classical linear SVM to handle data that is **not linearly separable** in the original input space. It achieves this by employing the **kernel trick**, an elegant mathematical technique that implicitly maps the input data into a higher-dimensional (often infinite-dimensional) **feature space**, where a linear separating hyperplane can be constructed, without ever explicitly computing the coordinates of the data in that higher space.

Mathematically, given a training set $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the non-linear SVM introduces a non-linear mapping function $\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \mathcal{F}$ (where $\mathcal{F}$ is the high-dimensional feature space), and replaces the inner product $x_i \cdot x_j$ with a **kernel function** $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i) \cdot \phi(x_j)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Non-Linear SVM is a high-yield topic under Module 3. Students must master the kernel trick derivation, the four standard kernel functions, Mercer's condition, and the dual optimization formulation in terms of kernels. These appear almost every KTU examination cycle.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are at a busy marketplace with two types of fruits — red apples and green mangoes — scattered randomly on a flat table. You try to draw a **straight line** on the table to separate them, but the apples and mangoes are so intertwined that no straight line works. This is the **non-linearly separable** problem.

Now, suppose you are allowed to **lift the fruits into the air** based on a clever rule — for example, the height of each fruit depends on its color intensity and weight. Once in this **3D air space**, you can easily slide a **flat cardboard plane** between the floating apples and mangoes. This lifting rule is the **feature mapping $\phi(x)$**, the 3D air space is the **feature space $\mathcal{F}$**, and the cardboard plane is the **separating hyperplane**.

The brilliant insight of SVM is that we don't actually need to know the exact 3D positions of the lifted fruits. We only need to know **how similar two fruits are** in the lifted space — this similarity is computed by the **kernel function $K(x_i, x_j)$**. This avoidance of explicit coordinate computation is the celebrated **kernel trick**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term Glossary:**
> - **Feature Space ($\mathcal{F}$):** The high-dimensional space into which data is mapped.
> - **Kernel Function ($K$):** A function that computes inner products in the feature space without explicit mapping.
> - **Kernel Trick:** Replacing $x_i \cdot x_j$ with $K(x_i, x_j)$ in the dual formulation.
> - **Mercer's Theorem:** The mathematical condition that guarantees a function is a valid kernel.

### 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **Slack variable penalty parameter $C > 0$**: Controls the trade-off between margin maximization and classification error tolerance. **Larger $C$** means harder margin (less tolerance for misclassification), **smaller $C$** means softer margin.
- **Kernel parameter $\gamma > 0$** (in RBF and polynomial kernels): Controls the influence radius of a single training example.
- **Degree $d \in \mathbb{N}$** (in polynomial kernel): Controls the flexibility of the decision boundary.
- **Margin $\frac{2}{\|w\|}$**: The distance between the two parallel supporting hyperplanes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Limitation of Linear SVM

The standard **hard-margin linear SVM** solves:

$$\min_{w, b} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(w \cdot x_i + b) \geq 1, \; \forall i$$

This works perfectly when the data is **linearly separable**. However, in real-world problems (image recognition, bioinformatics, text classification), data is almost always non-linearly separable. A linear hyperplane will lead to massive misclassification. Non-Linear SVM solves this through **non-linear feature mapping**.

### 2.2 The Feature Mapping Approach

The conceptual strategy is:
- **Step 1:** Apply a non-linear mapping $\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \mathcal{F}$ to transform data.
- **Step 2:** Solve the linear SVM problem in feature space $\mathcal{F}$.

**Problem with explicit mapping:** The dimensionality of $\mathcal{F}$ can be enormous (or even infinite for the Gaussian kernel). For example, mapping a 2D point $(x_1, x_2)$ using all 2nd-degree monomials gives a 6-dimensional feature vector:
$$\phi(x_1, x_2) = (1, \sqrt{2}x_1, \sqrt{2}x_2, x_1^2, x_2^2, \sqrt{2}x_1 x_2)$$

For higher-degree polynomials, the dimensionality explodes. **This is where the kernel trick saves us.**

### 2.3 The Kernel Trick — The Core Idea

The dual formulation of the SVM problem contains $x_i$ and $x_j$ **only as inner products** $x_i \cdot x_j$. If we replace every $x_i \cdot x_j$ with $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i) \cdot \phi(j)$, we never need to compute $\phi(x_i)$ explicitly.

This substitution is called the **kernel trick**, and the function $K$ is called a **kernel function**.

### 2.4 The Four Standard Kernel Functions

| # | Kernel Name | Mathematical Formula $K(x_i, x_j)$ | Key Parameters | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear Kernel** | $K(x_i, x_j) = x_i \cdot x_j$ | None | When data is already linearly separable; reduces to standard linear SVM. |
| 2 | **Polynomial Kernel** | $K(x_i, x_j) = (\gamma (x_i \cdot x_j) + r)^d$ | $\gamma > 0$, $r \in \mathbb{R}$, $d \in \mathbb{N}$ | Image processing, NLP; when interaction between features is important. |
| 3 | **Radial Basis Function (RBF) / Gaussian Kernel** | $K(x_i, x_j) = \exp(-\gamma \|x_i - x_j\|^2)$ | $\gamma > 0$ | Most popular default choice; works for complex, non-linear decision boundaries. |
| 4 | **Sigmoid Kernel** | $K(x_i, x_j) = \tanh(\gamma (x_i \cdot x_j) + r)$ | $\gamma > 0$, $r \in \mathbb{R}$ | Models neural network behavior; not always a valid Mercer kernel. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Fact:** The **RBF (Gaussian) kernel** is the most widely used kernel in practice because it maps data into an **infinite-dimensional feature space**, giving it tremendous flexibility. It has only one tunable parameter $\gamma$, making it computationally efficient.

### 2.5 Mercer's Theorem (Kernel Validity Condition)

Not every function can serve as a kernel. A function $K: \mathbb{R}^d \times \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is a **valid kernel** if and only if it satisfies **Mercer's Condition**:

For any finite set of points $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ and any real coefficients $\{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_N\}$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} c_i c_j K(x_i, x_j) \geq 0$$

In matrix form, the **kernel (Gram) matrix** $\mathbf{K}$ defined by $K_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ must be **positive semi-definite** for the function to be a valid kernel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real engineering applications, non-linear SVMs with RBF kernels are used in **biomedical signal classification (ECG/EEG)**, **fault diagnosis in rotating machinery**, **handwritten digit recognition**, and **remote sensing image classification** — all areas that demand capturing complex, non-linear decision boundaries.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Quick Reference)

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Feature Mapping | $\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \mathcal{F}$ | Maps input to feature space. |
| Kernel Definition | $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i) \cdot \phi(x_j)$ | Inner product in feature space. |
| Mercer's Condition | $\sum_i \sum_j c_i c_j K(x_i, x_j) \geq 0$ | Validity of a kernel function. |
| Dual Objective (Kernelized) | $\max_\alpha \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)$ | Optimization in kernel form. |
| Dual Constraints | $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0, \; 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ | Box and equality constraints. |
| Decision Function | $f(x) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_i \alpha_i y_i K(x, x_i) + b\right)$ | Class prediction using kernel. |
| Linear Kernel | $K(x_i, x_j) = x_i \cdot x_j$ | Reduces to standard SVM. |
| Polynomial Kernel | $K(x_i, x_j) = (\gamma (x_i \cdot x_j) + r)^d$ | Captures feature interactions. |
| RBF Kernel | $K(x_i, x_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert x_i - x_j \Vert^2)$ | Infinite-dimensional mapping. |
| Sigmoid Kernel | $K(x_i, x_j) = \tanh(\gamma (x_i \cdot x_j) + r)$ | Neural-net inspired. |
| Margin Width | $\frac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$ | Distance between supporting hyperplanes. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Primal SVM to Kernelized Dual Form

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU exams. We start from the **soft-margin primal SVM** and transform it step-by-step to its kernelized dual form.

**Step 1: Soft-Margin Primal Formulation**

In the feature space, the primal problem is:

$$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

subject to:

$$y_i(w \cdot \phi(x_i) + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

Here $\xi_i$ are the **slack variables** that permit violations of the margin, and $C > 0$ is the penalty parameter that controls the trade-off.

**Step 2: Construct the Lagrangian**

Introduce Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for the margin constraints and $\mu_i \geq 0$ for the non-negativity of slack variables:

$$\mathcal{L}(w, b, \xi, \alpha, \mu) = \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(w \cdot \phi(x_i) + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu_i \xi_i$$

**Step 3: Apply KKT Conditions — Stationarity**

Taking partial derivatives and setting them to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = w - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \phi(x_i) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i \phi(x_i)$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha_i \leq C \text{ (since } \mu_i \geq 0\text{)}$$

**Step 4: Substitute Back into the Lagrangian**

Substituting $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \phi(x_i)$ back into the Lagrangian eliminates $w$, $b$, and $\xi_i$. After algebraic simplification:

$$\mathcal{L}_D(\alpha) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \left[ \phi(x_i) \cdot \phi(x_j) \right]$$

**Step 5: Apply the Kernel Trick**

Now we replace the inner product $\phi(x_i) \cdot \phi(x_j)$ with the kernel function $K(x_i, x_j)$:

$$\max_{\alpha} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)$$

subject to:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$$

**This is the kernelized dual form of the non-linear SVM.** Notice that the data $x_i$ appears **only inside the kernel function $K$**, never in raw form. This means we never need to compute $\phi(x_i)$ explicitly.

**Step 6: Decision Function in Kernel Form**

The final classifier becomes:

$$f(x) = \text{sign}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i K(x, x_i) + b \right)$$

Only the support vectors (those with $\alpha_i > 0$) contribute to the decision function, keeping computation efficient.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Polynomial Kernel Expansion

**Problem:** Given the polynomial kernel $K(x, y) = (x \cdot y + 1)^2$ for 2D inputs $x = (x_1, x_2)$ and $y = (y_1, y_2)$, derive the explicit feature mapping $\phi$.

**Step 1: Expand the kernel algebraically**

$$K(x, y) = (x_1 y_1 + x_2 y_2 + 1)^2$$

**Step 2: Apply the binomial expansion $(a + b + c)^2 = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + 2ab + 2ac + 2bc$**

$$K(x, y) = (x_1 y_1)^2 + (x_2 y_2)^2 + 1^2 + 2(x_1 y_1)(x_2 y_2) + 2(x_1 y_1)(1) + 2(x_2 y_2)(1)$$

$$K(x, y) = x_1^2 y_1^2 + x_2^2 y_2^2 + 1 + 2 x_1 x_2 y_1 y_2 + 2 x_1 y_1 + 2 x_2 y_2$$

**Step 3: Group terms by their dependence on $x$ and $y$**

$$K(x, y) = (x_1^2)(y_1^2) + (\sqrt{2} x_1 x_2)(\sqrt{2} y_1 y_2) + (x_2^2)(y_2^2) + (\sqrt{2} x_1)(\sqrt{2} y_1) + (\sqrt{2} x_2)(\sqrt{2} y_2) + (1)(1)$$

**Step 4: Read off the explicit feature mapping**

$$\phi(x) = (1, \; \sqrt{2} x_1, \; \sqrt{2} x_2, \; x_1^2, \; x_2^2, \; \sqrt{2} x_1 x_2)$$

**Step 5: Verify the inner product**

$$\phi(x) \cdot \phi(y) = 1 + 2 x_1 y_1 + 2 x_2 y_2 + x_1^2 y_1^2 + x_2^2 y_2^2 + 2 x_1 x_2 y_1 y_2 = (x \cdot y + 1)^2 \checkmark$$

**Insight:** The 2D input space has been mapped to a **6-dimensional feature space**, but we never had to compute $\phi(x)$ or $\phi(y)$ during training — only $K(x, y) = (x \cdot y + 1)^2$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison: Linear vs Non-Linear SVM

| Property | Linear SVM | Non-Linear SVM (Kernelized) |
|---|---|---|
| Data Assumption | Linearly separable | Non-linearly separable |
| Mapping $\phi$ | Identity (no mapping) | Non-linear (implicit via kernel) |
| Computational Cost | $O(d)$ per kernel evaluation | $O(d)$ for RBF, $O(d)$ for poly |
| Decision Boundary | Hyperplane in input space | Non-linear surface (curve in input space, hyperplane in feature space) |
| Parameter $C$ | Controls margin softness | Controls margin softness |
| Additional Parameters | None | $\gamma$, $r$, $d$ (depending on kernel) |
| Risk of Overfitting | Lower | Higher (especially for high $\gamma$ or high $d$) |
| Common Use | Text classification, high-dimensional sparse data | Image recognition, bioinformatics, general-purpose |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Pipeline of Non-Linear SVM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Input Data X in R^d] --> B{Linear Separability Check}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Linear SVM Direct]
    B -- No --> D[Apply Non Linear Feature Mapping Phi]
    D --> E[Map to High Dimensional Feature Space F]
    E --> F[Use Kernel Function K to Compute Inner Products Implicitly]
    F --> G[Formulate Kernelized Dual Optimization Problem]
    G --> H[Solve for Lagrange Multipliers Alpha]
    H --> I[Identify Support Vectors with Alpha greater than zero]
    I --> J[Construct Kernelized Decision Function f of x]
    J --> K[Output Predicted Class Label]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Kernel Function Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Kernel Function] --> B{Nature of Data}
    B -- Linearly Separable --> C[Linear Kernel K equals x dot y]
    B -- Non Linear with Feature Interactions --> D[Polynomial Kernel K equals gamma x dot y plus r to power d]
    B -- Complex Non Linear Boundaries --> E[RBF Gaussian Kernel K equals exp minus gamma norm x minus y squared]
    B -- Neural Network Style Mapping --> F[Sigmoid Kernel K equals tanh gamma x dot y plus r]
    C --> G[Validate via Mercer Condition]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -- Valid --> H[Proceed with Kernelized SVM Training]
    G -- Not Valid --> I[Choose a Different Kernel Function]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: From Primal to Kernelized Dual

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Primal Formulation Minimize Half w squared plus C sum xi] --> P2[Construct Lagrangian with alpha and mu]
    P2 --> P3[Apply KKT Stationarity Conditions]
    P3 --> P4[Eliminate w b and xi]
    P4 --> P5[Dual Formulation with phi xi dot phi xj]
    P5 --> P6[Apply Kernel Trick K xi xj equals phi xi dot phi xj]
    P6 --> P7[Kernelized Dual Optimization Problem]
    P7 --> P8[Solve for alpha and b]
    P8 --> P9[Kernelized Decision Function f x equals sign sum alpha y K x xi plus b]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Schematic: Input Space vs Feature Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Input Space R squared - Non Linearly Separable]
        P1[Class Plus Points]
        P2[Class Minus Points]
        P3[Cannot separate with straight line]
    end
    subgraph B[Feature Space R cubed - Linearly Separable]
        Q1[Class Plus Lifted Points]
        Q2[Class Minus Lifted Points]
        Q3[Separable by flat plane]
    end
    A -- Apply Phi Mapping --> B
    Q3 -- Implicitly computed via Kernel K --> R[Decision Function f x]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above represent the **functional flow architecture** of non-linear SVM, including the data transformation pipeline, kernel selection logic, and the dual optimization journey. Since Mermaid cannot render 3D geometric separations, the diagrams capture the algorithmic structure rather than the physical geometry.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define the kernel trick in SVM. Why is it computationally advantageous over explicit feature mapping?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **kernel trick** is a mathematical technique in Support Vector Machines where the inner product $\phi(x_i) \cdot \phi(x_j)$ of two data points in the high-dimensional feature space $\mathcal{F}$ is replaced by a kernel function $K(x_i, x_j)$ computed directly in the original input space.

**Computational Advantage (2 marks):** Explicitly computing the feature mapping $\phi(x)$ can be extremely expensive or even impossible. For instance, the RBF kernel implicitly maps data to an **infinite-dimensional** feature space, where direct computation of $\phi(x)$ is infeasible. The kernel trick allows us to perform the SVM optimization in the feature space **without ever computing $\phi(x)$ explicitly**, by using only the kernel values $K(x_i, x_j)$.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** This makes non-linear SVM tractable even for very high or infinite-dimensional feature spaces, dramatically reducing computational cost.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024] State Mercer's theorem and explain its significance in kernel selection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1.5 marks):** Mercer's theorem states that a symmetric function $K: \mathbb{R}^d \times \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is a valid kernel (i.e., it corresponds to an inner product in some feature space) if and only if for any finite set of points $\{x_1, \ldots, x_N\}$ and any real coefficients $\{c_1, \ldots, c_N\}$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} c_i c_j K(x_i, x_j) \geq 0$$

Equivalently, the **kernel (Gram) matrix** $\mathbf{K}$ with entries $K_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ must be **positive semi-definite**.

**Significance (1.5 marks):** Mercer's condition serves as the **validity check** for any candidate kernel function. Only functions satisfying this condition can be used in kernelized SVM, ensuring the existence of a feature space $\mathcal{F}$ and a mapping $\phi$ such that $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i) \cdot \phi(x_j)$. Without this guarantee, the dual optimization problem may not be convex and the SVM may not converge to a meaningful solution.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the dual optimization problem of the soft-margin SVM in its kernelized form. Clearly state the role of the kernel function in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the polynomial kernel $K(x, y) = (x \cdot y + 1)^2$ with 2D input vectors $x = (x_1, x_2)$ and $y = (y_1, y_2)$, determine the explicit feature mapping $\phi(x)$ and verify that $\phi(x) \cdot \phi(y) = K(x, y)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Soft-Margin Primal (1 mark)**

The primal form in feature space is:

$$\min_{w, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

subject to $y_i(w \cdot \phi(x_i) + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ and $\xi_i \geq 0$ for all $i$.

**Step 2: Lagrangian Construction (1 mark)**

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\|w\|^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i - \sum_i \alpha_i [y_i(w \cdot \phi(x_i) + b) - 1 + \xi_i] - \sum_i \mu_i \xi_i$$

**Step 3: KKT Stationarity (2 marks)**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 0 \Rightarrow w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \phi(x_i)$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0 \Rightarrow \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = 0 \Rightarrow 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$$

**Step 4: Substitution and Kernel Trick (2 marks)**

Substituting $w$ back into the Lagrangian and eliminating $w$, $b$, $\xi_i$:

$$\max_\alpha \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \phi(x_i) \cdot \phi(x_j)$$

Replacing $\phi(x_i) \cdot \phi(x_j)$ with $K(x_i, x_j)$:

$$\max_\alpha \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)$$

subject to $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ and $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.

**Step 5: Role of Kernel (1 mark)**

The kernel function $K(x_i, x_j)$ allows the optimization to be performed entirely in the original input space using pairwise similarities, eliminating the need to compute the explicit high-dimensional mapping $\phi(x_i)$.

---

**(b) Feature Mapping Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Expand the kernel (2 marks)**

$$K(x, y) = (x_1 y_1 + x_2 y_2 + 1)^2$$

$$= x_1^2 y_1^2 + x_2^2 y_2^2 + 1 + 2 x_1 x_2 y_1 y_2 + 2 x_1 y_1 + 2 x_2 y_2$$

**Step 2: Factor as inner product (2 marks)**

$$= (1)(1) + (\sqrt{2} x_1)(\sqrt{2} y_1) + (\sqrt{2} x_2)(\sqrt{2} y_2) + (x_1^2)(y_1^2) + (x_2^2)(y_2^2) + (\sqrt{2} x_1 x_2)(\sqrt{2} y_1 y_2)$$

**Step 3: Read off the mapping (1 mark)**

$$\phi(x) = (1, \sqrt{2} x_1, \sqrt{2} x_2, x_1^2, x_2^2, \sqrt{2} x_1 x_2)$$

**Step 4: Verification (2 marks)**

$$\phi(x) \cdot \phi(y) = 1 + 2 x_1 y_1 + 2 x_2 y_2 + x_1^2 y_1^2 + x_2^2 y_2^2 + 2 x_1 x_2 y_1 y_2 = (x \cdot y + 1)^2 = K(x, y) \checkmark$$

The mapping is to a 6-dimensional feature space.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the kernel function in non-linear SVM. List and mathematically define any four commonly used kernel functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain Mercer's theorem with its mathematical condition. Given three data points $x_1 = 1$, $x_2 = 2$, $x_3 = 3$, verify whether $K(x, y) = xy + 1$ is a valid kernel by checking the positive semi-definiteness of the Gram matrix. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Kernel Function Concept and Types (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A **kernel function** $K(x, y)$ in non-linear SVM is a function that computes the inner product of two data points in a (possibly high-dimensional) feature space $\mathcal{F}$ without explicitly mapping the points to that space. Formally, $K(x, y) = \phi(x) \cdot \phi(y)$ where $\phi$ is the non-linear feature mapping.

**Four Standard Kernels (5 marks = 1.25 each):**

1. **Linear Kernel:** $K(x, y) = x \cdot y$ — Used when data is linearly separable.

2. **Polynomial Kernel:** $K(x, y) = (\gamma (x \cdot y) + r)^d$ — Captures interactions of order $d$ between features.

3. **RBF / Gaussian Kernel:** $K(x, y) = \exp(-\gamma \|x - y\|^2)$ — Maps to infinite-dimensional space; most popular.

4. **Sigmoid Kernel:** $K(x, y) = \tanh(\gamma (x \cdot y) + r)$ — Mimics neural network activation.

---

**(b) Mercer's Theorem and Gram Matrix Verification (7 marks):**

**Statement (2 marks):** Mercer's theorem states that a symmetric function $K(x, y)$ is a valid kernel if and only if the Gram matrix $\mathbf{K}$ with entries $K_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ is **positive semi-definite** for any finite set of points. Equivalently:

$$\sum_i \sum_j c_i c_j K(x_i, x_j) \geq 0 \quad \text{for all real } \{c_i\}$$

**Step 1: Construct the Gram matrix (2 marks)**

For $x_1 = 1$, $x_2 = 2$, $x_3 = 3$ and $K(x, y) = xy + 1$:

$$K_{11} = 1 \cdot 1 + 1 = 2, \quad K_{12} = 1 \cdot 2 + 1 = 3, \quad K_{13} = 1 \cdot 3 + 1 = 4$$

$$K_{22} = 2 \cdot 2 + 1 = 5, \quad K_{23} = 2 \cdot 3 + 1 = 7, \quad K_{33} = 3 \cdot 3 + 1 = 10$$

$$\mathbf{K} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 4 & 7 & 10 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Check positive semi-definiteness via eigenvalues (2 marks)**

The characteristic polynomial of $\mathbf{K}$:

$$\det(\mathbf{K} - \lambda I) = 0$$

Computing: $\text{tr}(\mathbf{K}) = 17$, determinant calculation yields eigenvalues $\lambda_1 = 0$, $\lambda_2 = 0.34$, $\lambda_3 = 16.66$ (all $\geq 0$).

**Step 3: Conclusion (1 mark)**

Since all eigenvalues are non-negative, $\mathbf{K}$ is **positive semi-definite**, and therefore $K(x, y) = xy + 1$ is a **valid kernel** by Mercer's theorem. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1 (Loses 2 marks):** Students often write the dual formulation but forget to **substitute the kernel function** explicitly, leaving $\phi(x_i) \cdot \phi(x_j)$ in the final answer. Always replace it with $K(x_i, x_j)$ to get full credit.
> - **Common Mistake #2 (Loses 2 marks):** When asked to find the feature mapping for a polynomial kernel, students often forget the **normalization constants** (e.g., $\sqrt{2}$ factors). The mapping must be such that $\phi(x) \cdot \phi(y)$ exactly equals $K(x, y)$, not just the squared terms.
> - **Common Mistake #3 (Loses 1-2 marks):** Confusing the **primal and dual** formulations. The kernel trick is applied in the **dual**, not the primal. Writing the kernelized primal will cost you marks.
> - **Common Mistake #4 (Loses 1 mark):** Forgetting the **box constraint** $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ in the dual. Both the equality and box constraints must be stated.
> - **Common Mistake #5 (Loses 1 mark):** In Mercer's verification problems, students often compute the Gram matrix but **fail to check its positive semi-definiteness** via eigenvalues or leading principal minors.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Non-Linear SVM** is used when data is **not linearly separable** in the input space.
- The **feature mapping** $\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \mathcal{F}$ transforms data into a high-dimensional feature space where a linear hyperplane can separate it.
- The **kernel trick** replaces $\phi(x_i) \cdot \phi(x_j)$ with $K(x_i, x_j)$, avoiding explicit computation of $\phi$.
- **Four standard kernels:**
  - Linear: $K(x, y) = x \cdot y$
  - Polynomial: $K(x, y) = (\gamma (x \cdot y) + r)^d$
  - RBF (Gaussian): $K(x, y) = \exp(-\gamma \|x - y\|^2)$
  - Sigmoid: $K(x, y) = \tanh(\gamma (x \cdot y) + r)$
- **RBF kernel** maps to **infinite-dimensional** space and is the most commonly used.
- **Mercer's theorem**: A function is a valid kernel iff its Gram matrix is **positive semi-definite**.
- **Kernelized dual form**: $\max_\alpha \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_i \sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)$ subject to $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ and $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.
- **Decision function**: $f(x) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_i \alpha_i y_i K(x, x_i) + b\right)$.
- **Slack penalty $C$**: Larger $C$ → harder margin, less misclassification tolerance. Smaller $C$ → softer margin.
- **Kernel parameter $\gamma$**: Controls the influence radius — large $\gamma$ gives complex, wiggly boundaries (overfitting risk); small $\gamma$ gives smoother boundaries.
- **Support vectors** are the training points with $\alpha_i > 0$ — they alone define the decision boundary.
- **Polynomial kernel feature mapping derivation** is a frequently asked 7-mark question — practice the algebraic expansion with normalization constants.
- **Always check Mercer's condition** (positive semi-definiteness of Gram matrix) to verify a candidate kernel.
- The **kernel trick** is applied in the **dual formulation**, never in the primal.
- The **decision boundary** in input space is **non-linear**, but it is a **linear hyperplane** in feature space $\mathcal{F}$.
- Non-linear SVMs are widely used in **biomedical imaging, fault diagnosis, OCR, and bioinformatics** applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kernels for Learning Non-Linear Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT414 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Kernels for Learning Non-Linear Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Kernels for Learning Non-Linear Functions

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

In the context of supervised learning, many real-world datasets are **not linearly separable** in their original input space. To handle such data, we can map the input vectors $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ into a higher-dimensional **feature space** $\mathcal{F}$ via a non-linear mapping function $\phi: \mathbb{R}^{d} \rightarrow \mathcal{F}$, where a linear classifier can be constructed.

A **kernel function** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is defined as the inner product of the transformed feature vectors:

$$K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \rangle = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^{\top} \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$$

The **Kernel Trick** is the mathematical technique of replacing the explicit dot product in feature space with a kernel function evaluated in the original input space, thereby avoiding the costly (and often intractable) computation of $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ explicitly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A function $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})$ is a valid kernel if and only if it satisfies **Mercer's Theorem** — i.e., the kernel matrix (Gram matrix) $K_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is **symmetric** and **positive semi-definite** for all finite input sets.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Shadow on the Wall"**
>
> Imagine you have a crumpled ball of paper (non-linear data) sitting on a table. From above, its shadow on the floor looks like a messy, inseparable blob. But if you shine a light from a clever angle (the mapping $\phi$), the shadow stretches out into a clean, linearly separable ellipse on the wall.
>
> A **kernel** is the smart trick of computing how two points relate **in that high-dimensional wall space**, without ever actually computing their shadows. It's like knowing how similar two people are by their life stories (input space) without simulating their entire lives (feature space).

### Why Kernels? The Core Motivation

1. **Dimensionality flexibility:** Implicitly operate in infinite-dimensional spaces (e.g., RBF kernel).
2. **Computational efficiency:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})$ is computed in $\mathcal{O}(d)$ instead of $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathcal{F} \vert)$ where $\vert \mathcal{F} \vert$ could be enormous.
3. **Modularity:** Any algorithm expressed solely in terms of dot products can be "kernelized" — including SVMs, PCA, Ridge Regression, and Perceptrons.
4. **No explicit feature engineering:** The non-linearity is learned automatically through the kernel choice.

---

### Key Terminology Table

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Input Space | $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{d}$ | Original space where raw data lives |
| Feature Space | $\mathcal{F}$ | Transformed (often high-dim) space |
| Feature Map | $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ | Non-linear mapping $\mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{F}$ |
| Kernel Function | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Inner product surrogate in feature space |
| Gram Matrix | $\mathbf{K} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | Matrix of pairwise kernel values |
| Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space | RKHS | Function space where $K$ acts as an inner product |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation of Kernels

### Mercer's Theorem (Verbatim Form)

Let $K: \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ be a continuous, symmetric function. Then $K$ is a **positive semi-definite kernel** (valid kernel) if and only if for all square-integrable functions $f$:

$$\int_{\mathcal{X}} \int_{\mathcal{X}} K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) f(\mathbf{x}) f(\mathbf{z}) \, d\mathbf{x} \, d\mathbf{z} \geq 0$$

Equivalently, for any finite dataset $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_n\}$, the Gram matrix $\mathbf{K}$ with entries $K_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ must satisfy:

$$\mathbf{v}^{\top} \mathbf{K} \mathbf{v} \geq 0, \quad \forall \mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Verifying that a custom function is a valid kernel often reduces to checking these two properties — symmetry and positive semi-definiteness of the Gram matrix. This is a guaranteed 7-mark question type.

### Constructing New Kernels from Old

If $K_1$ and $K_2$ are valid kernels, then the following are also valid kernels:

1. **Scaling:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = c \cdot K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})$, for $c > 0$
2. **Sum:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) + K_2(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})$
3. **Product:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) \cdot K_2(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})$
4. **Normalization:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \dfrac{K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})}{\sqrt{K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}) \cdot K_1(\mathbf{z}, \mathbf{z})}}$
5. **Exponentiation:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp(K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}))$
6. **Polynomial closure:** If $p(\cdot)$ is a polynomial with positive coefficients, $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = p(K_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}))$ is a valid kernel.

### Common Kernel Functions

| Kernel | Formula | Feature Space Type | Key Hyperparameter |
|--------|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Linear** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{z}$ | Same as input | None |
| **Polynomial** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (\gamma \mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{z} + c_0)^{p}$ | Finite-dim polynomial | Degree $p$, coef $c_0$, scale $\gamma$ |
| **RBF / Gaussian** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \Vert^{2}\right)$ | **Infinite-dimensional** | Bandwidth $\gamma > 0$ |
| **Laplacian** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \Vert_{1}\right)$ | Infinite-dim | Bandwidth $\gamma$ |
| **Sigmoid (Tanh)** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \tanh(\gamma \mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{z} + c_0)$ | Not always a valid kernel! | Scale $\gamma$, offset $c_0$ |
| **Cosine** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \dfrac{\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{z}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert}$ | Normalized similarity | None |

> [!WARNING]
> The **sigmoid kernel** is **not always a valid Mercer kernel** because $K_{ij}$ may not be positive semi-definite. KTU examiners love this trick question.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---------|---------|-------|
| Kernel Definition | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^{\top} \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ | Avoid explicit $\phi$ |
| Gram Matrix Entry | $K_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Symmetric matrix |
| Mercer PSD Condition | $\mathbf{v}^{\top} \mathbf{K} \mathbf{v} \geq 0$ | For all $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| RBF Kernel | $K = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \Vert^{2})$ | Most widely used |
| Polynomial Kernel | $K = (\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{z} + c)^{p}$ | Encodes interactions of order $p$ |
| Dual SVM Decision | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b$ | Only support vectors have $\alpha_i > 0$ |
| Kernel Ridge Regression | $\hat{\alpha} = (\mathbf{K} + \lambda \mathbf{I})^{-1} \mathbf{y}$ | Solved in dual |
| Kernel PCA Eigenvalue | $\mathbf{K} \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$ | Centering required |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bioinformatics:** String kernels for protein/DNA sequence classification (SVM-based).
- **Computer Vision:** RBF-kernel SVMs for image classification and face detection.
- **Natural Language Processing:** Tree kernels and graph kernels for parse-tree similarity.
- **Time-Series Analysis:** Dynamic Time Warping kernels for ECG, speech, sensor data.
- **Anomaly Detection:** One-class SVM with RBF kernel for network intrusion detection.
- **Geostatistics:** Gaussian processes (a kernel method) for spatial prediction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Polynomial Kernel as Inner Product of Feature Maps

### Problem Setup
Show that the polynomial kernel $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{z} + 1)^{2}$ corresponds to an explicit feature map $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ in some space $\mathcal{F}$, and identify that space.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

For $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^{\top}$ and $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, z_2)^{\top}$, we expand the kernel:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (x_1 z_1 + x_2 z_2 + 1)^{2}$$

Applying the binomial expansion $(a + b + c)^{2} = a^{2} + b^{2} + c^{2} + 2ab + 2ac + 2bc$:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (x_1 z_1)^{2} + (x_2 z_2)^{2} + 1^{2} + 2(x_1 z_1)(x_2 z_2) + 2(x_1 z_1)(1) + 2(x_2 z_2)(1)$$

Grouping into inner-product form:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \begin{aligned}[t] & (x_1^{2})(z_1^{2}) + (x_2^{2})(z_2^{2}) + (1)(1) \\ & + (\sqrt{2}\, x_1 x_2)(\sqrt{2}\, z_1 z_2) \\ & + (\sqrt{2}\, x_1)(\sqrt{2}\, z_1) + (\sqrt{2}\, x_2)(\sqrt{2}\, z_2) \end{aligned}$$

Recognizing this as $\phi(\mathbf{x})^{\top} \phi(\mathbf{z})$, we extract the feature map:

$$\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \left( x_1^{2}, \; x_2^{2}, \; 1, \; \sqrt{2}\, x_1 x_2, \; \sqrt{2}\, x_1, \; \sqrt{2}\, x_2 \right)^{\top}$$

Therefore $\phi: \mathbb{R}^{2} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{6}$, **a 2-D input lifted to 6 dimensions without ever computing the squared terms explicitly** in the kernel formulation.

### Key Insight
> [!NOTE]
> For degree-$p$ polynomial kernel on $d$-dimensional input, the feature space dimension grows as $\binom{d + p}{p}$ — exponentially. The kernel trick computes the **same dot product in $\mathcal{O}(d)$** instead of $\mathcal{O}(\binom{d+p}{p})$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: RBF Kernel is Infinite-Dimensional

### Goal
Show that the Gaussian (RBF) kernel $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \Vert^{2}\right)$ corresponds to an **infinite-dimensional** feature map.

### Derivation

Setting $\gamma = \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}$ for clarity, expand the squared norm:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp\!\left( -\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right)$$

$$\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \Vert^{2} = \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^{2} - 2\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{z} + \Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert^{2}$$

Substituting:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp\!\left( -\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right) \cdot \exp\!\left( \frac{\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{z}}{\sigma^{2}} \right) \cdot \exp\!\left( -\frac{\Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right)$$

Using the Taylor series expansion $\exp(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{t^{k}}{k!}$ on the middle term with $t = \frac{\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{z}}{\sigma^{2}}$:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp\!\left( -\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right) \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{k! \sigma^{2k}} (\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{z})^{k} \right] \exp\!\left( -\frac{\Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right)$$

By the multinomial theorem, $(\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{z})^{k}$ expands into monomials of degree $k$ in both $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{z}$. This infinite sum defines:

$$\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{k!}\,\sigma^{k}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right) \cdot \text{(monomial basis of all degrees)}$$

Since the sum runs over **all non-negative integers $k$**, $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ lies in an **infinite-dimensional Hilbert space**. Yet $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})$ is computed in closed form using only $\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert$ and $\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{z}$ — the hallmark of the kernel trick.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The RBF kernel is a universal approximator — by the **Micchelli Theorem**, as long as the points are distinct, the RBF kernel interpolates any training set exactly.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Verifying the Linear Kernel is a Valid Mercer Kernel

We need to show that $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{z}$ is positive semi-definite.

For any $\mathbf{v} = (v_1, \dots, v_n)^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ and dataset $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_n\}$:

$$\mathbf{v}^{\top} \mathbf{K} \mathbf{v} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} v_i v_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$$

$$= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} v_i v_j \mathbf{x}_i^{\top} \mathbf{x}_j$$

$$= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} v_i v_j \sum_{d=1}^{D} x_{id} x_{jd}$$

$$= \sum_{d=1}^{D} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} v_i v_j x_{id} x_{jd}$$

$$= \sum_{d=1}^{D} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i x_{id} \right) \left( \sum_{j=1}^{n} v_j x_{jd} \right)$$

$$= \sum_{d=1}^{D} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i x_{id} \right)^{2} \geq 0$$

Hence $\mathbf{K}$ is positive semi-definite, and the linear kernel is **valid**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 The Kernelized Dual SVM Decision Function

The primal SVM optimization (hard margin) is:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \; \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^{2} \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \; \forall i$$

By Lagrangian duality, the equivalent **dual problem** depends only on dot products $\mathbf{x}_i^{\top} \mathbf{x}_j$:

$$\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^{\top} \mathbf{x}_j$$

$$\text{subject to} \quad \alpha_i \geq 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i = 0$$

**Kernelization** replaces every dot product with the kernel:

$$\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$$

The resulting decision function for a new test point $\mathbf{x}$ is:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\!\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b \right)$$

By **Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) complementary slackness**, only points lying on the margin — the **support vectors** — have $\alpha_i > 0$. The decision depends only on these support vectors.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — Kernel Properties

| Kernel | Valid Mercer? | Computational Cost | Feature Space Dim | Best For |
|--------|---------------|--------------------|-------------------|----------|
| Linear | Always | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ | $d$ | High-dim sparse data (text) |
| Polynomial ($p$) | Always | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ | $\binom{d+p}{p}$ | Interaction features |
| RBF (Gaussian) | Always | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ | $\infty$ | General-purpose non-linear |
| Sigmoid | **Sometimes** | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ | Depends on params | Neural-net-like behavior |
| Laplacian | Always | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ | $\infty$ | Histogram, sparse features |

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Kernel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Choose a Kernel] --> q1{Is data linearly separable}
    q1 -->|Yes| linear[Use Linear Kernel]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Need bounded feature space}
    q2 -->|Yes, low interactions| poly[Use Polynomial Kernel degree 2 or 3]
    q2 -->|No, complex non-linearity| rbf[Use RBF Gaussian Kernel]
    rbf --> tune[Tune gamma and C via cross-validation]
    poly --> tune
    linear --> done[Train Kernelized Model]
    tune --> done
    done --> eval[Evaluate via K-fold CV]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the RBF is the "default" kernel in practice:** It corresponds to an infinite-dimensional feature space, is a universal approximator (can separate any data given enough bandwidth), and has a single hyperparameter $\gamma$ to tune — making it the most flexible default choice when no domain knowledge suggests otherwise.
