<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the kernel trick. Why is it computationally advantageous compared to explicit feature mapping?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **kernel trick** is a method in machine learning where the dot product between two data points in a high-dimensional feature space — represented as $\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^{\top} \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ — is replaced by a kernel function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ computed directly in the original input space.

**Computational advantage:** For an explicit feature map $\phi: \mathbb{R}^{d} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{D}$ with $D \gg d$, computing $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ takes $\mathcal{O}(D)$ time and storing the transformed data takes $\mathcal{O}(D)$ space. The kernel $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is computed in $\mathcal{O}(d)$ time. For the RBF kernel, the feature space is **infinite-dimensional** and explicit mapping is impossible — only the kernel trick makes such models tractable. **(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the computational argument with $\mathcal{O}(d)$ vs $\mathcal{O}(D)$ justification.)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State Mercer's theorem. What are the two conditions a function $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})$ must satisfy to be a valid kernel?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Mercer's Theorem:** A continuous symmetric function $K: \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is a valid (positive semi-definite) kernel if and only if for every finite set of input points $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \dots, \mathbf{x}_n\}$, the Gram matrix $\mathbf{K}$ with $K_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is **positive semi-definite**, i.e., $\mathbf{v}^{\top} \mathbf{K} \mathbf{v} \geq 0$ for all $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$.

The two conditions are:
1. **Symmetry:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = K(\mathbf{z}, \mathbf{x})$ for all $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z} \in \mathcal{X}$. **(1 mark)**
2. **Positive Semi-Definiteness:** The Gram matrix $\mathbf{K}$ must have all non-negative eigenvalues, equivalently $\mathbf{v}^{\top} \mathbf{K} \mathbf{v} \geq 0$ for all $\mathbf{v}$. **(2 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **concept of kernel functions** in machine learning. Derive the feature map $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ for the polynomial kernel $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{z} + 1)^{2}$ when $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^{\top}$. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** With the help of a **block diagram**, describe the **kernelized Support Vector Machine (SVM)** algorithm. Show the role of support vectors in the decision function and explain why only support vectors contribute to prediction. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Definition (2 marks):** A kernel function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ computes the inner product of two data points in some (possibly high or infinite-dimensional) feature space $\mathcal{F}$:

$$K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^{\top} \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$$

The kernel allows learning algorithms that depend only on dot products to operate non-linearly without ever explicitly computing the transformation $\phi(\mathbf{x})$.

**Derivation (5 marks):** Given $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^{\top}$ and $\mathbf{z} = (z_1, z_2)^{\top}$:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = (x_1 z_1 + x_2 z_2 + 1)^{2}$$

Expanding via $(a + b + c)^{2} = a^{2} + b^{2} + c^{2} + 2ab + 2ac + 2bc$:

$$K = (x_1 z_1)^{2} + (x_2 z_2)^{2} + 1 + 2 x_1 z_1 x_2 z_2 + 2 x_1 z_1 + 2 x_2 z_2$$

Rearranging into inner-product form:

$$K = \begin{aligned}[t] & (x_1^{2})(z_1^{2}) + (x_2^{2})(z_2^{2}) + (1)(1) \\ & + (\sqrt{2} x_1 x_2)(\sqrt{2} z_1 z_2) + (\sqrt{2} x_1)(\sqrt{2} z_1) + (\sqrt{2} x_2)(\sqrt{2} z_2) \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the explicit feature map is:

$$\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \left( x_1^{2}, \; x_2^{2}, \; 1, \; \sqrt{2}\, x_1 x_2, \; \sqrt{2}\, x_1, \; \sqrt{2}\, x_2 \right)^{\top}$$

The mapping lifts 2-D input to 6-D feature space, but the kernel computes the dot product in $\mathcal{O}(d) = \mathcal{O}(2)$ instead of $\mathcal{O}(6)$.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** *(Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2 — Kernelized SVM Pipeline)*

The kernelized SVM pipeline consists of: Training data → Kernel matrix computation → Gram matrix → Mercer PSD validation → Quadratic dual optimization → Support vector coefficients $\alpha_i$ → Decision function $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b$ → Predicted class label.

**Decision function with support vectors (3 marks):** The optimization yields a sparse solution where $\alpha_i > 0$ only for support vectors — points lying on the margin or violating it. By KKT complementary slackness, $\alpha_i (1 - y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{x}_i + b)) = 0$, so $\alpha_i > 0$ only if $y_i(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{x}_i + b) = 1$. For all other points, $\alpha_i = 0$.

**Why only support vectors contribute (1 mark):** Since $\alpha_i = 0$ for non-support vectors, their kernel terms $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x})$ vanish in the decision function. The prediction depends only on the few support vectors, making inference efficient and providing sparsity — a key advantage of kernelized SVMs.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Discuss the **Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel** in detail. Show mathematically that it corresponds to an infinite-dimensional feature space. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Compare the **RBF, Polynomial, and Sigmoid kernels** in terms of validity (Mercer condition), feature space dimensionality, computational cost, and typical applications. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Definition and properties (2 marks):** The RBF (Gaussian) kernel is defined as:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp\!\left( -\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \Vert^{2} \right), \quad \gamma > 0$$

It is **translation invariant** (depends only on $\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z}$), **positive semi-definite** (verified by Bochner's theorem for $\gamma > 0$), and a **universal approximator** — it can separate any data set given suitable $\gamma$.

**Infinite-dimensional feature space (5 marks):** Setting $\gamma = \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}$ and expanding the squared norm:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \exp\!\left( -\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right) \cdot \exp\!\left( \frac{\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{z}}{\sigma^{2}} \right) \cdot \exp\!\left( -\frac{\Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right)$$

Applying the Taylor expansion to the middle exponential:

$$\exp\!\left( \frac{\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{z}}{\sigma^{2}} \right) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{z})^{k}}{k! \, \sigma^{2k}}$$

Using the multinomial theorem, each $(\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{z})^{k}$ expands into a sum of monomials of degree $k$:

$$(\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{z})^{k} = \sum_{\vert \alpha \vert = k} \binom{k}{\alpha} \mathbf{x}^{\alpha} \mathbf{z}^{\alpha}$$

where the sum is over all multi-indices $\alpha$ with $\vert \alpha \vert = k$. Substituting:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \sum_{\vert \alpha \vert = k} \left[ \frac{\binom{k}{\alpha}}{k! \, \sigma^{2k}} \exp\!\left( -\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right) \mathbf{x}^{\alpha} \right] \left[ \exp\!\left( -\frac{\Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}} \right) \mathbf{z}^{\alpha} \right]$$

This represents an inner product $\phi(\mathbf{x})^{\top} \phi(\mathbf{z})$ where the feature map has one component **per monomial of every non-negative degree** — an **infinite-dimensional** $\phi(\mathbf{x})$.

> The kernel $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z})$ is computed in $\mathcal{O}(d)$ using only $\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \Vert$, while the explicit $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ lives in infinite dimensions and is incomputable directly — a triumph of the kernel trick.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Property | RBF | Polynomial | Sigmoid |
|----------|-----|------------|---------|
| Mercer validity | **Always valid** | **Always valid** | **Not always** |
| Feature space dim | **Infinite** | $\binom{d+p}{p}$ finite | Depends on params |
| Computation cost | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ |
| Hyperparameters | $\gamma$ | $p, c_0, \gamma$ | $\gamma, c_0$ |
| Universal approximator | Yes | No | No |
| Risk of overfit | High if $\gamma$ too large | Moderate | Moderate |

**Discussion (2 marks):**
- **RBF** is the **default choice** for non-linear SVMs due to its universal approximation property and single tuning knob. Works well for medium-sized datasets with no prior knowledge of feature structure.
- **Polynomial kernel** is appropriate when the data has known polynomial interactions of degree $p$. Cheaper for low $p$ but feature space dimension grows combinatorially.
- **Sigmoid kernel** mimics a two-layer neural network but **fails Mercer's condition** for some parameter settings (e.g., large $\gamma$), so it is not strictly a valid kernel. Despite this, it is sometimes used heuristically.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Mercer's condition:** Students often write $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z}) = \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert - \Vert \mathbf{z} \Vert$ as a "kernel" — it is **not symmetric** in general and **not PSD**. Always validate the two conditions.
> 2. **Confusing $\gamma$ definitions:** In RBF, some texts use $\gamma = \frac{1}{2\sigma^{2}}$ (LIB-SVM convention) and others use $\gamma = \sigma^{2}$ (statistics convention). **Read the question carefully** and state your definition explicitly.
> 3. **Assuming all kernels are valid:** The sigmoid kernel is a frequent trick question. Mention its non-PSD behavior in long-answer solutions to score the full 7 marks.
> 4. **Not expanding the polynomial kernel fully:** If the question says "show the feature map", you must **expand and rearrange term by term** — partial credit is given for each correct grouping.
> 5. **Mixing primal and dual SVM:** The kernel trick applies in the **dual** form because only the dual depends on dot products. Writing a kernelized primal SVM is a conceptual error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Kernel definition:** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^{\top} \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ — inner product in feature space.
- **Kernel trick:** Replace every dot product in the algorithm with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ — never compute $\phi(\mathbf{x})$ explicitly.
- **Mercer's theorem** (the gatekeeper): $K$ is valid **iff** the Gram matrix is symmetric and positive semi-definite.
- **RBF kernel** is the workhorse: $K = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{z} \Vert^{2})$, infinite-dimensional, universal approximator.
- **Polynomial kernel** $K = (\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{z} + c)^{p}$ has a finite-dim but exponentially-growing feature space.
- **Sigmoid kernel** is **not always a valid Mercer kernel** — a guaranteed KTU trick question.
- **Kernel closure rules:** sum, product, scaling, exponentiation of valid kernels yield valid kernels.
- **Kernelized SVM** decision: $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b$, sparse in $\alpha_i$.
- **Support vectors** are the only points with $\alpha_i > 0$ (KKT complementary slackness).
- **Any algorithm expressed via dot products is "kernelizable"** — SVM, PCA, Ridge Regression, Perceptron, LDA, k-means.
- **Computational cost** of evaluating $K$ is $\mathcal{O}(d)$, regardless of feature space dimensionality — the central payoff of the kernel trick.
- **Mercer's condition check** steps for any $K$: (1) verify symmetry, (2) construct Gram matrix $\mathbf{K}$, (3) verify all eigenvalues $\geq 0$, or (4) verify $\mathbf{v}^{\top}\mathbf{K}\mathbf{v} \geq 0$ for arbitrary $\mathbf{v}$.
- **Gram matrix** is symmetric ($K_{ij} = K_{ji}$), of size $n \times n$ for $n$ data points, and must be PSD for $K$ to be a valid kernel.
- **Gaussian kernel = RBF kernel** — the terms are interchangeable; mention this in exams to show breadth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polynomial Kernel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT414 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Polynomial Kernel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Polynomial Kernel** is a *kernel function* defined on two input vectors $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ as:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \left(\gamma \, \mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} + c \right)^{d}$$

where:
- $d \in \mathbb{N}$ is the **degree** of the polynomial (a positive integer, typically $d \geq 1$),
- $c \geq 0$ is a constant that controls the influence of higher-order versus lower-order terms (often called the *bias* or *trade-off*),
- $\gamma > 0$ is a scaling coefficient applied to the inner product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Kernel Trick Context):**
> The polynomial kernel is a member of the family of *Mercer kernels*. It implicitly maps input data into a higher-dimensional feature space $\mathcal{F}$ (of dimension $\binom{n+d}{d}$) **without ever computing the coordinates** in that space. The mapping is performed via the kernel function $K$, allowing linear learning algorithms (like SVMs) to learn *non-linear* decision boundaries.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Curved Ruler" Metaphor

Imagine you are trying to separate red and blue marbles scattered on a **flat table** (the input space). A ruler (a straight line) cannot always separate them — the marbles might be arranged in a circular or curved pattern. The trick used by the polynomial kernel is to **physically lift the marbles into 3D space** (the feature space) by adding a synthetic height coordinate, e.g., $z = x_1^2 + x_2^2$. Once lifted, the red and blue marbles become separable by a *flat sheet* (a hyperplane).

The polynomial kernel does exactly this — but instead of physically lifting the points, it computes the dot product of their lifted representations **algebraically** using only the original 2D coordinates and a single multiplication, addition, and power operation. This is the essence of the **Kernel Trick**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Kernel Trick Matters:**
> Explicitly computing coordinates in the lifted space of degree $d = 10$ in $\mathbb{R}^{100}$ would involve $\binom{110}{10} \approx 2.5 \times 10^{13}$ features per point — utterly infeasible. The polynomial kernel computes the same inner product in $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time.

### 1.3 Special Cases (Common Variants)

| Variant | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Homogeneous** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y})^{d}$ | Pure polynomial interactions, no constant offset |
| **Standard / Inhomogeneous** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} + 1)^{d}$ | Most commonly used in literature and `sklearn` defaults |
| **Generalized** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\gamma \, \mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} + c)^{d}$ | Full parametric flexibility (used in `sklearn.svm.SVC`) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism

The polynomial kernel operates in the following structured flow:

- **Step 1 — Input Reception:** Two feature vectors $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}$ are received by the kernel function from the learning algorithm (typically the SVM dual formulation).
- **Step 2 — Inner Product Computation:** The standard Euclidean inner product $\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i$ is calculated. This is a *linear*, scalar measure of similarity.
- **Step 3 — Affine Adjustment:** The constant $c$ is added: $\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} + c$. The term $c$ ensures that *all monomial degrees from 0 up to d* are represented in the implicit feature map.
- **Step 4 — Non-Linear Exponentiation:** The result is raised to power $d$, which **multiplies** the similarity score. Large inner products get amplified, small ones get suppressed, producing non-linear decision surfaces.
- **Step 5 — Output to Algorithm:** The scalar $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ is returned to the SVM (or any kernel method) as the "similarity score" in the implicit feature space $\mathcal{F}$.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Works — The "How" and "Why"

- **Why a power operation?** A power of a sum of products expands (via the Binomial/Multinomial Theorem) into *all possible monomial interactions* of degree $\leq d$ between the input features. Each monomial corresponds to one axis in the implicit feature space $\mathcal{F}$.
- **Why the constant $c$?** Without $c$, the kernel would be *homogeneous* of degree $d$, meaning it would only include monomials of *exact* degree $d$. Adding $c$ enables lower-order terms (e.g., degree $d-1$, $d-2$, ..., $0$), which is essential for capturing both fine and coarse patterns.
- **Why a valid kernel?** The polynomial kernel is provably a **Mercer kernel** — i.e., the associated Gram matrix $K_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is always **positive semi-definite** for all valid inputs, satisfying the conditions of Mercer's Theorem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dimension of Implicit Feature Space:**
> For input dimension $n$ and polynomial degree $d$, the feature space dimension is:
> $$\dim(\mathcal{F}) = \binom{n + d}{d} = \frac{(n + d)!}{n! \, d!}$$
> This grows **combinatorially**, which is precisely why the *implicit* computation via the kernel is essential.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **General Polynomial Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\gamma \, \mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} + c)^{d}$ | $d \in \mathbb{N}$, $c \geq 0$, $\gamma > 0$ |
| 2 | **Homogeneous Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y})^{d}$ | Special case with $\gamma = 1$, $c = 0$ |
| 3 | **Standard Kernel** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} + 1)^{d}$ | sklearn default; $c = 1$ |
| 4 | **Implicit Feature Map (degree 2)** | $\varphi(\mathbf{x})$ contains all monomials of degree $\leq 2$ | $\dim(\mathcal{F}) = \binom{n+2}{2}$ |
| 5 | **Feature Space Dimension** | $\dim(\mathcal{F}) = \binom{n+d}{d}$ | Combinatorial growth |
| 6 | **Gram Matrix Entry** | $K_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Symmetric, PSD |
| 7 | **Kernel Identity (Symmetry)** | $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = K(\mathbf{y}, \mathbf{x})$ | Required for all valid kernels |
| 8 | **Mercer Condition** | $\sum_{i,j} c_i c_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) \geq 0$ for all $\{c_i\}$ | Guarantees convex optimization |
| 9 | **SVM Dual Objective (kernel form)** | $\max_{\alpha} \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | Subject to $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$, $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$ |
| 10 | **Decision Function** | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\!\left( \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b \right)$ | $SV$ = support vectors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> The polynomial kernel is widely used in **image classification** (e.g., early hand-gesture and digit-recognition systems), **text categorization** (where interaction terms between word features are meaningful), and **bioinformatics** (gene-expression classification, where interactions of degree 2 or 3 between markers have biological interpretation). However, for very high-dimensional data, the RBF/Gaussian kernel is usually preferred due to numerical stability and bounded output.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Explicit Feature Map for Degree-2 Polynomial Kernel (2D Input)

> **Goal:** Show that $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} + 1)^{2}$ is equivalent to an inner product $\varphi(\mathbf{x})^{\top} \varphi(\mathbf{y})$ in some explicit feature space $\mathcal{F}$, and identify $\varphi(\mathbf{x})$.

Let $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^{\top}$ and $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2)^{\top}$. Both live in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$.

**Step 1 — Write the kernel explicitly:**

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (x_1 y_1 + x_2 y_2 + 1)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Binomial Theorem** $(a + b + c)^2 = a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + 2ab + 2ac + 2bc$ with $a = x_1 y_1$, $b = x_2 y_2$, $c = 1$:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (x_1 y_1)^{2} + (x_2 y_2)^{2} + 1 + 2 x_1 y_1 \cdot x_2 y_2 + 2 x_1 y_1 + 2 x_2 y_2$$

**Step 3 — Reorder the terms to reveal the structure** (group by monomial pairings):

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) &= (x_1^{2})(y_1^{2}) + (x_2^{2})(y_2^{2}) + (\sqrt{2} \, x_1 x_2)(\sqrt{2} \, y_1 y_2) \\
&\quad + (\sqrt{2} \, x_1)(\sqrt{2} \, y_1) + (\sqrt{2} \, x_2)(\sqrt{2} \, y_2) + (1)(1)
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* Each coefficient $2$ has been split into $\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{2}$ so that the expression becomes a sum of products of the form $(\text{function of } \mathbf{x})(\text{function of } \mathbf{y})$.

**Step 4 — Read off the feature map $\varphi(\mathbf{x})$:**

$$\varphi(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{pmatrix} x_1^{2} \\ x_2^{2} \\ \sqrt{2} \, x_1 x_2 \\ \sqrt{2} \, x_1 \\ \sqrt{2} \, x_2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$$

**Step 5 — Verification of the kernel identity** $\varphi(\mathbf{x})^{\top} \varphi(\mathbf{y}) = K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi(\mathbf{x})^{\top} \varphi(\mathbf{y}) &= x_1^{2} y_1^{2} + x_2^{2} y_2^{2} + (\sqrt{2}\, x_1 x_2)(\sqrt{2}\, y_1 y_2) + (\sqrt{2}\, x_1)(\sqrt{2}\, y_1) \\
&\quad + (\sqrt{2}\, x_2)(\sqrt{2}\, y_2) + 1 \cdot 1 \\
&= x_1^{2} y_1^{2} + x_2^{2} y_2^{2} + 2 x_1 x_2 y_1 y_2 + 2 x_1 y_1 + 2 x_2 y_2 + 1 \\
&= (x_1 y_1 + x_2 y_2 + 1)^{2} \\
&= K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The polynomial kernel of degree 2 in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ implicitly lifts the data into a **6-dimensional** feature space $\mathcal{F} = \mathbb{R}^{6}$, computed via only $\mathcal{O}(n)$ arithmetic operations on the original 2D inputs.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — General Pattern for Feature Map Dimension

For an input space $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ and polynomial kernel of degree $d$, the implicit feature space consists of **all monomials** of total degree $\leq d$ in the variables $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$.

**Counting monomials of total degree exactly $k$ in $n$ variables:**

$$N_k = \binom{n + k - 1}{k}$$

This is the well-known "stars and bars" combinatorial result.

**Total feature space dimension (summing over $k = 0, 1, \ldots, d$):**

$$
\dim(\mathcal{F}) = \sum_{k=0}^{d} \binom{n + k - 1}{k} = \binom{n + d}{d}
$$

**Example:** For $n = 2$, $d = 3$:

$$\dim(\mathcal{F}) = \binom{2 + 3}{3} = \binom{5}{3} = 10$$

So a degree-3 polynomial kernel in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ operates in a 10-dimensional implicit feature space.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Cubic Polynomial Kernel Feature Map (2D Input)

> **Goal:** Compute the explicit feature map for $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} + 1)^{3}$ with $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{2}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Multinomial Theorem** $(a + b + c)^3$ where $a = x_1 y_1$, $b = x_2 y_2$, $c = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(a + b + c)^{3} &= a^{3} + b^{3} + c^{3} \\
&\quad + 3 a^{2} b + 3 a^{2} c + 3 a b^{2} + 3 b^{2} c + 3 a c^{2} + 3 b c^{2} \\
&\quad + 6 a b c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $a, b, c$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) &= (x_1 y_1)^{3} + (x_2 y_2)^{3} + 1 \\
&\quad + 3 (x_1 y_1)^{2} (x_2 y_2) + 3 (x_1 y_1)^{2} + 3 (x_1 y_1)(x_2 y_2)^{2} + 3 (x_2 y_2)^{2} \\
&\quad + 3 (x_1 y_1) + 3 (x_2 y_2) + 6 (x_1 y_1)(x_2 y_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express as inner product** by splitting each coefficient $k$ into $\sqrt{k} \cdot \sqrt{k}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = &\; (x_1^{3})(y_1^{3}) + (\sqrt{3}\, x_1^{2} x_2)(\sqrt{3}\, y_1^{2} y_2) + (\sqrt{3}\, x_1^{2})(\sqrt{3}\, y_1^{2}) \\
& + (x_2^{3})(y_2^{3}) + (\sqrt{3}\, x_1 x_2^{2})(\sqrt{3}\, y_1 y_2^{2}) + (\sqrt{3}\, x_2^{2})(\sqrt{3}\, y_2^{2}) \\
& + (\sqrt{3}\, x_1)(\sqrt{3}\, y_1) + (\sqrt{3}\, x_2)(\sqrt{3}\, y_2) \\
& + (\sqrt{6}\, x_1 x_2)(\sqrt{6}\, y_1 y_2) + (1)(1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Read off the 10-dimensional feature map:**

$$\varphi(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{pmatrix} x_1^{3} \\ \sqrt{3}\, x_1^{2} x_2 \\ \sqrt{3}\, x_1^{2} \\ x_2^{3} \\ \sqrt{3}\, x_1 x_2^{2} \\ \sqrt{3}\, x_2^{2} \\ \sqrt{3}\, x_1 \\ \sqrt{3}\, x_2 \\ \sqrt{6}\, x_1 x_2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{10}$$

**Step 5 — Verify dimension against the formula:**

$$\dim(\mathcal{F}) = \binom{2 + 3}{3} = \binom{5}{3} = 10 \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

> Compute the polynomial kernel value for the following 2D vectors, then verify via the explicit feature map.
> $\mathbf{x} = (1, 2)^{\top}$, $\mathbf{y} = (3, 4)^{\top}$, $d = 2$, $c = 1$.

**Method A — Direct Kernel Computation:**

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (1 \cdot 3 + 2 \cdot 4 + 1)^{2} = (3 + 8 + 1)^{2} = 12^{2} = 144$$

**Method B — Explicit Feature Map (from Derivation I):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi(\mathbf{x}) &= (1^{2},\; 2^{2},\; \sqrt{2} \cdot 1 \cdot 2,\; \sqrt{2} \cdot 1,\; \sqrt{2} \cdot 2,\; 1)^{\top} \\
&= (1,\; 4,\; 2\sqrt{2},\; \sqrt{2},\; 2\sqrt{2},\; 1)^{\top}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi(\mathbf{y}) &= (3^{2},\; 4^{2},\; \sqrt{2} \cdot 3 \cdot 4,\; \sqrt{2} \cdot 3,\; \sqrt{2} \cdot 4,\; 1)^{\top} \\
&= (9,\; 16,\; 12\sqrt{2},\; 3\sqrt{2},\; 4\sqrt{2},\; 1)^{\top}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi(\mathbf{x})^{\top} \varphi(\mathbf{y}) &= (1)(9) + (4)(16) + (2\sqrt{2})(12\sqrt{2}) + (\sqrt{2})(3\sqrt{2}) + (2\sqrt{2})(4\sqrt{2}) + (1)(1) \\
&= 9 + 64 + 48 + 6 + 16 + 1 \\
&= 144 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> Both methods yield the same result $K = 144$, confirming the kernel-feature-map equivalence.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Polynomial Kernel Inside an SVM Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the polynomial kernel integrates into a Support Vector Machine classifier and how the implicit feature space relates to the original input space.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["RAW INPUT SPACE R^n"] --> nodeB["PAIR FORMATION x_i, x_j"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["COMPUTE INNER PRODUCT x_i^T x_j"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["ADD COEFFICIENT c"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"SELECT DEGREE d"}
    nodeE -- d=1 --> nodeF["LINEAR KERNEL OUTPUT"]
    nodeE -- d=2 --> nodeG["QUADRATIC KERNEL OUTPUT"]
    nodeE -- d=3 --> nodeH["CUBIC KERNEL OUTPUT"]
    nodeE -- d=4 --> nodeH4["QUARTIC KERNEL OUTPUT"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["GRAM MATRIX K"]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeH4 --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["SVM DUAL OPTIMIZATION max over alpha"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["SUPPORT VECTORS alpha_i greater than 0"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["DECISION FUNCTION f of x"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["CLASS LABEL sign of f of x"]
    nodeN["IMPLICIT FEATURE SPACE F of dim C(n+d, d)"] -.computed via.-> nodeD
    nodeN -.shadows.-> nodeA
```

### 4.2 Concept Hierarchy Diagram — Polynomial Kernel Family

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["KERNEL FAMILY"] --> nodeQ["STATIONARY KERNELS"]
    nodeP --> nodeR["NON-STATIONARY KERNELS"]
    nodeQ --> nodeS["RBF GAUSSIAN"]
    nodeQ --> nodeT["LAPLACIAN"]
    nodeR --> nodeU["POLYNOMIAL KERNEL"]
    nodeU --> nodeV["HOMOGENEOUS d, c=0"]
    nodeU --> nodeW["INHOMOGENEOUS c greater than 0"]
    nodeV --> nodeX["DOT PRODUCT KERNEL d=1"]
    nodeW --> nodeX
    nodeW --> nodeY["QUADRATIC d=2"]
    nodeW --> nodeZ["CUBIC d=3"]
    nodeW --> nodeAA["HIGHER d greater than 3"]
```

### 4.3 Mapping Diagram — From 2D Input to Implicit Feature Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn["x EQUALS x1, x2 in R2"] --> nodeMap["IMPLICIT FEATURE MAP phi"]
    nodeMap --> nodeOut["phi of x in R^6 for d=2 OR R^10 for d=3"]
    nodeKernel["K of x, y EQUALS phi of x TRANSPOSE phi of y"] -.shortcut.-> nodeMap
    nodeOut --> nodeSVM["LINEAR SVM IN FEATURE SPACE"]
    nodeSVM --> nodeBoundary["NON-LINEAR BOUNDARY IN INPUT SPACE"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The first diagram shows the **operational pipeline** — how a pair of inputs is transformed into a kernel value, then into a Gram matrix, then fed to SVM optimization.
> - The second diagram shows the **taxonomy** — the polynomial kernel is one member of the broader kernel family, distinguished by being *non-stationary* (its value depends on the absolute position of points, not only their relative displacement).
> - The third diagram emphasizes the **kernel trick**: instead of computing $\varphi(\mathbf{x})$ explicitly and then taking inner products, we compute $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ directly — but the *mathematical effect* is identical to working in the high-dimensional $\mathcal{F}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the polynomial kernel function. State the role of the parameters $d$, $c$, and $\gamma$ in $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\gamma \, \mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} + c)^{d}$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **polynomial kernel** is a kernel function that computes the inner product of two input vectors in an implicit feature space of all monomials up to degree $d$:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\gamma \, \mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} + c)^{d}$$

- **Degree $d$:** Controls the *complexity* of the implicit feature space. Higher $d$ produces more flexible (more curved) decision boundaries, but increases overfitting risk and computational cost.
- **Constant $c$:** Controls the influence of *lower-order monomials* in the feature map. With $c > 0$, monomials of degree $0$ through $d$ are all included; with $c = 0$ (homogeneous), only degree-$d$ monomials are present.
- **Scaling $\gamma$:** A multiplicative factor on the inner product. Larger $\gamma$ amplifies similarity scores; smaller $\gamma$ dampens them. It is often set to $\frac{1}{n}$ (where $n$ is the input dimension) for normalization.

**(3 marks awarded: 1 for definition, 1 for $d$ and $c$, 1 for $\gamma$)**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is the polynomial kernel said to implement the "kernel trick"? Explain briefly.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The polynomial kernel enables a learning algorithm (such as an SVM) to learn **non-linear decision boundaries** in the original input space $\mathbb{R}^{n}$, without ever explicitly computing the coordinates of data in the much higher-dimensional feature space $\mathcal{F}$ (of dimension $\binom{n+d}{d}$).

Specifically:
1. The kernel $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ returns a scalar that is **mathematically equal to** $\varphi(\mathbf{x})^{\top} \varphi(\mathbf{y})$ for some implicit feature map $\varphi$, but is computed in $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time.
2. The SVM dual formulation requires **only inner products** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ between training points — never the explicit features $\varphi(\mathbf{x}_i)$.
3. Hence, a *linear* algorithm in $\mathcal{F}$ becomes a *non-linear* algorithm in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$, achieved at the computational cost of a linear method.

This substitution of explicit feature computation by an inner-product function is called the **kernel trick**.

**(3 marks: 1 for the definition of the trick, 1 for the role of $K$, 1 for the complexity advantage)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question** (either Question A or Question B, as per internal choice).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Adapted]`

**(a) For the polynomial kernel $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} + 1)^{2}$ with $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^{\top}$ and $\mathbf{y} = (y_1, y_2)^{\top}$ in $\mathbb{R}^{2}$, derive the explicit feature map $\varphi(\mathbf{x})$ and verify that $\varphi(\mathbf{x})^{\top} \varphi(\mathbf{y}) = K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Expand using the Binomial Theorem:

$$K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (x_1 y_1 + x_2 y_2 + 1)^{2} = (x_1 y_1)^{2} + (x_2 y_2)^{2} + 1 + 2 x_1 y_1 x_2 y_2 + 2 x_1 y_1 + 2 x_2 y_2$$

**Step 2:** Re-express as a sum of products (splitting the factor 2 as $\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{2}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = &\; (x_1^{2})(y_1^{2}) + (x_2^{2})(y_2^{2}) + (\sqrt{2}\, x_1 x_2)(\sqrt{2}\, y_1 y_2) \\
& + (\sqrt{2}\, x_1)(\sqrt{2}\, y_1) + (\sqrt{2}\, x_2)(\sqrt{2}\, y_2) + (1)(1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Read off the feature map:

$$\varphi(\mathbf{x}) = (x_1^{2},\; x_2^{2},\; \sqrt{2}\, x_1 x_2,\; \sqrt{2}\, x_1,\; \sqrt{2}\, x_2,\; 1)^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$$

**Step 4:** Verify by computing the inner product:

$$\varphi(\mathbf{x})^{\top} \varphi(\mathbf{y}) = x_1^{2} y_1^{2} + x_2^{2} y_2^{2} + 2 x_1 x_2 y_1 y_2 + 2 x_1 y_1 + 2 x_2 y_2 + 1 = (x_1 y_1 + x_2 y_2 + 1)^{2} = K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \quad \checkmark$$

**Mark distribution:** 2 marks for correct expansion, 2 marks for identifying the feature map, 2 marks for correct square-root decomposition, 1 mark for verification.

---

**(b) Discuss the advantages and limitations of the polynomial kernel compared to the RBF (Gaussian) kernel for SVM classification. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Polynomial Kernel | RBF (Gaussian) Kernel |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $(\gamma \, \mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} + c)^{d}$ | $\exp(-\gamma \vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \vert^{2})$ |
| **Feature Space** | Finite-dimensional, $\binom{n+d}{d}$ | Infinite-dimensional |
| **Flexibility** | Tunable via $d$ (discrete) | Tunable via $\gamma$ (continuous) |
| **Output Range** | Unbounded (grows with $\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y}$) | Bounded: $0 < K \leq 1$ |
| **Numerical Stability** | Can overflow for large $\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y}$ and $d$ | Always numerically stable |
| **Hyperparameters** | 3 ($d, c, \gamma$) | 1 ($\gamma$) — simpler to tune |
| **Local vs. Global** | **Global** influence (all points affect all) | **Local** influence (only nearby points matter) |

**Advantages of Polynomial Kernel:**
- Provides a *direct, interpretable* feature space of polynomial interactions.
- Useful when domain knowledge suggests polynomial relationships (e.g., interactions between physical quantities).
- Faster to evaluate when $d$ is small.

**Limitations of Polynomial Kernel:**
- For large $d$ or large $\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y}$, the kernel value can grow without bound, causing **numerical overflow**.
- Choosing $d$ is a discrete decision; sub-optimal $d$ leads to under- or over-fitting.
- More hyperparameters to tune than RBF.

**Conclusion:** In practice, the RBF kernel is the *default* choice for SVMs because it is numerically stable, has fewer hyperparameters, and provides smooth, localized decision boundaries. The polynomial kernel is preferred when the underlying structure of the data is known to follow polynomial relationships or when a specific bounded-degree interaction is required for interpretability.

**Mark distribution:** 3 marks for tabular comparison, 2 marks for advantages, 2 marks for limitations and conclusion.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Adapted]`

**(a) Compute the explicit feature map $\varphi(\mathbf{x})$ for the cubic polynomial kernel $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} + 1)^{3}$ with $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^{\top}$. State the dimension of the feature space and verify with the formula $\binom{n+d}{d}$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the Multinomial Theorem:

$$(x_1 y_1 + x_2 y_2 + 1)^{3} = a^{3} + b^{3} + c^{3} + 3 a^{2} b + 3 a^{2} c + 3 a b^{2} + 3 b^{2} c + 3 a c^{2} + 3 b c^{2} + 6 a b c$$

with $a = x_1 y_1$, $b = x_2 y_2$, $c = 1$.

**Step 2:** Expand:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = &\; x_1^{3} y_1^{3} + x_2^{3} y_2^{3} + 1 \\
& + 3 x_1^{2} x_2 \, y_1^{2} y_2 + 3 x_1^{2} y_1^{2} + 3 x_1 x_2^{2} \, y_1 y_2^{2} + 3 x_2^{2} y_2^{2} \\
& + 3 x_1 y_1 + 3 x_2 y_2 + 6 x_1 x_2 \, y_1 y_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Split coefficients into $\sqrt{k} \cdot \sqrt{k}$ to form inner-product structure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = &\; (x_1^{3})(y_1^{3}) + (\sqrt{3}\, x_1^{2} x_2)(\sqrt{3}\, y_1^{2} y_2) + (\sqrt{3}\, x_1^{2})(\sqrt{3}\, y_1^{2}) \\
& + (x_2^{3})(y_2^{3}) + (\sqrt{3}\, x_1 x_2^{2})(\sqrt{3}\, y_1 y_2^{2}) + (\sqrt{3}\, x_2^{2})(\sqrt{3}\, y_2^{2}) \\
& + (\sqrt{3}\, x_1)(\sqrt{3}\, y_1) + (\sqrt{3}\, x_2)(\sqrt{3}\, y_2) \\
& + (\sqrt{6}\, x_1 x_2)(\sqrt{6}\, y_1 y_2) + (1)(1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Read off the feature map:

$$\varphi(\mathbf{x}) = (x_1^{3},\; \sqrt{3}\, x_1^{2} x_2,\; \sqrt{3}\, x_1^{2},\; x_2^{3},\; \sqrt{3}\, x_1 x_2^{2},\; \sqrt{3}\, x_2^{2},\; \sqrt{3}\, x_1,\; \sqrt{3}\, x_2,\; \sqrt{6}\, x_1 x_2,\; 1)^{\top}$$

This is a **10-dimensional** vector.

**Step 5:** Verify with the dimension formula:

$$\dim(\mathcal{F}) = \binom{n + d}{d} = \binom{2 + 3}{3} = \binom{5}{3} = 10 \quad \checkmark$$

**Mark distribution:** 2 marks for multinomial expansion, 2 marks for coefficient splitting, 2 marks for feature map identification, 1 mark for dimension verification.

---

**(b) Explain the three hyperparameters of the polynomial kernel — degree $d$, constant $c$, and scaling $\gamma$ — and describe how each affects the shape and complexity of the SVM decision boundary. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Degree $d$ (Controls the order of feature interactions):**
- $d = 1$ reduces the kernel to a linear classifier — the decision boundary is a straight line (or hyperplane in higher dimensions).
- $d = 2$ allows quadratic interactions (e.g., $x_1^2$, $x_1 x_2$). The boundary can be a conic section (ellipse, parabola, hyperbola, or pair of lines).
- $d = 3$ allows cubic interactions, producing more wavy, sinusoidal-like boundaries.
- As $d$ increases, the boundary becomes increasingly flexible and complex, eventually leading to overfitting on small datasets. Typical practical values: $d \in \{2, 3\}$.

**2. Constant $c$ (Controls the trade-off between higher- and lower-order terms):**
- With $c = 0$ (homogeneous), only monomials of *exact* degree $d$ are present in the feature space. The decision boundary has a fixed polynomial form with no linear or constant offsets.
- With $c > 0$, the feature space includes *all* monomials of degree $\leq d$. This allows the decision boundary to include both high-order interactions and lower-order corrections (linear and constant terms), making it more flexible and generally more robust.
- Large $c$ emphasizes low-order terms; small $c$ emphasizes high-order terms.

**3. Scaling $\gamma$ (Controls the influence/scale of the inner product):**
- A large $\gamma$ amplifies $\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y}$, causing the kernel values to grow rapidly and pushing the decision boundary to fit tighter around the data — increasing the risk of overfitting.
- A small $\gamma$ dampens the inner product, leading to smoother, more regularized boundaries.
- A common default is $\gamma = \frac{1}{n}$ (where $n$ is the input feature dimension), which normalizes the kernel's scale.

**Interplay of Hyperparameters:**
- The three parameters jointly control the **bias-variance trade-off**. Lower $d$, smaller $\gamma$, and larger $c$ generally yield higher bias (smoother boundaries) and lower variance. Higher $d$, larger $\gamma$, and smaller $c$ yield the opposite.
- Hyperparameter tuning is typically done via **grid search** or **cross-validation** on a held-out validation set.

**Mark distribution:** 2 marks for $d$, 2 marks for $c$, 2 marks for $\gamma$, 1 mark for bias-variance interplay.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant term $c$:** Students often write $K = (\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y})^{d}$ and lose marks for not considering the standard form with $c = 1$.
> 2. **Incorrect coefficient decomposition:** When expanding $(\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} + 1)^{d}$, students must split the multinomial coefficient $k$ as $\sqrt{k} \cdot \sqrt{k}$ to construct the feature map — failing to do this is a 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Missing the dimension formula:** The dimension $\binom{n+d}{d}$ is a *very common 1-mark question* in KTU; students must memorize it.
> 4. **Confusing $\gamma$ with $c$:** $\gamma$ scales the inner product; $c$ adds an offset. They are not interchangeable.
> 5. **Not verifying the kernel identity:** After stating $\varphi(\mathbf{x})$, students must explicitly show $\varphi(\mathbf{x})^{\top} \varphi(\mathbf{y}) = K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$. Examiners specifically look for this verification step.
> 6. **Confusing polynomial kernel with RBF:** In comparison questions, students often write vague statements like "RBF is better" without justifying with specific properties (numerical stability, hyperparameter count, local vs. global influence).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Polynomial Kernel (Module 3, KTU 2024):**

- **Core formula:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\gamma \, \mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} + c)^{d}$ — must be memorized verbatim.
- **Standard / default form:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y} + 1)^{d}$ (used in textbooks and `sklearn`).
- **Homogeneous variant:** $K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = (\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y})^{d}$ — contains only degree-$d$ monomials.
- **Feature space dimension:** $\dim(\mathcal{F}) = \binom{n+d}{d}$ — combinatorial (stars-and-bars) growth.
- **Implicit feature map $\varphi(\mathbf{x})$** contains *all monomials* of total degree $\leq d$ in $n$ variables, scaled by $\sqrt{\binom{d}{k_1, k_2, \ldots}}$ for each monomial's multinomial coefficient.
- **Coefficient-splitting trick:** For the kernel identity $\varphi(\mathbf{x})^{\top} \varphi(\mathbf{y}) = K(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ to hold, the multinomial coefficient $k$ in front of a monomial product must be written as $\sqrt{k} \cdot \sqrt{k}$, with one $\sqrt{k}$ attached to the $\mathbf{x}$-side and the other to the $\mathbf{y}$-side of the feature map.
- **Three hyperparameters:**
  - $d$ = degree (discrete; controls flexibility of the boundary),
  - $c$ = bias term (controls presence of lower-order monomials),
  - $\gamma$ = scale factor (controls amplification of inner product).
- **Mercer kernel property:** The polynomial kernel is *positive semi-definite*, satisfying Mercer's theorem; the Gram matrix is symmetric PSD.
- **Kernel trick mechanism:** The SVM dual formulation requires only $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ — never $\varphi(\mathbf{x}_i)$ — so non-linearity is achieved at the cost of a linear kernel computation.
- **Numerical caution:** For large $d$ or large $\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y}$, the polynomial kernel can overflow — prefer RBF for stability.
- **Comparison with RBF:** RBF has infinite-dimensional feature space, one hyperparameter, bounded output; polynomial has finite-dimensional feature space, three hyperparameters, unbounded output.
- **Domain application:** Image classification, bioinformatics, text categorization — wherever polynomial feature interactions have known meaning.
- **KTU-style computation tip:** Always verify by computing $K$ two ways — directly via the kernel formula and via $\varphi(\mathbf{x})^{\top} \varphi(\mathbf{y})$ — and confirm they match.
- **Hyperparameter tuning:** Use grid search with cross-validation over $(d, c, \gamma)$ jointly, with $d \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ as the typical search range.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radial Basis Function (RBF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT414 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radial Basis Function (RBF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3 — Radial Basis Function (RBF)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Radial Basis Function (RBF)** $\phi(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{c})$ is a real-valued scalar function whose value depends only on the **distance** between an input vector $\mathbf{x}$ and a fixed centre $\mathbf{c}$. Formally:

$$\phi(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{c}) = \phi(\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c} \Vert)$$

The function is **radially symmetric** about the centre $\mathbf{c}$, meaning the response is identical in all directions from the centre. When these RBF units are deployed as the activation functions of the hidden layer in a feed-forward neural network, the resulting architecture is called a **Radial Basis Function Network (RBFN)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECBT414 / Module 3):**
> The most commonly used RBF in the KTU syllabus is the **Gaussian function**, given by:
> $$\phi(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{c}_i) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}\right)$$
> where $\sigma_i$ is the **spread (width) parameter** of the $i^{\text{th}}$ hidden unit.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lighthouse" Viewpoint

Imagine a set of **lighthouses** placed strategically on a dark ocean (the input space). Each lighthouse emits a circular beam of light that is **brightest at the lighthouse (the centre)** and **fades with distance**. A ship sailing into a region of overlapping beams measures the total illumination it receives — this aggregated response forms the **network output**.

- **Centres $\mathbf{c}_i$** → Locations of the lighthouses.
- **Spread $\sigma_i$** → How wide each beam is (narrow = sharp response, wide = broad response).
- **Weights $w_i$** → How much each lighthouse contributes to the final answer.
- **Output $y(\mathbf{x})$** → The summed illumination the ship experiences.

This analogy makes it clear that an RBF network is essentially a **weighted sum of locally-tuned "bump" functions**, in contrast to the global, distributed representation offered by sigmoid-based multilayer perceptrons.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:**
> - RBF networks are **local approximators** — they respond strongly only to inputs near their centres.
> - They are biologically inspired by the **receptive fields** found in the human visual cortex (e.g., retinal ganglion cells).

### 1.3 Why RBFs Matter in Machine Learning

- They provide a **mathematically rigorous framework** for function approximation, classification, and time-series prediction.
- The hidden layer performs a **non-linear transformation** into a higher-dimensional feature space where the problem becomes (often) **linearly separable** — this is the essence of **Cover's Theorem**.
- RBF networks possess the **universal approximation property** — a single hidden layer of RBF units can approximate any continuous function to arbitrary accuracy, given enough centres.
- Training is **faster** than for back-propagation MLPs because the hidden layer can be trained **unsupervisedly** (using clustering) and only the output layer requires supervised learning.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an RBF Network

An RBFN is a **three-layer feed-forward neural network** consisting of:

| Layer | Name | Function | Parameters |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Input Layer** | Passes $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_0}$ unchanged | None (fan-out) |
| 2 | **Hidden Layer (RBF Layer)** | Computes $\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \phi(\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert)$ for $i = 1, \dots, n_1$ | Centres $\mathbf{c}_i$ and spreads $\sigma_i$ |
| 3 | **Output Layer (Linear)** | Computes weighted sum $y_k = \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} w_{ki}\,\phi_i(\mathbf{x})$ | Output weights $w_{ki}$ |

The **hidden layer is non-linear** (Gaussian, thin-plate-spline, multiquadric), while the **output layer is purely linear** — this is a defining structural feature of RBFNs.

## 2.2 Common RBF Kernels (KTU-Listed Types)

| RBF Type | Formula $\phi(r)$ with $r = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c} \Vert$ | Property |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gaussian** | $\exp(-r^{2}/2\sigma^{2})$ | Most popular; strictly positive, local, infinitely differentiable |
| **Multiquadric** | $\sqrt{r^{2} + \sigma^{2}}$ | Global support; used in scattered-data interpolation |
| **Inverse Multiquadric** | $1 / \sqrt{r^{2} + \sigma^{2}}$ | Local support; used in neural networks |
| **Thin Plate Spline** | $r^{2}\ln(r)$ | Global; minimises bending energy of interpolation surface |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Gaussian RBF** is the *de facto* choice in KTU exam questions and is the most common in engineering ML applications.

## 2.3 Mathematical Formulation of the Network Output

For an input pattern $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{n_0}]^{T}$, the response of the $i^{\text{th}}$ hidden neuron is:

$$\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}\right)$$

The $k^{\text{th}}$ output neuron produces:

$$y_k(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} w_{ki}\,\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) + b_k$$

In vector form (for a single output unit, dropping index $k$):

$$y(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} w_i\,\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x})$$

where $\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) = [\phi_1, \phi_2, \dots, \phi_{n_1}]^{T}$ and $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{n_1}]^{T}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gaussian RBF** | $\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}\right)$ | Local, bounded in $(0,1]$ |
| **Network Output** | $y_k(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} w_{ki}\,\phi_i(\mathbf{x})$ | Linear in weights $\Rightarrow$ closed-form solution possible |
| **Squared Euclidean Distance** | $\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^{2} = (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i)^{T}(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i)$ | Drives the radial decay |
| **Interpolation Matrix** | $\boldsymbol{\Phi}_{ji} = \phi(\Vert \mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert)$ | A square $N \times N$ matrix when $N$ centres = $N$ patterns |
| **Exact Interpolation Solve** | $\mathbf{w} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{-1}\mathbf{d}$ | Valid only when $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ is non-singular |
| **Pseudoinverse (Generalised)** | $\mathbf{w} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{+}\mathbf{d} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{d}$ | Used when $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ is non-square or rank-deficient |
| **LMS Weight Update** | $\Delta w_{ki} = \eta\,(d_k - y_k)\,\phi_i(\mathbf{x})$ | Gradient descent on MSE; $\eta$ is the learning rate |
| **MSE Objective** | $E = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k}\sum_{j}(d_{k}^{(j)} - y_{k}^{(j)})^{2}$ | Minimised to train output weights |
| **Cover's Theorem** | $P(n_1, N) = \left(\frac{2N}{n_1}\right)^{n_1} \cdot \binom{N-1}{n_1-1} \bigg/ 2^{N-1}$ | Probability that $N$ patterns are $\varepsilon$-separated in $\mathbb{R}^{n_1}$ |
| **Universal Approximation** | $\sup_{\mathbf{x} \in K} \vert f(\mathbf{x}) - \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} w_i\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) \vert \to 0$ as $n_1 \to \infty$ | Holds for any continuous $f$ on compact $K$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:**
> In RBFNs, the **hidden-to-output mapping is linear in the weights**. This means the output weights can be obtained via a **single-shot pseudoinverse computation** — no iterative gradient descent is required! Only the centres $\mathbf{c}_i$ and spreads $\sigma_i$ may need iterative/heuristic procedures (e.g., $k$-means).

## 2.5 Training Strategy (Hybrid Learning)

The RBF network employs a **two-stage hybrid training procedure**:

1. **Stage 1 — Unsupervised (Hidden Layer):**
   - Determine the centres $\mathbf{c}_i$ using the **$k$-means clustering** algorithm.
   - Determine the spread $\sigma_i$ for each centre, commonly as:
     $$\sigma_i = \frac{d_{\max}}{\sqrt{2\,n_1}}$$
     where $d_{\max}$ is the maximum distance between any two centres.

2. **Stage 2 — Supervised (Output Layer):**
   - With $\phi_i(\mathbf{x})$ frozen, solve the linear system $\boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{d}$ using the **pseudoinverse**:
     $$\mathbf{w} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{+}\mathbf{d}$$
   - Alternatively, apply the **delta rule / LMS** to iteratively refine $\mathbf{w}$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Control Systems** | Function approximation for adaptive control of non-linear plants |
| **Signal Processing** | Channel equalisation, time-series prediction, speech recognition |
| **Image Processing** | Image restoration, interpolation of scattered pixels |
| **Geostatistics** | Kriging interpolation for spatial data in mining/oil exploration |
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Pattern classification from biomedical signals (ECG, EEG) |
| **Finance** | Non-linear option pricing, stock-market prediction |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Response of a Single Gaussian RBF Neuron

**Given:** An input pattern $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{n_0}]^{T}$ and a centre $\mathbf{c}_i = [c_{i,1}, c_{i,2}, \dots, c_{i,n_0}]^{T}$.

**Step 1:** Compute the squared Euclidean distance (a scalar) between $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{c}_i$:

$$r_i^{2} = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^{2} = \sum_{j=1}^{n_0}(x_j - c_{i,j})^{2}$$

This is just the sum of squared element-wise differences — it is always non-negative.

**Step 2:** Form the ratio with the spread parameter $\sigma_i^{2}$:

$$z_i = \frac{r_i^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the exponential decay:

$$\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp(-z_i) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}\right)$$

**Behavioural Analysis:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi_i(\mathbf{x}_j = \mathbf{c}_i) &= \exp(0) = 1 \quad \text{(maximum response)} \\
\phi_i(\mathbf{x}_j \to \infty) &\to 0 \quad \text{(response vanishes)} \\
\phi_i(\mathbf{x}_j) &\in (0, 1] \quad \text{(bounded output)}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Complete Network Output (Vector Form)

**Given:** $n_1$ hidden RBF units, $n_2$ output units, and input $\mathbf{x}$.

**Step 1:** Compute the hidden-layer activations simultaneously:

$$\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \phi_1(\mathbf{x}) \\ \phi_2(\mathbf{x}) \\ \vdots \\ \phi_{n_1}(\mathbf{x}) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_1 \times 1}$$

**Step 2:** Form the output weight matrix $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_2 \times n_1}$:

$$\mathbf{W} = \begin{bmatrix} w_{11} & w_{12} & \cdots & w_{1,n_1} \\ w_{21} & w_{22} & \cdots & w_{2,n_1} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ w_{n_2,1} & w_{n_2,2} & \cdots & w_{n_2,n_1} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** The complete output vector is:

$$\mathbf{y}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{W}\,\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n_2 \times 1}$$

**Step 4:** For a single output neuron ($n_2 = 1$):

$$y(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} w_i\,\phi_i(\mathbf{x})$$

This is **linear in $\mathbf{w}$** but **non-linear in $\mathbf{x}$** (because $\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x})$ is a non-linear function of $\mathbf{x}$).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Solving for Output Weights via Pseudoinverse

**Problem Setup:** Given $N$ training pairs $\{(\mathbf{x}_j, d_j)\}_{j=1}^{N}$ and a chosen set of $n_1$ centres, determine the weights $\mathbf{w}$ that satisfy:

$$d_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} w_i\,\phi_i(\mathbf{x}_j) \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \dots, N$$

**Step 1:** Stack all $N$ equations into a single matrix equation. The **interpolation (or design) matrix** is:

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi} = \begin{bmatrix} \phi_1(\mathbf{x}_1) & \phi_2(\mathbf{x}_1) & \cdots & \phi_{n_1}(\mathbf{x}_1) \\ \phi_1(\mathbf{x}_2) & \phi_2(\mathbf{x}_2) & \cdots & \phi_{n_1}(\mathbf{x}_2) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \phi_1(\mathbf{x}_N) & \phi_2(\mathbf{x}_N) & \cdots & \phi_{n_1}(\mathbf{x}_N) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times n_1}$$

with entries $\boldsymbol{\Phi}_{ji} = \phi_i(\mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\Vert \mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert)$.

**Step 2:** The system becomes:

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi}\,\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{d}$$

where $\mathbf{d} = [d_1, d_2, \dots, d_N]^{T}$ and $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, \dots, w_{n_1}]^{T}$.

**Step 3 — Square case ($N = n_1$):** If $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ is non-singular, an **exact interpolation** solution exists:

$$\mathbf{w} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{-1}\mathbf{d}$$

This guarantees that the network produces the **exact target value at every training point** (zero empirical error).

**Step 4 — Over-determined case ($N > n_1$):** Typically, there are more training samples than centres. A least-squares solution is preferred to avoid overfitting:

$$\mathbf{w} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{d} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{+}\mathbf{d}$$

This minimises the **sum of squared errors** $\Vert \boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{d} \Vert^{2}$, yielding a smoother, better-generalising solution.

**Step 5 — Under-determined case ($N < n_1$):** Infinitely many solutions exist; the minimum-norm solution is $\mathbf{w} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}(\boldsymbol{\Phi}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T})^{-1}\mathbf{d}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Engineering Insight:**
> The pseudoinverse solution is **closed-form and non-iterative** — it is one of the major speed advantages of RBFNs over back-propagation-trained MLPs.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — LMS / Delta Rule for Output Weights

**Problem Setup:** When the pseudoinverse is computationally infeasible (e.g., online learning, streaming data), we minimise the instantaneous squared error using gradient descent.

**Step 1:** Define the instantaneous error for output $k$ on pattern $j$:

$$E_{k}^{(j)} = \frac{1}{2}\left(d_k^{(j)} - y_k^{(j)}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 2:** The output is $y_k^{(j)} = \sum_{i} w_{ki}\,\phi_i(\mathbf{x}^{(j)})$, so:

$$\frac{\partial y_k^{(j)}}{\partial w_{ki}} = \phi_i(\mathbf{x}^{(j)})$$

**Step 3:** Apply the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial E_{k}^{(j)}}{\partial w_{ki}} = -(d_k^{(j)} - y_k^{(j)})\,\phi_i(\mathbf{x}^{(j)})$$

**Step 4:** The gradient-descent update rule is:

$$\Delta w_{ki} = -\eta\,\frac{\partial E_{k}^{(j)}}{\partial w_{ki}} = \eta\,(d_k^{(j)} - y_k^{(j)})\,\phi_i(\mathbf{x}^{(j)})$$

**Step 5:** Final update equation:

$$w_{ki}^{\text{new}} = w_{ki}^{\text{old}} + \eta\,(d_k - y_k)\,\phi_i(\mathbf{x})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the **derivative of $\phi_i$ does NOT appear** in the update rule — this is because the centres $\mathbf{c}_i$ are fixed during output-layer training! Only the output weights need adjustment.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Selecting the Spread Parameter $\sigma$

A common heuristic (used in the KTU syllabus) is the **Poggio / Moody-Darken rule**:

$$\sigma_i = \frac{d_{\max}}{\sqrt{2\,n_1}}$$

**Derivation logic:**
- Let $d_{\max} = \max_{i,j}\Vert \mathbf{c}_i - \mathbf{c}_j \Vert$ be the largest distance between any two centres.
- For the Gaussian to provide **overlapping coverage** without leaving "holes" in the input space, the sum of $n_1$ Gaussians evaluated at the farthest point from any centre should still be appreciable.
- A common engineering rule-of-thumb is that $\sigma \approx d_{\max} / \sqrt{2\,n_1}$ gives a "Goldilocks" trade-off between overfitting (small $\sigma$) and underfitting (large $\sigma$).

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Why Hidden Layer is Non-Linear but Output is Linear

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Hidden response:} \quad \phi_i(\mathbf{x}) &= \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}\right) && \text{Non-linear in } \mathbf{x} \\
\text{Output response:} \quad y_k(\mathbf{x}) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} w_{ki}\,\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) && \text{Linear in } w_{ki} \\
\text{Combined:} \quad y_k(\mathbf{x}) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} w_{ki}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}\right) && \text{Non-linear in } \mathbf{x}, \text{ linear in } w_{ki}
\end{aligned}$$

This **decoupling** is the central architectural advantage: the non-linear feature extraction is done once, and the supervisory task reduces to a linear problem in the expanded feature space.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
