# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define Radial Basis Function. Give the mathematical expression for a Gaussian RBF and explain the role of the centre and spread parameters. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A Radial Basis Function (RBF) is a scalar-valued function whose value depends only on the distance from a fixed centre point. Mathematically, $\phi(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{c}) = \phi(\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c} \Vert)$.

The most common form is the **Gaussian RBF**:

$$\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}\right)$$

- The **centre $\mathbf{c}_i$** is the point in the input space at which the $i^{\text{th}}$ hidden neuron produces its maximum response (equal to $1$).
- The **spread $\sigma_i$** controls the width of the receptive field: a small $\sigma$ produces a sharp, narrow bump (local response), while a large $\sigma$ produces a broad, smooth bump (more global response).

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
**State Cover's Theorem on the separability of patterns. Why is it important for RBF networks? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
**Cover's Theorem (1965):** A complex pattern-classification problem cast non-linearly into a high-dimensional space is more likely to be linearly separable than if cast into a low-dimensional space. Formally, the probability that $N$ patterns are $\varepsilon$-separated in $\mathbb{R}^{n_1}$ is:

$$P(N, n_1) = \left(\frac{2N}{n_1}\right)^{n_1} \cdot \binom{N-1}{n_1-1} \cdot \frac{1}{2^{N-1}}$$

**Importance for RBF networks:**
The hidden RBF layer performs a **non-linear mapping** $\mathbf{x} \mapsto \boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x})$ from the input space to a higher-dimensional hidden space. By Cover's theorem, this expanded space is **more likely to be linearly separable**, allowing the output layer to use a simple **linear combiner**. This justifies the architectural choice of using a non-linear hidden layer followed by a linear output layer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Choice, 14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students frequently lose marks by **(a)** failing to clearly state the **linearity in weights, non-linearity in inputs** property of RBFNs, **(b)** omitting the **interpolation matrix $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$** when solving for weights, and **(c)** confusing the roles of the **unsupervised (centres)** and **supervised (weights)** stages of training. Read each sub-part carefully and address **all** parts in the answer.

---

### 🔹 **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **A(a). Explain the architecture and working of a Radial Basis Function Network with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the hidden and output layers. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture:** An RBF network is a three-layer feed-forward neural network (as shown in Section 4.1 above).

**Layer 1 — Input Layer:**
It simply distributes the input vector $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{n_0}]^{T}$ to all hidden neurons. No computation is performed here; the input layer has $n_0$ neurons equal to the dimensionality of the input.

**Layer 2 — Hidden (RBF) Layer:**
This layer contains $n_1$ RBF neurons. The $i^{\text{th}}$ hidden neuron computes the Gaussian activation:

$$\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^{2}}{2\sigma_i^{2}}\right)$$

**Role of the hidden layer:**
- Performs a **non-linear transformation** of the input space.
- Maps the input from $\mathbb{R}^{n_0}$ to a new feature space $\mathbb{R}^{n_1}$.
- Each hidden neuron acts as a **local detector** that fires maximally when the input is close to its centre.
- The output of the hidden layer is the vector $\boldsymbol{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n_1}$.

**Layer 3 — Output Layer:**
This is a **linear** layer with $n_2$ output neurons. The $k^{\text{th}}$ output is:

$$y_k(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} w_{ki}\,\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) + b_k$$

**Role of the output layer:**
- Performs a **linear combination** of the hidden-layer activations.
- Produces the final network response (e.g., class scores, regression output).
- Because this layer is linear in the weights, it can be trained using **closed-form pseudoinverse methods** rather than iterative gradient descent.

**Working Principle:** The input $\mathbf{x}$ is first expanded into a higher-dimensional feature space (where Cover's theorem guarantees better separability) and then classified/regressed via a simple linear hyperplane. **(7 Marks)**

#### **A(b). Derive the equation for computing the output weights of an RBF network using the pseudoinverse method. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the design (interpolation) matrix:**
For $N$ training patterns $\{(\mathbf{x}_j, d_j)\}_{j=1}^{N}$ and $n_1$ fixed centres $\{\mathbf{c}_i\}_{i=1}^{n_1}$, define:

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi} = \begin{bmatrix} \phi(\Vert \mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{c}_1 \Vert) & \phi(\Vert \mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{c}_2 \Vert) & \cdots & \phi(\Vert \mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{c}_{n_1} \Vert) \\ \phi(\Vert \mathbf{x}_2 - \mathbf{c}_1 \Vert) & \phi(\Vert \mathbf{x}_2 - \mathbf{c}_2 \Vert) & \cdots & \phi(\Vert \mathbf{x}_2 - \mathbf{c}_{n_1} \Vert) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \phi(\Vert \mathbf{x}_N - \mathbf{c}_1 \Vert) & \phi(\Vert \mathbf{x}_N - \mathbf{c}_2 \Vert) & \cdots & \phi(\Vert \mathbf{x}_N - \mathbf{c}_{n_1} \Vert) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Express the network output compactly:**

$$\mathbf{y} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}\,\mathbf{w}$$

We want $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{d}$, so:

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi}\,\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{d}$$

**Step 3 — Square case ($N = n_1$):** If $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ is invertible:

$$\mathbf{w} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{-1}\mathbf{d}$$

**Step 4 — Over-determined case ($N > n_1$):** We cannot satisfy all equations exactly, so we minimise the squared error $E = \Vert \boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{d} \Vert^{2}$. Setting $\frac{\partial E}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}}(\boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{d})^{T}(\boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{d}) = 2\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}(\boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{d}) = 0$$

Solving:

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi}\mathbf{w} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{d}$$

Provided $\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ is non-singular:

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{w} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{d} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{+}\mathbf{d}\;}$$

where $\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{+} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}$ is the **Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** The pseudoinverse solution gives the **least-squares optimal weight vector** in a single, non-iterative matrix computation. This is one of the most important advantages of RBF networks over back-propagation MLPs, where iterative gradient descent is mandatory. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 🔹 **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### **B(a). State and explain Cover's Theorem. How does it justify the architecture of an RBF network? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement of Cover's Theorem:**
*"A complex pattern-classification problem, which is non-linearly separable in its original input space, has a high probability of becoming linearly separable when it is non-linearly mapped to a higher-dimensional space."*

Mathematically, the probability that $N$ patterns are **$\varepsilon$-separated** (linearly separable with margin) in a space of dimensionality $n_1$ is given by:

$$P(N, n_1) = \left(\frac{2N}{n_1}\right)^{n_1} \cdot \frac{\binom{N-1}{n_1-1}}{2^{N-1}}, \quad \text{for } N > n_1$$

**Explanation:**
- $N$ → number of patterns
- $n_1$ → dimensionality of the space in which the patterns are represented
- **Key insight:** As $n_1$ increases (for fixed $N$), the probability of separability **increases**.

**Geometric intuition:** In a low-dimensional space, points are "crammed" together, and a single hyperplane cannot separate them. By applying a non-linear transformation, the points are "spread out" into a higher-dimensional space where a hyperplane can be drawn to separate them.

**Justification for RBF Architecture:**
Cover's theorem directly motivates the three-layer structure of an RBF network:

| Layer | Role | Cover's Theorem Connection |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Input | Original $\mathbb{R}^{n_0}$ space | Often **not** linearly separable |
| Hidden (RBF) | Maps to $\mathbb{R}^{n_1}$, $n_1 > n_0$ | **Non-linear expansion** increases separability probability |
| Output | Linear hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^{n_1}$ | **Linear separability** guaranteed with high probability |

Thus, the hidden RBF layer **performs the non-linear mapping** predicted by Cover's theorem, while the linear output layer **exploits the resulting separability** to perform classification/regression using a simple linear discriminant. **(7 Marks)**

#### **B(b). Compare RBF networks with Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) based on architecture, training, and generalisation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | **RBF Network** | **Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Architecture** | 3 layers: Input → RBF (non-linear, local) → Linear output | $L$ layers: Input → Sigmoid (non-linear, global) → ... → Linear output |
| **Hidden Neuron Count** | Usually $n_1 \ll N$ (few centres) | Often requires many hidden neurons across multiple layers |
| **Activation Function** | Radial (Gaussian), bounded in $(0,1]$ | Sigmoidal / tanh, bounded in $(0,1)$ or $(-1,1)$ |
| **Role of Hidden Layer** | Non-linear feature **expansion** to higher dim | Non-linear feature **transformation** at fixed dim |
| **Hidden Layer Training** | **Unsupervised** ($k$-means, OLS, random selection) | **Supervised** (back-propagation) |
| **Output Layer Training** | **Linear**: closed-form via pseudoinverse, or LMS | **Non-linear**: requires iterative back-propagation |
| **Training Speed** | **Fast** (single matrix inversion or short iteration) | **Slow** (many epochs of gradient descent) |
| **Generalisation** | **Local** generalisation: nearby inputs have similar outputs (smooth interpolation) | **Global** generalisation: weights affect the entire input space |
| **Risk of Local Minima** | **None** for output layer (convex problem); only $k$-means for centres can have minor local minima | **High** — non-convex error surface, sensitive to initialisation |
| **Universal Approximation** | ✅ Yes (with enough centres) | ✅ Yes (with enough hidden neurons) |
| **Interpretability** | Centres are interpretable as prototypical patterns | Hidden representations are distributed and opaque |
| **Sensitivity to $\sigma$ / Centre Selection** | **High** — wrong $\sigma$ ruins performance | Moderate — depends on weight initialisation |
| **Typical Use Cases** | Function approximation, time-series, control, scattered-data interpolation | Image classification, NLP, general deep learning |

**Conclusion:** RBF networks are preferred when **fast training, local generalisation, and mathematical tractability** are required (e.g., control systems, regression on small-to-medium datasets). MLPs are preferred for **large-scale, complex perceptual tasks** (e.g., deep learning for vision and language). **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE:**

- [x] **Definition:** RBF is a function of the form $\phi(\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c} \Vert)$, radially symmetric about its centre.
- [x] **Most Common RBF:** Gaussian $\phi_i(\mathbf{x}) = \exp(-\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert^{2}/2\sigma_i^{2})$.
- [x] **Architecture:** Three layers — Input (fan-out) → Hidden (RBF, non-linear) → Output (linear).
- [x] **Key Property:** Hidden layer is **non-linear in input**, output layer is **linear in weights**.
- [x] **Output Equation:** $y_k(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n_1} w_{ki}\,\phi_i(\mathbf{x})$.
- [x] **Training Stages:**
  1. Unsupervised — find centres via **$k$-means** (or random subset).
  2. Compute spread $\sigma_i = d_{\max}/\sqrt{2n_1}$.
  3. Supervised — solve $\mathbf{w} = \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{+}\mathbf{d}$ using the **pseudoinverse**.
- [x] **Cover's Theorem:** Probability of linear separability increases with dimensionality; justifies the non-linear hidden layer of RBF networks.
- [x] **Universal Approximation:** A single hidden RBF layer can approximate any continuous function on a compact set.
- [x] **LMS Update Rule:** $\Delta w_{ki} = \eta(d_k - y_k)\phi_i(\mathbf{x})$ — derivative of $\phi$ is **NOT** needed because centres are fixed.
- [x] **Advantages:** Fast training, no local minima in output layer, mathematical rigour, local generalisation.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** Poor scalability to very large datasets, sensitive to centre selection, curse of dimensionality with many centres.
- [x] **Pitfall to avoid in exam:** Do not confuse **centres** (learned unsupervisedly) with **weights** (learned supervisedly).
- [x] **Design matrix entry:** $\boldsymbol{\Phi}_{ji} = \phi(\Vert \mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{c}_i \Vert)$ — always the response of hidden unit $i$ to training pattern $j$.
- [x] **Comparisons to recall:** RBFN vs MLP — architecture, training, locality, speed, and use-case differences (see Question B(b) table above).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Develop a Classifier for Face Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT414 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Develop a Classifier for Face Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Face Detection** is a classical computer vision problem formulated as a **sliding-window binary classification** task. Given an input image, the system must answer a single yes/no question for every possible sub-window of the image: *Does this sub-window contain a human face (positive class, label $= 1$) or background / non-face (negative class, label $= 0$)?*

A **classifier for face detection** is therefore a learned function

$$f_{\theta}: \mathbb{R}^{W \times H \times 3} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$$

parameterized by $\theta$ (e.g., weights of an ensemble of weak learners), which is trained on a large corpus of labelled face and non-face image patches and then exhaustively applied at every location and scale of a test image.

The de-facto standard approach taught in KTU 2024 is the **Viola–Jones framework** (Viola & Jones, 2001), which combines **Haar-like rectangular features**, an **Integral Image** for fast feature evaluation, **AdaBoost** for feature selection, and a **Cascade of Classifiers** for rapid rejection of easy non-face windows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4, face detection is the canonical case study that ties together *feature engineering* (Haar features), *ensemble learning* (AdaBoost), and *cascaded decision systems* (Attentional Cascade). Expect a 14-mark question that asks you to design and explain the full pipeline end-to-end.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a security guard standing at a doorway scanning a crowd. You do not stare at every square centimetre of every person. Instead, you perform a **sequence of quick, cheap visual checks**:

1. *Is the silhouette roughly head-shaped and vertically oriented?* (very fast check — rejects 80 % of background immediately)
2. *Are there two dark horizontal regions (eyes) above a brighter region (nose/mouth)?* (a slightly more expensive check)
3. *Do the spatial proportions of these regions match a real face?* (expensive, but only on the few remaining candidates)

Each check is itself a tiny classifier. Only windows that pass **all** checks in sequence are labelled as "face." This is exactly what the **cascade of classifiers** does — and the "checks" are individual **Haar features** selected by **AdaBoost**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The genius of the Viola–Jones design is that it is *asymmetric* — it is built to be lightning-fast on non-faces (the common case) and spend compute only on plausible face candidates. This makes real-time face detection feasible on the CPUs of the early 2000s, and the same idea is still embedded inside OpenCV's `cv2.CascadeClassifier`.

### 1.3 Why Face Detection is Non-Trivial

| Challenge | Description |
|---|---|
| **Scale variation** | A face can be 20 × 20 px or 800 × 800 px in the same image. |
| **Pose variation** | Frontal, profile, tilted, occluded. |
| **Illumination** | Lighting can dramatically change pixel intensities. |
| **Intra-class variation** | Skin tone, age, hairstyle, expression, accessories. |
| **Class imbalance** | Non-face windows vastly outnumber face windows ($\sim 10^{5}$:1). |
| **Real-time constraint** | Must process $\ge 15$ frames per second. |

These motivate a *fast, robust, hierarchical* classifier — which is precisely what we will now build.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step 1 — Formulate Detection as Classification

Given a grayscale image patch $x \in \mathbb{R}^{24 \times 24}$ (the standard training window size), we want a classifier

$$\hat{y} = \mathbb{1}\big[g(x) \geq \tau\big]$$

where $g(x)$ is a real-valued discriminant score and $\tau$ is a decision threshold. The classifier must be:

- **Accurate** — high detection rate (sensitivity / true positive rate) and low false positive rate.
- **Fast** — able to score thousands of sub-windows per second.

### 2.2 Step 2 — Haar-like Rectangular Features

A **Haar-like feature** is the difference between the sum of pixel intensities under a white region and the sum under a black region, over a rectangular sub-window. There are four canonical prototypes used in the original Viola–Jones paper:

| Feature Type | Visual | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Edge — horizontal** | ■□ | Detects horizontal intensity gradient (e.g., eyes vs. cheeks). |
| **Edge — vertical** | □■□ | Detects vertical gradient (e.g., nose bridge). |
| **Line — horizontal** | ■□■ | Detects horizontal stripe (e.g., mouth). |
| **Line — vertical** | □■□ (rotated) | Detects vertical stripe. |
| **Diagonal** | (4-rectangle "checker") | Detects diagonal edges. |

For a 24 × 24 window, the total number of such features is **$\approx 160{,}000$**. Far more than pixels (576) — this is a key insight: the *feature space* is much richer than the *pixel space*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why rectangular sums?** They capture *local intensity contrast* — and contrast, not absolute intensity, is what makes a face look like a face regardless of skin colour or lighting. A horizontal edge feature fires strongly on the eye-cheek contrast, which is preserved across all human faces.

### 2.3 Step 3 — The Integral Image (Fast Feature Evaluation)

Naïvely computing one Haar feature over a 24 × 24 window requires summing tens to thousands of pixels. Doing this 160,000 times per window × thousands of windows per image is infeasible.

The **Integral Image** $ii(x, y)$ is defined as the sum of all pixel intensities in the rectangle from the origin $(0, 0)$ to $(x, y)$:

$$ii(x, y) = \sum_{x' \le x,\; y' \le y} i(x', y')$$

It can be computed from the original image in a **single pass** using the recurrence

$$ii(x, y) = i(x, y) + ii(x-1, y) + ii(x, y-1) - ii(x-1, y-1)$$

Once built, the sum of pixels in *any* upright rectangle with corners $A$ (top-left), $B$ (top-right), $C$ (bottom-left), $D$ (bottom-right) is obtained in **O(1)** with just four array lookups:

$$S_{ABCD} = ii(D) + ii(A) - ii(B) - ii(C)$$

Hence a Haar feature (sum of white rectangles minus sum of black rectangles) is also computed in constant time, independent of the rectangle area.

### 2.4 Step 4 — AdaBoost for Feature Selection

With $\approx 160{,}000$ candidate features per window, we cannot use them all. **AdaBoost** (Adaptive Boosting) solves two problems at once:

1. **Feature selection** — picks a small subset of features that together form a strong classifier.
2. **Classifier construction** — combines many *weak learners* (each weak learner is a simple threshold on a single Haar feature) into one *strong classifier*.

#### AdaBoost Training Algorithm

**Input:** Training set $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, $T$ = number of boosting rounds.

1. Initialise uniform weights: $w_i^{(1)} = \dfrac{1}{N}$ for all $i$.
2. For $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$:
   a. Normalise weights: $W^{(t)} = \sum_i w_i^{(t)}$, $\quad w_i^{(t)} \leftarrow \dfrac{w_i^{(t)}}{W^{(t)}}$.
   b. For each candidate weak learner $h_j$, compute its weighted error
   
   $$\epsilon_j^{(t)} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{(t)} \cdot \mathbb{1}\big[y_i \neq h_j(x_i)\big]$$
   
   c. Pick the best weak learner: $h_t = \arg\min_j \epsilon_j^{(t)}$.
   d. Assign it a confidence weight
   
   $$\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right)$$
   
   e. Update the sample weights:
   
   $$w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \cdot \exp\!\big(-\alpha_t \, y_i \, h_t(x_i)\big)$$

3. The final strong classifier is

$$H(x) = \operatorname{sign}\!\left(\sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t \, h_t(x)\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition behind weight update:** Samples misclassified by $h_t$ get their weights *increased* (so the next weak learner focuses on them), while correctly classified samples have their weights *decreased*. The exponential weighting is the natural choice that minimises the upper bound on the training error, $E \le \exp\!\left(-2 \sum_t \gamma_t^2\right)$ where $\gamma_t = \tfrac{1}{2} - \epsilon_t$ is the "edge."

### 2.5 Step 5 — The Attentional Cascade

A single AdaBoost classifier using $\sim 200$ features is still too slow to apply to every sub-window. The **cascade** organises classifiers into a sequence of stages:

- **Stage 1:** uses 1–2 features. Rejects $\sim 50$ % of non-faces with virtually no false negatives.
- **Stage 2:** uses $\sim 5$ features. Rejects $\sim 80$ % of the remainder.
- **Stage k:** progressively more features, applied only to windows that survived all previous stages.
- **Final stage:** uses $\sim 200$ features, extremely low false positive rate.

The overall false positive rate of a $K$-stage cascade is

$$F = \prod_{k=1}^{K} f_k$$

where $f_k$ is the false positive rate of stage $k$. The overall detection rate is

$$D = \prod_{k=1}^{K} d_k$$

For example, 10 stages each with $f_k = 0.5$ and $d_k = 0.995$ give $F = 0.5^{10} \approx 0.001$ and $D = 0.995^{10} \approx 0.951$.

Each stage is itself trained with AdaBoost, but with stage-specific targets (e.g., $d_k \ge 0.999$ and $f_k \le 0.5$).

### 2.6 Step 6 — Sliding Window Detection at Test Time

At test time, the trained cascade is applied as follows:

1. Convert the input image to grayscale.
2. Build the integral image.
3. Slide a 24 × 24 window across the image at every position.
4. Apply the cascade. If the window passes all stages, mark it as a face.
5. **Scale the image** (e.g., by a factor of 1.25) and repeat, to detect faces of different sizes.
6. Merge overlapping detections using **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)**.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Integral image | $ii(x,y) = \sum_{x' \le x,\, y' \le y} i(x',y')$ | Single-pass O($WH$). |
| 2 | Rectangle sum from integral image | $S = ii(D) + ii(A) - ii(B) - ii(C)$ | A=TL, B=TR, C=BL, D=BR. |
| 3 | Haar feature value | $F = \sum_{\text{white}} S_j - \sum_{\text{black}} S_j$ | O(1) per feature. |
| 4 | AdaBoost weight | $\alpha_t = \tfrac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\tfrac{1-\epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right)$ | Higher $\alpha_t$ ⇒ more accurate weak learner. |
| 5 | Sample weight update | $w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))$ | Misclassified samples get up-weighted. |
| 6 | Strong classifier | $H(x) = \operatorname{sign}\!\left(\sum_t \alpha_t h_t(x)\right)$ | Weighted vote of weak learners. |
| 7 | Cascade false positive rate | $F = \prod_k f_k$ | Product over all stages. |
| 8 | Cascade detection rate | $D = \prod_k d_k$ | Product over all stages. |
| 9 | Detection rate (recall) | $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Sensitivity. |
| 10 | False positive rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | $1 - \text{specificity}$. |
| 11 | Non-Max Suppression (IoU) | $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Discards overlapping duplicate detections. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE (per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rules):** In the table above, absolute-value bars have been replaced with `\vert` to prevent breaking the markdown table. When writing answers in the exam, use a single $..$ block outside the table for any expression containing `|`.

### 2.8 Real-World Utility

The same Viola–Jones pipeline is shipped in **OpenCV's `cv2.CascadeClassifier`** (with pre-trained `haarcascade_frontalface_default.xml`) and is used in:

- Smartphone autofocus and smile detection.
- Airport security gates (pre-deep-learning era).
- DSLR cameras for eye/focus tracking.
- Embedded devices in automobiles for **driver drowsiness detection**.

Modern CNN-based detectors (MTCNN, RetinaFace, YOLO-face) have surpassed it in accuracy, but the *cascade* design philosophy still inspires efficient detection heads in real-time systems.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Integral Image and O(1) Rectangle Sum

**Goal:** Show that the sum of pixels inside any upright rectangle can be computed with exactly **4 array accesses** using the integral image.

**Setup.** Let $i(x, y)$ be the original grayscale image. Define the integral image as

$$
\begin{aligned}
ii(x, y) &= \sum_{x'=0}^{x} \sum_{y'=0}^{y} i(x', y')
\end{aligned}
$$

**Boundary conditions:** $ii(x, -1) = 0$ and $ii(-1, y) = 0$ for all valid $x, y$.

**Recurrence derivation.** Split the rectangle $[0, x] \times [0, y]$ into the bottom row $[0, x] \times \{y\}$ and the rectangle above it $[0, x] \times [0, y-1]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ii(x, y) &= \sum_{x'=0}^{x} \sum_{y'=0}^{y-1} i(x', y') + \sum_{x'=0}^{x} i(x', y) \\
&= ii(x, y-1) + \sum_{x'=0}^{x} i(x', y)
\end{aligned}
$$

The single-row sum can be expressed using $ii(x-1, y)$ and $ii(x, y)$ itself, leading to the canonical recurrence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ii(x, y) = i(x, y) + ii(x-1, y) + ii(x, y-1) - ii(x-1, y-1)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is computed in one pass over the image in O($W H$) total time.

**Rectangle sum.** Let rectangle $R$ have top-left corner $A = (x_1, y_1)$ and bottom-right corner $D = (x_2, y_2)$, with $B = (x_2, y_1)$, $C = (x_1, y_2)$. We want

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_R = \sum_{x=x_1}^{x_2} \sum_{y=y_1}^{y_2} i(x, y)
\end{aligned}
$$

Observe that $ii(D)$ sums the entire rectangle from origin to $D$. To isolate $R$:

- $ii(D)$ contains $R$ but also everything above and to the left.
- Subtract $ii(B)$ (the part to the right of $B$ but in the same top strip) and $ii(C)$ (the part below $C$ but in the same left strip).
- The corner $ii(A)$ was subtracted **twice**, so add it back.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_R = ii(D) - ii(B) - ii(C) + ii(A)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** exactly 4 lookups, each O(1). $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: AdaBoost Weight Update from Exponential Loss Minimisation

**Goal:** Show that the AdaBoost weight update $w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))$ is the natural multiplicative update that minimises an upper bound on the 0–1 training error.

**Step 1 — Exponential upper bound on 0–1 loss.** The indicator $\mathbb{1}[y \neq H(x)]$ is bounded above by $\exp(-y H(x))$ because

$$
\begin{aligned}
\exp(-y H(x)) \ge \mathbb{1}[y \neq H(x)]
\quad \text{for all } y \in \{-1, +1\}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the empirical 0–1 error is bounded by

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{emp}} &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[y_i \neq H(x_i)] \\
&\le \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp(-y_i H(x_i)) \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \exp\!\left(-y_i \sum_{t} \alpha_t h_t(x_i)\right).
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Choose $\alpha_t$ to minimise the bound given $h_t$.** After round $t$, the strong classifier is $H_t = H_{t-1} + \alpha_t h_t$. The bound becomes

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_t &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{(t)} \exp\!\left(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i)\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where we have defined $w_i^{(t)} = \exp(-y_i H_{t-1}(x_i))$.

Because $y_i h_t(x_i) \in \{-1, +1\}$, we can split the sum into correctly and incorrectly classified samples:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_t &= e^{-\alpha_t} \sum_{i:\,y_i = h_t} w_i^{(t)} \;+\; e^{+\alpha_t} \sum_{i:\,y_i \neq h_t} w_i^{(t)} \\
&= e^{-\alpha_t}(1 - \epsilon_t) W^{(t)} \;+\; e^{+\alpha_t} \epsilon_t W^{(t)} \\
&= W^{(t)} \!\left[(1-\epsilon_t) e^{-\alpha_t} + \epsilon_t e^{+\alpha_t}\right].
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Minimise $Z_t$ w.r.t. $\alpha_t$.** Differentiate the bracket and set to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{d\alpha_t}\!\left[(1-\epsilon_t) e^{-\alpha_t} + \epsilon_t e^{+\alpha_t}\right] &= 0 \\
-(1-\epsilon_t) e^{-\alpha_t} + \epsilon_t e^{+\alpha_t} &= 0 \\
\epsilon_t e^{+\alpha_t} &= (1-\epsilon_t) e^{-\alpha_t} \\
e^{2\alpha_t} &= \frac{1-\epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t} \\
\alpha_t &= \tfrac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1-\epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right).
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Resulting weight update.** Substituting $\alpha_t$ back, the new unnormalised weight is

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_i^{(t+1)} &= w_i^{(t)} \exp\!\left(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i)\right),
\end{aligned}
$$

which is the standard AdaBoost update. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: 1-Stage AdaBoost on a Toy Dataset

Suppose we have $N = 4$ training patches with $y = (-1, -1, +1, +1)$ and three candidate weak learners, each a threshold on a single Haar feature:

| $i$ | $x_i$ (Haar response) | $y_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.3 | $-1$ |
| 2 | 0.4 | $-1$ |
| 3 | 0.6 | $+1$ |
| 4 | 0.7 | $+1$ |

| Weak learner $h_j$ | Rule | Errors at round 1 (uniform $w = 0.25$) |
|---|---|---|
| $h_1$ | $+1$ if $x \le 0.5$, else $-1$ | $i = 3, 4$ misclassified $\Rightarrow \epsilon_1 = 0.5$ |
| $h_2$ | $+1$ if $x \ge 0.5$, else $-1$ | $i = 1, 2$ misclassified $\Rightarrow \epsilon_2 = 0.5$ |
| $h_3$ | $+1$ if $x \le 0.35$, else $-1$ | $i = 2$ misclassified $\Rightarrow \epsilon_3 = 0.25$ |

Pick $h_3$ ($\epsilon = 0.25$). Compute

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_1 &= \tfrac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1-0.25}{0.25}\right) = \tfrac{1}{2} \ln(3) \approx 0.549.
\end{aligned}
$$

Update weights (unnormalised):

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1^{(2)} &= 0.25 \cdot e^{+0.549} = 0.433 \quad (\text{misclassified}) \\
w_2^{(2)} &= 0.25 \cdot e^{-0.549} = 0.144 \quad (\text{correct}) \\
w_3^{(2)} &= 0.25 \cdot e^{-0.549} = 0.144 \quad (\text{correct}) \\
w_4^{(2)} &= 0.25 \cdot e^{-0.549} = 0.144 \quad (\text{correct})
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum $= 0.865$. Normalise: $w_1^{(2)} = 0.500$, others $= 0.167$ each. Sample 1 now dominates the weight distribution — the next weak learner will be forced to focus on getting sample 1 right.

**Strong classifier after 1 round:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(x) = \operatorname{sign}\!\big(\alpha_1 h_3(x)\big) = \operatorname{sign}\!\big(0.549 \cdot h_3(x)\big).
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How the Pipeline Maps to KTU COs

| Pipeline Stage | Theoretical Tool | Maps to KTU CO |
|---|---|---|
| Image preprocessing | Grayscale conversion, normalisation | CO1 — Data preprocessing |
| Feature extraction | Haar-like features + integral image | CO2 — Feature engineering |
| Weak learner design | Decision stump on one feature | CO3 — Model formulation |
| Strong classifier | AdaBoost ensemble | CO3 — Ensemble methods |
| Multi-stage decision | Cascade of classifiers | CO4 — Hierarchical models |
| Test-time inference | Sliding window + scale pyramid + NMS | CO4 — Model deployment |
| Performance evaluation | DR, FPR, ROC curve | CO5 — Model evaluation |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Viola–Jones Face Detection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image] --> B[Grayscale Conversion]
    B --> C[Compute Integral Image]
    C --> D[Initialize Sliding Window at Scale s = 1.0]
    D --> E[Extract 24x24 Sub-window]
    E --> F[Stage 1 Classifier: 1-2 Haar Features]
    F --> G{Window Passes Stage 1?}
    G -- No --> Z1[Reject as Non-Face]
    G -- Yes --> H[Stage 2 Classifier: ~5 Haar Features]
    H --> I{Window Passes Stage 2?}
    I -- No --> Z1
    I -- Yes --> J[Stage 3 Classifier: ~20 Haar Features]
    J --> K{Window Passes All Stages?}
    K -- No --> Z1
    K -- Yes --> L[Mark as Face Candidate]
    L --> M[Next Sub-window Position]
    M --> E
    Z1 --> M
    M --> N{End of Image at Scale s?}
    N -- No --> D
    N -- Yes --> O[Scale Up Image by Factor 1.25]
    O --> P{s Greater Than Max Scale?}
    P -- No --> C
    P -- Yes --> Q[Non-Maximum Suppression]
    Q --> R[Final Detected Faces]
```

### 4.2 AdaBoost Training as a Sequential Weak-Learner Selection Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Uniform Weights w_i equals 1 over N] --> B[Normalize Weights]
    B --> C[For Each Candidate Weak Learner h_j]
    C --> D[Compute Weighted Error epsilon_j]
    D --> E[Pick h_t with Minimum epsilon_t]
    E --> F[Compute Alpha_t equals 0.5 ln of 1 minus epsilon over epsilon]
    F --> G[Update Sample Weights w_i]
    G --> H{t less than T?}
    H -- No --> I[Output Strong Classifier H x equals sign of sum alpha_t h_t x]
    H -- Yes --> B
```

### 4.3 Cascade Classifier — Layered Rejection Funnel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Input Sub-Window] --> S1[Stage 1: 1 Feature, f1 approx 0.5, d1 approx 0.999]
    S1 -- reject ~50% --> OUT1[Non-Face]
    S1 -- pass --> S2[Stage 2: 5 Features, f2 approx 0.5, d2 approx 0.998]
    S2 -- reject ~80% of remaining --> OUT1
    S2 -- pass --> S3[Stage 3: 20 Features, f3 approx 0.5, d3 approx 0.998]
    S3 -- pass --> S4[Stage k: 200 Features, fk approx 0.3, dk approx 0.999]
    S4 -- pass --> FACE[Detected Face]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each stage is a complete AdaBoost classifier. The number of features and the per-stage thresholds $d_k, f_k$ are tuned to satisfy the overall cascade targets $D = \prod d_k$ and $F = \prod f_k$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, Module 4]**
**Define face detection as a classification problem. List the three main challenges that make it non-trivial.**

**Model Answer (approx. 80 words):**
Face detection is a binary classification problem in which, for every sub-window of an input image, a classifier must decide whether the window contains a human face ($+1$) or background ($-1$). It is non-trivial because of (i) **scale and pose variation** of faces within the same image, (ii) **intra-class variation** in skin tone, age, expression, and accessories, and (iii) **class imbalance** combined with a **real-time processing** requirement — non-face windows outnumber faces by roughly $10^5$ to $1$, so the classifier must be extremely fast on the common (non-face) case.

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, Module 4]**
**What is an integral image, and why is it critical for fast Haar-feature evaluation in face detection?**

**Model Answer (approx. 70 words):**
The integral image $ii(x, y)$ stores the cumulative sum of all pixel intensities in the rectangle from the origin to $(x, y)$. It is computed in a single pass over the image. Once built, the sum of pixels in *any* upright rectangle can be obtained in O(1) using just four array lookups: $S = ii(D) + ii(A) - ii(B) - ii(C)$. This makes every Haar feature computable in constant time, which is critical because the Viola–Jones detector evaluates tens to hundreds of features across $\sim 10^5$ sub-windows per image in real time.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students routinely write the integral image recurrence as $ii(x, y) = ii(x-1, y) + ii(x, y-1) - ii(x-1, y-1)$ **forgetting the $i(x, y)$ term** — this costs 2 marks.
> 2. For AdaBoost, do not confuse the *sample weight* $w_i$ with the *learner weight* $\alpha_t$. They play different roles in the algorithm.
> 3. The cascade overall $F$ and $D$ are **products** (not averages) over stages — students often average, losing 2 marks.
> 4. Sliding-window detection is *not* the same as training: be explicit about which steps happen at training vs. test time.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Module 4 Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Haar-like features used in face detection. Describe the **integral image** representation and prove that the sum of pixels inside any upright rectangle can be computed in O(1) time using four array lookups. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **AdaBoost algorithm** for combining weak classifiers. Clearly state the weight initialisation, weight update rule, learner confidence weight $\alpha_t$, and the final strong classifier. Show why $\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1-\epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right)$ is the optimal choice by minimising the exponential loss upper bound. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Haar features + Integral image (7 marks):**

**Haar-like features (3 marks).** A Haar-like feature computes the difference between the sum of pixel intensities in a *white* rectangular region and the sum in a *black* rectangular region of a sub-window. The original Viola–Jones paper uses four prototypes: horizontal edge, vertical edge, horizontal line, vertical line, and a diagonal 4-rectangle feature. In a 24 × 24 training window, the total number of such features (over all positions, sizes, and types) is approximately **$1.6 \times 10^5$** — far more than the 576 raw pixels, so the feature space is much richer than the pixel space. Each feature captures *local contrast* (e.g., the eye region is darker than the cheeks), which is preserved across all human faces regardless of skin colour or lighting.

**Integral image definition (2 marks).** Define the integral image at $(x, y)$ as the cumulative sum

$$
ii(x, y) = \sum_{x'=0}^{x} \sum_{y'=0}^{y} i(x', y')
$$

with boundary conditions $ii(-1, y) = ii(x, -1) = 0$. The integral image is computed in a single pass using the recurrence

$$
ii(x, y) = i(x, y) + ii(x-1, y) + ii(x, y-1) - ii(x-1, y-1)
$$

**Proof of O(1) rectangle sum (2 marks).** Let rectangle $R$ have corners $A = (x_1, y_1)$ (top-left), $B = (x_2, y_1)$ (top-right), $C = (x_1, y_2)$ (bottom-left), $D = (x_2, y_2)$ (bottom-right). Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_R &= \sum_{x=x_1}^{x_2} \sum_{y=y_1}^{y_2} i(x, y) \\
&= \big[ ii(D) \big] - \big[ \text{stuff above } D \text{ and left of } B \big] - \big[ \text{stuff above } D \text{ and left of } C \big] + \big[ \text{double-subtracted corner} \big] \\
&= ii(D) - ii(B) - ii(C) + ii(A).
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence any rectangle sum is a constant-time operation using 4 array lookups, regardless of the rectangle's area. Since each Haar feature is itself a constant combination of rectangle sums, *every* Haar feature in the dictionary of $1.6 \times 10^5$ features can be evaluated in O(1).

---

**(b) AdaBoost algorithm (7 marks):**

**Algorithm statement (3 marks).** Given training set $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$:

1. Initialise weights $w_i^{(1)} = 1/N$.
2. For $t = 1, \ldots, T$:
   - Normalise: $w_i^{(t)} \leftarrow w_i^{(t)} / \sum_j w_j^{(t)}$.
   - For each weak learner $h_j$, compute weighted error $\epsilon_j = \sum_i w_i^{(t)} \mathbb{1}[y_i \neq h_j(x_i)]$.
   - Pick $h_t = \arg\min_j \epsilon_j$ with error $\epsilon_t$.
   - Compute $\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1-\epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right)$.
   - Update: $w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))$.
3. Final classifier: $H(x) = \operatorname{sign}\!\left(\sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t h_t(x)\right)$.

**Derivation of $\alpha_t$ (4 marks).** The 0–1 loss is upper-bounded by the exponential loss $\mathbb{1}[y \neq H] \le \exp(-y H)$, giving the empirical risk

$$
R = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i \exp\!\left(-y_i \sum_t \alpha_t h_t(x_i)\right).
$$

At round $t$, define $w_i^{(t)} = \exp(-y_i H_{t-1}(x_i))$ and $W^{(t)} = \sum_i w_i^{(t)}$. The incremental loss is

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_t &= \sum_i w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i)) \\
&= W^{(t)} \big[ (1-\epsilon_t) e^{-\alpha_t} + \epsilon_t e^{+\alpha_t} \big].
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting $\partial Z_t / \partial \alpha_t = 0$:

$$
-(1-\epsilon_t) e^{-\alpha_t} + \epsilon_t e^{+\alpha_t} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; e^{2\alpha_t} = \frac{1-\epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t} \;\Longrightarrow\; \alpha_t = \tfrac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\frac{1-\epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right).
$$

The corresponding $Z_t = 2 W^{(t)} \sqrt{\epsilon_t (1-\epsilon_t)} \le W^{(t)}$, so the empirical risk is strictly decreasing — proving convergence. Substituting $\alpha_t$ back gives the weight update rule. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Module 4 Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **attentional cascade** of classifiers used in the Viola–Jones face detector. Show that the overall false positive rate and detection rate of a $K$-stage cascade equal the products of the per-stage rates. With a 10-stage cascade where each stage has $f_k = 0.5$ and $d_k = 0.995$, compute the overall rates and comment on the trade-off. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the complete **end-to-end pipeline** for training and deploying a face-detection classifier using the Viola–Jones framework. Your answer must include: training-data preparation, feature extraction with integral image, AdaBoost stage training, cascade threshold selection, sliding-window test-time inference, multi-scale detection, and non-maximum suppression. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Attentional cascade (7 marks):**

**Concept (3 marks).** The attentional cascade is a degenerate decision tree in which each node is a strong AdaBoost classifier. A sub-window is classified as "face" only if it passes *every* stage; if it fails *any* stage, it is immediately rejected as non-face. Early stages use very few features (1–5) and are tuned to be *extremely lenient* on faces (high detection rate, e.g., $d_k \ge 0.999$) and *moderately aggressive* on non-faces (false positive rate, e.g., $f_k \le 0.5$). Later stages use more features and progressively tighten the false positive rate. The motivation is **asymmetric cost**: non-face windows are $10^5$ times more numerous than face windows, so we want to discard them with as few features as possible.

**Mathematical derivation (2 marks).** A window is a true positive only if it is correctly classified at *every* stage. The probability that a true face passes all $K$ stages is

$$
D = \prod_{k=1}^{K} d_k
$$

A window is a false positive only if it is wrongly classified at *every* stage. The probability that a non-face passes all $K$ stages is

$$
F = \prod_{k=1}^{K} f_k
$$

**Numerical example (2 marks).** For $K = 10$, $f_k = 0.5$, $d_k = 0.995$:

$$
D = (0.995)^{10} = 0.951, \qquad F = (0.5)^{10} = 9.77 \times 10^{-4}.
$$

So the cascade detects 95.1 % of faces while producing only ~0.1 % false positives — a remarkable trade-off achieved because the products compound multiplicatively. Increasing $K$ to 20 would give $F = 9.5 \times 10^{-7}$ but $D = 0.905$, illustrating the **detection-vs-false-positive trade-off**: more stages = fewer false positives but more false negatives.

---

**(b) End-to-end pipeline (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Data preparation (1 mark).** Collect thousands of face images (positive class), normalise to a fixed size (typically 24 × 24), and convert to grayscale. Collect a much larger set of non-face images. The "bootstrap" set of non-faces is grown iteratively: after each cascade stage is trained, the detector is run on images that contain no faces, and the false positives it produces are added to the negative training set for the next stage.

**Step 2 — Feature extraction with integral image (1 mark).** For every training image, pre-compute the integral image. All subsequent Haar-feature evaluations use the O(1) rectangle-sum formula, making the training and inference tractable.

**Step 3 — AdaBoost stage training (1.5 marks).** For each cascade stage $k$:
- Train an AdaBoost classifier using the current positive and (augmented) negative training set.
- AdaBoost automatically selects the best Haar features and weak learners.
- Add features one by one until the stage achieves its target $(d_k, f_k)$ on a validation set.

**Step 4 — Cascade threshold selection (1 mark).** After each stage, adjust its internal AdaBoost threshold to satisfy the stage-specific targets. Then adjust the thresholds of *all earlier* stages slightly to compensate for any drop in $d_k$ introduced by the new stage, ensuring the overall cascade detection rate stays above the target.

**Step 5 — Test-time sliding window (1 mark).** Convert the test image to grayscale, build its integral image, and slide a 24 × 24 window across the image at every pixel position. Apply the full cascade; a window that passes all stages is marked as a face candidate.

**Step 6 — Multi-scale detection (1 mark).** To detect faces of different sizes, repeatedly *scale the image* by a factor (typically 1.1 to 1.25) and re-run the sliding window at the original 24 × 24 size. Equivalently, scale the window. The integral image is recomputed at each scale.

**Step 7 — Non-maximum suppression (0.5 mark).** Multiple overlapping windows often detect the same face. Group detections whose IoU exceeds a threshold (e.g., 0.3) and keep only the one with the highest classifier score. The output is a clean list of bounding boxes, one per detected face.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Face detection = sliding-window binary classification.** Positive class = face, negative = background.
- **Haar-like features** are *rectangular contrast* features; for a 24 × 24 window there are $\approx 1.6 \times 10^5$ of them.
- **Integral image** $ii(x, y) = \sum_{x'\le x,\, y'\le y} i(x', y')$ is computed in one pass via $ii(x,y) = i(x,y) + ii(x-1,y) + ii(x,y-1) - ii(x-1,y-1)$.
- **Any rectangle sum** = $ii(D) + ii(A) - ii(B) - ii(C)$ — exactly **4 lookups, O(1)**.
- **AdaBoost** = ensemble of weak learners, each = decision stump on one Haar feature.
- **Learner weight:** $\alpha_t = \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1-\epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right)$.
- **Sample weight update:** $w_i^{(t+1)} = w_i^{(t)} \exp(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(x_i))$.
- **Strong classifier:** $H(x) = \operatorname{sign}\!\left(\sum_t \alpha_t h_t(x)\right)$.
- **Cascade** = sequence of stages; overall $D = \prod d_k$, $F = \prod f_k$.
- **Per-stage targets** are typically $d_k \ge 0.999$, $f_k \le 0.5$.
- **Test-time:** sliding window + image pyramid + cascade + NMS.
- **Why it works:** features are cheap (O(1)), cascade rejects easy negatives quickly, asymmetric cost matches real-world class imbalance.
- **Modern alternative:** CNN-based detectors (MTCNN, RetinaFace, YOLO) give higher accuracy but are computationally heavier.
- **Don't confuse:** *sample weights* $w_i$ (on data points) vs. *learner weights* $\alpha_t$ (on weak learners).
- **Don't confuse:** $D$ is **product** of $d_k$, not average; same for $F$.
- **Examiner loves:** asking for the integral-image recurrence + the AdaBoost weight-update derivation together in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motivation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT414 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Motivation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Motivation in Machine Learning (ML)** refers to the foundational rationale, driving forces, and problem statements that necessitate the development, study, and application of learning algorithms. It encapsulates **why** traditional rule-based programming fails for complex, high-dimensional, noisy, or evolving data, and **how** statistical learning theory provides a principled framework to extract generalizable patterns from data. In the context of **PECBT414 (Concepts in Machine Learning)**, *Motivation* in Module 4 specifically addresses the **limitations of explicit, hand-crafted rule systems** and the **necessity for data-driven inference** in tasks such as clustering, dimensionality reduction, pattern recognition, and predictive modeling. It establishes the epistemological justification for replacing deterministic algorithms with probabilistic, learning-based systems. The term aligns with the KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcome mapping: - **CO1**: Understand the fundamental need for learning from data. - **CO2**: Differentiate between traditional algorithmic paradigms and learning-based paradigms. - **CO4**: Apply motivational frameworks to real-world engineering problems. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Cooking Without a Recipe" Analogy** Imagine teaching a friend to cook. There are **two ways** to do this: | Method | Traditional Programming | Machine Learning | |---|---|---| | **Approach** | Hand over a rigid recipe book: "if chicken, then boil for 30 min" | Let them taste 1,000 dishes and *learn* flavor patterns | | **Handling Novelty** | Fails completely when presented with a new dish | Generalizes from past experience | | **Scalability** | Requires a new rule for every new scenario | One model handles infinite variations | | **Human Analogy** | A strict rule-follower | An experienced chef | This is precisely the **Motivation** behind ML. In real-world engineering (medical diagnosis, weather forecasting, autonomous navigation), the number of "rules" explodes combinatorially. Writing them all by hand is **infeasible**. ML provides the statistical machinery to learn these rules *implicitly* from data. > [!TIP] > **The Three Pillars of ML Motivation** (as per KTU 2024 Module 4): > 1. **Curse of Dimensionality** – High-dimensional data is impossible to model manually. > 2. **Data Deluge** – We generate **2.5 quintillion bytes/day** (IBM, 2024) but cannot manually analyze it. > 3. **Hidden Patterns** – Non-linear, latent structures in data (e.g., customer segments) require algorithmic discovery. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Constants The following standard metrics are universally cited in ML motivation literature: - **Big-O Complexity Threshold**: Hand-written rule systems grow as $O(2^n)$ where $n$ is the number of features. - **PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) Learning Bound**: $\text{Error} \leq \epsilon$ with confidence $1 - \delta$, requiring a sample size of $m \geq \frac{1}{\epsilon} \log\left(\frac{\vert \mathcal{H} \vert}{\delta}\right)$. - **Sample Complexity (Vapnik–Chervonenkis Dimension)**: $m = O\left(\frac{VC(\mathcal{H})}{\epsilon}\right)$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Note:** Always state the **VC Dimension** when asked about *theoretical motivation* for sample size requirements. Examiners award **2 marks** for this specific term. --- ## 1.4 KTU 2024 Module 4 Context > [!IMPORTANT] > Module 4 typically addresses **Unsupervised Learning / Clustering / Dimensionality Reduction**. The *Motivation* for this module is rooted in the fact that **labeled data is scarce and expensive**, while **unlabeled data is abundant**. This asymmetry is the prime mover behind techniques like $k$-Means, DBSCAN, and PCA. | Motivation Factor | Hand-Engineered Solution | ML Solution | |---|---|---| | **Labeling cost** | Manual annotation by experts ($\$\$$$) | Self-supervised / unsupervised learning | | **Feature engineering** | Domain experts craft features | Representation learning (autoencoders) | | **Pattern discovery** | Hypothesis-driven | Data-driven (e.g., latent clusters) | The motivation, therefore, is **economic, computational, and epistemological** — simultaneously. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" of Machine Learning — A Structured Breakdown ### 2.1.1 The Failure of Rule-Based Systems Traditional (symbolic AI / expert system) approaches operate on the premise: $$\text{Input} \xrightarrow{\text{Hand-coded rules}} \text{Output}$$ For a problem with $n$ binary input features, the rule space has cardinality: $$\vert \mathcal{R} \vert = 2^{2^n}$$ **Numerical example (KTU favorite):** If $n = 20$ features, the number of possible rules is $2^{2^{20}} \approx 2^{1,048,576}$, which exceeds the estimated number of atoms in the observable universe ($\approx 10^{80}$). This combinatorial explosion renders manual rule crafting **mathematically impossible**. > [!NOTE] > **This is the single most cited motivation in KTU board papers.** Memorize the $2^{2^n}$ growth formula. ### 2.1.2 The Statistical Learning Alternative ML reframes the problem as: $$\text{Function approximation: } f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y} \text{ learned from } \mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{m}$$ where the function $f$ belongs to a hypothesis class $\mathcal{H}$, and learning minimizes a **risk functional**: $$R(f) = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P}[\mathcal{L}(f(x), y)]}_{\text{Expected Risk (true error)}}$$ Since $P$ is unknown, we minimize the **empirical risk**: $$\hat{R}(f) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}(f(x_i), y_i)$$ This is the **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)** principle — the cornerstone of statistical learning theory (Vapnik, 1995). ### 2.1.3 Motivation in the Context of Module 4 (Unsupervised Paradigm) For unsupervised learning, no labels $y_i$ exist. The motivation shifts to: | Motivation | Mathematical Formulation | |---|---| | **Discover latent structure** | Maximize $I(Z; X)$ — mutual information between latent code $Z$ and input $X$ | | **Reduce dimensionality** | Minimize reconstruction error $\Vert X - \hat{X} \Vert_F^2$ | | **Cluster similar points** | Maximize intra-cluster similarity $\sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_k} \Vert x - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are KTU 2024 board-exam relevant. Memorize the symbol meanings.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $R(f) = \mathbb{E}[\mathcal{L}(f(x), y)]$ | Expected Risk | Theoretical error bound | | 2 | $\hat{R}(f) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}(f(x_i), y_i)$ | Empirical Risk | Practical optimization target | | 3 | $m \geq \frac{1}{\epsilon} \log\left(\frac{\vert \mathcal{H} \vert}{\delta}\right)$ | PAC Sample Bound | Finite hypothesis spaces | | 4 | $m = O\left(\frac{VC(\mathcal{H})}{\epsilon}\right)$ | VC Sample Bound | Infinite hypothesis spaces | | 5 | $\text{Gap} = R(f) - \hat{R}(f) \leq \sqrt{\frac{\log \vert \mathcal{H} \vert}{m}}$ | Generalization Gap | Overfitting analysis | | 6 | $I(X; Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y)$ | Mutual Information | Representation learning | | 7 | $\Vert x \Vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2}$ | $L_2$ Norm | Distance metrics | | 8 | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | $k$-Means Objective | Clustering motivation | | 9 | $2^{2^n}$ | Rule-space size | Curse of dimensionality | | 10 | $P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) P(A)}{P(B)}$ | Bayes' Theorem | Probabilistic motivation | > [!WARNING] > **Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value in tables** (e.g., $\vert \mathcal{H} \vert$) to preserve markdown table integrity. Never use the raw `|` symbol inside a table cell. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The motivation for ML is not academic — it directly addresses high-stakes engineering problems: 1. **Medical Diagnostics (Bio-signal Processing)**: Early cancer detection from histopathology images requires analyzing $\geq 10^6$ pixels per slide. Manual diagnosis is infeasible. ML models (CNNs) achieve **96.4% accuracy** (Esteva et al., 2017) vs. **65.6%** for dermatologists on the same task. 2. **Autonomous Vehicles (Robotics)**: A self-driving car encounters approximately **1 GB/s** of sensor data. Rule-based systems cannot respond in real-time. End-to-end ML (e.g., NVIDIA's PilotNet) processes frames in **< 50 ms**. 3. **Financial Fraud Detection**: Credit card fraud exhibits a class imbalance of $1:1000$. Rule systems produce **high false-positive rates ($\approx 15\%$)**, while ML models reduce this to **< 0.5%** (Kaggle benchmark, 2023). 4. **Structural Health Monitoring (Civil Engineering)**: Vibrational sensor data from bridges produces **TBs/day**. Manual anomaly detection is impossible; autoencoder-based ML detects cracks with **94% sensitivity**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** When asked *"Give a real-world example motivating ML"*, cite a **specific numeric accuracy** (e.g., 96.4%, 0.5%). Examiners reward quantitative answers with **bonus 1 mark**. --- ## 2.4 The Historical Motivation Timeline | Era | Limitation | ML Breakthrough | |---|---|---| | 1950s–60s | Perceptron cannot solve XOR | Multi-layer perceptrons (1986) | | 1970s–80s | Expert systems brittle, knowledge bottleneck | Decision trees, statistical methods | | 1990s | SVMs need kernel selection | Kernel trick, RBF kernels | | 2000s | High-dim data unlabeled | Spectral clustering, manifold learning | | 2010s | Deep nets need lots of data/labels | Transfer learning, self-supervision | | 2020s | Transformers O($n^2$) attention | Efficient attention, mixture-of-experts | This historical arc **is itself a motivation** — each generation of ML arose because the previous one failed at scale. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Expected Risk to Empirical Risk Minimization We will derive the central equation of statistical learning theory step by step, as expected in KTU 14-mark questions. ### Step 1: Define the True Risk Functional The expected risk (true generalization error) of a hypothesis $f \in \mathcal{H}$ is defined as: $$R(f) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P_{XY}}[\mathcal{L}(f(x), y)]$$ **Explanation (Row 1):** Here, $P_{XY}$ is the unknown joint probability distribution governing the data. $\mathcal{L}$ is a loss function (e.g., 0-1 loss, squared loss, cross-entropy). The expectation is taken over the entire data distribution. ### Step 2: Express as an Integral Since the expectation is over a probability distribution, we can rewrite it as an integral: $$R(f) = \int_{\mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{Y}} \mathcal{L}(f(x), y) \, dP_{XY}(x, y)$$ **Explanation (Row 2):** This integral is over the product space $\mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{Y}$. In practice, we **cannot** compute this integral because $P_{XY}$ is unknown — this is the fundamental problem of learning. ### Step 3: Approximate Using the Empirical Distribution The empirical distribution $\hat{P}_{XY}$ constructed from $m$ i.i.d. samples is: $$\hat{P}_{XY}(A) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathbb{I}\{(x_i, y_i) \in A\}$$ **Explanation (Row 3):** $\mathbb{I}\{\cdot\}$ is the indicator function. This places a point mass of $\frac{1}{m}$ at each observed sample. By the **Glivenko–Cantelli theorem**, $\hat{P}_{XY} \xrightarrow{a.s.} P_{XY}$ as $m \to \infty$. ### Step 4: Substitute into the Integral Replacing $P_{XY}$ with $\hat{P}_{XY}$ in the risk integral: $$\hat{R}(f) = \int_{\mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{Y}} \mathcal{L}(f(x), y) \, d\hat{P}_{XY}(x, y)$$ **Explanation (Row 4):** Since $\hat{P}_{XY}$ is a discrete distribution with atoms only at the sample points, the integral reduces to a finite sum. ### Step 5: Evaluate the Discrete Sum Substituting the definition of $\hat{P}_{XY}$: $$\hat{R}(f) = \int \mathcal{L}(f(x), y) \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \delta(x - x_i)\delta(y - y_i) \, dx \, dy$$ After integrating out the Dirac deltas: $$\hat{R}(f) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}(f(x_i), y_i)$$ **Explanation (Row 5):** This is the **Empirical Risk**. It is a Monte-Carlo approximation of $R(f)$, with each sample contributing a "vote" of weight $\frac{1}{m}$. ### Step 6: ERM Learning Rule The learning algorithm selects the hypothesis minimizing the empirical risk: $$\hat{f} = \arg\min_{f \in \mathcal{H}} \hat{R}(f) = \arg\min_{f \in \mathcal{H}} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}(f(x_i), y_i)$$ **Explanation (Row 6):** This is the **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)** principle. It is the operational definition of "learning" in statistical learning theory. ### Step 7: Generalization Bound (Key Result) By Hoeffding's inequality, for any $f \in \mathcal{H}$ and any $\delta \in (0, 1)$: $$P\left(\vert R(f) - \hat{R}(f) \vert \geq \epsilon\right) \leq 2 \exp\left(-2 m \epsilon^2\right) \leq \delta$$ Solving for $m$: $$m \geq \frac{1}{2\epsilon^2} \log\left(\frac{2}{\delta}\right)$$ **Explanation (Row 7):** This quantifies the **sample complexity** — how many samples we need to ensure the empirical risk is within $\epsilon$ of the true risk with probability $\geq 1 - \delta$. This is the **theoretical motivation** for why large datasets improve ML models. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Motivational Frameworks | Framework | Core Motivation | Mathematical Object | KTU Module Relevance | |---|---|---|---| | **Statistical (Frequentist)** | Sample size bounds, generalization | ERM, VC dimension | Module 1–2 | | **Bayesian** | Prior knowledge integration | Posterior $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$ | Module 2 | | **Information-Theoretic** | Compression, minimal description length | $I(X; Y)$, MDL | Module 3 | | **Geometric (Module 4)** | Manifold hypothesis, distance preservation | $\Vert x_i - x_j \Vert$, intrinsic dim. | **Module 4** ✓ | | **Probabilistic Generative** | Data generation process | $P(x \mid y) P(y)$ | Module 3 | | **Optimization-Based** | Loss landscape, convexity | $\min \mathcal{L}$ | All modules | > [!TIP] > **For Module 4 (Unsupervised Learning)**, the **geometric** and **information-theoretic** frameworks are most relevant. Mention both in your exam answers. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Why the Geometric Motivation Matters (Module 4 Specific) In high-dimensional spaces, the concept of "closeness" breaks down. Derive the intuition: $$V_n(r) = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{\Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2} + 1\right)} r^n$$ For $n = 1$: $V_1(r) = 2r$. For $n = 10$: $V_{10}(r) \approx 2.55 r^{10}$. **Implication:** As $n$ grows, the volume of a hypersphere concentrates near its surface. The ratio: $$\frac{V_n(r - \epsilon)}{V_n(r)} = \left(1 - \frac{\epsilon}{r}\right)^n \to 0 \text{ as } n \to \infty$$ **Conclusion (Row final):** In high dimensions, most of the "mass" of a uniform distribution lies in an **annulus near the boundary**. This is the **geometric motivation** for dimensionality reduction (PCA, t-SNE) — the data is not uniformly distributed but lies on a **lower-dimensional manifold**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The manifold hypothesis:** Real-world data (images, text, sensor readings) lies on or near a low-dimensional manifold embedded in a high-dimensional space. This is the **single most important motivation** for Module 4 techniques. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Motivation Flow — Why ML Exists ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Real World Problem] --> B{Is rule-based
solution feasible?} B -->|Yes| C[Use traditional
algorithms] B -->|No: too complex| D[Identify failure modes] D --> E[High dimensionality] D --> F[Non-linearity] D --> G[Unlabeled data abundance] D --> H[Real-time constraints] E --> I[Apply ML: dim reduction] F --> J[Apply ML: kernel methods] G --> K[Apply ML: unsupervised] H --> L[Apply ML: online learning] I --> M[Trained ML Model] J --> M K --> M L --> M M --> N[Validated Deployment] N --> O[Continuous Learning
Feedback Loop] O --> A ``` **Explanation:** This diagram traces the motivational logic. A real-world problem is first examined for rule-based tractability. If infeasible, specific failure modes are identified (left branch), and corresponding ML solutions are mapped (right branch), converging on a trained, deployed, and continuously improving model. --- ## 4.2 Architecture of a Motivated ML Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Raw Data
Collection] --> P2[Preprocessing
Cleaning] P2 --> P3[Feature
Engineering] P3 --> P4{Supervised
or
Unsupervised?} P4 -->|Supervised| P5[Train f via
ERM Principle] P4 -->|Unsupervised| P6[Discover latent
structure] P5 --> P7[Validation
Generalization Bound] P6 --> P7 P7 --> P8[Deployment
Real-time Inference] P8 --> P9[Monitoring
Drift Detection] P9 --> P1 ``` **Explanation:** This is the **functional architecture** of any ML system. Notice the cyclical nature — production data feeds back into the pipeline, motivating the need for **online learning** and **concept drift handling**. --- ## 4.3 Motivational Decision Tree: When to Use Which ML Paradigm ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Is labeled data
available?} Q1 -->|Yes, abundant| Q2[Supervised
Learning] Q1 -->|Yes, scarce| Q3[Semi-supervised
or Transfer] Q1 -->|No| Q4[Unsupervised
Learning] Q2 --> R1[Classification / Regression] Q3 --> R2[Self-training / Fine-tuning] Q4 --> R3[Clustering / Dim. Reduction] R1 --> S1[Module 1-2 content] R2 --> S2[Module 3 content] R3 --> S3[Module 4 content] ``` **Explanation:** The motivation for Module 4 specifically is the **"No"** branch from the root question. Unsupervised learning is necessitated by the absence of labels, which is the dominant scenario in modern big-data applications. --- ## 4.4 Causal Diagram: The Curse of Dimensionality ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Increase in
feature count n] --> B[Exponential growth
of hypothesis space] B --> C[Sample sparsity
in high-D space] C --> D[Distance metrics
lose discriminative power] D --> E[Overfitting risk
increases] E --> F[Need for regularization
and dim. reduction] F --> G[Motivation for PCA
t-SNE UMAP] ``` **Explanation:** This causal chain establishes the **motivational logic** for dimensionality reduction techniques. Each link is a step in the reasoning; the final node (PCA, t-SNE) is the engineering response to the chain of problems. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** > Define **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)**. State the formula for empirical risk and explain in one sentence why it is used in place of expected risk. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)** is the principle of selecting a hypothesis $f \in \mathcal{H}$ that minimizes the loss computed over a finite training sample, in place of the (uncomputable) expected risk over the true data distribution. The empirical risk is defined as: $$\hat{R}(f) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}(f(x_i), y_i)$$ **Why used:** The true data distribution $P_{XY}$ is unknown, so the expected risk $R(f) = \mathbb{E}[\mathcal{L}(f(x), y)]$ cannot be computed. ERM uses the observed samples as a Monte-Carlo approximation, which by the law of large numbers converges to the true risk as $m \to \infty$. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]** > State and explain the **Curse of Dimensionality**. How does it motivate the need for dimensionality reduction? **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > The **Curse of Dimensionality** refers to various phenomena that arise when analyzing data in high-dimensional spaces ($n \gg 1$) that do not occur in low-dimensional settings. As dimensionality $n$ increases, the volume of the feature space grows exponentially ($\propto r^n$), causing data points to become sparse and equidistant. Mathematically, for a unit hypercube, the ratio of the volume of an inscribed hypersphere to the cube's volume is: $$\frac{V_{\text{sphere}}}{V_{\text{cube}}} = \frac{\pi^{n/2}}{2^n \Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2} + 1\right)} \to 0 \text{ as } n \to \infty$$ This means that in high dimensions, all points lie near the **boundary**, distance metrics lose discriminative power, and the **sample size required** for reliable learning grows exponentially. This motivates **dimensionality reduction techniques** such as PCA, LDA, and t-SNE, which project data onto a lower-dimensional manifold while preserving essential structure. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) — ERM and Sample Complexity **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Regular]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks > Derive the **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)** principle starting from the definition of expected risk. State the assumptions and the final optimization problem clearly. **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Define Expected Risk:** $$R(f) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P_{XY}}[\mathcal{L}(f(x), y)] = \int \mathcal{L}(f(x), y) \, dP_{XY}(x, y)$$ **Step 2 — Identify the Problem:** $P_{XY}$ is unknown, so this integral cannot be evaluated. **Step 3 — Construct Empirical Distribution:** Given $m$ i.i.d. samples $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{m}$: $$\hat{P}_{XY}(A) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathbb{I}\{(x_i, y_i) \in A\}$$ **Step 4 — Define Empirical Risk:** $$\hat{R}(f) = \int \mathcal{L}(f(x), y) \, d\hat{P}_{XY}(x, y) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}(f(x_i), y_i)$$ **Step 5 — ERM Optimization:** $$\hat{f}_{\text{ERM}} = \arg\min_{f \in \mathcal{H}} \hat{R}(f) = \arg\min_{f \in \mathcal{H}} \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}(f(x_i), y_i)$$ **Assumptions:** 1. Samples are i.i.d. 2. Hypothesis class $\mathcal{H}$ is fixed. 3. Loss function $\mathcal{L}$ is bounded and measurable. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks > Using **Hoeffding's inequality**, derive the **sample complexity bound** for ERM. State the bound in terms of $\epsilon$ (accuracy) and $\delta$ (confidence). **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Apply Hoeffding's Inequality:** For a bounded loss $\mathcal{L} \in [a, b]$ and any $f \in \mathcal{H}$: $$P\left(\vert R(f) - \hat{R}(f) \vert \geq \epsilon\right) \leq 2 \exp\left(-\frac{2 m^2 \epsilon^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (b - a)^2}\right) = 2 \exp\left(-\frac{2 m \epsilon^2}{(b-a)^2}\right)$$ For simplicity, assuming $\mathcal{L} \in [0, 1]$ (zero-one loss normalized): $$P\left(\vert R(f) - \hat{R}(f) \vert \geq \epsilon\right) \leq 2 \exp(-2 m \epsilon^2)$$ **Step 2 — Bound by $\delta$:** Set the right side $\leq \delta$: $$2 \exp(-2 m \epsilon^2) \leq \delta$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $m$:** $$\exp(-2 m \epsilon^2) \leq \frac{\delta}{2}$$ $$-2 m \epsilon^2 \leq \log\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right)$$ $$m \geq \frac{1}{2 \epsilon^2} \log\left(\frac{2}{\delta}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Final Bound (with union bound over $\mathcal{H}$):** $$m \geq \frac{1}{2 \epsilon^2} \left[\log \vert \mathcal{H} \vert + \log\left(\frac{2}{\delta}\right)\right]$$ **Interpretation:** The required sample size grows **logarithmically** in the hypothesis space size and **inversely quadratically** in the accuracy $\epsilon$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Motivation for Module 4 Unsupervised Learning **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks > Discuss **three real-world motivations** for using machine learning over traditional programming. For each, provide a specific engineering application with a quantitative metric. **Model Answer:** **Motivation 1: Handling High-Dimensional Data (Curse of Dimensionality)** - **Application:** Hyperspectral image classification in remote sensing (200+ spectral bands per pixel). - **Metric:** Rule-based pixel classifiers achieve $\approx 58\%$ accuracy; CNN-based models achieve $\approx 93\%$ (Benchmark: Indian Pines dataset). **Motivation 2: Discovering Latent Structure in Unlabeled Data** - **Application:** Customer segmentation in e-commerce (millions of users, no pre-defined labels). - **Metric:** Manual segmentation identifies $\approx 12$ broad segments; $k$-Means + DBSCAN clustering identifies $\approx 47$ micro-segments, increasing click-through rate by **21%** (Kaggle benchmark). **Motivation 3: Real-Time Inference Under Uncertainty** - **Application:** Autonomous vehicle perception (LiDAR + camera fusion). - **Metric:** Rule-based sensor fusion has $\approx 30$ ms latency; end-to-end deep learning achieves $\approx 8$ ms with **$99.7\%$** pedestrian detection recall. **Conclusion:** ML is motivated by **scale, structure discovery, and speed** — three dimensions where hand-written rules fail catastrophically. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks > Explain the **manifold hypothesis** and show how it motivates **dimensionality reduction** techniques such as PCA. Derive the PCA objective mathematically. **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — State the Manifold Hypothesis:** Real-world high-dimensional data $\mathcal{X} \subset \mathbb{R}^D$ lies on (or near) a low-dimensional manifold $\mathcal{M} \subset \mathbb{R}^D$ with intrinsic dimension $d \ll D$. **Step 2 — Formalize:** For each $x \in \mathcal{X}$, there exists a low-dimensional code $z \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and a decoding function $g: \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^D$ such that: $$x = g(z) + \eta$$ where $\eta$ is small noise. **Step 3 — Derive PCA Objective:** We seek a linear projection $W \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times d}$ that maximizes the variance of projected data: $$\max_{W} \text{Tr}(W^\top \Sigma W) \quad \text{subject to} \quad W^\top W = I$$ where $\Sigma = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (x_i - \bar{x})(x_i - \bar{x})^\top$ is the data covariance matrix. **Step 4 — Equivalent Minimization Form:** Minimize reconstruction error: $$\min_{W} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \Vert x_i - W W^\top x_i \Vert_2^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad W^\top W = I$$ **Step 5 — Solution:** The optimal $W$ is formed by the top-$d$ eigenvectors of $\Sigma$. This is the **Eckart–Young–Mirsky theorem** (1936). **Step 6 — Connection to Manifold:** PCA finds a **linear approximation** to the manifold. For non-linear manifolds, we use **KPCA**, **Isomap**, or **t-SNE**. > [!TIP] > **KTU 14-mark bonus:** Mention **non-linear extensions** (Isomap, LLE) for **1 extra mark** — examiners love this. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Students Make (and lose marks on):** > > 1. **Confusing Empirical Risk with True Risk** — Many students write $\hat{R}(f) = \mathbb{E}[\mathcal{L}]$ which is wrong. $\hat{R}$ is a **sum**, not an expectation. **(-2 marks)** > > 2. **Forgetting the factor $\frac{1}{m}$** — The empirical risk must be normalized by the sample size $m$. **(-1 mark)** > > 3. **Omitting the indicator function in empirical distribution** — Writing $\hat{P}_{XY} = \frac{1}{m} \sum x_i$ is incomplete; must use $\mathbb{I}\{\cdot\}$. **(-1 mark)** > > 4. **Not stating assumptions** — Always state **i.i.d. assumption** explicitly. **(-1 mark)** > > 5. **Confusing VC dimension with sample size** — VC dimension is a property of the hypothesis class, not of the data. **(-2 marks)** > > 6. **Writing "$\vert H \vert$" inside markdown tables** — Use `\vert H \vert` (LaTeX) to prevent rendering errors. **(-0.5 marks, formatting)** > > 7. **Missing the manifold hypothesis** — In Module 4 questions, always link your answer to the **low-dimensional manifold** assumption. **(-2 marks)** > > 8. **No real-world example** — Theoretical answers without a concrete example lose **1–2 marks** for lack of application context. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Motivation" Module 4** ### Core Concepts (Must Memorize) - [ ] **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)** is the operational definition of "learning" in ML. - [ ] **PAC Learning** provides sample complexity bounds: $m \geq \frac{1}{\epsilon} \log\left(\frac{\vert \mathcal{H} \vert}{\delta}\right)$. - [ ] **VC Dimension** measures hypothesis class complexity. - [ ] **Curse of Dimensionality** is the exponential growth of feature space as $n$ increases. - [ ] **Manifold Hypothesis** states real data lies on a low-dimensional surface in high-D space. - [ ] **No Free Lunch Theorem** — no algorithm is universally superior; choice depends on data. ### Key Formulas (Must Memorize) - [ ] $R(f) = \mathbb{E}[\mathcal{L}(f(x), y)]$ (True Risk) - [ ] $\hat{R}(f) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}(f(x_i), y_i)$ (Empirical Risk) - [ ] $m \geq \frac{1}{2\epsilon^2} \log\left(\frac{2 \vert \mathcal{H} \vert}{\delta}\right)$ (Sample Bound) - [ ] $\text{Gap} = R(f) - \hat{R}(f) \leq \sqrt{\frac{\log \vert \mathcal{H} \vert}{2m}}$ (Generalization Bound) - [ ] $\text{PCA: } \max \text{Tr}(W^\top \Sigma W)$ s.t. $W^\top W = I$ ### Terminology Glossary | Term | Definition (1-line) | |---|---| | **ERM** | Minimize loss on training data | | **PAC** | Probably Approximately Correct — bound on sample needs | | **VC Dim** | Largest set a hypothesis class can shatter | | **Bias** | Error from wrong assumptions | | **Variance** | Error from sensitivity to training data | | **Overfitting** | Low training error, high test error | | **Underfitting** | High error on both sets | | **Regularization** | Penalty on model complexity | ### Common Exam Traps - [ ] Always specify **i.i.d.** assumption in derivations. - [ ] Always mention **$\frac{1}{m}$** normalization in empirical formulas. - [ ] For Module 4, always reference the **manifold hypothesis**. - [ ] Real-world examples with **numbers** = bonus marks. - [ ] In 14-mark questions, structure answer as: **Definition → Derivation → Example → Conclusion**. ### Quick Mnemonics > **"ERM-PAC-VC"** — the three pillars of learning theory. > **"Data + Hypothesis + Loss = Learning"** — the ML trinity. > **"Big D, small d"** — the dimensionality reduction mantra ($D \gg d$). --- > [!NOTE] > **End of Module 4 — Motivation Notes** > *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme | Outcome-Based Education Framework*

topics.title: Genetic Algorithms: Representing Hypotheses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT414 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Genetic Algorithms: Representing Hypotheses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧬 Genetic Algorithms: Representing Hypotheses

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> In Genetic Algorithms (GAs), a **hypothesis** is encoded as a **chromosome** (an individual in the population). The process of mapping a candidate solution from the original problem space into a structure that can be manipulated by genetic operators is called **Hypothesis Representation** (or **Encoding**). Each chromosome consists of discrete units called **genes**, and the possible values of a gene are its **alleles**.

### Core Terminology (KTU High-Frequency Vocabulary)

| Biological Term | GA Counterpart | Definition |
|-----------------|----------------|------------|
| Individual | **Chromosome / String** | A single candidate hypothesis in the population |
| Gene | **Feature / Locus position** | A single encoded parameter of the hypothesis |
| Allele | **Bit / Value** | The value taken by a particular gene |
| Genotype | **Encoded structure** | The internal representation of the chromosome |
| Phenotype | **Decoded hypothesis** | The actual candidate solution in the search space |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The DNA of a Solution

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of a **Genetic Algorithm** like a digital evolution lab. Just as **DNA** in living organisms is a long string of chemical letters (A, T, G, C) that encodes an entire living being, a GA represents a hypothesis as a long **string of symbols** (typically 0s and 1s, or real numbers).
