> 2. **Confusing rank ordering direction** — Some textbooks rank best as 1, others as $N$. Always STATE your convention explicitly in the exam.
> 3. **Skipping the normalization check** — Always show $\sum P_i = 1$ verification; examiners reward this as a full-mark earner.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** — Tournament selection requires stating whether replacement/sampling is with or without replacement.
> 5. **Ignoring fitness scaling** in long answers — Examiners look for awareness of premature convergence as a problem.
> 6. **Sign errors** in linear scaling derivation — re-derive $a, b$ carefully.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Fitness function** is a *non-negative scalar evaluator* of chromosome quality; the design of $\mathcal{F}$ defines the *search landscape*.
- [ ] **Selection** is the *Darwinian reproduction operator* that allocates trials to individuals based on their fitness or rank.
- [ ] **Roulette Wheel Probability:** $P_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$; **Tournament Probability:** $P_i = \dfrac{\binom{N-i}{k-1}}{\binom{N}{k}}$; **Linear Rank Probability:** $P_i = \dfrac{1}{N}\left[\eta^{-} + (\eta^{+} - \eta^{-})\dfrac{i-1}{N-1}\right]$.
- [ ] **Selection Pressure** $SP = N \cdot P_{best}$ — controls convergence speed vs. diversity tradeoff.
- [ ] **Premature convergence** occurs when a single dominant fitness monopolizes selection; remedied via **rank selection, tournament selection, fitness scaling, or niching**.
- [ ] **Fitness Scaling** (Linear, Sigma, Power Law) dynamically adjusts pressure across generations.
- [ ] **Tournament Selection** is widely used in practice due to its parallelism and tunable $k$.
- [ ] **Stochastic Universal Sampling (SUS)** reduces selection variance compared to plain RWS.
- [ ] **Boltzmann Selection** mimics simulated annealing by annealing a temperature parameter $T$.
- [ ] For minimization problems, **transform cost → fitness** before any FPS-based selection.
- [ ] Always **verify normalization** $\sum P_i = 1$ in derivation problems.
- [ ] **Schema Theorem** (Holland, 1975) underlies the theoretical justification of fitness-proportional reproduction.
- [ ] Choice of selection + fitness scaling + variation operators together determines GA's effective search behavior.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: An Illustrative Example
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT414 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** An Illustrative Example

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Illustrative Example** in Machine Learning refers to a complete, end-to-end worked-out case study that demonstrates the application of theoretical concepts on a real (or simulated) dataset. It is a pedagogical instrument used to consolidate the abstract mathematical formulations of learning algorithms — such as **Entropy**, **Information Gain**, **Gini Index**, or **Bayes' Theorem** — into a tangible, traceable computational pipeline.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)** framework, this topic serves as the *integrative capstone* of Module 4. It binds together the building blocks of a learning system:
- **Problem formulation** (supervised vs. unsupervised framing)
- **Feature engineering & data representation**
- **Algorithm instantiation** (e.g., ID3 Decision Tree)
- **Decision boundary visualization**
- **Performance evaluation** (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> An illustrative example is *not* a programming exercise. It is a **mathematical trace** of how raw data flows through a learning model, gets transformed at each node, and produces a generalization. Examiners expect **explicit calculation of every numerical step** — not just the final answer.

## 1.2 The Reference Dataset — "Play Tennis"

To make the example concrete, we will use the canonical **"Play Tennis" (Outlook Dataset)** introduced by *Tom Mitchell (1997)*. It is a 14-tuple binary classification dataset with **4 features** and **1 target class**.

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | Play Tennis |
|-----|---------|-------------|----------|------|-------------|
| 1   | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Weak | No          |
| 2   | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Strong | No       |
| 3   | Overcast| Hot         | High     | Weak | Yes         |
| 4   | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Weak | Yes         |
| 5   | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Weak | Yes         |
| 6   | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Strong | No        |
| 7   | Overcast| Cool        | Normal   | Strong | Yes        |
| 8   | Sunny   | Mild        | High     | Weak | No          |
| 9   | Sunny   | Cool        | Normal   | Weak | Yes         |
| 10  | Rain    | Mild        | Normal   | Weak | Yes         |
| 11  | Sunny   | Mild        | Normal   | Strong | Yes       |
| 12  | Overcast| Mild        | High     | Strong | Yes       |
| 13  | Overcast| Hot         | Normal   | Weak | Yes         |
| 14  | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Strong | No        |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Doctor's Diagnosis"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):**
>
> Imagine a **doctor** trying to decide whether a patient has *Flu* or *Common Cold*. She looks at symptoms one by one — *fever?* *runny nose?* *body ache?* — and after each question, the set of possible diseases *shrinks*. Some questions are more *informative* (like "fever > 102°F") because they eliminate many possibilities at once, while others (like "is it a Monday?") barely help.
>
> A **Decision Tree** does exactly this: it picks the *most informative* feature at each step (the one with the highest **Information Gain**) to split the data, mimicking a doctor's diagnostic reasoning. The "Illustrative Example" is the doctor working through **14 real patients**, one symptom at a time, with **paper and calculator**, until she can confidently classify each case.

### 1.4 Core Metrics Defined

- **Entropy (H(S))** — *Bold* metric measuring the **impurity / disorder** in a dataset.
- **Information Gain (IG(S, A))** — *Bold* metric measuring the **reduction in entropy** achieved by splitting on attribute $A$.
- **Gini Impurity** — Alternative impurity measure (used in CART).
- **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score** — Evaluation metrics for the trained model.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Building a Decision Tree (ID3 Algorithm)

The **ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3)** algorithm by *Quinlan (1986)* builds a decision tree through a top-down, greedy search. The logic unfolds in **5 explicit steps**:

1. **Step 1 — Compute Target Entropy:** Calculate the entropy of the entire dataset $S$ with respect to the target class $C$.
2. **Step 2 — Evaluate Each Attribute:** For every feature $A$, compute the **weighted average entropy** after splitting $S$ on $A$.
3. **Step 3 — Compute Information Gain:** $\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$. The attribute with the **maximum IG** is selected as the splitting node.
4. **Step 4 — Recurse on Subsets:** The dataset is partitioned by the chosen attribute. Steps 1–3 are applied recursively on each subset.
5. **Step 5 — Termination:** Recursion halts when a node is **pure** (entropy = 0), **empty**, or when no attribute remains.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Greedy?** ID3 does not look ahead. It commits to the locally best split at each node. This is computationally cheap but **not guaranteed** to produce the globally optimal tree — a classic bias-variance trade-off examined frequently in KTU papers.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Information Gain

**Why does it work?** Information theory tells us that entropy is the *expected number of bits* needed to encode the class of a randomly drawn sample. A feature that **reduces entropy the most** is the one that makes the class label *most predictable*. In Bayesian terms, it is the feature whose value gives the **largest shift in posterior probability** $P(C \mid A)$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Notation / Notes** |
|:-----:|:-------------|:------------|:---------------------|
| 1 | **Entropy of Set $S$** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2(p_i)$ | $p_i$ = proportion of class $i$; $c$ = number of classes |
| 2 | **Entropy of Split on $A$** | $H(S \mid A) = \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | $S_v$ = subset where $A = v$ |
| 3 | **Information Gain** | $\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ | Higher = better attribute |
| 4 | **Gini Impurity** | $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$ | Used by CART (faster, no log) |
| 5 | **Split Info (for Gain Ratio)** | $\text{SI}(S, A) = -\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | Normalizes IG in C4.5 |
| 6 | **Gain Ratio** | $\text{GR}(S, A) = \dfrac{\text{IG}(S, A)}{\text{SI}(S, A)}$ | Penalizes high-cardinality features |
| 7 | **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Range: $[0, 1]$ |
| 8 | **Precision** | $\text{Prec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | "Of those predicted positive, how many are right?" |
| 9 | **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $\text{Rec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | "Of actual positives, how many did we catch?" |
| 10 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean of P & R |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Note:** In the entropy formula, if $p_i = 0$, define $0 \log_2 0 = 0$ (by convention). Forgetting this leads to **undefined expressions** — a common 1-mark deduction in KTU papers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Decision trees built via Information Gain are the **backbone of modern ensemble systems**:
- **Random Forests** (Bagging) — average of many deep trees.
- **XGBoost / Gradient Boosting** — sequential trees correcting predecessors' errors.
- **Medical Diagnosis Systems** — interpretable rules for clinicians.
- **Credit Risk Scoring** — banks use tree-based models for transparent lending decisions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Pre-Computation: Class Distribution

From the dataset of **14 days**:
- **Yes (Play Tennis)** = 9 days
- **No (Do Not Play)** = 5 days

## 3.2 Step 1 — Entropy of the Full Dataset $S$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -p_{\text{Yes}} \log_2 p_{\text{Yes}} - p_{\text{No}} \log_2 p_{\text{No}} \\[6pt]
&= -\frac{9}{14} \log_2 \left(\frac{9}{14}\right) - \frac{5}{14} \log_2 \left(\frac{5}{14}\right) \\[6pt]
&= -\frac{9}{14} \cdot (-0.6374) - \frac{5}{14} \cdot (-1.4854) \\[6pt]
&= 0.4098 + 0.5305 \\[6pt]
&= 0.9403 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We first convert fractions to base-2 logs: $\log_2(9/14) = \log_2(0.6429) = -0.6374$ and $\log_2(5/14) = \log_2(0.3571) = -1.4854$. Multiplying by their respective proportions and negating gives the entropy.

## 3.3 Step 2 — Compute Information Gain for **Outlook** (3 values: Sunny, Overcast, Rain)

### 3.3.1 Subset: Outlook = Sunny (Days 1, 2, 8, 9, 11) → 5 days

Class counts: **Yes = 2, No = 3**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) &= -\frac{2}{5} \log_2 \left(\frac{2}{5}\right) - \frac{3}{5} \log_2 \left(\frac{3}{5}\right) \\[6pt]
&= -0.4 \cdot (-1.3219) - 0.6 \cdot (-0.7370) \\[6pt]
&= 0.5288 + 0.4422 \\[6pt]
&= 0.9710 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.2 Subset: Outlook = Overcast (Days 3, 7, 12, 13) → 4 days

Class counts: **Yes = 4, No = 0** (PURE subset!).

$$
H(S_{\text{Overcast}}) = -\frac{4}{4} \log_2 \left(\frac{4}{4}\right) - 0 = 0 \text{ bits}
$$

### 3.3.3 Subset: Outlook = Rain (Days 4, 5, 6, 10, 14) → 5 days

Class counts: **Yes = 3, No = 2**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{\text{Rain}}) &= -\frac{3}{5} \log_2 \left(\frac{3}{5}\right) - \frac{2}{5} \log_2 \left(\frac{2}{5}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 0.4422 + 0.5288 \\[6pt]
&= 0.9710 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.4 Weighted Conditional Entropy & Information Gain for Outlook

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) &= \frac{5}{14} \cdot 0.9710 + \frac{4}{14} \cdot 0 + \frac{5}{14} \cdot 0.9710 \\[6pt]
&= 0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468 \\[6pt]
&= 0.6936 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{IG}(S, \text{Outlook}) = H(S) - H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) = 0.9403 - 0.6936 = 0.2467 \text{ bits}
$$

## 3.4 Step 3 — Information Gain for **Wind** (2 values: Weak, Strong)

### 3.4.1 Subset: Wind = Weak (Days 1, 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, 10, 13) → 8 days

Class counts: **Yes = 6, No = 2**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{\text{Weak}}) &= -\frac{6}{8} \log_2 \left(\frac{6}{8}\right) - \frac{2}{8} \log_2 \left(\frac{2}{8}\right) \\[6pt]
&= -0.75 \cdot (-0.4150) - 0.25 \cdot (-2) \\[6pt]
&= 0.3113 + 0.5 \\[6pt]
&= 0.8113 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.2 Subset: Wind = Strong (Days 2, 6, 7, 11, 12, 14) → 6 days

Class counts: **Yes = 3, No = 3**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{\text{Strong}}) &= -0.5 \log_2(0.5) - 0.5 \log_2(0.5) \\[6pt]
&= 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ bit}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.3 Weighted Entropy & IG for Wind

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Wind}) &= \frac{8}{14} \cdot 0.8113 + \frac{6}{14} \cdot 1.0 \\[6pt]
&= 0.4636 + 0.4286 \\[6pt]
&= 0.8922 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{IG}(S, \text{Wind}) = 0.9403 - 0.8922 = 0.0481 \text{ bits}
$$

## 3.5 Step 4 — Information Gain for **Humidity** (2 values: High, Normal)

- **Humidity = High** (Days 1, 2, 3, 4, 8, 12, 14) → 7 days; **Yes = 3, No = 4**.
- **Humidity = Normal** (Days 5, 6, 7, 9, 10, 11, 13) → 7 days; **Yes = 6, No = 1**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{\text{High}}) &= -\frac{3}{7} \log_2 \left(\frac{3}{7}\right) - \frac{4}{7} \log_2 \left(\frac{4}{7}\right) \\[6pt]
&= -0.4286 \cdot (-1.2224) - 0.5714 \cdot (-0.8074) \\[6pt]
&= 0.5240 + 0.4613 \\[6pt]
&= 0.9852 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{\text{Normal}}) &= -\frac{6}{7} \log_2 \left(\frac{6}{7}\right) - \frac{1}{7} \log_2 \left(\frac{1}{7}\right) \\[6pt]
&= -0.8571 \cdot (-0.2224) - 0.1429 \cdot (-2.8074) \\[6pt]
&= 0.1906 + 0.4011 \\[6pt]
&= 0.5917 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Humidity}) &= \frac{7}{14} \cdot 0.9852 + \frac{7}{14} \cdot 0.5917 \\[6pt]
&= 0.4926 + 0.2959 \\[6pt]
&= 0.7885 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{IG}(S, \text{Humidity}) = 0.9403 - 0.7885 = 0.1518 \text{ bits}
$$

## 3.6 Step 5 — Information Gain for **Temperature** (3 values: Hot, Mild, Cool)

- **Hot** (Days 1, 2, 3, 13) → 4 days; **Yes = 2, No = 2**.
- **Mild** (Days 4, 8, 10, 11, 12, 14) → 6 days; **Yes = 4, No = 2**.
- **Cool** (Days 5, 6, 7, 9) → 4 days; **Yes = 3, No = 1**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{\text{Hot}}) &= -0.5 \log_2(0.5) - 0.5 \log_2(0.5) = 1.0 \text{ bit} \\[6pt]
H(S_{\text{Mild}}) &= -\frac{4}{6} \log_2 \left(\frac{4}{6}\right) - \frac{2}{6} \log_2 \left(\frac{2}{6}\right) \\[6pt]
&= -0.6667 \cdot (-0.5850) - 0.3333 \cdot (-1.5850) \\[6pt]
&= 0.3900 + 0.5283 \\[6pt]
&= 0.9183 \text{ bits} \\[6pt]
H(S_{\text{Cool}}) &= -\frac{3}{4} \log_2 \left(\frac{3}{4}\right) - \frac{1}{4} \log_2 \left(\frac{1}{4}\right) \\[6pt]
&= -0.75 \cdot (-0.4150) - 0.25 \cdot (-2) \\[6pt]
&= 0.3113 + 0.5 \\[6pt]
&= 0.8113 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Temperature}) &= \frac{4}{14} \cdot 1.0 + \frac{6}{14} \cdot 0.9183 + \frac{4}{14} \cdot 0.8113 \\[6pt]
&= 0.2857 + 0.3935 + 0.2318 \\[6pt]
&= 0.9110 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{IG}(S, \text{Temperature}) = 0.9403 - 0.9110 = 0.0293 \text{ bits}
$$

## 3.7 The Decision Matrix & Root Node Selection

| **Attribute** | **Conditional Entropy $H(S \mid A)$** | **Information Gain $\text{IG}(S, A)$** | **Rank** |
|:--------------|:-------------------------------------:|:--------------------------------------:|:--------:|
| **Outlook**     | **0.6936** | **0.2467** | **🥇 1st** |
| Humidity      | 0.7885   | 0.1518   | 🥈 2nd     |
| Wind          | 0.8922   | 0.0481   | 🥉 3rd     |
| Temperature   | 0.9110   | 0.0293   | 4th        |

> **Root Node = Outlook** (highest Information Gain = **0.2467 bits**).

## 3.8 Recursive Splitting (Branch: Outlook = Sunny)

Re-applying the IG calculation on the 5 Sunny days (Yes=2, No=3):

$$
H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) = 0.9710 \text{ bits}
$$

| **Attribute on Sunny subset** | **$\text{IG}$** |
|:------------------------------|:----------------:|
| **Humidity**                  | **0.9710**      |
| Temperature                   | 0.5709          |
| Wind                          | 0.0199          |

- **Humidity split on Sunny:**
  - High → Days {1, 2, 8} → **No** (3/3 pure)
  - Normal → Days {9, 11} → **Yes** (2/2 pure)

**Outlook = Sunny → Humidity = High → No; Humidity = Normal → Yes** ✔️

The remaining branches (Overcast → Yes, Rain → splits on Wind) are similarly derived.

## 3.9 Final Decision Tree Structure

```
[Outlook]
   ├── Overcast  →  Yes ✔
   ├── Sunny
   │      ├── Humidity = High    →  No ✗
   │      └── Humidity = Normal  →  Yes ✔
   └── Rain
          ├── Wind = Weak    →  Yes ✔
          └── Wind = Strong  →  No ✗
```

## 3.10 Confusion Matrix & Evaluation

Applying the tree to the training set, the model achieves **perfect classification** on these 14 days (overfitting risk noted in KTU valuation):

|               | Predicted Yes | Predicted No |
|:-------------:|:-------------:|:------------:|
| **Actual Yes** | TP = 9        | FN = 0       |
| **Actual No**  | FP = 0        | TN = 5       |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{9+5}{14} = 1.000 \\[6pt]
\text{Precision} &= \frac{9}{9} = 1.000 \\[6pt]
\text{Recall} &= \frac{9}{9} = 1.000 \\[6pt]
F_1 &= 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Machine Learning Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset: 14 days, 4 features] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Class Distribution Analysis: 9 Yes / 5 No]
    C --> D[Compute H of S equals 0.9403 bits]
    D --> E[Iterate over 4 Attributes]
    E --> F1[Outlook: IG equals 0.2467]
    E --> F2[Humidity: IG equals 0.1518]
    E --> F3[Wind: IG equals 0.0481]
    E --> F4[Temperature: IG equals 0.0293]
    F1 --> G[Select Root: Outlook]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Partition S into 3 Subsets]
    H --> I1[Overcast subset: Pure Yes]
    H --> I2[Sunny subset: 5 days, recurse]
    H --> I3[Rain subset: 5 days, recurse]
    I2 --> J1[Split on Humidity]
    J1 --> K1[High equals No]
    J1 --> K2[Normal equals Yes]
    I3 --> J2[Split on Wind]
    J2 --> K3[Weak equals Yes]
    J2 --> K4[Strong equals No]
    I1 --> L[Final Decision Tree Built]
    K1 --> L
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
    L --> M[Generate Confusion Matrix]
    M --> N[Compute Accuracy, P, R, F1]
    N --> O[Model Evaluation Complete]
```

## 4.2 Information Gain Comparison Chart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Rank1[Rank 1 - Winner]
        D1[Outlook: 0.2467 bits]
    end
    subgraph Rank2[Rank 2]
        D2[Humidity: 0.1518 bits]
    end
    subgraph Rank3[Rank 3]
        D3[Wind: 0.0481 bits]
    end
    subgraph Rank4[Rank 4]
        D4[Temperature: 0.0293 bits]
    end
    Root[Root Selection Logic] --> D1
    Root --> D2
    Root --> D3
    Root --> D4
    D1 --> Winner[Selected as Root Node]
    D2 --> Runner[Standby Attribute]
    D3 --> Discard1[Discarded]
    D4 --> Discard2[Discarded]
```

## 4.3 Recursive Splitting Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Full Dataset S, 14 samples]) --> CalcRoot[Compute IG for all 4 attributes]
    CalcRoot --> Split{Outlook selected as Root}
    Split -->|Overcast| LeafA[Leaf: Yes, 4 samples, H=0]
    Split -->|Sunny| RecurseSunny[Recurse on 5 samples]
    Split -->|Rain| RecurseRain[Recurse on 5 samples]
    RecurseSunny --> SunCalc[Compute IG over Humidity, Wind, Temp]
    SunCalc --> SunSplit{Humidity selected}
    SunSplit -->|High| LeafB[Leaf: No, 3 samples, H=0]
    SunSplit -->|Normal| LeafC[Leaf: Yes, 2 samples, H=0]
    RecurseRain --> RainCalc[Compute IG over Humidity, Wind, Temp]
    RainCalc --> RainSplit{Wind selected}
    RainSplit -->|Weak| LeafD[Leaf: Yes, 3 samples, H=0]
    RainSplit -->|Strong| LeafE[Leaf: No, 3 samples, H=0]
    LeafA --> Done([Decision Tree Complete])
    LeafB --> Done
    LeafC --> Done
    LeafD --> Done
    LeafE --> Done
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**"Define Information Gain. Why is it preferred over raw entropy in attribute selection for decision tree induction?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Information Gain (IG)** is the reduction in entropy of the target class achieved by partitioning the dataset $S$ on attribute $A$. It is given by:

$$
\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)
$$

**Why preferred over raw entropy (2 marks):**
1. **Comparative power:** Entropy $H(S)$ is a *constant* for a dataset — it cannot rank attributes. IG, being a *difference*, directly quantifies *how much* an attribute reduces uncertainty.
2. **Greedy optimization:** ID3's split criterion $\arg\max_A \text{IG}(S, A)$ is a deterministic, locally optimal decision rule.
3. **Bias toward informative features:** A high-IG attribute produces purer child nodes, leading to shorter, more interpretable trees.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]
**"Why does a decision tree with zero training error not guarantee good generalization performance?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A tree that perfectly classifies all 14 training samples (as in our illustrative example) has **zero training error** but suffers from **overfitting (high variance)**. The reasons are:
1. The tree memorizes noise and idiosyncrasies specific to the 14 training days.
2. With only 14 samples and 4 features, the model has **insufficient evidence** to distinguish true patterns from random fluctuations.
3. On unseen data (e.g., a new day with different weather), the rigid leaf assignments will likely fail, yielding **low test accuracy**.

This is the classic **bias-variance trade-off**, and it motivates techniques like **pruning**, **cross-validation**, and **ensemble learning**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (Module Internal Choice):** Answer **ONE** of the two questions below.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected]**

**(a)** Given the "Play Tennis" dataset of 14 instances with 4 attributes (Outlook, Temperature, Humidity, Wind) and a binary target (Yes/No), compute the **Information Gain** for all four attributes and identify the **root node** of the decision tree. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the tree constructed in (a), classify a new instance:
**{Outlook = Sunny, Temperature = Cool, Humidity = High, Wind = Strong}**
Also compute the **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score** of the model on the training set. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) — Step-by-step IG calculation (7 marks):**

Following the derivations in **Section 3** above:
- $H(S) = 0.9403$ bits
- $\text{IG}(S, \text{Outlook}) = 0.2467$ bits ← **Root**
- $\text{IG}(S, \text{Humidity}) = 0.1518$ bits
- $\text{IG}(S, \text{Wind}) = 0.0481$ bits
- $\text{IG}(S, \text{Temperature}) = 0.0293$ bits

**Root Node = Outlook** (max IG). On the *Sunny* branch, recursing gives *Humidity* as the next split; on the *Rain* branch, *Wind* is the next split; *Overcast* is a pure leaf (Yes).

**(b) — Classification & Evaluation (7 marks):**

Trace the new instance through the tree:
1. Outlook = **Sunny** → go to *Sunny* branch
2. Humidity = **High** → leaf predicts **No**

**Prediction = "Do Not Play Tennis"** ✗

Confusion Matrix (training set):

| | Pred Yes | Pred No |
|---|:---:|:---:|
| **Actual Yes** | 9 (TP) | 0 (FN) |
| **Actual No** | 0 (FP) | 5 (TN) |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{9+5}{14} = 100\% \\[4pt]
\text{Precision} &= \frac{9}{9+0} = 100\% \\[4pt]
\text{Recall} &= \frac{9}{9+0} = 100\% \\[4pt]
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{1 \cdot 1}{1+1} = 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**

**(a)** For the "Play Tennis" dataset, compute the **Gini Impurity** for the *Outlook* attribute and compare it with the **Entropy-based** information gain. Which criterion would ID3 select as the root? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Suppose the same dataset is re-weighted with the following instance weights: $w_i = 2$ for the first 7 days and $w_i = 1$ for the remaining 7 days. Recompute the **weighted Information Gain for Humidity** and discuss how instance weighting changes attribute selection. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) — Gini vs Entropy (7 marks):**

Gini impurity is computed as $1 - \sum p_i^2$.

- $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - (9/14)^2 - (5/14)^2 = 1 - 0.4133 - 0.1276 = 0.4592$

- **Outlook = Sunny** (5 days; Yes=2, No=3):
$\text{Gini}(S_{\text{Sunny}}) = 1 - (2/5)^2 - (3/5)^2 = 1 - 0.16 - 0.36 = 0.48$

- **Outlook = Overcast** (4 days; Yes=4, No=0):
$\text{Gini}(S_{\text{Overcast}}) = 1 - 1 - 0 = 0$

- **Outlook = Rain** (5 days; Yes=3, No=2):
$\text{Gini}(S_{\text{Rain}}) = 1 - (3/5)^2 - (2/5)^2 = 0.48$

Weighted Gini:
$$
\text{Gini}(S \mid \text{Outlook}) = \frac{5}{14}(0.48) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.48) = 0.3429
$$

Gini reduction = $0.4592 - 0.3429 = 0.1163$.

**Comparison:** Both Entropy and Gini rank Outlook as the best root attribute. ID3 uses **Entropy/IG**; CART uses **Gini**. They differ in computation (Gini avoids logs) but produce **nearly identical splits** for binary classification.

**(b) — Weighted IG for Humidity (7 marks):**

Total weight = $7 \times 2 + 7 \times 1 = 21$.

Re-derive weighted class counts within Humidity subsets (assuming weights distributed with their days):
- **Humidity = High:** Original 7 days, all from first 7 days → weight = $7 \times 2 = 14$. Class: Yes (3) = weight 6, No (4) = weight 8.
- **Humidity = Normal:** 7 days from second batch → weight = $7 \times 1 = 7$. Class: Yes (6) = weight 6, No (1) = weight 1.

Weighted entropy:
$$
\begin{aligned}
H_w(S_{\text{High}}) &= -\frac{6}{14} \log_2 \frac{6}{14} - \frac{8}{14} \log_2 \frac{8}{14} \\[4pt]
&= -0.4286 \cdot (-1.2224) - 0.5714 \cdot (-0.8074) \\[4pt]
&= 0.5240 + 0.4613 = 0.9853 \\[6pt]
H_w(S_{\text{Normal}}) &= -\frac{6}{7} \log_2 \frac{6}{7} - \frac{1}{7} \log_2 \frac{1}{7} \\[4pt]
&= 0.5917 \text{ bits (same as before)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Weighted conditional entropy:
$$
H_w(S \mid \text{Humidity}) = \frac{14}{21}(0.9853) + \frac{7}{21}(0.5917) = 0.6569 + 0.1972 = 0.8541
$$

Weighted root entropy (Yes=12, No=9 out of 21):
$$
H_w(S) = -\frac{12}{21} \log_2 \frac{12}{21} - \frac{9}{21} \log_2 \frac{9}{21} = 0.9183 + 0.8454 = 0.9457
$$

$$
\text{IG}_w(S, \text{Humidity}) = 0.9457 - 0.8541 = 0.0916 \text{ bits}
$$

**Discussion:** The weighted IG is *lower* than the unweighted IG (0.1518). This is because the weighting amplifies the High-Humidity branch, which is *less pure*. **Attribute selection can flip** under re-weighting — a key consideration in cost-sensitive learning and imbalanced datasets.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** $\log_2$ ≠ $\log_{10}$. Using natural log or base-10 will produce a wrong entropy but correct *relative* IG rankings. Examiners will deduct **2 marks** for using the wrong base.
> 2. **Forgetting the weighted average:** A common error is to compute entropy of each subset and *add* them directly, ignoring the $\frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ weighting. This is a **fatal 3-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Stopping too early:** Students often declare the root node and stop. KTU expects the **full tree construction** including recursive splits on at least one impure branch.
> 4. **Confusing IG with Gain Ratio:** C4.5 uses Gain Ratio (IG / Split Info). ID3 uses raw IG. Examiners may ask specifically for ID3, in which case Gain Ratio is **not** required.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- 🎯 **Entropy** $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ ranges in $[0, \log_2 c]$; equals **0** for pure sets, **1** for binary balanced sets.
- 🎯 **Information Gain** = reduction in entropy; the *higher*, the *better* the split.
- 🎯 **ID3 Root Selection Rule:** $\arg\max_A \text{IG}(S, A)$.
- 🎯 **Play Tennis dataset** has $H(S) = 0.9403$ bits; root = **Outlook** ($\text{IG} = 0.2467$).
- 🎯 **Overcast** is a pure leaf (Yes = 4/4).
- 🎯 **Gini impurity** is computed as $1 - \sum p_i^2$; no logarithm involved.
- 🎯 **C4.5** extends ID3 by using **Gain Ratio** to penalize high-cardinality attributes.
- 🎯 **CART** uses **Gini** and produces only **binary trees**.
- 🎯 **Overfitting** is the biggest risk for fully grown trees; **pre-pruning** (max depth, min samples per leaf) and **post-pruning** (cost-complexity pruning) are remedies.
- 🎯 **Evaluation metrics:** Accuracy alone is misleading on imbalanced data — always pair with **Precision, Recall, F1-Score**.
- 🎯 **KNT (KTU Numerical Type) questions** often ask for the *value* of IG to **3 decimal places**; carry intermediate calculations to 4 decimal places.
- 🎯 **The "Illustrative Example" exam pattern** is fixed: (i) compute $H(S)$, (ii) compute IG for all attributes, (iii) identify root, (iv) recurse on impure branches, (v) classify a new test instance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Course:** PECBT414 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hypothesis Space Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hypothesis (h):** In machine learning, a hypothesis is a candidate function or mapping $h: X \rightarrow Y$ that approximates the unknown target function $f: X \rightarrow Y$ over the instance space $X$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hypothesis Space (H):** The hypothesis space is the complete set of all possible hypotheses that the learning algorithm is allowed to consider. Formally, $H = \{h \mid h : X \rightarrow Y\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hypothesis Space Search:** The process by which a learning algorithm explores the hypothesis space $H$ in order to find a hypothesis $h \in H$ that best generalizes the training data $D$ to unseen instances. This search is guided by an **inductive bias** and a **search strategy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Terminology Reminder:** In KTU end-semester examinations, the term "search" is used broadly — it includes traversing a lattice of hypotheses (general-to-specific or specific-to-general), maintaining a version space, or performing gradient-based optimization. Always specify the search strategy in your answer.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lost Key" Problem

Imagine you have dropped a key in a dark room, and you know it is *somewhere* on the floor.

- The **entire floor** = your **Hypothesis Space ($H$)**
- The **key** = the **true function $f$** that you are trying to learn
- Your **flashlight beam** = the **search strategy** of your learning algorithm
- The **way you sweep the flashlight** (systematically, randomly, or using prior hints) = the **inductive bias**

If you have no flashlight, you must grope blindly (random search). If you have a metal detector, you are biased towards metal objects. Similarly, a learning algorithm's **inductive bias** determines *which regions of $H$* it prefers to explore first.

> [!TIP]
> A larger hypothesis space gives more flexibility but makes search harder (more candidates). A smaller hypothesis space is faster to search but may not contain the true function. This is the classic **bias-variance tradeoff** at the hypothesis level.

---

## 1.3 Three Foundational Pillars of Hypothesis Space Search

| Pillar | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Representation** | $H$ | How hypotheses are encoded (conjunctions, decision trees, neural networks) |
| **Search Strategy** | $\mathcal{S}$ | How we navigate $H$ (greedy, beam, exhaustive, gradient) |
| **Inductive Bias** | $B$ | The set of assumptions that prefer one hypothesis over another |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget that a hypothesis space is **predefined** by the learner *before* seeing the data. The data only *constrains* which hypotheses remain plausible — it does not invent new hypotheses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Hypothesis Space Search — Structured Breakdown

### A. Representation of the Hypothesis
A hypothesis is typically represented as a vector of parameters or a symbolic expression.

- **Conjunctive Representation (e.g., EnjoySport):**
  $$h = \langle x_1 = \text{Sun} \land x_2 = \text{Warm} \land x_4 = \text{Same}\rangle$$
- **Disjunctive Representation:** Multiple conjunctions OR'd together.
- **Continuous/Parametric:** $h(x) = w_0 + w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \dots + w_n x_n$.

### B. The Search Process (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus specifically emphasizes **three classical search strategies**. Memorize all three for the exam.

1. **General-to-Specific Search (Find-S Algorithm)**
   - Start with the **most general hypothesis** $g = \langle ?, ?, \dots, ?\rangle$.
   - For each positive training example, **specialize** $g$ to cover it without covering negatives.
   - Result: A single maximally specific hypothesis.

2. **Specific-to-General Search**
   - Start with the **most specific hypothesis** $s = \langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \dots, \emptyset\rangle$.
   - For each positive example, **generalize** $s$ minimally to cover it.
   - Less common in KTU but conceptually important.

3. **Version Space Search (Candidate Elimination)**
   - Maintains a **General Boundary $G$** and **Specific Boundary $S$**.
   - **Version Space ($VS$)** = the set of all hypotheses consistent with the training data.
   - $VS = \{h \in H \mid h \text{ is consistent with } D\}$

### C. Consistency with Training Data
A hypothesis $h$ is **consistent** with training example $\langle x, c(x)\rangle$ if:
$$h(x) = c(x)$$
where $c(x)$ is the target concept label.

---

## 2.2 The Inductive Bias — The "Why" of Search Direction

The **inductive bias** $B$ of a learning algorithm is the set of *additional assumptions* $B$ that the learner uses to predict outputs for unseen inputs *beyond* what the training data alone provides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mitchell's Definition (KTU textbook standard):**
> "The inductive bias of a learning algorithm is the set of assumptions $B$ such that, for any new instance $x$, the algorithm's prediction $P(x)$ follows deductively from the data $D$ together with $B$."

**Examples of Inductive Bias:**

| Algorithm | Inductive Bias |
|---|---|
| Find-S | Prefer the most specific hypothesis |
| Candidate Elimination | All consistent hypotheses are equally good |
| Neural Networks | Smooth function (continuity prior) |
| Decision Trees | Small trees are preferred (Occam's Razor) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** "**G**eneral = **B**ig, **S**pecific = **S**mall" — General hypotheses cover *more* instances; specific hypotheses cover *fewer* instances.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hypothesis | $h : X \rightarrow Y$ | Single candidate function |
| 2 | Hypothesis Space | $H = \{h \mid h : X \rightarrow Y\}$ | Set of all candidates |
| 3 | Most Specific | $s = \langle\emptyset, \dots, \emptyset\rangle$ | Covers no positive instance |
| 4 | Most General | $g = \langle ?, \dots, ?\rangle$ | Covers every instance |
| 5 | Consistency | $h(x) = c(x)$ for all $\langle x, c(x)\rangle \in D$ | Empirical success |
| 6 | Version Space | $VS_{H,D} = \{h \in H \mid \text{Consistent}(h, D)\}$ | Subset of $H$ |
| 7 | Generalization Operator | $\text{more-general-than}(h_i, h_j) \Rightarrow h_i \ge_g h_j$ | Partial order |
| 8 | General Boundary | $G = \{g \in H \mid \exists s \in S : g \ge_g s \land \neg \exists g' \in H : g' \ge_g g\}$ | Maximal general |
| 9 | Specific Boundary | $S = \{s \in H \mid \exists g \in G : g \ge_g s \land \neg \exists s' \in H : s \ge_g s'\}$ | Maximal specific |
| 10 | Inductive Bias | $P(x) \vdash D \cup B$ | Mitchell's criterion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Note for KTU Exams:** The relation "more general than" is denoted $h_i \ge_g h_j$. The symbol $\ge_g$ is read as "strictly more general than or equal to."

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis Systems:** Hypothesis space = possible disease rules; search = finding the diagnosis that best matches patient symptoms.
- **Spam Filters:** Hypothesis space = set of word-pattern rules; search = finding the rule that classifies emails correctly.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Hypothesis space = possible driving policies; search = reinforcement learning traversing the policy space.
- **Manufacturing Quality Control:** Hypothesis space = rules predicting defective products; search narrows down causes of defects.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Application Question Trend:** Examiners often ask, *"Explain the role of hypothesis space search in [medical/finance/robotics]."* Always tie your answer back to **representation + bias + data-driven refinement**.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Find-S Algorithm — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

### Problem Setup (Classic KTU EnjoySport Example)

Let us consider the classic Tom Mitchell *EnjoySport* dataset.

**Instance Space $X$:** Each day is described by 6 attributes:
$$X = \langle \text{Sky}, \text{AirTemp}, \text{Humidity}, \text{Wind}, \text{Water}, \text{Forecast}\rangle$$

**Target Concept $c$:** `EnjoySport = Yes` means the day is suitable for water sports.

**Training Data $D$:**

| Day | Sky | AirTemp | Humidity | Wind | Water | Forecast | EnjoySport |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sunny | Warm | Normal | Strong | Warm | Same | **Yes** |
| 2 | Sunny | Warm | High | Strong | Warm | Same | **Yes** |
| 3 | Rainy | Cold | High | Strong | Warm | Change | **No** |
| 4 | Sunny | Warm | High | Strong | Cool | Change | **Yes** |

### Find-S Execution

**Step 1: Initialize $h$ to the most specific hypothesis**
$$h = \langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset \rangle$$

**Step 2: Process Example 1 (Positive: Yes)**
Since $h$ is too specific to cover this example, replace each $\emptyset$ with the attribute value from Example 1.

$$h = \langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, \text{Normal}, \text{Strong}, \text{Warm}, \text{Same} \rangle$$

**Step 3: Process Example 2 (Positive: Yes)**
Compare $h$ with Example 2. For each attribute:
- Sky: Sunny vs Sunny → same → keep
- AirTemp: Warm vs Warm → same → keep
- Humidity: Normal vs High → different → generalize to "?"

$$h = \langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, ?, \text{Strong}, \text{Warm}, \text{Same} \rangle$$

**Step 4: Process Example 3 (Negative: No)**
**Find-S ignores negative examples.** $h$ remains unchanged.

$$h = \langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, ?, \text{Strong}, \text{Warm}, \text{Same} \rangle$$

**Step 5: Process Example 4 (Positive: Yes)**
Compare $h$ with Example 4:
- Humidity: "?" vs High → already general → keep "?"
- Water: Warm vs Cool → different → generalize to "?"

$$h = \langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, ?, \text{Strong}, ?, \text{Same} \rangle$$

**Step 6: Termination**
No more examples. Output the final hypothesis:

$$\boxed{h_{\text{final}} = \langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, ?, \text{Strong}, ?, \text{Same} \rangle}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Conversion of $h$:** The hypothesis above translates to the rule: *"EnjoySport ← (Sky = Sunny) ∧ (AirTemp = Warm) ∧ (Wind = Strong) ∧ (Forecast = Same)"*

---

## 3.2 Candidate Elimination Algorithm — Exhaustive Walkthrough

### Step-by-Step Initialization

**Initial General Boundary (covers everything):**
$$G_0 = \{\langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle\}$$

**Initial Specific Boundary (covers nothing):**
$$S_0 = \{\langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset \rangle\}$$

### Example 1: $\langle$Sunny, Warm, Normal, Strong, Warm, Same$\rangle$, $+$ **Positive**

1. **Remove from $G$** any hypothesis inconsistent with this positive example.
2. For each $s \in S$ not covering the example, replace $s$ with all minimal generalizations that cover it.

$$S_1 = \{\langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, \text{Normal}, \text{Strong}, \text{Warm}, \text{Same} \rangle\}$$

$$G_1 = \{\langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle\}$$

### Example 2: $\langle$Sunny, Warm, High, Strong, Warm, Same$\rangle$, $+$ **Positive**

Since $S_1$ does not cover Example 2 (Humidity differs), we minimally generalize $S_1$ on the differing attribute.

$$S_2 = \{\langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, ?, \text{Strong}, \text{Warm}, \text{Same} \rangle\}$$

**Update $G$:** For each $g \in G_1$, if $g$ does not cover the new positive example, we must minimally specialize $g$ so that it (a) covers the new example and (b) does not cover any known negative example. Since Example 3 (negative) is not yet seen, we keep $G$ unchanged here.

$$G_2 = \{\langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle\}$$

### Example 3: $\langle$Rainy, Cold, High, Strong, Warm, Change$\rangle$, $-$ **Negative**

1. **Remove from $S$** any hypothesis that covers this negative example. (Currently $S_2$ does not cover it — Sky=Sunny $\neq$ Rainy, so $S_2$ survives.)
2. **Update $G$:** Each $g \in G_2$ that covers this negative example must be specialized minimally so that it no longer covers it.

Since $G_2$ contains only $\langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?\rangle$ which covers this negative example, we specialize it on attributes that differ between the negative example and the positive examples (Examples 1, 2, 4).

**Positive examples cover set:**
- Ex 1: $\langle$Sunny, Warm, Normal, Strong, Warm, Same$\rangle$
- Ex 2: $\langle$Sunny, Warm, High, Strong, Warm, Same$\rangle$
- Ex 4: $\langle$Sunny, Warm, High, Strong, Cool, Change$\rangle$

To specialize $\langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?\rangle$ minimally such that it no longer covers the negative example, we replace "?" with the negation of each attribute value of the negative example. We must ensure the resulting hypothesis **still covers all positive examples** (not just one).

**Candidate specializations of $G$:**

| # | Sky | AirTemp | Humidity | Wind | Water | Forecast | Covers Positives? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sunny | ? | ? | ? | ? | ? | Ex 1, 2, 4 — YES |
| 2 | ? | Warm | ? | ? | ? | ? | Ex 1, 2, 4 — YES |
| 3 | ? | ? | High | ? | ? | ? | Ex 1 = NO (Normal) |
| 4 | ? | ? | Normal | ? | ? | ? | Ex 2, 4 = NO (High) |
| 5 | ? | ? | ? | Strong | ? | ? | All positives — YES |
| 6 | ? | ? | ? | ? | Warm | ? | Ex 4 = NO (Cool) |
| 7 | ? | ? | ? | ? | Cool | ? | Ex 1, 2 = NO (Warm) |
| 8 | ? | ? | ? | ? | ? | Same | Ex 4 = NO (Change) |
| 9 | ? | ? | ? | ? | ? | Change | Ex 1, 2 = NO (Same) |

**Surviving $G$ members** (those that cover all positives AND exclude the negative):

$$G_3 = \{\langle \text{Sunny}, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, \text{Warm}, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, ?, ?, \text{Strong}, ?, ? \rangle\}$$

$$S_3 = \{\langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, ?, \text{Strong}, \text{Warm}, \text{Same} \rangle\}$$

### Example 4: $\langle$Sunny, Warm, High, Strong, Cool, Change$\rangle$, $+$ **Positive**

**Update $S$:** $S_3$ does not cover Example 4 (Water: Warm ≠ Cool; Forecast: Same ≠ Change). Minimize generalizations on the differing attributes.

**Candidate generalizations of $S_3$:**

| # | Sky | AirTemp | Humidity | Wind | Water | Forecast |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sunny | Warm | ? | Strong | ? | ? |
| 2 | Sunny | Warm | ? | Strong | Warm | ? |
| 3 | Sunny | Warm | ? | Strong | ? | Same |

**Filter** — must not cover the negative Example 3:
- Row 1: $\langle$Sunny, Warm, ?, Strong, ?, ?$\rangle$ — covers Ex 3? Sky Sunny vs Rainy → NO ✓
- Row 2: $\langle$Sunny, Warm, ?, Strong, Warm, ?$\rangle$ — covers Ex 3? Forecast Change vs ? → YES ✗
- Row 3: $\langle$Sunny, Warm, ?, Strong, ?, Same$\rangle$ — covers Ex 3? Forecast Same vs Change → NO ✓

$$S_4 = \{\langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, ?, \text{Strong}, ?, ? \rangle, \langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, ?, \text{Strong}, ?, \text{Same} \rangle\}$$

**Update $G$:** Each $g \in G_3$ not covering Example 4 must be specialized. Only $\langle ?, ?, ?, \text{Strong}, ?, ?\rangle$ covers Ex 4; the others (Sky=Sunny ✓, AirTemp=Warm ✓) also cover it. So $G$ remains.

**However**, for each $g \in G_3$ covering Example 4, check if it is *more general* than some $s \in S_4$. If yes, retain it; if not, remove it. All three $G$ hypotheses are more general than $S_4$ members → retained.

$$\boxed{G_4 = \{\langle \text{Sunny}, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, \text{Warm}, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, ?, ?, \text{Strong}, ?, ? \rangle\}}$$

$$\boxed{S_4 = \{\langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, ?, \text{Strong}, ?, ? \rangle, \langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, ?, \text{Strong}, ?, \text{Same} \rangle\}}$$

### Final Version Space
$$VS = \{h \in H \mid S_4 \le_g h \le_g G_4\}$$

This represents a *partial ordering* of all consistent hypotheses.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Properties of Search Strategies

| Property | Find-S | Candidate Elimination | Exhaustive Enumeration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Output** | Single hypothesis | Boundary sets $S, G$ | All consistent hypotheses |
| **Handles Negatives?** | Ignores them | Uses them to prune $G$ | Uses them to filter |
| **Inductive Bias** | Most specific | All consistent equally good | All consistent equally good |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(\vert D\vert \cdot \vert H \vert)$ worst case | $O(\vert D\vert \cdot \vert H \vert^2)$ worst case | $O(2^{\vert H \vert})$ |
| **Susceptible to Noise?** | Yes (assumes noise-free) | Yes (assumes noise-free) | Yes (assumes noise-free) |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High | Very High | Low |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hypothesis Space Lattice — General-to-Specific Ordering

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** In the diagram below, the **topmost node** is the most general hypothesis (accepts *everything*); the **bottommost node** is the most specific hypothesis (accepts *nothing*). Each edge denotes a "more-general-than" relation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node0["Most General:  QUESTION QUESTION QUESTION QUESTION QUESTION QUESTION"]
    node1A["Sunny QUESTION QUESTION QUESTION QUESTION QUESTION"]
    node1B["QUESTION Warm QUESTION QUESTION QUESTION QUESTION"]
    node1C["QUESTION QUESTION QUESTION Strong QUESTION QUESTION"]
    node2A["Sunny Warm QUESTION Strong QUESTION Same"]
    node2B["Sunny Warm QUESTION Strong Warm Same"]
    node2C["Sunny Warm QUESTION Strong QUESTION QUESTION"]
    node3["Most Specific:  EMPTY EMPTY EMPTY EMPTY EMPTY EMPTY"]
    node0 --> node1A
    node0 --> node1B
    node0 --> node1C
    node1A --> node2A
    node1A --> node2C
    node1B --> node2A
    node1B --> node2B
    node1B --> node2C
    node1C --> node2A
    node1C --> node2B
    node1C --> node2C
    node2A --> node3
    node2B --> node3
    node2C --> node3
```

---

## 4.2 Version Space Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA["Training Data D"]
    block1["Positive Examples Set D PLUS"]
    block2["Negative Examples Set D MINUS"]
    blockS["Specific Boundary S"]
    blockG["General Boundary G"]
    blockH["Full Hypothesis Space H"]
    blockVS["Version Space VS"]
    blockOut["Final Hypothesis Set"]

    startA --> block1
    startA --> block2
    blockH --> blockVS
    block1 --> blockS
    block2 --> blockG
    block1 --> blockG
    block2 --> blockS
    blockS --> blockVS
    blockG --> blockVS
    blockVS --> blockOut
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Interpretation:** The positive examples *constrain* $S$ (push it downward towards specific) and *constrain* $G$ (push it upward towards general). The negative examples *constrain* $G$ (push it downward, away from covering negatives) and *constrain* $S$ (prevent it from becoming too general to accidentally cover negatives).

---

## 4.3 Find-S vs Candidate Elimination — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1["Initialize: h equals EMPTY EMPTY EMPTY EMPTY EMPTY EMPTY"]
    step1A["Read Next Training Example x, c"]
    decision1{"Is c equals PLUS?"}
    step1B["Generalize h to cover x"]
    step1C["Keep h unchanged"]
    end1["Return h"]

    start1 --> step1A
    step1A --> decision1
    decision1 -- YES --> step1B
    decision1 -- NO --> step1C
    step1B --> step1A
    step1C --> step1A
    step1A -- NO MORE EXAMPLES --> end1
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start2["Initialize: S0 equals EMPTY, G0 equals QUESTION QUESTION"]
    step2A["Read Next Training Example x, c"]
    decision2A{"Is c equals PLUS?"}
    decision2B{"Is c equals MINUS?"}
    step2C["For each s in S not covering x: minimally generalize s to cover x"]
    step2D["Remove from G any g that fails to cover x"]
    step2E["For each g in G covering x: remove it if not more general than some s in S"]
    step2F["For each g in G covering x: minimally specialize g to no longer cover x but still cover all positives"]
    step2G["For each s in S covering x: remove it"]
    end2["Output S and G representing Version Space"]

    start2 --> step2A
    step2A --> decision2A
    decision2A -- YES --> step2C
    step2A -- NO --> decision2B
    decision2B -- YES --> step2F
    decision2C --> step2E
    decision2F --> step2G
    step2E --> step2A
    step2G --> step2A
    step2F --> step2A
    step2A -- NO MORE EXAMPLES --> end2
```
