# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Question Paper Pattern]**

### Q1. Define hypothesis space. Why is the choice of hypothesis space important in machine learning? `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> A **hypothesis space $H$** is the set of all possible hypotheses that a learning algorithm is permitted to consider when learning a target concept. Formally, $H = \{h \mid h : X \rightarrow Y\}$.

The choice of hypothesis space is important because:
1. **Representational Power:** If the true function $f \notin H$, the learner cannot represent it, regardless of data.
2. **Search Complexity:** A larger $H$ means more candidates to search through (higher computational cost).
3. **Inductive Bias:** The choice of $H$ embeds prior assumptions about the form of the solution.
4. **Generalization:** An overly flexible $H$ may overfit; an overly restrictive $H$ may underfit.

---

### Q2. What is the version space? How is it related to the specific boundary $S$ and general boundary $G$? `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> The **version space $VS_{H,D}$** with respect to hypothesis space $H$ and training data $D$ is the subset of hypotheses in $H$ that are consistent with all training examples:
> $$VS_{H,D} = \{h \in H \mid \forall \langle x, c(x) \rangle \in D : h(x) = c(x)\}$$

It is the set of all hypotheses lying between the **specific boundary $S$** (the most specific consistent hypotheses) and the **general boundary $G$** (the most general consistent hypotheses):
$$VS_{H,D} = \{h \in H \mid \exists s \in S, \exists g \in G : s \le_g h \le_g g\}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — With Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

### Question A: Hypothesis Space Search with Candidate Elimination `[14 Marks]`

Consider the following training data for a concept $c$ defined over 4 attributes ($\text{Color}$, $\text{Size}$, $\text{Shape}$, $\text{Price}$), where $+$ means positive example and $-$ means negative.

| # | Color | Size | Shape | Price | Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Red | Small | Round | Low | $+$ |
| 2 | Red | Small | Round | High | $-$ |
| 3 | Red | Large | Square | Low | $+$ |
| 4 | Green | Small | Round | Low | $-$ |
| 5 | Red | Small | Square | High | $+$ |
| 6 | Green | Large | Round | High | $+$ |

#### Part (a) Trace the Candidate Elimination algorithm on the above data. Show $S$ and $G$ after each step. `[7 Marks]`

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Initialization:**
$$S_0 = \{\langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset \rangle\}$$
$$G_0 = \{\langle ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle\}$$

**After Example 1: $\langle$Red, Small, Round, Low$\rangle$, $+$ Positive**
- $S_0$ does not cover Example 1 → generalize minimally:
$$S_1 = \{\langle \text{Red}, \text{Small}, \text{Round}, \text{Low} \rangle\}$$
- $G_0$ covers Example 1 → retain; check more-general-than $S_1$ relation: yes, $\langle ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ is more general.
$$G_1 = \{\langle ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle\}$$

**After Example 2: $\langle$Red, Small, Round, High$\rangle$, $-$ Negative**
- $S_1$ does not cover Example 2 (Price differs) → $S$ unchanged.
- $G_1 = \{\langle ?, ?, ?, ?\rangle\}$ covers Example 2 → must specialize to exclude it while still covering all positives (Ex 1, 3, 5, 6).

**Candidate minimal specializations of $G$ that exclude Example 2 AND cover all positives:**

| # | Color | Size | Shape | Price | Covers Positives? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Red | ? | ? | ? | Ex 4 (Color=Green) fails ✗ |
| 2 | ? | Small | ? | ? | Ex 3 (Size=Large) fails ✗ |
| 3 | ? | ? | Round | ? | Ex 3 (Shape=Square), Ex 5 (Shape=Square) fail ✗ |
| 4 | ? | ? | ? | Low | Ex 2 (Price=High) fails ✗ |

None of the single-attribute specializations cover all positives! Therefore:

$$G_2 = \{\langle \text{Red}, \text{Small}, ?, ? \rangle, \langle \text{Red}, ?, \text{Round}, ? \rangle, \langle ?, \text{Small}, \text{Round}, ? \rangle, \langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, \text{Low} \rangle, \langle ?, \text{Small}, ?, \text{Low} \rangle, \langle ?, ?, \text{Round}, \text{Low} \rangle\}$$

After applying the *more-general-than* $S$ filter (all six are more general than $S_1$), all six are retained.

**After Example 3: $\langle$Red, Large, Square, Low$\rangle$, $+$ Positive**
- $S_1$ does not cover Example 3 (Size: Small≠Large, Shape: Round≠Square) → generalize on differing attributes:

$$S_2 = \{\langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, \text{Low} \rangle\}$$

- $G_2$ updates: remove any $g$ that does not cover Example 3. The members that cover Example 3 are: $\langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, \text{Low} \rangle, \langle \text{Red}, ?, \text{Round}, ? \rangle, \langle ?, ?, \text{Round}, \text{Low} \rangle$ ... but for full coverage, we recheck.

After applying all Candidate Elimination rules:

$$G_3 = \{\langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, ?, ?, \text{Low} \rangle\}$$

**After Example 4: $\langle$Green, Small, Round, Low$\rangle$, $-$ Negative**
- $S_2$ does not cover Example 4 (Color: Red≠Green) → $S$ unchanged.
- $G_3$ must be specialized to exclude Example 4. $\langle ?, ?, ?, \text{Low}\rangle$ covers Ex 4 (Price=Low ✓) → specialize on Color:
  - $\langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, \text{Low} \rangle$ — covers Ex 3 (positive) and Ex 1, 5 ✓; excludes Ex 4 ✓.
- $\langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, ?\rangle$ does not cover Ex 4 (Color=Red ≠ Green) → keep.

$$G_4 = \{\langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, ? \rangle, \langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, \text{Low} \rangle\}$$

**After Example 5: $\langle$Red, Small, Square, High$\rangle$, $+$ Positive**
- $S_2$ does not cover Example 5 (Shape: ? matches, but Price: Low≠High) → generalize on Price:
$$S_3 = \{\langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, ? \rangle\}$$

- $G_4$: both members cover Example 5. Check if each is *more general than* some $s \in S_3$. Both are. Retain. But $G_4$ contains $\langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, \text{Low} \rangle$ which is *not* more general than $S_3 = \langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, ? \rangle$? Wait — $\langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ is more general than $\langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, \text{Low} \rangle$, so the latter is removed.

$$G_5 = \{\langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, ? \rangle\}$$

**After Example 6: $\langle$Green, Large, Round, High$\rangle$, $+$ Positive**
- $S_3 = \langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ does not cover Example 6 (Color: Red≠Green) → generalize on Color:
$$S_4 = \{\langle ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle\}$$

- $G_5 = \langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ does not cover Example 6 → specialize to cover it while still covering all positives AND not covering Ex 2, 4 (negatives).

Candidates: $\langle ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ covers all positives AND negatives → fails.

Single-attribute minimal specializations that cover all positives (Ex 1, 3, 5, 6):
- $\langle ?, \text{Small}, ?, ? \rangle$ — fails (Ex 3 Size=Large)
- $\langle ?, \text{Large}, ?, ? \rangle$ — fails (Ex 1, 5 Size=Small)
- $\langle ?, ?, \text{Round}, ? \rangle$ — fails (Ex 3, 5 Shape=Square)
- $\langle ?, ?, \text{Square}, ? \rangle$ — fails (Ex 1, 6 Shape=Round)
- $\langle ?, ?, ?, \text{Low} \rangle$ — fails (Ex 5, 6 Price=High)
- $\langle ?, ?, ?, \text{High} \rangle$ — fails (Ex 1, 3 Price=Low)

No single-attribute specialization works → must consider multi-attribute, but standard CE only allows single-attribute specializations → $G$ becomes empty → **version space collapses to empty set**, meaning the data is not consistent with the chosen hypothesis space.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** This problem is *intentionally* designed to test whether students recognize when a hypothesis space is *inconsistent* with data. A common error is to keep $G_5 = \{\langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, ? \rangle\}$ blindly, ignoring that Example 6 violates it. The correct conclusion is that **no hypothesis in $H$ is consistent with all training data** — implying we need a richer $H$ (e.g., disjunctive hypotheses).

#### Part (b) Define inductive bias. With reference to the above example, explain the inductive bias of (i) Find-S and (ii) Candidate Elimination algorithm. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Inductive Bias (Definition):** The set of additional assumptions $B$ that, together with the training data $D$, are sufficient to derive the learner's prediction for any new instance $x$. Formally, the inductive bias is the minimal set $B$ such that $P(x) \land D \land B \vdash c(x)$ for all instances $x$.

**(i) Find-S Inductive Bias:**
- **Bias:** *"The most specific hypothesis that fits the training data is the best."*
- **Implication:** Find-S will output $\langle \text{Red}, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ after Example 5, ignoring Example 6's requirement.
- **Effect:** This bias is *too narrow* — it underfits when negative examples (like Ex 2, 4) reveal that the true concept must be more flexible.

**(ii) Candidate Elimination Inductive Bias:**
- **Bias:** *"All consistent hypotheses (i.e., those in the version space) are equally plausible."*
- **Implication:** When the version space is non-empty, CE defers the choice of $h$ to the user; when it becomes empty (as in our case after Ex 6), the algorithm signals a *misspecification* of $H$.
- **Effect:** CE is *more honest* about uncertainty but does not commit to a single best hypothesis.

| Bias Type | Predicts Single $h$? | Handles Noise? | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Find-S | Yes (most specific) | No | 3 |
| Candidate Elimination | No (returns $S, G$) | No | 4 |

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]**

### Question B: Hypothesis Space Representation and Search Strategies `[14 Marks]`

#### Part (a) Explain the general-to-specific and specific-to-general search strategies for hypothesis space search. Illustrate with a suitable example. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**General-to-Specific Search:**
This strategy begins with the most general hypothesis $\langle ?, ?, \dots, ? \rangle$ and iteratively specializes it to exclude negative examples while still covering all positive examples. The Find-S algorithm is the canonical example.

**Specific-to-General Search:**
This strategy begins with the most specific hypothesis $\langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \dots, \emptyset \rangle$ and iteratively generalizes it to cover each new positive example. The Candidate Elimination algorithm uses this as one of its two boundary maintenance procedures.

**Illustration using the dataset:**

| Day | Sky | Temp | Humidity | Wind | Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sunny | Warm | Normal | Strong | $+$ |
| 2 | Rainy | Cold | High | Weak | $-$ |

**General-to-Specific Trace:**
- Start: $G = \langle ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$
- After Ex 1 ($+$): $S = \langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, \text{Normal}, \text{Strong} \rangle$
- After Ex 2 ($-$): $S$ does not cover Ex 2 (Sky=Sunny ≠ Rainy) → $S$ retained as the maximally specific consistent hypothesis.

**Specific-to-General Trace:**
- Start: $S = \langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset \rangle$
- After Ex 1 ($+$): $S = \langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Warm}, \text{Normal}, \text{Strong} \rangle$
- After Ex 2 ($-$): $G$ becomes $\{\langle ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle\}$ specialized to exclude Ex 2: candidates include $\langle \text{Sunny}, ?, ?, ? \rangle$, $\langle ?, \text{Warm}, ?, ? \rangle$, $\langle ?, ?, \text{Normal}, ? \rangle$, $\langle ?, ?, ?, \text{Strong} \rangle$ (those that cover Ex 1 but not Ex 2).

#### Part (b) Discuss the limitations of hypothesis space search in the context of noisy data and large hypothesis spaces. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Limitation 1: Noise Sensitivity**
Both Find-S and Candidate Elimination assume the training data is *noise-free* (i.e., labels are always correct). When data contains mislabeled examples:
- A single noisy negative example can incorrectly specialize a general hypothesis.
- A single noisy positive example can incorrectly generalize a specific hypothesis.
- The version space may become *empty* even when the true concept is in $H$, simply due to label noise.

**Limitation 2: Inconsistent Data**
When two examples have the same input $x$ but different labels (due to noise), no hypothesis in $H$ is consistent with $D$, and Candidate Elimination fails outright.

**Limitation 3: Computational Complexity**
For a hypothesis space with $n$ attributes, each having $k$ possible values, the size of $H$ for conjunctive hypotheses is:
$$\vert H \vert = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (k_i + 1)$$
where the $+1$ accounts for the "?" (any) value. With $n=6$ and $k_i=3$ for each attribute:
$$\vert H \vert = 4^6 = 4096$$
This grows **exponentially**, making exhaustive search infeasible for real-world problems.

**Limitation 4: Hypothesis Space Misspecification**
If the true target function $f \notin H$ (e.g., the true function is a neural network but $H$ only contains decision trees), no amount of search can recover $f$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):** Many students write only about noise and forget to mention **inconsistent data** and **hypothesis space misspecification**. KTU 2024 scheme examiners expect all four limitations for full 7 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4, Hypothesis Space Search**

- [ ] **Hypothesis $h$** is a candidate mapping $X \to Y$, not the true function.
- [ ] **Hypothesis Space $H$** is the predefined set of all candidates.
- [ ] **Hypothesis Space Search** = exploring $H$ using a strategy + inductive bias.
- [ ] **Find-S** = general-to-specific; outputs the *single most specific* hypothesis; **ignores negative examples**.
- [ ] **Candidate Elimination** = bidirectional; maintains $S$ (specific) and $G$ (general) boundaries; uses both positives and negatives.
- [ ] **Version Space** = the set of all consistent hypotheses = those between $S$ and $G$ in the generalization order.
- [ ] **Inductive Bias** = the *extra* assumptions a learner uses beyond the data to predict unseen instances.
- [ ] **Consistency** means $h(x) = c(x)$ for all training examples.
- [ ] **More-General-Than ($\ge_g$)** is a *partial order*, not a total order.
- [ ] **Exponential Blow-Up:** $\vert H \vert$ grows exponentially with attributes and their value counts.
- [ ] **Noise-Free Assumption:** Both classical algorithms fail in the presence of label noise.
- [ ] **KTU Exam Hot Words:** "$G$ boundary", "$S$ boundary", "version space", "inductive bias", "consistency".
- [ ] **Mnemonic:** "**G**eneral = **B**ig coverset, **S**pecific = **S**mall coverset" — the more specific a hypothesis, the fewer instances it covers.
- [ ] **Key Pitfall:** If two positive examples have the same input but different classes, the version space becomes **empty** → indicates hypothesis space misspecification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip for KTU 2024 Scheme:** Always (1) state the inductive bias of the algorithm you are using, (2) show $S$ and $G$ at *every* step of the Candidate Elimination trace, and (3) explicitly mention the **more-general-than** relation when justifying why a hypothesis is added/removed. These three items together account for ~50% of marks in a 14-mark question on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Genetic Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT414 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Genetic Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Genetic Programming (GP)** is a domain-independent, problem-independent evolutionary computation methodology that automatically solves problems by evolving computer programs, represented as tree structures, using operations inspired by biological evolution such as **selection**, **crossover (recombination)**, and **mutation**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Genetic Programming is classified under **Evolutionary Machine Learning** algorithms. It belongs to the family of **Evolutionary Algorithms (EAs)** and is considered a specialized extension of **Genetic Algorithms (GA)**, where the individuals being evolved are not fixed-length binary strings, but **hierarchical, variable-sized computer programs**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Father of Genetic Programming:** John R. Koza (1992) is widely credited as the founder of modern GP through his seminal work *"Genetic Programming: On the Programming of Computers by Means of Natural Selection."*

### Formal KTU Board Definition
$$\text{GP} = \{P, F, S, C, M, T\}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Population of program trees
- $F$ = Fitness function (evaluates program quality)
- $S$ = Selection mechanism
- $C$ = Crossover operator
- $M$ = Mutation operator
- $T$ = Termination criterion

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of GP as "Survival of the Fittest Code"!**

Imagine you are a teacher and you give **1,000 randomly written math formulas** to a class. You then **test each formula** to see which one gets closest to the correct answer. The **best formulas are kept and combined** (like breeding two strong plants), while the **worst are discarded**. Over many generations, the formulas get progressively better — eventually producing a perfect mathematical expression.

**Real-World Analogy — Dog Breeding:**
- A population of **random dog breeds** (initial random programs) is created.
- Each dog is **rated on traits** (fitness function) such as speed, intelligence.
- The **best dogs are selected** (selection) and **bred together** (crossover), creating offspring with mixed traits.
- Occasionally, a **random genetic mutation** introduces new traits (mutation).
- After many generations, you have a **super-dog** (optimized program) tailored to your needs.

In GP, instead of dogs, we breed **mathematical expressions and computer programs**, and instead of speed, we use a **fitness function that measures how close a program's output is to the desired output**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Glossary — Must Memorize for Examinations**

| Term | Definition | Significance |
|------|------------|--------------|
| **Individual** | A candidate solution (program tree) in the population | Basic unit of evolution |
| **Population** | A collection of individuals | Search space of solutions |
| **Fitness Function** | A measure of how well a program solves the problem | Drives selection pressure |
| **Selection** | Process of choosing individuals for reproduction | Implements "survival of the fittest" |
| **Crossover** | Combining two parent programs to produce offspring | Exploitation of good solutions |
| **Mutation** | Random alteration of a program | Maintains genetic diversity |
| **Terminal Set** | Leaves of the program tree (variables, constants) | Input data |
| **Function Set** | Internal nodes of the tree (operators, functions) | Building blocks |
| **Generation** | One iteration of the evolutionary cycle | Time step in GP |
| **Ramped Half-and-Half** | Standard initialization method for trees | Creates diverse initial population |

## 1.4 Genetic Programming vs. Genetic Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a frequently asked 3-mark question in KTU examinations!**

$$\text{GA} : \text{Fixed-length binary strings} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{GP} : \text{Variable-length tree structures}$$

| Feature | Genetic Algorithm (GA) | Genetic Programming (GP) |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Chromosome** | Fixed-length binary string | Variable-length tree |
| **Solution Type** | Parameters / values | Programs / expressions |
| **Representation** | Linear (1D array) | Hierarchical (tree) |
| **Application** | Optimization | Automatic program synthesis |
| **Complexity** | Lower | Higher |
| **Output** | Set of optimal values | Executable program or formula |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Genetic Programming Pipeline — Step-by-Step

The GP algorithm follows a structured **6-stage evolutionary cycle** that iterates across multiple generations until a termination condition is met.

### Stage 1: Initialization of Population
The first step is to generate an **initial random population** of program trees.

**Two Standard Methods (KTU Board Frequently Asked):**

> [!TIP]
> **Method 1: Full Method**
> All trees have nodes at every depth level from root to a fixed maximum depth $D$.
> $$\text{Depth of every leaf} = D$$

> [!TIP]
> **Method 2: Grow Method**
> Trees can have varying shapes and sizes, with nodes randomly chosen from the combined terminal and function sets.
> $$\text{Depth of leaves} \leq D$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ramped Half-and-Half (Koza's Standard):**
> Half the population is initialized using the **Full method**, and the other half using the **Grow method**, across a range of depths. This produces **diverse tree shapes**, preventing premature convergence.

### Stage 2: Fitness Evaluation
Each program tree in the population is **executed**, and its output is compared against target values.

$$f_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \left\vert y_{ij} - t_j \right\vert$$

Where:
- $f_i$ = fitness of individual $i$
- $y_{ij}$ = output of program $i$ on fitness case $j$
- $t_j$ = target (desired) output for case $j$
- $N$ = total number of fitness cases

> [!NOTE]
> **Lower fitness = Better individual** (minimization problem). In symbolic regression, this represents the **sum of absolute errors** across all training examples.

### Stage 3: Selection
Parents are chosen for reproduction. Common methods include:

> [!NOTE]
> **Fitness-Proportionate Selection (Roulette Wheel):**
> $$P_i = \frac{f_i^{-1}}{\sum_{k=1}^{M} f_k^{-1}}$$
> Where $P_i$ is the probability of selecting individual $i$ and $M$ is the population size.

**Other methods:** Tournament Selection, Rank Selection, Truncation Selection.

### Stage 4: Genetic Operators
**Crossover (Sexual Recombination):**
1. Select two parent trees.
2. Randomly choose a **crossover point** (subtree) in each parent.
3. Swap the two subtrees to produce two offspring.

**Mutation:**
1. Select a parent tree.
2. Randomly choose a node in the tree.
3. Replace the subtree rooted at that node with a **randomly generated subtree**.

### Stage 5: Replacement
The new generation replaces the old population. Strategies:
- **Generational Replacement:** Entire population replaced.
- **Steady-State Replacement:** Only a few individuals replaced per generation.
- **Elitism:** Best individuals always preserved.

### Stage 6: Termination
The loop terminates when:
1. **Maximum generations** reached.
2. **Satisfactory fitness** achieved.
3. **No improvement** over a fixed number of generations.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — They form the core of all KTU 14-mark questions!**

| # | Formula | Description | Usage |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $f_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \left\vert y_{ij} - t_j \right\vert$ | Sum of absolute errors (fitness) | Symbolic regression |
| 2 | $f_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} (y_{ij} - t_j)^2$ | Sum of squared errors (MSE-based fitness) | Curve fitting |
| 3 | $P_i = \frac{f_i^{-1}}{\sum_{k=1}^{M} f_k^{-1}}$ | Fitness-proportionate selection probability | Selection operator |
| 4 | $M_{\text{max}} = \sum_{i=0}^{D-1} F^{(D-i)}$ | Maximum number of nodes (Full method) | Tree size estimation |
| 5 | $H(t) = M \cdot (1 - p)^t$ | Population diversity decay | Convergence analysis |
| 6 | $P(\text{crossover}) = p_c \in [0.7, 0.9]$ | Crossover probability (standard) | Operator setting |
| 7 | $P(\text{mutation}) = p_m \in [0.01, 0.1]$ | Mutation probability (standard) | Operator setting |
| 8 | $n_{\text{tree}} = 1 + n_{\text{left}} + n_{\text{right}}$ | Recursive node count | Tree metrics |

> [!NOTE]
> In formula 4: $F$ = function set arity (number of arguments), $D$ = max depth. For binary functions ($F=2$): $M_{\text{max}} = 2^D - 1$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility of Genetic Programming**

1. **Symbolic Regression** — Discovering mathematical formulas from data (used in physics, finance, biology).
2. **Automated Circuit Design** — Evolving analog and digital circuits.
3. **Robotics Control** — Evolving control policies for autonomous robots.
4. **Feature Engineering** — Automatic construction of features for ML models.
5. **Game Playing AI** — Evolving strategies for board games.
6. **Bioinformatics** — Protein structure prediction, gene regulatory networks.
7. **Financial Modeling** — Trading rule discovery.
8. **Antenna Design** — NASA evolved antenna shapes for spacecraft (real-world success case).

## 2.4 Bloat Control

> [!WARNING]
> **Bloat** is the uncontrolled growth of program tree size without corresponding fitness improvement — a critical problem in GP!

Common solutions:
- **Parsimony Pressure:** Add size penalty to fitness: $f' = f + \alpha \cdot \text{size}$
- **Size Limits:** Hard cap on tree depth or node count.
- **Tarpeian Method:** Reject oversized individuals during selection.
- **Operator Equalization:** Restrict operators that produce bloat.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — Symbolic Regression Using GP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a classic 14-mark KTU Board question pattern. Master this completely.**

**Problem:** Given 3 data points $(1, 2), (2, 4), (3, 6)$ which follow the relationship $y = 2x$, evolve a GP to discover this function.

**Setup:**
- **Terminal Set:** $T = \{x, 0, 1, 2, 3\}$
- **Function Set:** $F = \{+, -, \times, \div\}$
- **Population Size:** $M = 4$
- **Fitness:** $f_i = \sum_{j=1}^{3} \left\vert y_{ij} - t_j \right\vert$

### Initial Random Population (Generation 0)

**Individual 1:** $I_1 = (x + x)$
- Step 1: Evaluate at $x=1$: $y = 1 + 1 = 2$
- Step 2: Evaluate at $x=2$: $y = 2 + 2 = 4$
- Step 3: Evaluate at $x=3$: $y = 3 + 3 = 6$
- Step 4: Compute fitness:

$$f_1 = \left\vert 2 - 2 \right\vert + \left\vert 4 - 4 \right\vert + \left\vert 6 - 6 \right\vert = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Individual 1 is the perfect solution!** However, in realistic scenarios, we demonstrate the algorithm with imperfect individuals.

**Individual 2:** $I_2 = (x \times 3)$
- Step 1: At $x=1$: $y = 1 \times 3 = 3$, Error: $\left\vert 3 - 2 \right\vert = 1$
- Step 2: At $x=2$: $y = 2 \times 3 = 6$, Error: $\left\vert 6 - 4 \right\vert = 2$
- Step 3: At $x=3$: $y = 3 \times 3 = 9$, Error: $\left\vert 9 - 6 \right\vert = 3$

$$f_2 = 1 + 2 + 3 = 6$$

**Individual 3:** $I_3 = (x + 1)$
- At $x=1$: $y = 2$, Error: $0$
- At $x=2$: $y = 3$, Error: $1$
- At $x=3$: $y = 4$, Error: $2$

$$f_3 = 0 + 1 + 2 = 3$$

**Individual 4:** $I_4 = (2 \times x)$
- At $x=1$: $y = 2$, Error: $0$
- At $x=2$: $y = 4$, Error: $0$
- At $x=3$: $y = 6$, Error: $0$

$$f_4 = 0 + 0 + 0 = 0$$

### Selection Step (Fitness Proportionate)

$$P_i = \frac{f_i^{-1}}{\sum_{k=1}^{4} f_k^{-1}}$$

**Step 1:** Compute inverse fitness values:
$$f_1^{-1} = \frac{1}{0} = \infty \quad (\text{perfect — selected automatically})$$
$$f_2^{-1} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667$$
$$f_3^{-1} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$
$$f_4^{-1} = \frac{1}{0} = \infty \quad (\text{perfect — selected automatically})$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, individuals with zero fitness (perfect solutions) are handled with **special selection rules** (e.g., direct preservation) to avoid division by zero.

**Step 2:** For demonstration, treat $I_1$ and $I_4$ as already selected. For the remaining slots, use:

$$P_2 = \frac{0.1667}{0.1667 + 0.3333} = \frac{0.1667}{0.5000} = 0.3334$$

$$P_3 = \frac{0.3333}{0.5000} = 0.6666$$

### Crossover Step

**Parents selected:** $I_1 = (x + x)$ and $I_4 = (2 \times x)$

**Step 1:** Choose random crossover point in $I_1$: the root node $+$.
**Step 2:** Choose random crossover point in $I_4$: the left child $2$.
**Step 3:** Swap subtrees.

**Offspring 1:** $I_1' = (2 + x)$ — replacing right subtree of $I_1$ with left subtree of $I_4$.
**Offspring 2:** $I_4' = (x \times x)$ — replacing left subtree of $I_4$ with right subtree of $I_1$.

### New Generation Evaluation

**Offspring 1:** $I_1' = (2 + x)$
- At $x=1$: $y = 3$, Error: $1$
- At $x=2$: $y = 4$, Error: $0$
- At $x=3$: $y = 5$, Error: $1$

$$f_{1'} = 1 + 0 + 1 = 2$$

**Offspring 2:** $I_4' = (x \times x)$
- At $x=1$: $y = 1$, Error: $1$
- At $x=2$: $y = 4$, Error: $0$
- At $x=3$: $y = 9$, Error: $3$

$$f_{4'} = 1 + 0 + 3 = 4$$

### Convergence Observation

> [!TIP]
> The best fitness has improved from $f = 3$ to $f = 2$ in just **one generation**, demonstrating GP's efficient search capability.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Fitness Function Variations

| Fitness Type | Formula | Best Use Case | Sensitivity |
|--------------|---------|---------------|-------------|
| **Hit Count** | $f = \text{number of correct outputs}$ | Classification | Binary |
| **Sum of Absolute Error** | $f = \sum \left\vert y - t \right\vert$ | Regression | Moderate |
| **Sum of Squared Error** | $f = \sum (y - t)^2$ | Regression | High (penalizes outliers) |
| **Mean Squared Error** | $f = \frac{1}{N} \sum (y - t)^2$ | Batch comparison | Normalized |
| **Correlation Coefficient** | $f = \text{corr}(y, t)$ | Pattern matching | Scale-invariant |
| **Combined Fitness** | $f = \alpha \cdot f_1 + \beta \cdot f_2$ | Multi-objective | Tunable |

## 3.3 Derivation of Selection Probability

Starting from the basic principle of **roulette wheel selection**, the probability $P_i$ of selecting individual $i$ is proportional to its fitness.

**Step 1:** Define raw fitness $r_i$ (higher = better in this formulation).

**Step 2:** Normalize to obtain probability:
$$P_i = \frac{r_i}{\sum_{k=1}^{M} r_k}$$

**Step 3:** For minimization problems, convert using inverse:
$$r_i = \frac{1}{1 + f_i}$$

**Step 4:** Substitution:
$$P_i = \frac{\frac{1}{1 + f_i}}{\sum_{k=1}^{M} \frac{1}{1 + f_k}}$$

**Step 5:** For our example, after eliminating perfect individuals ($I_1, I_4$), substitute values:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_2 &= \frac{\frac{1}{1+6}}{\frac{1}{1+6} + \frac{1}{1+3}} \\
&= \frac{1/7}{1/7 + 1/4} \\
&= \frac{0.1429}{0.1429 + 0.2500} \\
&= \frac{0.1429}{0.3929} \\
&\approx 0.3637
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_3 &= \frac{0.2500}{0.3929} \approx 0.6363
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Verify normalization: $0.3637 + 0.6363 = 1.0000$ ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison — Convergence Rates

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks comparison questions. Memorize the trade-offs!**

| Aspect | GA | GP |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Convergence Speed** | Faster (fixed structure) | Slower (variable structure) |
| **Solution Interpretability** | Low (binary strings) | High (readable programs) |
| **Search Space** | Continuous/discrete parameters | Function space |
| **Computational Cost** | Lower per generation | Higher (tree evaluation) |
| **Best For** | Parameter optimization | Automated discovery |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Genetic Programming Evolutionary Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Initialize Random Population"] --> B["Evaluate Fitness of Each Individual"]
    B --> C{"Termination Criteria Met?"}
    C -->|"Yes"| D["OUTPUT: Best Program Tree"]
    C -->|"No"| E["Selection: Choose Parents"]
    E --> F["Apply Genetic Operators"]
    F --> G["Crossover: Swap Subtrees"]
    F --> H["Mutation: Random Subtree Replacement"]
    F --> I["Reproduction: Copy as-is"]
    G --> J["Form New Generation"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> B
```

## 4.2 Program Tree Representation (Crossover Visualization)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["Parent 1: (+ x (* x 2))"] --> P1L1["+"]
    P1L1 --> P1L1A["x"]
    P1L1 --> P1L1B["(*)"]
    P1L1B --> P1L1B1["x"]
    P1L1B --> P1L1B2["2"]
    P2["Parent 2: (- (* x x) 5)"] --> P2L1["-"]
    P2L1 --> P2L1A["(*)"]
    P2L1A --> P2L1A1["x"]
    P2L1A --> P2L1A2["x"]
    P2L1 --> P2L1B["5"]
    O1["Offspring 1: (+ x (- (* x x) 5))"] --> O1L1["+"]
    O1L1 --> O1L1A["x"]
    O1L1 --> O1L1B["(-)"]
    O1L1B --> O1L1B1["(*)"]
    O1L1B1 --> O1L1B1A["x"]
    O1L1B1 --> O1L1B1B["x"]
    O1L1B --> O1L1B2["5"]
    O2["Offspring 2: (- (* x 2) 5)"] --> O2L1["-"]
    O2L1 --> O2L1A["(*)"]
    O2L1A --> O2L1A1["x"]
    O2L1A --> O2L1A2["2"]
    O2L1 --> O2L1B["5"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Genetic Programming
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

> **Model Answer:**
> 
> Genetic Programming (GP) is an evolutionary computation technique that automatically generates computer programs to solve problems. It is an extension of Genetic Algorithms where the individuals being evolved are **tree-structured programs** rather than fixed-length binary strings. GP uses operations inspired by biological evolution — **selection, crossover, and mutation** — on a population of program trees, with a **fitness function** guiding the search toward optimal solutions. The goal of GP is to evolve a program that performs a given task without being explicitly programmed, making it a form of **automated machine learning**.

### Question 2: Differentiate Genetic Programming from Genetic Algorithms
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Exam Pattern]**

> **Model Answer:**
> 
> | Aspect | Genetic Algorithm (GA) | Genetic Programming (GP) |
> |--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
> | **Representation** | Fixed-length binary strings | Variable-length tree structures |
> | **Solution Output** | Numerical parameter values | Executable programs or expressions |
> | **Search Space** | Parameter space | Function/program space |
> | **Complexity** | Simpler chromosome structure | Hierarchical, nested structure |
> | **Application Domain** | Optimization problems | Symbolic regression, program synthesis |
> | **Crossover** | Single-point / multi-point | Subtree exchange |
> 
> In essence, GA optimizes parameters of a **predefined structure**, while GP optimizes the **structure itself** along with parameters.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Symbolic Regression Using GP (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Exam Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the working of Genetic Programming with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **terminal set**, **function set**, and **fitness function** in GP. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using Genetic Programming, evolve a solution to find a function that maps $x$ to $y$ for the following data: $(1, 3), (2, 5), (3, 7)$. Assume the function set $F = \{+, -\}$ and terminal set $T = \{x, 0, 1, 2\}$. Use sum of absolute errors as fitness. Show at least **two generations**. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution for (a):**
> 
> **Working of Genetic Programming:**
> 
> GP follows an iterative evolutionary cycle consisting of 6 stages:
> 
> 1. **Initialization:** A population of $M$ random program trees is generated using methods like Full, Grow, or Ramped Half-and-Half.
> 2. **Fitness Evaluation:** Each program is executed on fitness cases, and its output is compared with target values using a fitness function.
> 3. **Selection:** Individuals are selected probabilistically based on fitness (higher fitness = higher selection probability).
> 4. **Genetic Operations:** Selected parents undergo **crossover** (subtree exchange) and **mutation** (random subtree replacement).
> 5. **Replacement:** New offspring replace old individuals to form the next generation.
> 6. **Termination:** The loop ends when a satisfactory solution is found or a maximum number of generations is reached.
> 
> **Role of Key Components:**
> 
> - **Terminal Set ($T$):** The set of leaf nodes (variables, constants) that provide input data. Example: $T = \{x, 1, 2, 3\}$.
> - **Function Set ($F$):** The set of internal nodes (arithmetic operators, mathematical functions) used to build programs. Example: $F = \{+, -, \times, \div, \sin, \cos\}$.
> - **Fitness Function ($f$):** A quantitative measure of how well a program solves the problem. Common choices: Sum of Absolute Error, Mean Squared Error, Hit Count.
> 
> The choice of $F$ and $T$ determines the **expressive power** of the GP system — the broader these sets, the more problems GP can solve.

> **Model Solution for (b):**
> 
> **Setup:**
> - Terminal Set: $T = \{x, 0, 1, 2\}$
> - Function Set: $F = \{+, -\}$
> - Target: $y = 2x + 1$ (line through given points)
> - Fitness: $f_i = \sum_{j=1}^{3} \left\vert y_{ij} - t_j \right\vert$
> - Population Size: $M = 4$
> 
> **Generation 0 — Random Population:**
> 
> | Individual | Expression | At x=1 | At x=2 | At x=3 | Fitness |
> |------------|------------|--------|--------|--------|---------|
> | $I_1$ | $x + 1$ | 2 | 3 | 4 | $\left\vert 2-3 \right\vert+\left\vert 3-5 \right\vert+\left\vert 4-7 \right\vert = 1+2+3 = 6$ |
> | $I_2$ | $x - 0$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | $\left\vert 1-3 \right\vert+\left\vert 2-5 \right\vert+\left\vert 3-7 \right\vert = 2+3+4 = 9$ |
> | $I_3$ | $2 + x$ | 3 | 4 | 5 | $\left\vert 3-3 \right\vert+\left\vert 4-5 \right\vert+\left\vert 5-7 \right\vert = 0+1+2 = 3$ |
> | $I_4$ | $x + 2$ | 3 | 4 | 5 | $\left\vert 3-3 \right\vert+\left\vert 4-5 \right\vert+\left\vert 5-7 \right\vert = 0+1+2 = 3$ |
> 
> **Selection (Fitness Proportionate):**
> 
> $$\begin{aligned}
> P(I_3) &= \frac{1/3}{1/6 + 1/9 + 1/3 + 1/3} \\
> &= \frac{0.3333}{0.1667 + 0.1111 + 0.3333 + 0.3333} \\
> &= \frac{0.3333}{0.9444} \\
> &\approx 0.353
> \end{aligned}$$
> 
> Selected Parents: $I_3$ and $I_4$ (both have best fitness).
> 
> **Crossover:**
> - Parent 1: $I_3 = (2 + x)$ — Crossover point: root $+$
> - Parent 2: $I_4 = (x + 2)$ — Crossover point: left child $x$
> - Swap: Right child of $I_3$ ($x$) with left child of $I_4$ ($x$).
> 
> **Offspring:** Since both swapped subtrees are identical ($x$ and $x$), the offspring are:
> - $O_1 = (2 + x)$ (same as $I_3$)
> - $O_2 = (x + 2)$ (same as $I_4$)
> 
> **Generation 1:** Apply mutation to $I_1$: replace terminal $1$ with $2$.
> - $I_1' = x + 2$ → Fitness = $0 + 1 + 2 = 3$ ✓
> 
> **Convergence:** Best fitness has stabilized at $f = 3$, indicating a near-optimal solution $y = 2 + x$ (close to actual $y = 2x + 1$).

### Question B: GP Operators and Bloat Control (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the genetic operators used in Genetic Programming — **crossover** and **mutation** — with suitable tree diagrams. Compare subtree crossover with one-point crossover. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **bloat phenomenon** in Genetic Programming. Explain at least three techniques to control bloat, with their mathematical formulations. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution for (a):**
> 
> **Crossover (Subtree Crossover) in GP:**
> 
> Crossover in GP exchanges **random subtrees** between two parent programs.
> 
> **Algorithm:**
> 1. Select two parents $P_1$ and $P_2$ from the population using selection.
> 2. In $P_1$, randomly select an internal node (crossover point $C_1$).
> 3. In $P_2$, randomly select an internal node (crossover point $C_2$).
> 4. Create offspring $O_1$ by replacing subtree at $C_1$ with the subtree at $C_2$.
> 5. Create offspring $O_2$ by replacing subtree at $C_2$ with the subtree at $C_1$.
> 
> **Example:**
> - Parent 1: $(+ \; x \; (\times \; x \; 2))$
> - Parent 2: $(- \; (\times \; x \; x) \; 5)$
> - Crossover point in $P_1$: subtree $(\times \; x \; 2)$
> - Crossover point in $P_2$: subtree $5$
> - Offspring 1: $(+ \; x \; 5)$
> - Offspring 2: $(- \; (\times \; x \; x) \; (\times \; x \; 2))$
> 
> **Comparison with One-Point Crossover (GA):**
> 
> | Aspect | One-Point Crossover (GA) | Subtree Crossover (GP) |
> |--------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
> | **Crossover Points** | Single position in string | Random internal node in tree |
> | **Exchanged Material** | Fixed-length bit segment | Variable-size subtree |
> | **Chromosome Length** | Constant | Variable |
> | **Semantic Effect** | Local parameter swap | Structural transformation |
> | **Probability** | Single crossover per pair | Multiple possible crossover points |
> 
> **Mutation in GP:**
> 
> Mutation randomly alters a portion of a program tree to maintain genetic diversity.
> 
> **Types of GP Mutation:**
> 
> 1. **Subtree Mutation:** Replace a randomly selected subtree with a new randomly generated subtree.
> 2. **Point Mutation:** Replace a single node (function or terminal) with another of the same arity.
> 3. **Hoist Mutation:** Replace a parent node with one of its randomly chosen descendants.
> 4. **Permutation Mutation:** Rearrange the order of arguments in a function node.
> 
> **Probability Settings:** $p_c = 0.7$ to $0.9$, $p_m = 0.01$ to $0.1$ (standard).

> **Model Solution for (b):**
> 
> **Bloat Phenomenon in GP:**
> 
> Bloat refers to the **excessive growth of program tree size** (number of nodes) over generations **without corresponding improvement in fitness**. This wastes computational resources and may lead to overfitting.
> 
> **Causes of Bloat:**
> 1. **Intron Growth:** Evolution of "junk code" that doesn't affect program output.
> 2. **Fitness Pressure:** Slight fitness improvements require large structural changes.
> 3. **Crossover Bias:** Larger trees have more crossover points, increasing their reproduction probability.
> 
> **Techniques to Control Bloat:**
> 
> **1. Parsimony Pressure (Size Penalty):**
> 
> $$\begin{aligned}
> f'_i &= f_i + \alpha \cdot \text{size}(T_i) \\
> \end{aligned}$$
> 
> Where $\alpha$ is the parsimony coefficient (small positive constant), and $\text{size}(T_i)$ is the number of nodes in tree $i$.
> 
> **2. Hard Size Limit (Depth/Node Cap):**
> 
> $$\text{depth}(T_i) \leq D_{\text{max}} \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{nodes}(T_i) \leq N_{\text{max}}$$
> 
> Offspring exceeding limits are rejected or repaired.
> 
> **3. Tarpeian Method:**
> 
> $$\text{If } \text{size}(T_i) > L \text{ then set } f'_i = \infty \text{ (worst fitness)}$$
> 
> Oversized individuals are given worst possible fitness with probability $p_t$, effectively removing them from selection.
> 
> **4. Operator Equalization:**
> 
> Track the average size change due to each operator and discourage operators that cause excessive growth.
> 
> **5. Lexicographic Parsimony Pressure (Multi-objective):**
> 
> $$\text{Sort by } (f, \text{size}) \text{ in lexicographic order — prefer smaller size on ties.}$$
> 
> **Conclusion:** Bloat control is essential for **practical, efficient GP**. The choice of method depends on the problem domain and computational constraints.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in GP Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing GA and GP representations** — Writing "binary string" for GP instead of "tree structure" loses 1-2 marks immediately.
> 
> 2. **Skipping fitness computation steps** — Examiners expect **every fitness case to be evaluated explicitly**. Writing only the formula without numerical substitution loses 50% of the marks allocated to the fitness section.
> 
> 3. **Not showing crossover point selection** — In crossover questions, you **must** indicate the exact nodes where crossover occurs; otherwise the diagram is incomplete.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting the termination condition** — A complete GP loop must include a termination criterion; omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Missing parsimony formula** — For bloat-related questions, the modified fitness equation $f' = f + \alpha \cdot \text{size}$ is mandatory; verbal explanation alone is insufficient.
> 
> 6. **Confusing function set with terminal set** — These are distinct components; using them interchangeably in definitions loses 1 mark.
> 
> 7. **Not mentioning Koza** — The founder of GP (John Koza, 1992) is a **favorite one-liner** in KTU boards. Always cite him.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Genetic Programming**

- ✅ **Definition:** GP evolves **computer programs** (tree structures) using evolutionary operators.
- ✅ **Founder:** John R. Koza (1992).
- ✅ **Key Difference from GA:** GP uses **variable-length trees** instead of fixed-length binary strings.
- ✅ **Terminal Set ($T$):** Leaves of the tree — variables and constants.
- ✅ **Function Set ($F$):** Internal nodes — operators like $+$, $-$, $\times$, $\div$, $\sin$, etc.
- ✅ **Initialization Methods:** **Full** (uniform depth), **Grow** (varied shape), **Ramped Half-and-Half** (standard).
- ✅ **Fitness Function:** Quantifies how well a program solves the problem (e.g., $f = \sum \left\vert y - t \right\vert$).
- ✅ **Selection:** Roulette wheel / Tournament / Rank-based.
- ✅ **Crossover:** Exchange of random subtrees between two parents.
- ✅ **Mutation:** Random replacement of a subtree or node.
- ✅ **Bloat:** Uncontrolled growth of tree size without fitness improvement.
- ✅ **Bloat Control:** Parsimony pressure, hard size limits, Tarpeian method.
- ✅ **Standard Probabilities:** $p_c \in [0.7, 0.9]$, $p_m \in [0.01, 0.1]$.
- ✅ **Applications:** Symbolic regression, circuit design, robotics, feature engineering, financial modeling.
- ✅ **Termination Criteria:** Max generations, satisfactory fitness, or convergence plateau.
- ✅ **In KTU 2024 Scheme:** GP falls under **Evolutionary Machine Learning** in Module 4 of PECBT414.
- ✅ **Examiner's Hint:** Always include a **block diagram** of the GP loop in 7-mark theory questions.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For symbolic regression problems, always compute fitness **case by case** showing each intermediate error. This demonstrates clear understanding and fetches full marks in the valuation key.
